149 entities
GASTROINTESTINAL STROMAL TUMOUR (GIST)
LGI Mesenchymal Spindle cell Variable
Derived from interstitial cells of Cajal (pacemaker cells of GI tract).
Clinical Context
  • Derived from interstitial cells of Cajal (pacemaker cells of GI tract).
  • Most common GI mesenchymal tumour in adults.
  • Stomach 60%, small bowel 30%, colorectum/oesophagus <10%.
  • Intramural, submucosal, or subserosal location.
  • Most often spindled (70%); epithelioid (20%) or mixed.
  • Trigger: incidental mass; GI bleed; obstruction.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Spindle cells in short fascicles/storiform pattern; perinuclear vacuolation (cytoplasmic vacuoles flanking nucleus) — characteristic.
  • Skeinoid fibres (extracellular collagen globules) — especially small intestinal GISTs; nuclear palisading may mimic schwannoma.
  • Epithelioid variant: round/polygonal cells, clear or eosinophilic cytoplasm; multinodular growth in SDH-deficient; multinucleate giant cells.
✚ Positive
CD117 (cKIT) – 95%DOG-1 – 95-98% (most sensitive; retained post-imatinib)CD34 – 60-70%SMA – 30% focalPKC-theta – diffuse cytoplasmic
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
S100 – <2%Desmin – <5%Keratin – negativeNote: CD117-negative GISTs exist (SDH-deficient; PDGFRA D842V — DOG1 retained)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
GI Schwannoma⚡S100+, SOX10+; CD117−, DOG1−; peritumoural lymphoid cuff; no Antoni A/B
Leiomyoma↗Desmin+, h-caldesmon+, SMA+; CD117−, DOG1−; pink fibrillar cytoplasm
Inflammatory Fibroid Polyp⚡CD34+, PDGFRA mut; CD117−, DOG1−; eosinophil-rich stroma; perivascular whorling
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Prognostication: size + mitotic count (per 5mm²/20HPF, highest density area) + site. Use NIH/Miettinen criteria.
  • KIT exon 11 del/ins (50%) – most common; best imatinib response. Exon 9 – double-dose imatinib. Exon 17/18 – imatinib resistance.
  • PDGFRA exon 18 D842V (10%) – good prognosis; imatinib-resistant → avapritinib.
  • Wild-type (10%): SDH-deficient, NF1-associated, BRAF V600E (~10% of wt), NTRK-fused — all imatinib-insensitive.
  • SDH-deficient: young adults/children; gastric; multinodular/multifocal; LVI common but indolent; lymph node mets (unique). SDHB IHC loss = surrogate.
  • Syndromic: NF1 (multiple small bowel GISTs); Carney triad (GIST + pulmonary chondroma + paraganglioma); Carney-Stratakis (GIST + paraganglioma, SDH germline, AD).
  • NACT: imatinib pre-op to downsize. ACT: all high-risk (adjuvant imatinib 3 years).
  • Post-imatinib: CD117 may be lost; DOG1 retained. No lymph node mets except SDH-deficient.
  • Surgical margins most important prognostic factor in non-metastatic disease. Mutational analysis mandatory for all high-risk/metastatic.
Related / See Also
⚡ GI Schwannoma↗ Leiomyoma⚡ Inflammatory Fibroid Polyp
GI SCHWANNOMA
LGI Mesenchymal Spindle cell Benign
Benign peripheral nerve sheath tumour of Schwannian differentiation.
Clinical Context
  • Benign peripheral nerve sheath tumour of Schwannian differentiation.
  • Stomach 60-70%, then colorectum; oesophagus rare.
  • Adults; no sex predilection.
  • Often incidental — radiologists frequently call these GISTs.
  • No NF2 association (unlike conventional schwannoma).
Key Microscopic Features
  • Spindle cells in loose fascicles; uniform elongated nuclei; NO Antoni A/B, NO Verocay bodies, NO thick-walled hyalinised vessels.
  • Characteristic peritumoural lymphoid cuff — virtually diagnostic.
  • Variants: microcystic/reticular (GFAP+); signet-ring cell variant (intracytoplasmic mucin, EMA−).
✚ Positive
S100 – diffuse strongSOX10 – diffuseGFAP – variable (strong in microcystic variant)CD34 – focal/stromal only
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CD117 – negativeDOG1 – negativeDesmin – negativeSMA – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
GIST↗CD117+, DOG1+; no lymphoid cuff; skeinoid fibres; perinuclear vacuolation
Leiomyoma↗Desmin+, SMA+, h-caldesmon+; pink fibrillar cytoplasm; S100−
Conventional Schwannoma↗Antoni A/B, Verocay bodies, thick vessels, NF2 mutation; rarely in GI tract
Neurofibroma↗Wavy collagen ('shredded carrot'); entrapped axons (NF+); CD34+ stromal cells; less uniform S100; plexiform variant pathognomonic NF1; NF2 mutation absent in both
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Benign; no recurrence after complete excision. No NF2 mutation — distinct from peripheral schwannoma.
  • No malignant variant described in GI tract.
  • Microcystic/reticular subtype: GFAP+, alarming morphology but still benign.
  • Signet-ring cell variant: intracytoplasmic mucin; S100+ distinguishes from mucinous carcinoma.
  • H. pylori gastritis association reported (controversial).
Related / See Also
↗ NEUROFIBROMA↗ GIST↗ Leiomyoma↗ Conventional Schwannoma↗ Neurofibroma
LEIOMYOMA (GI)
LGI Mesenchymal Spindle cell Benign 📋 2×
Most common mesenchymal tumour of oesophagus (muscularis propria) and colorectum (muscularis mucosae).
Clinical Context
  • Most common mesenchymal tumour of oesophagus (muscularis propria) and colorectum (muscularis mucosae).
  • Stomach uncommon (unlike GIST). Anorectal: muscularis mucosae, women.
  • Adults; oesophageal favours men.
  • Usually incidental; may present as intramural mass or polyp.
  • Syndromic: oesophago-gastric leiomyomatosis in Alport syndrome (COL4A5/COL4A6 deletion at Xq22).
Key Microscopic Features
  • Fascicles of bland spindle cells with abundant eosinophilic fibrillar cytoplasm (pink at low power); cigar-shaped blunt-ended nuclei.
  • Low cellularity, minimal nuclear atypia, no/rare mitoses (<1/50HPF), no necrosis.
  • Secondary hyperplasia of interstitial cells of Cajal at periphery (focal CD117+ staining — do not misdiagnose as GIST).
✚ Positive
SMA – diffuseDesmin – diffuseh-caldesmon – diffuseCalponin – positive
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CD117 – negative (except ICC hyperplasia periphery)DOG1 – negativeS100 – negativeCD34 – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
GIST↗CD117+/DOG1+; perinuclear vacuolation; skeinoid fibres; smooth muscle markers absent/focal
Leiomyosarcoma↗Cytological atypia, mitoses ≥5/50HPF, necrosis; same IHC profile
GI Schwannoma⚡S100+, SOX10+, lymphoid cuff; Desmin/SMA−
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Benign; no malignant transformation in GI tract (unlike uterine smooth muscle tumours).
  • ICC hyperplasia at periphery: focal CD117 — do not misdiagnose as GIST; DOG1 negative, context is key.
  • Alport syndrome leiomyomatosis: COL4A5-COL4A6 contiguous deletion; oesophageal thickening.
  • EBV-associated smooth muscle tumour (EBV-SMT): immunocompromised; SMA/Desmin+; EBER ISH+ — distinct entity.
Related / See Also
↗ GIST↗ Leiomyosarcoma⚡ GI Schwannoma
LEIOMYOSARCOMA (GI)
LGI Mesenchymal Spindle cell Malignant 📋 1×
Rare in GI tract; most retroperitoneal/somatic soft tissue.
Clinical Context
  • Rare in GI tract; most retroperitoneal/somatic soft tissue.
  • Stomach and small bowel most common GI sites; can arise from muscularis propria or vascular wall.
  • Adults (median 6th-7th decade).
  • IMPORTANT: exclude GIST first — most spindle cell GI sarcomas are GIST.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Intersecting fascicles of spindle cells at right angles; moderate-to-severe nuclear atypia; hyperchromatic cigar-shaped nuclei.
  • Mitoses ≥5/50HPF (often very high); atypical mitoses; coagulative necrosis common.
  • Eosinophilic fibrillar cytoplasm; epithelioid morphology possible (worse prognosis).
✚ Positive
SMA – diffuseDesmin – diffuse (may be focal)h-caldesmon – diffuse (most specific)Calponin – positive
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CD117 – negativeDOG1 – negativeS100 – negativeMDM2/CDK4 – negative (excludes dedifferentiated LPS)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
GIST↗CD117+, DOG1+; KIT/PDGFRA mutation; smooth muscle markers absent/focal
Dedifferentiated LiposarcomaMDM2+, CDK4+; MDM2 amplification (FISH); retroperitoneal; heterologous LMS component
IMT↗ALK+; inflammatory infiltrate (plasma cells); less atypia; younger patients
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Grade by FNCLCC: differentiation + mitotic count + necrosis.
  • Surgery primary; wide excision. Chemotherapy (doxorubicin ± ifosfamide) for metastatic/unresectable.
  • No KIT/PDGFRA mutations; TP53 alterations common; RB1 loss.
  • Prognosis poor for high-grade; 5-year OS ~50% localised, ~15% metastatic.
  • Vascular leiomyosarcoma (IVC, pulmonary veins): distinct site-specific entity.
Related / See Also
↗ GIST+ Add: Dedifferentiated Liposarcoma↗ IMT
INFLAMMATORY FIBROID POLYP (IFP)
LGI Mesenchymal Myxoid Benign
Benign intramural/submucosal lesion; not a true inflammatory process.
Clinical Context
  • Benign intramural/submucosal lesion; not a true inflammatory process.
  • Stomach (antrum) most common; also terminal ileum (can cause intussusception); colorectum rare.
  • Adults; mean age ~50.
  • Ileum: intussusception (lead point) — classic trigger phrase.
  • Associated with H. pylori (gastric).
Key Microscopic Features
  • Loose oedematous myxoid stroma with bland spindle/stellate cells; 'onion-skin' perivascular whorling — characteristic.
  • Prominent eosinophilic infiltrate throughout stroma; mast cells.
  • Thin-walled vessels; no atypia; no/rare mitoses.
✚ Positive
CD34 – diffuseVimentin – positiveSMA – focal/weak only
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CD117 – negative (key GIST exclusion)DOG1 – negativeS100 – negativeDesmin – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
GIST↗CD117+, DOG1+; no eosinophil-rich stroma; no perivascular whorling
Eosinophilic GI Disorder↗Diffuse mucosal eosinophilia without spindle cell component
GI Schwannoma⚡S100+; lymphoid cuff; no eosinophil-rich stroma
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • PDGFRA mutations (exon 12, 14, 18) — same gene as GIST but different location/biology; benign.
  • Endoscopic or surgical resection curative; no recurrence. No malignant potential.
  • Ileal IFP is classic cause of adult intussusception.
  • H. pylori eradication may resolve small gastric IFPs.
Related / See Also
↗ GIST↗ Eosinophilic GI Disorder⚡ GI Schwannoma
DESMOID TUMOUR / INTRA-ABDOMINAL FIBROMATOSIS
LGI Mesenchymal Fibrous/collagenous Intermediate
Locally aggressive myofibroblastic neoplasm; no metastatic potential.
Clinical Context
  • Locally aggressive myofibroblastic neoplasm; no metastatic potential.
  • Mesenteric: 50% associated with FAP/APC (Gardner); sporadic ~50% (CTNNB1).
  • FAP-associated: younger; follows colectomy; mesentery; multiple.
  • Sporadic: young women; abdominal wall; prior trauma/surgery/pregnancy.
  • Trigger: FAP patient with abdominal mass post-colectomy; Gardner syndrome.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Long sweeping fascicles of bland fibroblasts/myofibroblasts with abundant collagen; low-to-moderate cellularity.
  • Uniform elongated nuclei; small nucleoli; vesicular chromatin; no significant atypia; no/rare mitoses; no necrosis.
  • Infiltrative margins — tongues into surrounding fat/muscle; may encase mesenteric vessels.
✚ Positive
Nuclear beta-catenin – positive (CTNNB1/APC mutation surrogate)SMA – variableVimentin – positive
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
S100 – negativeCD117 – negativeDOG1 – negativeSTAT6 – negative (excludes SFT)MUC4 – negative (excludes LGFMS)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Solitary Fibrous Tumour↗STAT6+, CD34+, NAB2-STAT6 fusion; hemangiopericytoma pattern; no infiltrative margins
LGFMS↗MUC4+, FUS-CREB3L2; alternating fibrous/myxoid zones; no nuclear beta-catenin
IMT↗ALK+; inflammatory infiltrate; younger patients; less collagenous
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • APC (FAP): germline APC → nuclear beta-catenin. Test APC if no known FAP.
  • Sporadic: CTNNB1 exon 3 hotspot (T41A, S45F — worse prognosis; higher recurrence).
  • Active surveillance preferred over surgery (surgery can trigger growth).
  • Systemic: sulindac/tamoxifen; sorafenib/imatinib for progressive disease.
  • Monitor with MRI (no radiation). Local recurrence up to 70%; distant spread does not occur.
  • Screen all desmoid patients for FAP. MDT essential before any resection.
Related / See Also
↗ Solitary Fibrous Tumour↗ LGFMS↗ IMT
INFLAMMATORY MYOFIBROBLASTIC TUMOUR (IMT)
LGI Mesenchymal Myxoid Intermediate 📋 1×
Intermediate malignant potential: ~25% local recurrence; rare distant mets (~5%).
Clinical Context
  • Intermediate malignant potential: ~25% local recurrence; rare distant mets (~5%).
  • Children and young adults (median ~10 years); most common mesenchymal tumour of lung in children.
  • Mesentery, omentum, retroperitoneum, lung; GI primary uncommon.
  • Systemic symptoms: fever, weight loss, anaemia, thrombocytosis (paraneoplastic IL-6).
  • Trigger: young patient, soft tissue mass, systemic inflammatory symptoms.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Three patterns: (1) myxoid/vascular — loose stroma, abundant plasma cells + lymphocytes; (2) compact spindle cell — dense fascicles; (3) hypocellular fibrous — collagen-rich.
  • Spindle to epithelioid myofibroblasts; vesicular nuclei; prominent nucleoli; mild-to-moderate atypia.
  • Mixed inflammatory infiltrate (plasma cells, lymphocytes, eosinophils) — always present.
✚ Positive
ALK – 50-60% (cytoplasmic, perinuclear, or nuclear depending on fusion partner)SMA – diffuseDesmin – variableVimentin – positive
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CD117 – negativeDOG1 – negativeS100 – negativeCD34 – negativeSTAT6 – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Desmoid FibromatosisNuclear beta-catenin+; no inflammatory infiltrate; paucicellular collagenous; ALK−
Leiomyosarcoma↗Significant atypia; mitoses; no inflammatory infiltrate; h-caldesmon+; ALK−
Follicular Dendritic Cell Sarcoma↗CD21+, CD35+; storiform; Castleman association; whorling around lymphoid aggregates
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • ALK rearrangements 50-60%: partners include TPM3, TPM4, CLTC, RANBP2, EML4 (50+ described).
  • ALK FISH (break-apart) for confirmation; RNA-seq if ALK IHC+/FISH−.
  • ALK-negative IMT: NTRK, ROS1, RET, PDGFRB fusions — use RNA-seq panel.
  • RANBP2-ALK: epithelioid IMT; perinuclear staining; more aggressive.
  • ALK inhibitors (crizotinib, entrectinib) for unresectable/metastatic ALK+ disease.
  • Prognosis excellent for resectable; aggressive epithelioid variant worst prognosis.
Related / See Also
+ Add: Desmoid Fibromatosis↗ Leiomyosarcoma↗ Follicular Dendritic Cell Sarcoma
GLOMUS TUMOUR (GI)
UGI Mesenchymal Perivascular Benign
Perivascular smooth muscle neoplasm differentiating toward glomus cells.
Clinical Context
  • Perivascular smooth muscle neoplasm differentiating toward glomus cells.
  • Stomach is the most common GI site (>90%); antrum/body.
  • Adults; wide age range; no sex predilection.
  • Usually submucosal; often incidental endoscopic finding.
  • Trigger: gastric submucosal mass, GIST excluded on IHC.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Sheets/nests of monotonous round cells; central round nuclei, inconspicuous nucleoli; pale-to-clear cytoplasm with sharp cell borders.
  • Prominent perivascular growth — tumour cells surround capillary-sized thin-walled vessels.
  • Hyalinised/myxoid stroma; no nuclear atypia; mitoses rare; no necrosis.
✚ Positive
SMA – diffuseCollagen IV – pericellular (around individual cells) — distinctiveLaminin – pericellularCalponin – positive
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CD117 – negativeDOG1 – negativeS100 – negativeSynaptophysin/Chromogranin – negativeDesmin – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
GIST (epithelioid)CD117+, DOG1+; KIT/PDGFRA mutation; Collagen IV not pericellular
Well-differentiated NET G1/G2⚡Synaptophysin+, Chromogranin+; salt-and-pepper nuclei; no SMA; no pericellular ColIV
Paraganglioma↗S100+ sustentacular cells; Synaptophysin+; Zellballen pattern; no SMA
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Majority benign. Malignant criteria: size >2cm + deep location, OR mitoses >5/50HPF, OR atypical mitoses, OR moderate-to-high nuclear grade.
  • No recurrent molecular alterations in most; rare NOTCH alterations in malignant.
  • Surgery curative for benign; no adjuvant therapy needed.
  • Collagen IV pericellular staining is key discriminator from other round cell tumours.
Related / See Also
+ Add: GIST (epithelioid)⚡ Well-differentiated NET G1/G2↗ Paraganglioma
PLEXIFORM FIBROMYXOMA
UGI Mesenchymal Myxoid Benign
Previously 'plexiform angiomyxoid myofibroblastic tumour (PAMT)'.
Clinical Context
  • Previously 'plexiform angiomyxoid myofibroblastic tumour (PAMT)'.
  • Stomach — antrum and pylorus almost exclusively (>95%).
  • Adults; wide age range.
  • Present: obstruction, bleeding, incidental.
  • Trigger: antral/pyloric intramural mass, GIST excluded.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Multinodular/plexiform architecture: multiple rounded nodules of bland spindle cells in myxoid stroma separated by fibromuscular septa — the plexiform pattern.
  • Prominent thin-walled vessels within each nodule; low cellularity.
  • No nuclear atypia; no/rare mitoses; no necrosis.
✚ Positive
SMA – variable, focal-to-diffuseCD34 – focalVimentin – positive
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CD117 – negativeDOG1 – negativeS100 – negativeDesmin – negativeALK – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
GIST↗CD117+, DOG1+; fascicular/storiform; perinuclear vacuolation; non-plexiform
Inflammatory Fibroid Polyp⚡CD34+, eosinophil-rich stroma; perivascular whorling; PDGFRA mutation
GI Schwannoma⚡S100+, SOX10+, lymphoid cuff; not plexiform multinodular
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Benign; no recurrence after complete excision. No malignant counterpart described.
  • No known molecular alterations (PDGFRA, KIT, SDH all wild-type).
  • Antral location + plexiform multinodular architecture + myxoid stroma = virtually diagnostic.
  • Small biopsies may be insufficient — plexiform pattern only apparent on resection.
Related / See Also
↗ GIST⚡ Inflammatory Fibroid Polyp⚡ GI Schwannoma
SYNOVIAL SARCOMA (GI)
LGI Mesenchymal Biphasic Malignant 📋 1×
Rare in GI tract; defined by SS18-SSX translocation regardless of site.
Clinical Context
  • Rare in GI tract; defined by SS18-SSX translocation regardless of site.
  • Oesophagus and stomach most reported GI sites.
  • Young adults (peak 15-40 years); male predominance.
  • Trigger: young adult, spindle cell malignancy — always exclude SS18-SSX fusion.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Monophasic (most common in GI): dense sheets/fascicles of uniform spindle cells; scant cytoplasm; herringbone pattern; hemangiopericytoma-like vasculature.
  • Biphasic: spindle cell + epithelial component (glands/nests/cords); both share fusion.
  • Mast cells often prominent; focal calcification; necrosis in high-grade.
✚ Positive
TLE1 – diffuse nuclear (~95% sensitive — best screening marker)EMA – focal-to-diffuseBCL2 – diffuseCD99 – diffuse membranousCK – focal (monophasic) to diffuse (biphasic)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CD117 – negativeSMA – negative or focal onlyDesmin – negativeSOX10 – negative (helpful vs MPNST)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
GIST↗CD117+, DOG1+; KIT/PDGFRA mutation; TLE1−
MPNST↗S100 focal/lost; SOX10+; NF1 association; loss of H3K27me3; no SS18-SSX
Solitary Fibrous Tumour↗STAT6+, CD34+; hemangiopericytoma pattern; NAB2-STAT6 fusion
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • SS18-SSX1, SS18-SSX2, or SS18-SSX4 fusion — define the entity (FISH or RNA-seq).
  • SS18-SSX1: more often biphasic; SS18-SSX2: more often monophasic.
  • TLE1 IHC: excellent screening but can be focally positive in SFT and MPNST — confirm with molecular testing.
  • Aggressive: high local recurrence; distant mets (lung, bone, nodes).
  • Ifosfamide-based chemotherapy (higher sensitivity than most sarcomas).
  • Prognosis: 5-year OS ~50-60% for localised. Radiotherapy for positive/close margins.
  • HDAC inhibitor (entinostat) under investigation.
Related / See Also
↗ GIST↗ MPNST↗ Solitary Fibrous Tumour
LOW-GRADE APPENDICEAL MUCINOUS NEOPLASM (LAMN)
LGI Appendiceal Mucinous Variable
Often incidental; can present as 'mucocele' on imaging.
Clinical Context
  • Often incidental; can present as 'mucocele' on imaging.
  • Any age; mean 50s-60s.
  • Risk of pseudomyxoma peritonei (PMP) even after appendicectomy.
  • KRAS (codon 12/13) and GNAS mutations most common.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Villous/flat mucinous epithelium with low-grade cytology: compressed pseudostratified columnar cells, nuclei basal.
  • Broad pushing border with obliteration of lamina propria and muscularis mucosae; submucosal fibrosis — key feature.
  • Mucin may dissect into wall or beyond serosa (with or without epithelium); loss of normal appendiceal architecture.
✚ Positive
N/A — morphological diagnosis
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
N/A
Top Differentials + Discriminators
HAMN↗Complex micropapillary/cribriform architecture; high-grade cytological atypia; same pushing invasion
Mucinous Adenocarcinoma (appendiceal)Infiltrative invasion; desmoplasia; signet ring cells; complex growth; lymph node risk
Retention cyst / simple mucoceleLined by flat non-neoplastic epithelium; no atypia; benign
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Staging: pTis(LAMN) if confined to mucosa; pT3 through muscularis into periappendiceal fat; pT4a if serosa involved.
  • Acellular peritoneal deposits → pM1a (lower risk); cellular deposits → pM1b (higher risk).
  • Pseudomyxoma peritonei: treatment = CRS + HIPEC.
  • Appendicectomy sufficient if confined; right hemicolectomy NOT required for LAMN.
  • Prognosis stage-dependent; acellular PMP (G1) best prognosis.
Related / See Also
↗ HAMN+ Add: Mucinous Adenocarcinoma (appendiceal)+ Add: Retention cyst / simple mucocele
HIGH-GRADE APPENDICEAL MUCINOUS NEOPLASM (HAMN)
LGI Appendiceal Mucinous Variable
Rare; defined by high-grade cytological atypia OR complex architecture with pushing borders.
Clinical Context
  • Rare; defined by high-grade cytological atypia OR complex architecture with pushing borders.
  • Similar demographics to LAMN.
  • Behaves like LAMN (pushing invasion) but higher risk if perforated.
  • Staged as CIS (pTis) like LAMN.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Similar to LAMN: pushing border, obliterated muscularis mucosae, mucinous epithelium.
  • Complex architecture: micropapillary or cribriform growth; AND/OR high-grade cytological features (loss of polarity, prominent nucleoli).
  • Infiltrative invasion absent — if present → reclassify as adenocarcinoma.
✚ Positive
N/A — morphological diagnosis
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
N/A
Top Differentials + Discriminators
LAMN↗Low-grade cytology; simpler architecture; same staging framework
Mucinous Adenocarcinoma (appendiceal)Infiltrative invasion; desmoplasia; signet ring cells; lymph node/systemic mets
Conventional AdenocarcinomaColonic-type, non-mucinous, different molecular biology
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • KRAS, GNAS mutations (same as LAMN).
  • Staged identically to LAMN: pTis(HAMN) if mucosa; same T-staging framework.
  • Risk of PMP if perforated; cellular peritoneal deposits = G2 PMP.
  • True extraluminal mucin: neovascularisation, tissue response, dissecting mucin = confirmed extravasation.
  • CRS + HIPEC for peritoneal disease.
Related / See Also
↗ LAMN+ Add: Mucinous Adenocarcinoma (appendiceal)+ Add: Conventional Adenocarcinoma
GOBLET CELL ADENOCARCINOMA (GCA)
LGI Appendiceal Amphicrine Malignant
Previously 'goblet cell carcinoid' or 'adenocarcinoma ex goblet cell carcinoid' (WHO 2022).
Clinical Context
  • Previously 'goblet cell carcinoid' or 'adenocarcinoma ex goblet cell carcinoid' (WHO 2022).
  • Appendix only (virtually exclusive); usually arises at tip.
  • Adults (mean ~55 years); equal sex distribution.
  • Present: acute appendicitis (most common), mass, peritoneal disease.
  • Stage as adenocarcinoma — NOT NET staging.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Amphicrine (dual endocrine + exocrine): goblet-like mucinous cells, endocrine cells, and Paneth-like cells in same clusters/tubules.
  • MUST have low-grade (LG) architectural pattern for diagnosis; if entirely high-grade → classify as adenocarcinoma.
  • LG: small tubules/clusters, mild atypia, no stromal reaction. HG: infiltrating single cells, cribriform/anastomosing tubules, signet ring cells, desmoplasia.
✚ Positive
Synaptophysin – focal positiveMUC2 – positiveCDX2 – positiveCK20 – positiveChromogranin – variable/weak
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
Strong diffuse Chromogranin/Synaptophysin (unlike conventional NET)S100 – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Metastatic Mucinous/Signet Ring Adenocarcinoma↗No LG GCA architectural component; CK7/CK20 panel; clinical context; SATB2 for colorectal primary
Well-differentiated NET G1/G2⚡No goblet/mucinous component; Chromogranin diffuse strong; organoid architecture; no adenocarcinoma component
MiNEN↗Two distinct components each >30%; clearly demarcated; distinct from admixed GCA
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Grading (Tang): Grade 1 = >75% LG; Grade 2 = 50-75% LG; Grade 3 = <50% LG.
  • Grade 3 behaves aggressively; worse prognosis than 1/2.
  • Right hemicolectomy standard (for LN staging and resection).
  • Peritoneal dissemination common; CRS + HIPEC for peritoneal disease.
  • Somatostatin analogues not effective (unlike conventional NET).
  • Fluoropyrimidine-based chemotherapy for metastatic disease.
  • Molecular: KRAS; TP53 in HG; different from conventional NET (no MEN1/DAXX/ATRX).
Related / See Also
↗ Metastatic Mucinous/Signet Ring Adenocarcinoma⚡ Well-differentiated NET G1/G2↗ MiNEN
WELL-DIFFERENTIATED NET G1/G2 (GI)
LGI Neuroendocrine Well-differentiated Variable 📋 4×
WHO 2022: G1 Ki67 <3%, mitoses <2/2mm²; G2 Ki67 3-20%, mitoses 2-20/2mm².
Clinical Context
  • WHO 2022: G1 Ki67 <3%, mitoses <2/2mm²; G2 Ki67 3-20%, mitoses 2-20/2mm².
  • Site-specific biology: ileal (midgut) — serotonin-producing; carcinoid syndrome only with liver mets. Rectal — L-cell (glucagon/PYY); usually small, low risk. Gastric Type 1 — ECL cell hyperplasia, chronic atrophic gastritis/autoimmune; Type 2 — ZES/MEN1; Type 3 — sporadic, aggressive. Appendiceal — most common appendiceal neoplasm; tip; overwhelmingly G1.
  • Appendiceal NET <2cm: appendicectomy curative. >2cm: right hemicolectomy.
  • Carcinoid syndrome: flushing, diarrhoea, bronchospasm — only with hepatic/systemic mets (serotonin bypasses portal metabolism).
  • Trigger: incidental polyp; Octreoscan/68Ga-DOTATATE avid lesion; elevated CgA/5-HIAA.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Organoid/insular/trabecular/acinar architecture; uniform round-to-oval nuclei; finely stippled 'salt-and-pepper' chromatin; inconspicuous nucleoli; amphophilic cytoplasm.
  • Mitoses rare (G1); moderate (G2); no/minimal necrosis. Rosette formation in some sites.
  • Ki67 MIB-1 index mandatory — count 500-2000 cells in hotspot; grade determines prognosis and treatment.
✚ Positive
Synaptophysin – diffuse strongChromogranin A – diffuse (site-dependent; may be weaker in rectal L-cell NETs)INSM1 – diffuse nuclear (most sensitive pan-NE marker)CD56 – variableCDX2 – midgut NETsTTF1 – rectal NETs (~70%)Somatostatin receptor 2 (SSTR2) – most GI NETs
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
p53 – wild-type pattern (not mutant)Rb – retainedCK7 – usually negative in midgut NETsKi67 <20% (G1 <3%, G2 3-20%)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Well-differentiated NET G3↗Ki67 >20%; morphologically WD (organoid retained); RB/TP53 intact; same IHC
Poorly differentiated NEC (Small Cell)⚡Ki67 typically >80%; nuclear moulding; crush artefact; Azzopardi effect; p53 mutant; Rb lost
Glomus Tumour (GI)↗SMA+, Collagen IV pericellular; Synaptophysin/CgA−; no salt-and-pepper chromatin
Goblet Cell Adenocarcinoma↗Goblet/mucinous component; amphicrine; CgA weak/variable; appendix only
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Staging: TNM8 site-specific staging applies (different for stomach/duodenum/jejunoileum/appendix/colorectum).
  • Somatostatin analogues (octreotide LAR, lanreotide): antisecretory + antiproliferative for SSTR2+ G1/G2.
  • PRRT (177Lu-DOTATATE): SSTR2+ progressive G1/G2 (NETTER-1 trial); landmark therapy.
  • Everolimus (mTOR inhibitor): non-pancreatic GI NETs G1/G2 (RADIANT-4).
  • Surgical resection primary for localised; liver debulking/ablation/TACE for hepatic mets.
  • MEN1 association: duodenal gastrinomas, pancreatic NETs, parathyroid, pituitary. Test all duodenal NETs for germline MEN1.
  • DAXX/ATRX mutations: alternative lengthening of telomeres (ALT) — worse prognosis even in G1/G2 (mainly pancreatic).
  • Chromogranin A: best serum marker but elevated in PPI use, renal failure, atrophic gastritis — interpret with caution.
  • 5-HIAA: urinary 24hr; midgut serotonin-producing NETs; avoid serotonin-rich foods 48h before test.
Related / See Also
↗ Well-differentiated NET G3⚡ Poorly differentiated NEC (Small Cell)↗ Glomus Tumour (GI)↗ Goblet Cell Adenocarcinoma
WELL-DIFFERENTIATED NET G3 (GI)
LGI Neuroendocrine Well-differentiated Malignant
WHO 2019/2022: Ki67 >20% BUT morphologically well-differentiated (organoid architecture retained). NOT the same as NEC.
Clinical Context
  • WHO 2019/2022: Ki67 >20% BUT morphologically well-differentiated (organoid architecture retained). NOT the same as NEC.
  • Critical distinction from NEC: different molecular landscape, different prognosis, different treatment.
  • Most described in pancreas; GI NET G3 less common but recognised.
  • Present with metastatic disease; aggressive course but better prognosis than NEC.
  • Trigger: high Ki67 on biopsy of morphologically well-differentiated NET — requires full assessment before NEC treatment.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Organoid/trabecular/insular architecture retained (same as G1/G2); uniform nuclei; salt-and-pepper chromatin.
  • Ki67 >20% (often 20-50%; rarely >80%); increased mitoses.
  • No nuclear moulding, no crush artefact, no Azzopardi effect — these features favour NEC.
✚ Positive
Synaptophysin – diffuseChromogranin A – diffuse (retained, unlike NEC where may be weak)INSM1 – diffuseSSTR2 – often retained (unlike NEC)Rb – retainedp53 – wild-type pattern
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
p53 – NOT mutant pattern (key distinction from NEC)Rb – NOT lost (key distinction from NEC)Ki67 cutoff distinguishes from G2 (>20%) but no upper limit in WD NET G3
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Poorly differentiated NEC (Large Cell)⚡p53 mutant; Rb lost; no organoid architecture; necrosis; Ki67 typically >80%; SSTR2 negative/weak
Well-differentiated NET G2↗Ki67 3-20%; same morphology; grade distinction only — biopsy sampling can undergrade
Metastatic well-differentiated NET from pancreas⚡PDX1+, ISL1+; clinical/radiological correlation; pancreatic primaries more often DAXX/ATRX mutant
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Molecular: MEN1, DAXX, ATRX mutations — same as G1/G2, distinct from NEC (TP53, RB1).
  • SSTR2 often retained → 68Ga-DOTATATE PET avid; PRRT potentially applicable.
  • Do NOT treat as NEC (platinum/etoposide) without molecular confirmation — worse outcomes.
  • Ki67 heterogeneity: biopsy may undergrade; resection Ki67 is more reliable.
  • Prognosis better than NEC even with same Ki67 >20%; 5-year OS ~40-50% vs <15% for NEC.
  • ENETS/WHO recommend molecular profiling (MEN1, DAXX, ATRX, TP53, RB1) for all G3 NEN to classify correctly.
  • ALT (alternative lengthening of telomeres): DAXX/ATRX loss → ALT+ by FISH — poor prognosis marker.
Related / See Also
⚡ Poorly differentiated NEC (Large Cell)↗ Well-differentiated NET G2⚡ Metastatic well-differentiated NET from pancreas
POORLY DIFFERENTIATED NEC — SMALL CELL TYPE (GI)
LGI Neuroendocrine Poorly differentiated Malignant
Aggressive, high-grade malignancy. Ki67 >20% (typically >80%).
Clinical Context
  • Aggressive, high-grade malignancy. Ki67 >20% (typically >80%).
  • Oesophagus and colorectum most common GI sites; any GI site possible.
  • Adults; older age group. No sex predilection.
  • Often presents with advanced/metastatic disease; rapid progression.
  • De novo or arising in adenoma/adenocarcinoma (mixed lesions).
  • Trigger: rapidly progressive GI malignancy; FDG-avid on PET (unlike WD NET).
Key Microscopic Features
  • Small cells with scant cytoplasm; high N:C ratio; nuclear moulding — cells mould to each other.
  • Crush artefact on biopsy — characteristic (fragile cells); Azzopardi effect (DNA encrustation of vessel walls).
  • Geographic necrosis; extremely high mitotic rate; apoptotic bodies. No organoid architecture.
✚ Positive
Synaptophysin – diffuse (most reliable in SC-NEC)CD56 – diffuseINSM1 – diffuse nuclearTTF1 – ~50% of GI SC-NEC (do not interpret as lung primary without clinical context)CK – dot-like/paranuclear patternp53 – mutant pattern (strong diffuse or complete loss)Ki67 – typically >80%
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
Chromogranin A – weak/negative (less reliable than in WD NET)Rb – lost (complete absence)SSTR2 – negative or weakCK7/CK20 – variable; not site-specific
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Well-differentiated NET G3⚡Organoid architecture retained; p53 wild-type; Rb retained; SSTR2+; CgA diffuse; Ki67 usually 20-50%
Poorly differentiated NEC Large Cell⚡Larger cells; more cytoplasm; prominent nucleoli; vesicular nuclei; same molecular profile
Lymphoma (small cell/mantle cell)↗LCA+, PAX5+, CD20+; Synaptophysin/CgA−; no crush artefact pattern
Merkel Cell Carcinoma (metastatic)↗CK20 paranuclear dot+; TTF1−; Merkel cell polyomavirus IHC (CM2B4)
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Molecular: TP53 (>90%), RB1 loss — shared with pulmonary SCLC. MEN1/DAXX/ATRX wild-type (distinguishes from WD NET).
  • Treatment: platinum/etoposide (cisplatin or carboplatin + etoposide) — same as pulmonary SCLC.
  • SSTR2 negative → PRRT not applicable; 68Ga-DOTATATE usually negative.
  • FDG-PET positive (high metabolic activity) — use for staging, not DOTATATE.
  • Prognosis dismal: median OS ~12 months; 5-year OS <5%.
  • Second-line: FOLFIRI or temozolomide-based; no established standard.
  • Immunotherapy (PD-L1/TMB): limited data; TMB usually low in GI NEC.
  • If morphology WD but Ki67 >20%: always check p53/Rb IHC before committing to NEC treatment — reclassify as NET G3 if intact.
Related / See Also
⚡ Well-differentiated NET G3⚡ Poorly differentiated NEC Large Cell↗ Lymphoma (small cell/mantle cell)↗ Merkel Cell Carcinoma (metastatic)
POORLY DIFFERENTIATED NEC — LARGE CELL TYPE (GI)
LGI Neuroendocrine Poorly differentiated Malignant
High-grade poorly differentiated NEC; Ki67 >20% (typically >60-80%).
Clinical Context
  • High-grade poorly differentiated NEC; Ki67 >20% (typically >60-80%).
  • Less common than SC-NEC in GI tract; oesophagus, stomach, colorectum.
  • Adults; aggressive presentation, often metastatic at diagnosis.
  • Clinically and molecularly indistinguishable from SC-NEC — treatment identical.
  • Trigger: large cell malignancy with NE markers; high Ki67; aggressive course.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Large cells with moderate cytoplasm; prominent nucleoli; vesicular chromatin — distinct from small cell.
  • Organoid/nested/sheet-like growth; no salt-and-pepper chromatin; no moulding.
  • Geographic necrosis; high mitotic rate; atypical mitoses; apoptosis.
✚ Positive
Synaptophysin – diffuseChromogranin A – variable (more often positive than in SC-NEC)INSM1 – diffuse nuclearCD56 – variableCK – diffuse (more diffuse than SC-NEC)p53 – mutant patternKi67 – typically >60%TTF1 – variable (~30%, less common than SC-NEC)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
Rb – lostSSTR2 – negative/weak
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Poorly differentiated NEC Small Cell⚡Smaller cells; nuclear moulding; crush artefact; Azzopardi; TTF1 more common; same molecular/treatment
Well-differentiated NET G3⚡Organoid architecture; p53 wild-type; Rb retained; SSTR2+; CgA diffuse; better prognosis
Undifferentiated carcinoma / MANECCK diffuse; NE markers absent or focal; no neuroendocrine architecture
Metastatic large cell NEC from lung⚡TTF1+ favours lung; clinical/radiological correlation; molecular overlap
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Molecular profile identical to SC-NEC: TP53, RB1 loss; MEN1/DAXX/ATRX wild-type.
  • Treatment: same as SC-NEC — platinum/etoposide doublet.
  • Prognosis equally poor to SC-NEC; subtype distinction has no current treatment implications.
  • WHO 2022 retains SC vs LC subtype distinction for morphological classification but emphasises molecular identity.
  • FDG-PET positive; DOTATATE negative. Use FDG-PET for staging.
  • MANEC (Mixed Adenoneuroendocrine Carcinoma) — older term; replaced by MiNEN (WHO 2022).
  • If any morphologically WD component present: classify as MiNEN, not pure NEC.
Related / See Also
⚡ Poorly differentiated NEC Small Cell⚡ Well-differentiated NET G3+ Add: Undifferentiated carcinoma / MANEC⚡ Metastatic large cell NEC from lung
MIXED NEUROENDOCRINE–NON-NEUROENDOCRINE NEOPLASM (MiNEN)
LGI Neuroendocrine Mixed Malignant
WHO 2022: each component (NE and non-NE) must comprise ≥30% of tumour volume — the defining threshold.
Clinical Context
  • WHO 2022: each component (NE and non-NE) must comprise ≥30% of tumour volume — the defining threshold.
  • Most common combination: colorectal-type adenocarcinoma + SC-NEC or LC-NEC.
  • Also: gastric, oesophageal, pancreatic, biliary — any GI site.
  • Replaces MANEC (Mixed Adenoneuroendocrine Carcinoma) — broader term; NE component can be WD NET or PD NEC.
  • Clinical behaviour driven by highest-grade component — usually the NEC.
  • Trigger: GI malignancy with morphologically distinct NE and glandular/squamous areas.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Two morphologically distinct components: NE component (organoid/solid NE architecture) AND non-NE component (glandular/mucinous/squamous/undifferentiated) — each ≥30%.
  • Components may be intermingled or geographically distinct; interface often abrupt.
  • Each component assessed separately: grade NEC component (SC vs LC); grade/stage adenocarcinoma component independently.
✚ Positive
NE component: Synaptophysin+, Chromogranin A+, INSM1+Non-NE component: CK7/CK20/CDX2 per site and lineagep53 – often mutant in NEC componentKi67 – high in NEC component (>20%); lower in WD NET component if present
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
Rb – lost in NEC componentSSTR2 – negative in NEC component
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Goblet Cell Adenocarcinoma↗Amphicrine (admixed NE + mucinous cells in same units); appendix; each component NOT ≥30% discrete; no MiNEN threshold
Poorly differentiated NEC with entrapped benign glands⚡Entrapped glands non-neoplastic; glandular component <30%; single neoplastic process
Collision tumourTwo independent tumours at same site; distinct from true mixed neoplasm arising from common progenitor
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Common clonal origin: both components share early molecular alterations (APC, KRAS) — arising from same progenitor, not collision.
  • Staging: by highest pT/pN/pM across both components.
  • Treatment: address both components. Platinum/etoposide for NEC component; FOLFOX/FOLFIRI for adenocarcinoma component. Sequential or concurrent.
  • Prognosis: determined by NEC component — poor. Median OS ~12-18 months.
  • Biopsy undersampling risk: NE component may be missed; re-biopsy or surgical specimen may be needed.
  • NE component can be WD NET (rare MiNEN) — prognosis better than those with NEC component.
  • Report as: 'MiNEN comprising [adenocarcinoma, % grade] and [SC-NEC/LC-NEC/NET G?, %]'.
Related / See Also
↗ Goblet Cell Adenocarcinoma⚡ Poorly differentiated NEC with entrapped benign glands+ Add: Collision tumour
METASTATIC MUCINOUS / SIGNET RING CELL ADENOCARCINOMA
LGI Appendiceal Mucinous Malignant
Diagnostic context: appendiceal peritoneal mucinous disease (PMP) or appendiceal mass — must distinguish primary appendiceal vs metastatic mucinous adenocarcinoma from colorectum, stomach, ovary, or pancreas.
Clinical Context
  • Diagnostic context: appendiceal peritoneal mucinous disease (PMP) or appendiceal mass — must distinguish primary appendiceal vs metastatic mucinous adenocarcinoma from colorectum, stomach, ovary, or pancreas.
  • Clinical red flags for metastasis: bilateral ovarian involvement, upper GI primary, known synchronous colorectal cancer, no appendiceal mass.
  • Peritoneal signet ring disease: gastric (Lauren diffuse type) most common primary — CDH1 germline mutation in hereditary diffuse gastric cancer (HDGC).
  • Krukenberg tumour: bilateral ovarian mets, usually from gastric signet ring primary.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Mucinous: pools of mucin with neoplastic epithelium; infiltrative invasion and desmoplasia distinguish from LAMN/HAMN — key discriminator.
  • Signet ring: single cells with intracytoplasmic mucin displacing nucleus; desmoplastic stroma; no organoid architecture.
  • Infiltrative invasion pattern (irregular glands, single cells, perineural/lymphovascular invasion) absent in LAMN/HAMN — use this to reclassify.
✚ Positive
CK20 + CDX2 + SATB2: colorectal primaryCK7 + CK20 (variable) + MUC5AC: gastric or pancreatobiliary primaryCK7 + WT1 + PAX8: ovarian primary (high-grade serous)CK7 + CK20 (variable) + CDX2 (variable): appendiceal primary — overlaps with colorectal
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
SATB2 – negative in gastric/ovarian primaries (useful colorectal/appendiceal marker)WT1/PAX8 – negative in colorectal and gastric primaries
Top Differentials + Discriminators
LAMN↗Pushing invasion; obliterated muscularis mucosae; low-grade cytology; no desmoplasia; no infiltrative growth
HAMN↗Pushing invasion only; high-grade cytology; no infiltrative glands or single cells
Primary appendiceal mucinous adenocarcinomaCK20+/CDX2+/SATB2+; appendiceal mass; infiltrative invasion; clinical context
Goblet Cell Adenocarcinoma↗Amphicrine; NE markers focal+; LG architectural component; appendix only
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • CK7/CK20 panel: colorectal = CK7−/CK20+; gastric signet ring = CK7+/CK20−; ovarian mucinous = CK7+/CK20 variable; appendiceal = CK7−/CK20+/CDX2+ (overlaps colorectal).
  • SATB2: sensitive and specific for colorectal/appendiceal origin; negative in upper GI, ovary, lung.
  • WT1 + PAX8: high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (mucinous ovarian rarely WT1+).
  • MUC5AC: gastric foveolar-type marker; retained in gastric and pancreatobiliary mucinous carcinomas.
  • CDH1: E-cadherin loss (loss of membranous staining) in signet ring cells — germline CDH1 in HDGC. Refer to genetics.
  • PMP grading (DPAM vs PMCA / WHO G1-G3): acellular mucin (G1, best prognosis), mucinous carcinoma peritonei (G2/G3).
  • Treatment for peritoneal disease: CRS + HIPEC. Systemic chemotherapy for systemic mets (FOLFOX/FOLFIRI ± targeted therapy per molecular profile).
  • KRAS/BRAF/NRAS/MSI testing on primary for systemic therapy selection.
Related / See Also
↗ LAMN↗ HAMN+ Add: Primary appendiceal mucinous adenocarcinoma↗ Goblet Cell Adenocarcinoma
AUTOIMMUNE HEPATITIS (AIH)
Hepatobiliary Medical Liver Hepatitic Variable 📋 1×
Immune-mediated hepatitis; F>M (3:1); bimodal onset (adolescent/young adult + perimenopausal).
Clinical Context
  • Immune-mediated hepatitis; F>M (3:1); bimodal onset (adolescent/young adult + perimenopausal).
  • Type 1 (ANA, ASMA); Type 2 (anti-LKM1, anti-LC1 — children); anti-SLA most specific.
  • Trigger: elevated transaminases + raised IgG + positive serology — simplified IAIHG score ≥7 = definite AIH.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Interface hepatitis — lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate spilling periportally into parenchyma: cardinal feature.
  • Conspicuous plasma cells; rosette formation; emperipolesis (lymphocyte within hepatocyte cytoplasm).
  • Bile duct injury absent or minimal — if prominent, reconsider PBC/PSC overlap.
✚ Positive
IgG plasma cells – >30/HPF supports diagnosisCK7 – intact bile ducts
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CK7 ductular reaction – absent (biliary disease pattern absent)HBsAg – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Drug-induced Liver Injury↗Drug history; eosinophils; no seropositivity; resolves on withdrawal; histologically identical
Primary Biliary Cholangitis↗AMA+; florid duct lesion; granulomatous bile duct destruction; ductopenia
Viral Hepatitis (HCV)PCR+; lymphoid follicles; steatosis; less plasma cells
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Treatment: prednisolone + azathioprine; budesonide for mild/moderate non-cirrhotic disease.
  • Remission target: transaminases + IgG normalisation. Histological remission lags 3–6 months.
  • Relapse on withdrawal >80% — most require lifelong azathioprine maintenance.
  • Acute severe AIH: IV methylprednisolone; no Day 7 response → ciclosporin or transplant listing.
  • AIH-PBC overlap (Paris criteria): UDCA + immunosuppression. AIH-PSC overlap: MRCP mandatory.
Related / See Also
↗ Drug-induced Liver Injury↗ Primary Biliary Cholangitis+ Add: Viral Hepatitis (HCV)
DRUG-INDUCED LIVER INJURY (DILI)
Hepatobiliary Medical Liver Hepatitic Variable
Most common cause of acute liver failure in Western countries. Idiosyncratic (unpredictable, immune/metabolic) >> intrinsic (paracetamol — dose-dependent).
Clinical Context
  • Most common cause of acute liver failure in Western countries. Idiosyncratic (unpredictable, immune/metabolic) >> intrinsic (paracetamol — dose-dependent).
  • Common culprits: amoxicillin-clavulanate (cholestatic), flucloxacillin (cholestatic/VBDS), isoniazid, nitrofurantoin, immune checkpoint inhibitors.
  • Trigger: any hepatitis — always take full drug/supplement/herbal history. R-ratio = (ALT/ULN)÷(ALP/ULN): >5 hepatocellular; <2 cholestatic; 2–5 mixed.
Key Microscopic Features
  • No single diagnostic pattern — DILI mimics any liver injury. Zone 3 necrosis (paracetamol); cholestasis ± bile duct loss (flucloxacillin, amoxicillin-clavulanate); steatohepatitis (amiodarone); granulomas (allopurinol, carbamazepine).
  • Clues: eosinophilic infiltrate (immune DILI); microvesicular steatosis (mitochondrial toxicity — valproate); phospholipidosis/foamy macrophages (amiodarone).
  • VBDS (vanishing bile duct syndrome): ductopenia >50% portal tracts; flucloxacillin, carbamazepine — may be irreversible.
✚ Positive
CK7 – ductular reaction; bile duct loss assessment
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
N/A — exclude other causes by serology/PCR
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Autoimmune Hepatitis↗Plasma cells; rosettes; elevated IgG; autoantibodies; responds to immunosuppression
Primary Biliary Cholangitis↗AMA+; florid duct lesion; chronic cholestatic; no drug history
Viral HepatitisSerology/PCR+; no drug history
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Causality: RUCAM score (temporal relationship, dechallenge/rechallenge, risk factors, alternative causes).
  • Paracetamol: zone 3 necrosis; N-acetylcysteine even in late presentation. Kings College Criteria for transplant listing.
  • ICI hepatitis (anti-PD1/CTLA-4): hepatocellular or mixed; CD8-rich lobular infiltrate; treat with steroids ± mycophenolate.
  • Flucloxacillin: HLA-B*57:01 associated; VBDS may persist — UDCA palliative.
  • Histology cannot diagnose DILI — excludes mimics. Clinical context mandatory.
Related / See Also
↗ Autoimmune Hepatitis↗ Primary Biliary Cholangitis+ Add: Viral Hepatitis
PRIMARY BILIARY CHOLANGITIS (PBC)
Hepatobiliary Medical Liver Cholestatic Variable
Autoimmune destruction of small interlobular bile ducts (<100µm); medium/large ducts spared. F:M 9:1; peak 40–60 years.
Clinical Context
  • Autoimmune destruction of small interlobular bile ducts (<100µm); medium/large ducts spared. F:M 9:1; peak 40–60 years.
  • Often asymptomatic (raised ALP); symptomatic: fatigue, pruritus, sicca. Associations: Sjögren, CREST, Hashimoto's.
  • Trigger: middle-aged woman + raised ALP + AMA+ (≥1:40) — diagnosis established without biopsy.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Florid duct lesion: granulomatous/lymphocytic destruction of interlobular bile ducts — pathognomonic but only Stage 1–2.
  • Staging (Ludwig): 1 portal inflammation; 2 interface hepatitis/ductular reaction; 3 bridging fibrosis; 4 biliary cirrhosis.
  • CK7+ intermediate hepatocytes (hepatocyte metaplasia) and periportal copper accumulation in advanced disease.
✚ Positive
CK7 – ductular reaction; intermediate hepatocytesOrcein/rhodanine – periportal copper (advanced)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
IgG4 – negative (<10 IgG4+ cells/HPF — excludes IgG4-SC)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis⚡Medium/large duct involvement; MRCP strictures; IBD association; AMA negative; male predominance
AIH-PBC OverlapAMA+ AND interface hepatitis + elevated IgG (Paris criteria) — treat with UDCA + immunosuppression
Drug-induced cholestasis↗Drug history; no AMA; acute onset; resolves on withdrawal
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • UDCA 13–15mg/kg/day first-line — improves LFTs, delays progression.
  • Inadequate response (Paris II at 1 year: ALP >1.5× ULN): add obeticholic acid or bezafibrate.
  • AMA-negative PBC (~5%): anti-sp100 (nuclear dots) or anti-gp210 (nuclear rim) — same treatment.
  • Complications: osteoporosis, fat-soluble vitamin deficiency, portal hypertension.
  • HCC surveillance (6-monthly USS) once cirrhotic. Transplant for decompensation/intractable pruritus.
Related / See Also
⚡ Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis+ Add: AIH-PBC Overlap↗ Drug-induced cholestasis
PRIMARY SCLEROSING CHOLANGITIS (PSC)
Hepatobiliary Medical Liver Cholestatic Variable 📋 1×
Fibro-inflammatory destruction of intra- and extrahepatic ducts; M:F 2:1; peak 30–50 years; 70–80% associated with UC.
Clinical Context
  • Fibro-inflammatory destruction of intra- and extrahepatic ducts; M:F 2:1; peak 30–50 years; 70–80% associated with UC.
  • Raised ALP ± GGT; episodic cholangitis; MRCP: multifocal strictures and dilatations ('beading') — diagnostic.
  • Trigger: male + IBD + raised ALP. Lifetime cholangiocarcinoma risk ~10–20% — surveillance mandatory.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Periductal 'onion-skin' fibrosis around medium ducts → obliterative fibrous scar ('tombstone' duct) — pathognomonic but present in minority of biopsies.
  • Portal oedema/inflammation; progressive ductopenia; ductular reaction. Biliary cirrhosis in end-stage.
  • Small-duct PSC: cholangiographic features absent; histology shows PSC pattern — better prognosis, lower CCA risk.
✚ Positive
CK7 – ductular reactionIgG4 – MUST perform to exclude IgG4-SC (>10 IgG4+/HPF = IgG4-SC)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
AMA – negativeIgG4 – negative in true PSC
Top Differentials + Discriminators
IgG4-Related Sclerosing Cholangitis↗IgG4 serum >2×ULN; >10 IgG4+/HPF; storiform fibrosis; obliterative phlebitis; dramatic steroid response; no IBD
Primary Biliary Cholangitis↗AMA+; small ducts only; women; florid duct lesion; no MRCP strictures
CholangiocarcinomaDominant stricture; rising CA19-9; brush cytology/FISH; exclude in any PSC patient deteriorating
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • No proven medical therapy — UDCA not recommended at high dose (harm signal in RCTs).
  • Dominant stricture: ERCP + dilation ± stenting; brush cytology + FISH for CCA.
  • CCA surveillance: 6-monthly MRI/MRCP + CA19-9.
  • PSC-IBD: annual colonoscopy from IBD diagnosis (highest colorectal cancer risk of any IBD subgroup).
  • Transplant only cure; 5-year OS ~85%; recurrence ~25%.
Related / See Also
↗ IgG4-Related Sclerosing Cholangitis↗ Primary Biliary Cholangitis+ Add: Cholangiocarcinoma
MASLD / MASH
Hepatobiliary Medical Liver Steatotic Variable
MASLD (2023) replaces NAFLD: steatosis + ≥1 cardiometabolic risk factor. MASH (progressive form) ~20% of MASLD. Most common liver disease globally.
Clinical Context
  • MASLD (2023) replaces NAFLD: steatosis + ≥1 cardiometabolic risk factor. MASH (progressive form) ~20% of MASLD. Most common liver disease globally.
  • Presentation: incidental steatosis/raised ALT; advanced disease → cirrhosis, portal hypertension, HCC (can arise without cirrhosis).
  • Trigger: metabolic syndrome + steatosis on imaging — exclude alcohol (MetALD: 14–35 units/week), drugs, viral.
Key Microscopic Features
  • MASLD: macrovesicular steatosis ≥5% hepatocytes, zone 3 predominant.
  • MASH triad: steatosis + lobular inflammation (mixed, including neutrophils) + hepatocyte ballooning (wispy pale cytoplasm; Mallory-Denk bodies in severe). Zone 3.
  • Perisinusoidal 'chicken-wire' fibrosis (zone 3) — characteristic fibrosis pattern. Stage F0–F4; F3/F4 drives mortality.
✚ Positive
N/A — morphological diagnosis
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
N/A — exclude viral/autoimmune by serology
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Alcoholic Liver Disease↗Histologically identical — AST:ALT >2:1 favours ALD; alcohol history (AUDIT) is key discriminator
Drug-induced steatohepatitis↗Amiodarone (phospholipidosis); tamoxifen; methotrexate — drug history
Wilson's Disease↗Young patient; glycogenated nuclei; low caeruloplasmin; ATP7B mutation
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Non-invasive staging: FIB-4 <1.3 = low fibrosis risk; >2.67 = high. FibroScan/MRE for indeterminate.
  • Treatment: 7–10% weight loss → histological improvement. Resmetirom (THR-β agonist) — first approved MASH treatment (2024). GLP-1 agonists (semaglutide) positive Phase 3 data.
  • Fibrosis stage determines prognosis — not NAS/activity score.
  • HCC surveillance (6-monthly USS) from F3; HCC can occur without cirrhosis in MASH.
  • MASH cirrhosis now 2nd commonest transplant indication in USA.
Related / See Also
↗ Alcoholic Liver Disease↗ Drug-induced steatohepatitis↗ Wilson's Disease
ALCOHOLIC LIVER DISEASE / ALCOHOL-ASSOCIATED HEPATITIS (ALD/AAH)
Hepatobiliary Medical Liver Hepatitic Variable
Spectrum: steatosis → steatohepatitis → cirrhosis. Alcohol-associated hepatitis (AAH): acute onset, heavy use (>80g/day); jaundice, tender hepatomegaly, leukocytosis.
Clinical Context
  • Spectrum: steatosis → steatohepatitis → cirrhosis. Alcohol-associated hepatitis (AAH): acute onset, heavy use (>80g/day); jaundice, tender hepatomegaly, leukocytosis.
  • Maddrey Discriminant Function (MDF): 4.6×(PT prolongation) + bilirubin(µmol/L÷17.1). MDF ≥32 = severe — consider corticosteroids.
  • Trigger: heavy alcohol + acute jaundice + elevated WBC — calculate MDF immediately.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Steatohepatitis: macrovesicular steatosis + lobular inflammation + ballooning + Mallory-Denk bodies. Zone 3. Histologically identical to MASH.
  • Severe AAH: confluent/bridging necrosis; neutrophilic infiltrate; bilirubinostasis; satellite necrosis (neutrophils around necrotic hepatocytes).
  • Perisinusoidal 'chicken-wire' fibrosis zone 3 first; biliary cirrhosis in end-stage.
✚ Positive
N/A — morphological + clinical diagnosis
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
N/A
Top Differentials + Discriminators
MASH↗Histologically identical — AST:ALT >2:1 and alcohol history (AUDIT) favour ALD; metabolic syndrome favours MASH
Autoimmune Hepatitis↗Plasma cells; elevated IgG; autoantibodies — careful distinction required as steroids used in both
Ischaemic HepatitisZone 3 necrosis; transaminases >1000 IU/L; hypotensive episode; rapid recovery
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Lille score Day 7: ≥0.45 = steroid non-responder → stop (infection risk). <0.45 = responder → complete 28 days.
  • Corticosteroids (prednisolone 40mg): MDF ≥32 or encephalopathy. Contraindicated: active infection, GI bleed.
  • AST:ALT >2:1: mitochondrial AST release + pyridoxine depletion reducing ALT synthesis.
  • Abstinence: most important long-term intervention. IV thiamine (Pabrinex) before any glucose.
  • Early transplant for severe AAH non-responsive to medical therapy — evidence base growing.
Related / See Also
↗ MASH↗ Autoimmune Hepatitis+ Add: Ischaemic Hepatitis
HEREDITARY HAEMOCHROMATOSIS (HH)
Hepatobiliary Medical Liver Hepatitic Variable
AR iron overload; HFE C282Y homozygote (~85%); incomplete penetrance (~28% of male homozygotes develop disease). Northern European.
Clinical Context
  • AR iron overload; HFE C282Y homozygote (~85%); incomplete penetrance (~28% of male homozygotes develop disease). Northern European.
  • Classic triad: cirrhosis + diabetes + skin bronzing. Arthropathy (2nd/3rd MCP — characteristic); hypogonadism; cardiomyopathy.
  • Trigger: transferrin saturation >45% (fasting) + elevated ferritin — HFE genotyping. Screen first-degree relatives.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Perls' Prussian blue stain: periportal (zone 1) hepatocyte siderosis early — progressive panacinar involvement.
  • HH spares Kupffer cells until late — Kupffer cell iron predominant = secondary/transfusional overload.
  • Portal fibrosis → bridging → cirrhosis. HCC risk 200× in cirrhotic HH.
✚ Positive
Perls' Prussian blue – grade and distribution essential
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
N/A
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Secondary haemosiderosisKupffer cell predominant (reticuloendothelial pattern); transfusion/haemolysis history; HFE wild-type
Dysmetabolic iron overload (DIOS)Metabolic syndrome; mixed hepatocyte/Kupffer cell iron; ferritin elevated out of proportion to transferrin saturation
Juvenile haemochromatosis (HJV/HAMP)Age <30; severe overload; cardiac/endocrine predominant; HFE negative
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • HFE genotyping: C282Y homozygote = HH. C282Y/H63D = mild phenotype. Non-HFE: HFE2, HAMP, TFR2, SLC40A1.
  • Venesection: weekly 450ml until ferritin <50µg/L; then maintenance. Prevents progression if pre-cirrhotic.
  • MRI T2*: non-invasive hepatic iron quantification — increasingly replaces biopsy for staging.
  • HCC surveillance: 6-monthly USS ± AFP in cirrhotic HH — risk persists post-venesection.
  • Exam trap: Kupffer cell sparing in early HH distinguishes from secondary overload.
Related / See Also
+ Add: Secondary haemosiderosis+ Add: Dysmetabolic iron overload (DIOS)+ Add: Juvenile haemochromatosis (HJV/HAMP)
WILSON'S DISEASE
Hepatobiliary Medical Liver Hepatitic Variable
AR copper accumulation; ATP7B mutation (13q); defective biliary copper excretion. Presents age 5–35.
Clinical Context
  • AR copper accumulation; ATP7B mutation (13q); defective biliary copper excretion. Presents age 5–35.
  • Hepatic: acute hepatitis; ALF (Coombs-negative haemolytic anaemia + liver failure = Wilson's until proven otherwise); cirrhosis. Neuropsychiatric: dysarthria, tremor, dystonia, psychiatric symptoms.
  • Trigger: young patient + liver disease + neuropsychiatric + KF rings on slit lamp + low caeruloplasmin.
Key Microscopic Features
  • No pathognomonic feature. Early: macrovesicular steatosis; glycogenated nuclei; focal necrosis — mimics MASLD.
  • Rhodanine (copper) or orcein (copper-associated protein) staining: periportal distribution in established disease. May be absent in acute presentation.
  • Advanced: chronic hepatitis pattern; interface hepatitis; Mallory-Denk bodies; bridging fibrosis → cirrhosis.
✚ Positive
Rhodanine – copper (periportal)Orcein – copper-associated protein (more sensitive than rhodanine early)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
N/A — diagnosis requires clinical + biochemical + histological correlation
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Autoimmune Hepatitis↗Positive autoantibodies; elevated IgG; plasma cells; normal copper studies; responds to immunosuppression
MASLD↗Metabolic risk factors; older age; normal caeruloplasmin; normal copper
Acute viral hepatitisSerology/PCR+; no copper accumulation; no KF rings
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Leipzig score ≥4 = diagnosis established: KF rings (2pt) + neurological symptoms (2pt) + Coombs-negative haemolysis (1pt) + low caeruloplasmin <0.1g/L (2pt) + liver copper >250µg/g (2pt) + mutation (4pt).
  • Caeruloplasmin: acute phase reactant — may be normal in acute Wilson's/pregnancy. 24hr urinary copper >100µg confirms diagnosis.
  • Treatment: D-penicillamine (chelation); trientine (better tolerated, preferred for neurological presentation); zinc (maintenance).
  • Acute Wilson's + ALF: urgent transplant — medical treatment insufficient. New Wilson's Index ≥11 = list for OLT.
  • Exam trap: copper stains may be negative in acute presentation — clinical picture drives diagnosis.
Related / See Also
↗ Autoimmune Hepatitis↗ MASLD+ Add: Acute viral hepatitis
ALPHA-1 ANTITRYPSIN DEFICIENCY (A1ATD)
Hepatobiliary Medical Liver Hepatitic Variable 📋 1×
AR/codominant; SERPINA1 gene; misfolded Z-protein retained in hepatocyte ER → proteotoxic injury. PiZZ most severe.
Clinical Context
  • AR/codominant; SERPINA1 gene; misfolded Z-protein retained in hepatocyte ER → proteotoxic injury. PiZZ most severe.
  • Hepatic: neonatal cholestasis (most common genetic cause); chronic hepatitis; cirrhosis; HCC. Pulmonary: panacinar emphysema (lower lobes) — smoking accelerates dramatically.
  • Trigger: unexplained cirrhosis + emphysema; neonatal cholestasis; raised GGT in child — PAS-D globules on biopsy.
Key Microscopic Features
  • PAS-positive, diastase-resistant (PAS-D) periportal globules — hallmark. Round eosinophilic intracytoplasmic inclusions (dilated ER containing misfolded Z-protein).
  • Variable background: neonatal hepatitis; steatosis; chronic hepatitis; bridging fibrosis → cirrhosis.
  • Globule number correlates poorly with liver disease severity — proteotoxic injury drives hepatitis.
✚ Positive
PAS-D – periportal diastase-resistant globules (essential stain)A1AT IHC – confirms A1AT in globules (useful in PiMZ where globules subtle)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
N/A
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Biliary atresiaGGT markedly elevated; bile duct proliferation; no PAS-D globules; absent extrahepatic ducts on cholangiogram
Other neonatal cholestasis (CMV, Alagille)Serology/PCR; JAG1 mutation; paucity of bile ducts; facial/cardiac features in Alagille
MASLD↗No PAS-D globules; metabolic risk factors; normal A1AT genotype
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Diagnosis: serum A1AT level + Pi phenotype (isoelectric focusing) or SERPINA1 genotyping. PiZZ = diagnostic.
  • No specific liver treatment — avoid hepatotoxins; HAV/HBV vaccination; manage metabolic cofactors.
  • Transplant cures liver disease (donor phenotype replaces PiZZ) and normalises serum A1AT.
  • PiMZ: ~3–4× increased cirrhosis risk — important cofactor with ALD/MASLD/viral hepatitis.
  • Fazirsiran (RNAi targeting Z-A1AT production) — Phase 3 trials ongoing.
Related / See Also
+ Add: Biliary atresia+ Add: Other neonatal cholestasis (CMV, Alagille)↗ MASLD
BUDD-CHIARI SYNDROME (BCS)
Hepatobiliary Medical Liver Vascular Variable 📋 1×
Hepatic venous outflow obstruction (hepatic veins/IVC). Primary (~75%): thrombosis due to prothrombotic state. Secondary: tumour compression/invasion.
Clinical Context
  • Hepatic venous outflow obstruction (hepatic veins/IVC). Primary (~75%): thrombosis due to prothrombotic state. Secondary: tumour compression/invasion.
  • Causes: JAK2 V617F/MPN (most common, ~40–50%); antiphospholipid syndrome; PNH; factor V Leiden; OCP.
  • Trigger: abdominal pain + hepatomegaly + ascites + caudate lobe hypertrophy on imaging — JAK2 + PNH screen mandatory.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Zone 3 sinusoidal congestion and haemorrhagic necrosis — 'reversed lobular pattern' (zone 3 most affected).
  • Progressive centrilobular fibrosis radiating outward; eventual cardiac-pattern cirrhosis.
  • Caudate lobe relatively spared (drains directly to IVC) — biopsy site selection critical.
✚ Positive
Reticulin – collapse and condensation in necrotic zones
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
N/A — Doppler USS + venography diagnostic
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Sinusoidal Obstruction Syndrome↗Hepatic veins patent on Doppler; post-HSCT/oxaliplatin; sinusoidal fibrin; no thrombophilia
Congestive hepatopathy (cardiac)Right heart failure; dilated hepatic veins; JVP elevated; no hepatic vein thrombosis
Ischaemic hepatitisZone 3 necrosis; AST/ALT >1000 IU/L; hypotensive episode; rapid resolution
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Investigation: Doppler USS first (hepatic vein reversal/absence); CT/MRI (nutmeg liver, caudate hypertrophy).
  • Treatment step-up: anticoagulation → TIPS → liver transplantation.
  • JAK2 V617F positive → bone marrow biopsy. PNH screen (flow cytometry) in all primary BCS.
  • Post-transplant anticoagulation lifelong — underlying thrombophilia persists.
  • Rotterdam BCS score predicts 5-year mortality without transplant.
Related / See Also
↗ Sinusoidal Obstruction Syndrome+ Add: Congestive hepatopathy (cardiac)+ Add: Ischaemic hepatitis
SINUSOIDAL OBSTRUCTION SYNDROME (SOS / HEPATIC VOD)
Hepatobiliary Medical Liver Vascular Variable
Sinusoidal endothelial cell injury → fibrin in sinusoids → outflow obstruction. Hepatic veins patent (distinguishes from BCS).
Clinical Context
  • Sinusoidal endothelial cell injury → fibrin in sinusoids → outflow obstruction. Hepatic veins patent (distinguishes from BCS).
  • Post-HSCT (busulfan/cyclophosphamide conditioning); oxaliplatin (colorectal liver metastasis setting); pyrrolizidine alkaloids (herbal).
  • Trigger: post-HSCT + weight gain + RUQ pain + hepatomegaly + jaundice within 21 days — Baltimore criteria.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Sinusoidal fibrin deposition (zone 3); sinusoidal endothelial cell detachment; zone 3 hepatocyte necrosis.
  • Central vein (terminal hepatic venule) thickening/obliteration — may not be visible early; sinusoidal changes are more reliable.
  • Reticulin collapse zone 3; centrilobular fibrosis in established disease.
✚ Positive
Fibrin/PTAH stain – sinusoidal fibrin deposition
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
N/A — hepatic veins patent on Doppler (key BCS distinction)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Budd-Chiari Syndrome↗Hepatic vein/IVC thrombosis on Doppler; caudate hypertrophy; underlying thrombophilia; not post-HSCT
Hepatic GVHDBile duct damage/destruction; lobular hepatitis; cutaneous/GI GVHD; later onset than SOS
Drug-induced liver injury↗Drug history; no sinusoidal fibrin pattern; not post-HSCT context
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Baltimore criteria: bilirubin >34µmol/L + ≥2 of hepatomegaly/ascites/weight gain >5% — within 21 days post-HSCT.
  • Treatment: defibrotide — approved for severe SOS (endothelial protective/pro-fibrinolytic); prophylaxis for high-risk patients.
  • Oxaliplatin SOS: sinusoidal dilatation + NRH on biopsy — increases surgical risk at hepatic resection.
  • Severe SOS + multi-organ failure: mortality >80% without defibrotide — early treatment critical.
Related / See Also
↗ Budd-Chiari Syndrome+ Add: Hepatic GVHD↗ Drug-induced liver injury
HEPATIC GRANULOMA — DIFFERENTIAL FRAMEWORK
Hepatobiliary Medical Liver Granulomatous Variable 📋 7×
Found in ~2–10% of liver biopsies. Requires clinicopathological correlation — never a standalone diagnosis.
Clinical Context
  • Found in ~2–10% of liver biopsies. Requires clinicopathological correlation — never a standalone diagnosis.
  • Common causes: sarcoidosis (most common UK), PBC (florid duct lesion), TB (caseating), drug (allopurinol, carbamazepine), Q fever (fibrin ring), EBV/CMV, fungal (immunocompromised).
  • Trigger: granulomas on biopsy → Mantoux/IGRA, CXR, serum ACE, AMA, drug history, HIV, LDH.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Non-caseating compact granulomas ± Langhans giant cells: sarcoidosis, PBC (adjacent to damaged bile duct), drug, berylliosis.
  • Caseating necrosis: TB — ZN stain (30–40% sensitivity); PCR more sensitive. Culture essential from fresh tissue.
  • Fibrin ring granuloma (doughnut — central lipid vacuole + fibrin ring): Q fever (pathognomonic); also EBV, CMV, drug.
✚ Positive
CD68 – epithelioid macrophages/histiocytesIgG4 – if IgG4-related disease suspected
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
N/A — special stains mandatory: ZN + PAS-D + Grocott + Gram on all hepatic granulomas
Top Differentials + Discriminators
SarcoidosisNon-caseating; portal + parenchymal; bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy; ACE elevated; diagnosis of exclusion
TuberculosisCaseating; ZN+ (30–40%); PCR more sensitive; IGRA+; fever/night sweats/weight loss
Q Fever (Coxiella burnetii)Fibrin ring granuloma (pathognomonic); fever + thrombocytopenia; serology (Phase I/II IgG)
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Stain protocol: ZN + PAS-D + Grocott + Gram minimum on all hepatic granulomas.
  • Sarcoidosis: portal hypertension can occur without cirrhosis — treat with steroids if symptomatic.
  • Drug granulomas: eosinophilic infiltrate common; resolve on withdrawal.
  • Idiopathic: ~10–30% remain unexplained after full workup — follow-up required.
  • Lymphoma: elevated LDH + B symptoms → consider even with granulomas (flow cytometry + clonality).
Related / See Also
+ Add: Sarcoidosis+ Add: Tuberculosis+ Add: Q Fever (Coxiella burnetii)
ACUTE LIVER FAILURE — MORPHOLOGICAL PATTERNS
Hepatobiliary Medical Liver Massive necrosis Variable 📋 1×
ALF: coagulopathy (INR ≥1.5) + encephalopathy without pre-existing liver disease, <26 weeks. Hyperacute (<7 days, paracetamol/ischaemia) — best spontaneous recovery. Subacute (>21 days, seronegative/drug) — worst.
Clinical Context
  • ALF: coagulopathy (INR ≥1.5) + encephalopathy without pre-existing liver disease, <26 weeks. Hyperacute (<7 days, paracetamol/ischaemia) — best spontaneous recovery. Subacute (>21 days, seronegative/drug) — worst.
  • UK causes: paracetamol (~50%), seronegative (~15%), DILI (~10%), viral (HAV, HEV, HBV reactivation), Wilson's, BCS.
  • Trigger: encephalopathy + coagulopathy + jaundice in previously well patient — apply Kings College Criteria urgently.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Massive necrosis: >70–80% hepatocyte loss; panacinar necrosis; reticulin framework collapse; residual regenerating hepatocytes at portal tracts.
  • Zone 3 necrosis (paracetamol/ischaemia); panacinar (viral/seronegative/AIH); submassive (<70%) better prognosis for regeneration.
  • Cause-specific: sparse inflammation (paracetamol); lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate (AIH/viral); eosinophils/granulomas (DILI); periportal copper + Mallory bodies (Wilson's).
✚ Positive
Reticulin – extent of necrosis/collapseKi67 – hepatocyte regeneration indexOrcein/rhodanine – Wilson's copperHBsAg/HBcAg – occult HBV
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
N/A — aetiology-directed panel
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Acute-on-chronic liver failure (ACLF)Pre-existing cirrhosis; precipitant (infection/bleed/alcohol); background fibrosis on biopsy
Ischaemic hepatitisZone 3 necrosis; AST/ALT >1000 acutely; hypotensive episode; rapid recovery
Infiltrative malignancy (lymphoma/HCC)Hepatomegaly; elevated LDH; imaging abnormality; biopsy shows malignant infiltrate
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Kings College Criteria — paracetamol: pH <7.30 OR all three of INR >6.5 + creatinine >300 + grade III–IV encephalopathy.
  • Non-paracetamol KCC: INR >6.5 OR any 3 of: age <10/>40, seronegative/DILI aetiology, jaundice→encephalopathy >7 days, INR >3.5, bilirubin >300.
  • Paracetamol: N-acetylcysteine even late. Wilson's ALF: New Wilson's Index ≥11 → urgent transplant listing.
  • Seronegative ALF: worst natural history — screen thoroughly (HEV, HSV, EBV, CMV, autoimmune, Wilson's, drug).
  • Complications: cerebral oedema (mannitol/hypothermia); HRS; coagulopathy; hypoglycaemia.
Related / See Also
+ Add: Acute-on-chronic liver failure (ACLF)+ Add: Ischaemic hepatitis+ Add: Infiltrative malignancy (lymphoma/HCC)
POLYMORPHOUS ADENOCARCINOMA (PAC)
Head & Neck Epithelial Mixed Intermediate 📋 2×
Minor salivary glands predominantly — palate most common (second most common salivary gland carcinoma of oral cavity/palate).
Clinical Context
  • Minor salivary glands predominantly — palate most common (second most common salivary gland carcinoma of oral cavity/palate).
  • F:M 2:1; mean age 50–60s. Presents as slow-growing palpable submucosal mass; occasionally ulcerated.
  • Trigger: palatal mass in middle-aged woman — PAC high in differential alongside AdCC and mucoepidermoid carcinoma.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Cytological uniformity: monotonous ovoid/round nuclei with pale vesicular chromatin — resembles PTC nuclei. Low nuclear grade throughout.
  • Architectural diversity (same tumour): single-file infiltration, tubular, trabecular, cribriform, papillary, solid, reticular — the hallmark contrast with cytological uniformity.
  • Targetoid (concentric) perineural invasion — frequent and characteristic. Perineural tracking a key exam feature.
✚ Positive
S100 – diffuse strongSOX10 – diffuse strongCK7 – diffuse strongp63 – variable (positive in majority)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
p40 – negative (key AdCC distinction)SMA – negative or weak focalEMA – negative or weak focalMYB – negative (AdCC positive)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Adenoid Cystic Carcinoma↗Biphasic (ductal + myoepithelial); hyperchromatic angulated nuclei; p40+, MYB+/MYBL1 rearrangement; cylindromatous matrix; no cytological uniformity
Secretory CarcinomaETV6-NTRK3 (or other ETV6) fusion; GCDFP-15+, mammaglobulin+; zymogen-like secretions; bubbled vacuolated cytoplasm
Myoepithelial CarcinomaSMA+, calponin+, p40+; no PRKD alteration; more cytological atypia; clear/plasmacytoid/spindle cells
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • PRKD1 E710D hotspot mutation (~73–89%) — most common; defines the entity molecularly. PRKD1/2/3 gene fusions (~6–11%).
  • Cribriform adenocarcinoma of salivary gland (CASG): now considered a variant of PAC — lobulated architecture, fibrous septa, cribriform/microcystic pattern; same PRKD alterations.
  • Indolent course overall; local recurrence ~17%; lymph node metastasis ~15%; distant mets uncommon.
  • Surgery (wide local excision) primary. Neck dissection if clinically node-positive. Radiation for positive margins/perineural invasion.
  • Exam trap: cytological uniformity with PTC-like nuclei may suggest PTC metastasis — site (palate), S100/SOX10 profile, and salivary gland architecture clarify.
Related / See Also
↗ Adenoid Cystic Carcinoma+ Add: Secretory Carcinoma+ Add: Myoepithelial Carcinoma
TYPICAL CARCINOID (LUNG)
Thoracic Neuroendocrine Well-differentiated Intermediate 📋 2×
WHO 2021: low-grade WD-NET. Mitoses <2/2mm²; no necrosis; Ki67 typically <5%.
Clinical Context
  • WHO 2021: low-grade WD-NET. Mitoses <2/2mm²; no necrosis; Ki67 typically <5%.
  • Adults; slight female predominance. Central > peripheral. May present with carcinoid syndrome (rare — only with liver mets), haemoptysis, or obstructive pneumonia.
  • Trigger: incidental pulmonary nodule; endobronchial lesion; 68Ga-DOTATATE avid on PET.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Organoid/trabecular/insular architecture; uniform round-to-oval cells; finely stippled 'salt-and-pepper' chromatin; inconspicuous nucleoli; moderate amphophilic cytoplasm.
  • Mitoses <2/2mm² (10HPF); necrosis absent — both required for diagnosis.
  • Spindle cell morphology in ~25%; pseudoglandular/acinar patterns. Stroma may be fibrovascular or hyalinised.
✚ Positive
Synaptophysin – diffuse strongChromogranin A – diffuseINSM1 – diffuse nuclearCD56 – positiveTTF1 – positive (~50%)OTP (Orthopedia homeobox) – diffuse nuclear (carcinoid family marker — negative in NEC)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
p53 – wild-type patternRb – retainedKi67 – <5% (typically <2%)SSTR2 – positive (relevant for PRRT eligibility)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Atypical Carcinoid↗Mitoses 2–10/2mm² OR focal necrosis; same IHC; higher risk of metastasis
LCNEC↗Ki67 >50%; necrosis extensive; p53 mutant; Rb lost; large cells with prominent nucleoli; no OTP
Paraganglioma (pulmonary)↗S100+ sustentacular cells; Zellballen architecture; Synaptophysin+; Chromogranin variable; TTF1−; no OTP
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • OTP (Orthopedia homeobox): diffuse nuclear in TC and AC (carcinoid family); negative in LCNEC/SCLC — key discriminator for WD vs PD NE tumours.
  • DIPNECH association: TC/AC may arise on background of DIPNECH — assess surrounding lung for neuroendocrine cell hyperplasia/tumourlets.
  • Prognosis: TC excellent — 5-year OS >90%. Surgical resection curative for most. Nodal mets in ~5%.
  • SSTR2 expression: 68Ga-DOTATATE PET for staging; PRRT applicable for progressive unresectable/metastatic TC.
  • Molecular: MEN1 mutations most common; ATRX/DAXX; distinct from SCLC/LCNEC (no TP53/RB1).
Related / See Also
↗ ATYPICAL CARCINOID (LUNG)↗ LARGE CELL NEUROENDOCRINE CARCINOMA (LCNEC, LUNG)↗ SMALL CELL LUNG CARCINOMA (SCLC)↗ DIPNECH / CARCINOID TUMOURLET (LUNG)↗ Atypical Carcinoid↗ LCNEC↗ Paraganglioma (pulmonary)
ATYPICAL CARCINOID (LUNG)
Thoracic Neuroendocrine Well-differentiated Malignant
WHO 2021: intermediate-grade WD-NET. Mitoses 2–10/2mm² OR focal necrosis (punctate/comedo); Ki67 typically 5–20%.
Clinical Context
  • WHO 2021: intermediate-grade WD-NET. Mitoses 2–10/2mm² OR focal necrosis (punctate/comedo); Ki67 typically 5–20%.
  • Adults; slight male predominance vs TC. Peripheral > central. Higher risk of nodal and distant metastasis than TC.
  • Trigger: pulmonary mass with necrosis or higher mitotic activity on biopsy — grade before treatment.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Organoid/trabecular/insular architecture retained (same as TC); uniform 'salt-and-pepper' chromatin.
  • Mitoses 2–10/2mm² AND/OR focal necrosis (punctate or comedo-type) — the defining criteria vs TC.
  • Ki67 typically 5–20%; architectural and cytological features otherwise identical to TC — grading is mitosis/necrosis based, not morphological.
✚ Positive
Synaptophysin – diffuse strongChromogranin A – diffuseINSM1 – diffuse nuclearCD56 – positiveTTF1 – positive (~50%)OTP – diffuse nuclear (retained in AC; negative in NEC)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
p53 – wild-type patternRb – retainedKi67 – 5–20% (distinguishes from TC <5% and NEC >20%)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Typical Carcinoid↗Mitoses <2/2mm²; no necrosis; Ki67 <5%; better prognosis
LCNEC↗Ki67 >50%; geographic necrosis; large cells; prominent nucleoli; p53 mutant; Rb lost; OTP negative
WD NET G3 (metastatic to lung)↗Clinical history of extrapulmonary primary; OTP may be negative (site-dependent); PDX1/ISL1 for pancreatic origin
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • OTP retained in AC — critical distinction from LCNEC/SCLC which are OTP negative.
  • Grading is entirely based on mitotic count and necrosis — morphology alone insufficient. Count in 2mm² (not 10HPF).
  • Prognosis: 5-year OS ~70%. Nodal mets ~40–50%; distant mets ~20%. Worse than TC, better than NEC.
  • Surgery primary for resectable. Adjuvant chemotherapy not proven. SSA for metastatic SSTR2+ disease.
  • Molecular: MEN1, ATRX/DAXX — same as TC. TP53/RB1 wild-type (distinguishes from NEC).
Related / See Also
↗ TYPICAL CARCINOID (LUNG)↗ LARGE CELL NEUROENDOCRINE CARCINOMA (LCNEC, LUNG)↗ SMALL CELL LUNG CARCINOMA (SCLC)↗ Typical Carcinoid↗ LCNEC↗ WD NET G3 (metastatic to lung)
LARGE CELL NEUROENDOCRINE CARCINOMA (LCNEC, LUNG)
Thoracic Neuroendocrine Poorly differentiated Malignant
High-grade PD NEC. Ki67 typically >50–80%. Strong smoking association; peripheral predominance.
Clinical Context
  • High-grade PD NEC. Ki67 typically >50–80%. Strong smoking association; peripheral predominance.
  • Adults; male predominance; heavy smoker. Presents with rapidly progressive pulmonary mass; often metastatic at diagnosis.
  • Trigger: large peripheral lung mass, smoker, NE markers on biopsy — requires IHC confirmation.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Large cells with moderate cytoplasm; vesicular nuclei; prominent nucleoli — distinct from SCLC. Organoid/trabecular/rosette architecture (neuroendocrine pattern).
  • Geographic/comedo necrosis; high mitotic rate (>10/2mm²); peripheral palisading.
  • Must show NE morphology (organoid, rosettes, trabecular) AND IHC NE marker positivity — both required for diagnosis.
✚ Positive
Synaptophysin – positive (≥1 marker required)Chromogranin A – variableINSM1 – diffuse nuclearCD56 – positivep53 – mutant patternKi67 – typically >50%
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
Rb – lostOTP – negative (key distinction from AC/TC carcinoid family)TTF1 – variable (~50%; less reliable than in adenocarcinoma)SSTR2 – typically negative/weak
Top Differentials + Discriminators
