Atypical Carcinoid⚡OTP+; Ki67 5–20%; mitoses 2–10/2mm²; Rb retained; p53 wild-type; better prognosis
SCLC↗Small cells; nuclear moulding; crush artefact; Azzopardi; scant cytoplasm; same molecular profile as LCNEC
Large cell carcinoma (non-NE)NE markers absent; no organoid architecture; TTF1/p40 may be positive per lineage
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • OTP negative — critical distinction from TC/AC (OTP+). This is the key IHC discriminator for WD vs PD pulmonary NETs.
  • Molecular: TP53 + RB1 co-mutation (~40%); STK11, KEAP1 alterations. Two subtypes: SCLC-like (TP53+RB1) and NSCLC-like (STK11/KEAP1) — different chemotherapy sensitivity.
  • Treatment: SCLC-like LCNEC → platinum/etoposide. NSCLC-like → platinum/pemetrexed. Immunotherapy emerging.
  • Prognosis poor: 5-year OS ~15–20% for resected; worse for metastatic.
  • Exam trap: LCNEC requires BOTH NE morphology AND IHC — large cell carcinoma with focal NE markers alone is NOT LCNEC.
Related / See Also
↗ SMALL CELL LUNG CARCINOMA (SCLC)↗ ATYPICAL CARCINOID (LUNG)↗ TYPICAL CARCINOID (LUNG)⚡ Atypical Carcinoid↗ SCLC+ Add: Large cell carcinoma (non-NE)
SMALL CELL LUNG CARCINOMA (SCLC)
Thoracic Neuroendocrine Poorly differentiated Malignant 📋 7×
High-grade PD NEC. Ki67 typically >80%. Strongest smoking association of any lung cancer. Central/hilar mass; mediastinal involvement common.
Clinical Context
  • High-grade PD NEC. Ki67 typically >80%. Strongest smoking association of any lung cancer. Central/hilar mass; mediastinal involvement common.
  • Paraneoplastic syndromes: SIADH (hyponatraemia), Cushing's (ectopic ACTH), Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome, sensory neuropathy.
  • Trigger: central lung mass + mediastinal adenopathy in heavy smoker + paraneoplastic syndrome — SCLC until proven otherwise.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Small cells (2× lymphocyte size); scant cytoplasm; high N:C ratio; nuclear moulding — cells conform to each other.
  • Crush artefact on small biopsy — characteristic (fragile cells); Azzopardi effect (DNA encrustation of vessel walls); apoptotic bodies.
  • Geographic necrosis; extremely high mitotic rate. No organoid architecture (unlike LCNEC).
✚ Positive
Synaptophysin – diffuse (most reliable)CD56 – diffuseINSM1 – diffuse nuclearTTF1 – positive (~90% — highest of all lung NE tumours)CK – dot-like/paranuclearp53 – mutant patternKi67 – typically >80%
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
Rb – lost (complete absence — nearly universal)Chromogranin A – weak/variable (less reliable than Synaptophysin)OTP – negativeSSTR2 – negative/weakp40/CK5/6 – negative (excludes basaloid SCC)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
LCNEC↗Larger cells; moderate cytoplasm; prominent nucleoli; organoid architecture; same molecular profile; treatment may differ
Basaloid squamous cell carcinoma⚡p40+, CK5/6+; NE markers negative; peripheral palisading; squamous pearls; no crush/Azzopardi
Lymphoma (diffuse large B-cell)↗LCA+, PAX5+, CD20+; NE markers negative; no crush artefact typical of SCLC
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • TTF1 positive in ~90% of SCLC — highest rate of any pulmonary NE tumour. Do not interpret as adenocarcinoma without full panel.
  • OTP negative — same as LCNEC; both NEC family. TC/AC are OTP positive.
  • Staging: Limited disease (LD, one hemithorax) vs extensive disease (ED). LD: concurrent chemoradiotherapy (cisplatin/etoposide + RT). ED: platinum/etoposide ± atezolizumab.
  • Prophylactic cranial irradiation (PCI): standard in LD-SCLC responding to treatment.
  • Prognosis dismal: LD median OS ~18–24 months; ED median OS ~10–12 months. 5-year OS <10%.
  • Combined SCLC/NSCLC: ~10%; treat as SCLC component. Transformation of EGFR-mutant adenocarcinoma to SCLC under TKI therapy — important resistance mechanism.
Related / See Also
↗ LARGE CELL NEUROENDOCRINE CARCINOMA (LCNEC, LUNG)↗ ATYPICAL CARCINOID (LUNG)↗ TYPICAL CARCINOID (LUNG)↗ LCNEC⚡ Basaloid squamous cell carcinoma↗ Lymphoma (diffuse large B-cell)
DIPNECH / CARCINOID TUMOURLET (LUNG)
Thoracic Neuroendocrine Well-differentiated Benign
DIPNECH (Diffuse Idiopathic Pulmonary Neuroendocrine Cell Hyperplasia): WHO 2021 preneoplastic/neoplastic condition — proliferation of pulmonary neuroendocrine cells (PNEC) within bronchial/bronchiolar epithelium.
Clinical Context
  • DIPNECH (Diffuse Idiopathic Pulmonary Neuroendocrine Cell Hyperplasia): WHO 2021 preneoplastic/neoplastic condition — proliferation of pulmonary neuroendocrine cells (PNEC) within bronchial/bronchiolar epithelium.
  • Middle-aged women; non-smokers. Presents with cough, dyspnoea, air trapping (bronchiolar obstruction by NE cell proliferation). CT: multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules + mosaic attenuation.
  • Trigger: multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules + obstructive pattern on CT/PFTs in non-smoking woman — DIPNECH until proven otherwise.
Key Microscopic Features
  • PNEC hyperplasia: increased NE cells within bronchial/bronchiolar epithelium (>5 NE cells/bronchiole, or 3+ consecutive bronchioles); linear/nodular proliferation confined within basement membrane.
  • Carcinoid tumourlet: nodular NE proliferation ≤5mm — by definition confined, no invasion. Multiple, bilateral.
  • Typical Carcinoid: >5mm — same morphology but size-defined. TC/AC may arise from DIPNECH background. No mitoses/necrosis in tumourlets.
✚ Positive
Synaptophysin – diffuseChromogranin A – diffuseINSM1 – diffuse nuclearOTP – diffuse nuclearTTF1 – variable
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
Ki67 – very low (<2%)p53 – wild-typeRb – retained
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Typical Carcinoid⚡Size >5mm by definition; same histology/IHC; DIPNECH and TC/AC can co-exist — assess entire biopsy/resection
Metastatic WD-NET to lung↗Clinical history; site-specific IHC (CDX2 for midgut, PDX1/ISL1 for pancreas); OTP may help (lung NE tumours OTP+)
Peribronchiolar metaplasia (Lambertosis)Airway epithelial cells extending into alveoli; NE markers negative; no neuroendocrine morphology
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • DIPNECH is both preneoplastic (for TC/AC) and causes obstructive airway disease in its own right — dual pathological role.
  • Size thresholds: NE cell hyperplasia = within BM, any size. Tumourlet = nodular, ≤5mm. TC = >5mm.
  • Prognosis: DIPNECH/tumourlets themselves indolent; morbidity from obstructive lung disease. SSA (octreotide) may improve symptoms.
  • OTP: expressed in lung carcinoid family (DIPNECH/tumourlet/TC/AC) — supports lung origin and WD NE lineage.
  • Exam trap: multiple bilateral nodules in non-smoker = DIPNECH/carcinoid tumourlets until proven otherwise — not metastatic disease.
Related / See Also
↗ TYPICAL CARCINOID (LUNG)↗ ATYPICAL CARCINOID (LUNG)⚡ Typical Carcinoid↗ Metastatic WD-NET to lung+ Add: Peribronchiolar metaplasia (Lambertosis)
MALIGNANT PERIPHERAL NERVE SHEATH TUMOUR (MPNST)
Soft Tissue / Bone Mesenchymal Spindle cell Malignant 📋 1×
Arises from peripheral nerve, nerve sheath, or in background of neurofibroma. ~50% NF1-associated; ~10% radiation-induced; ~40% sporadic.
Clinical Context
  • Arises from peripheral nerve, nerve sheath, or in background of neurofibroma. ~50% NF1-associated; ~10% radiation-induced; ~40% sporadic.
  • NF1-associated: lifetime risk ~10%; younger patients; deeper location; worse prognosis than sporadic. Trigger: rapidly enlarging mass in known NF1 patient — assume MPNST until proven otherwise.
  • Sporadic: adults 20–50 years; large proximal nerve (sciatic, brachial plexus, sacral plexus) or paraspinal. Pain/neurological deficit suggests nerve origin.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Fascicles of spindle cells with hyperchromatic wavy/buckled nuclei; alternating hypercellular and hypocellular zones ('marbled' pattern); geographic necrosis; perivascular hypercellularity — characteristic.
  • Nuclear palisading may mimic schwannoma but Verocay bodies absent; no Antoni A/B. Mitoses brisk (typically >4/10HPF). Heterologous differentiation in ~15%: rhabdomyoblasts (Triton tumour), osteosarcoma, angiosarcoma.
  • Low-grade MPNST: infiltrative spindle cell neoplasm with increased cellularity and mild atypia arising within neurofibroma — subtle diagnosis; S100 focal/lost, H3K27me3 loss confirms.
✚ Positive
SOX10 – positive (~50–70%; more sensitive than S100 in high-grade)S100 – focal/weak (~50%; diffuse strong in schwannoma — focal/lost in MPNST)H3K27me3 – LOSS (trimethylation loss by IHC) — most useful marker; ~50–70% sensitivity, ~95% specificity for MPNST vs benign PNSTp53 – mutant pattern in manyKi67 – high (>10–20% in high-grade)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
H3K27me3 – retained in schwannoma, neurofibroma, synovial sarcoma (key exclusions)TLE1 – negative or weak (synovial sarcoma TLE1 diffuse strong)STAT6 – negative (excludes SFT)Desmin/MyoD1 – negative unless heterologous rhabdomyoblastic (Triton tumour)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Synovial Sarcoma (monophasic)↗TLE1 diffuse strong; SS18-SSX fusion (FISH/RNA-seq); H3K27me3 retained; no nerve association; hemangiopericytoma vasculature
Cellular Schwannoma↗S100/SOX10 diffuse strong; H3K27me3 retained; Antoni A without Verocay bodies; encapsulated; no necrosis; NF2 mutation
Leiomyosarcoma↗SMA+, h-caldesmon+, Desmin+; S100/SOX10 negative; H3K27me3 retained; no nerve association; intersecting fascicles
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • H3K27me3 loss (PRC2 complex inactivation via EED or SUZ12 mutation): present in ~70% NF1-associated MPNST, ~50% sporadic, ~90% radiation-induced. Absent in benign PNSTs — most useful single IHC marker.
  • CDKN2A/p16 loss (FISH or IHC): common in MPNST; supports malignancy in borderline cases. Combined H3K27me3 loss + CDKN2A loss highly specific.
  • Triton tumour (MPNST + rhabdomyoblastic differentiation): MyoD1+, Desmin+ in heterologous component; NF1 association strong; worse prognosis.
  • Grading: FNCLCC — Grade 2 or 3 in most cases. Low-grade MPNST (Grade 1) rare — arising within neurofibroma, H3K27me3 loss confirms.
  • Prognosis: 5-year OS ~35–50% overall; NF1-associated worse than sporadic (~20–30% vs ~50%). Size >5cm, NF1, positive margins = adverse factors.
  • Surgery: wide local excision primary. Adjuvant radiotherapy for positive/close margins. Chemotherapy (doxorubicin/ifosfamide) for metastatic — modest response.
  • Exam trap: focal S100 positivity does NOT exclude MPNST — diffuse strong S100 favours schwannoma. H3K27me3 loss is the discriminating marker.
Related / See Also
↗ SYNOVIAL SARCOMA (GI)↗ GI SCHWANNOMA↗ NEUROFIBROMA↗ SCHWANNOMA (CONVENTIONAL / PERIPHERAL)↗ Synovial Sarcoma (monophasic)↗ Cellular Schwannoma↗ Leiomyosarcoma
NEUROFIBROMA
Soft Tissue Neural Spindle cell Benign
Benign peripheral nerve sheath tumour arising from nerve fascicles (intraneural) or dermis/soft tissue (cutaneous/plexiform). Sporadic solitary cutaneous neurofibroma is common and incidental.
Clinical Context
  • Benign peripheral nerve sheath tumour arising from nerve fascicles (intraneural) or dermis/soft tissue (cutaneous/plexiform). Sporadic solitary cutaneous neurofibroma is common and incidental.
  • NF1 (neurofibromatosis type 1, AD, 17q11.2): multiple cutaneous neurofibromas, plexiform neurofibromas (pathognomonic), café-au-lait macules (≥6), Lisch nodules, axillary/inguinal freckling. 5–10% lifetime MPNST risk (arising from plexiform neurofibroma).
  • Trigger: rapidly enlarging or painful plexiform neurofibroma in NF1 patient → biopsy urgently to exclude MPNST transformation. NF1 is the most important predisposing condition for MPNST.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Loosely arranged spindle cells with wavy/buckled (comma-shaped) nuclei and tapered cytoplasmic processes; ill-defined pale cytoplasm. Nuclei less uniform than schwannoma — admixture of Schwann cells, perineural fibroblasts, and endoneurial fibroblasts.
  • Abundant myxocollagenous stroma with 'shredded carrot' wavy collagen bundles — characteristic at low power.
  • Entrapped axons within lesion (highlighted by neurofilament IHC) — distinguishes from schwannoma which displaces axons to periphery.
  • Plexiform variant: expanded individual nerve fascicles in a multinodular configuration — pathognomonic for NF1 when plexiform architecture present.
  • Atypical/ancient neurofibroma: scattered enlarged hyperchromatic nuclei (degenerative atypia); NO mitoses. Mitoses or increased cellularity → H3K27me3 IHC mandatory to exclude MPNST.
  • Lacks Antoni A/B areas, Verocay bodies, thick-walled hyalinised vessels, and nuclear palisading of schwannoma.
✚ Positive
S100 – positive but less diffuse/uniform than schwannoma (subset of cells)SOX10 – positive (Schwann cell component)CD34 – positive (stromal fibroblast-like cells — characteristic)Neurofilament – highlights entrapped axons (key distinguishing feature)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
H3K27me3 – RETAINED (loss confirms MPNST transformation — single most important marker)EMA – negative (unlike perineurioma)SMA – negativeSTAT6 – negative (excludes SFT)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Schwannoma⚡Antoni A/B areas; Verocay bodies; thick-walled hyalinised vessels; well encapsulated; no entrapped axons; S100 diffuse uniform; CD34 only in capsule; H3K27me3 retained; no NF1 association
MPNST↗H3K27me3 loss (most important); increased mitoses; fascicular hyperchromatic spindle cells; perivascular condensation; geographic necrosis; loss of S100 uniformity
Perineurioma↗EMA+, Claudin-1+; S100−; whorled storiform architecture around blood vessels; no NF1 association
Dermatofibrosarcoma Protuberans↗CD34 diffuse+; COL1A1-PDGFB fusion; infiltrative storiform; more cellular and uniform; S100 negative
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • H3K27me3 IHC: RETAINED in benign neurofibroma — loss of nuclear staining (with positive internal stromal/vascular control) confirms MPNST transformation. This is the single most important IHC in the neurofibroma→MPNST diagnostic pathway.
  • Plexiform neurofibroma: always consider NF1; refer for clinical genetics. Surgical resection difficult due to involvement of normal nerve fascicles.
  • Sporadic solitary cutaneous neurofibroma: excision curative; no recurrence risk; no malignant potential.
  • Exam trap: atypical neurofibroma — bizarre nuclei may alarm; key message is NO mitoses + H3K27me3 retained = still benign. Even a single mitosis warrants very careful consideration.
  • NF1 patients: selumetinib (MEK inhibitor) approved for inoperable plexiform neurofibromas in children.
Related / See Also
↗ MALIGNANT PERIPHERAL NERVE SHEATH TUMOUR (MPNST)↗ GI SCHWANNOMA⚡ Schwannoma↗ MPNST↗ Perineurioma↗ Dermatofibrosarcoma Protuberans
SCHWANNOMA (CONVENTIONAL / PERIPHERAL)
Soft Tissue Neural Spindle cell Benign
Benign peripheral nerve sheath tumour of pure Schwannian differentiation. Sporadic or NF2-associated (bilateral vestibular schwannomas = pathognomonic NF2). Any site; most common in posterior mediastinum, retroperitoneum, head/neck, extremity flexor surfaces.
Clinical Context
  • Benign peripheral nerve sheath tumour of pure Schwannian differentiation. Sporadic or NF2-associated (bilateral vestibular schwannomas = pathognomonic NF2). Any site; most common in posterior mediastinum, retroperitoneum, head/neck, extremity flexor surfaces.
  • Vestibular schwannoma (acoustic neuroma): most common cerebellopontine angle tumour — sensorineural hearing loss, tinnitus, vertigo. NF2 germline (22q12.2): bilateral = diagnostic.
  • Ancient schwannoma: long-standing lesion with degenerative nuclear atypia (hyperchromatic nuclei, no mitoses) — do not over-diagnose as malignant; H3K27me3 retained.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Encapsulated; biphasic: Antoni A (hypercellular — compact spindle cells with nuclear palisading forming Verocay bodies) and Antoni B (hypocellular — loose myxoid stroma, lipid-laden macrophages).
  • Verocay bodies: nuclear palisading with acellular eosinophilic fibrillar processes between — pathognomonic when present.
  • Thick-walled hyalinised vessels — characteristic; often with haemosiderin deposition.
  • Entrapped axons absent (displaced to capsule) — distinguishes from neurofibroma.
  • Cellular schwannoma: hypercellular Antoni A, no/rare Antoni B, mitoses up to 4/10HPF — benign but can mimic MPNST. H3K27me3 retained = key.
✚ Positive
S100 – diffuse strong (most uniform S100 of all peripheral nerve sheath tumours)SOX10 – diffuse nuclearGFAP – positive (variable)CD34 – positive in capsule only (not within tumour)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
H3K27me3 – RETAINED (loss = MPNST)EMA – negative within tumour (positive in perineural capsule only)SMA – negativeNeurofilament – negative within lesion (axons displaced to capsule)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Neurofibroma↗No Antoni A/B; no Verocay bodies; wavy 'shredded carrot' collagen; entrapped axons (NF+); CD34+ stromal cells; S100 patchy not diffuse; plexiform = NF1
MPNST↗H3K27me3 loss (key); fascicular hyperchromatic cells; perivascular condensation; geographic necrosis; mitoses >4/10HPF; S100 focal/lost; NF1 background
Solitary Fibrous Tumour↗STAT6 nuclear+; CD34 diffuse+; hemangiopericytoma 'staghorn' vessels; NAB2-STAT6 fusion; S100 negative
Leiomyoma / Leiomyosarcoma⚡SMA+, Desmin+, h-caldesmon+; pink fibrillar cytoplasm; cigar-shaped blunt nuclei; S100 negative
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • H3K27me3: retained in ALL schwannomas including cellular and ancient variants — loss definitively excludes schwannoma and confirms MPNST in the right context.
  • Cellular schwannoma: mitoses ≤4/10HPF with retained H3K27me3 = still benign. If mitoses >4 + any H3K27me3 loss → reclassify as MPNST.
  • NF2 association: bilateral vestibular schwannomas. GI schwannoma is a distinct entity — no NF2 mutation, peripheral lymphoid cuff.
  • Ancient/degenerate change: nuclear hyperchromasia, pleomorphism, no mitoses — benign degenerative atypia; H3K27me3 retained confirms.
  • Exam trap: cellular schwannoma can have high cellularity and focal mitotic activity — always check H3K27me3 before diagnosing MPNST.
Related / See Also
↗ NEUROFIBROMA↗ MALIGNANT PERIPHERAL NERVE SHEATH TUMOUR (MPNST)↗ GI SCHWANNOMA↗ Neurofibroma↗ MPNST↗ Solitary Fibrous Tumour⚡ Leiomyoma / Leiomyosarcoma
DERMATOFIBROSARCOMA PROTUBERANS (DFSP)
Skin / Dermatopath Fibroblastic Spindle cell Intermediate
Low-grade fibroblastic sarcoma of dermis/subcutis. Locally aggressive with high recurrence rate; low metastatic potential (<5%) unless fibrosarcomatous transformation (FS-DFSP, ~10-15%).
Clinical Context
  • Low-grade fibroblastic sarcoma of dermis/subcutis. Locally aggressive with high recurrence rate; low metastatic potential (<5%) unless fibrosarcomatous transformation (FS-DFSP, ~10-15%).
  • Young-middle aged adults; trunk and proximal limbs most common. Slow-growing indurated plaque → nodular protuberance; often misdiagnosed clinically as scar/keloid for years.
  • FS-DFSP (fibrosarcomatous transformation): herringbone fascicular pattern superimposed on classic DFSP — metastatic risk rises to ~15%; treat as intermediate/high-grade sarcoma.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Dense monotonous uniform spindle cells in tight storiform (cartwheel/pinwheel) pattern — characteristic at low power.
  • Infiltrates dermis and subcutaneous fat in a characteristic 'honeycomb' pattern — lace-like entrapment of fat lobules; extends along fibrous septa.
  • Low mitotic rate (<5/10HPF in classic DFSP); minimal atypia; no necrosis.
  • FS-DFSP: abrupt transition to cellular herringbone fascicles with increased mitoses (>5/10HPF) and atypia — must be reported as changes treatment.
  • Myxoid DFSP variant: prominent myxoid stroma — can mimic myxofibrosarcoma; CD34+ confirms.
✚ Positive
CD34 – diffuse strong (most useful; retained in classic DFSP)Vimentin – diffuseNestin – positiveCOL1A1-PDGFB fusion – diagnostic (FISH/RT-PCR)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
S100 – negative (key; excludes neurofibroma/schwannoma/desmoplastic melanoma)SMA – negative (excludes nodular fasciitis/leiomyoma)Desmin – negativeCD68 – negative (excludes dermatofibroma)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Dermatofibroma (fibrous histiocytoma)CD34 negative (or only at periphery); CD68+; EMA+ (surrounding epidermis hyperplastic); smaller, well-demarcated; no fat infiltration; no COL1A1-PDGFB
Neurofibroma↗S100+ (subset); SOX10+; wavy collagen; entrapped axons; NF1 association; CD34 in stroma only (not diffuse)
Nodular Fasciitis↗SMA+; rapid growth (weeks); self-limiting; 'tissue culture' myofibroblasts; USP6 rearrangement; no storiform; no fat infiltration
Solitary Fibrous Tumour↗STAT6 nuclear+; NAB2-STAT6 fusion; hemangiopericytoma vessels; CD34+ but patternless not storiform; S100 negative
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • COL1A1-PDGFB fusion t(17;22)(q21;q13): constitutively activates PDGFR-β signalling. Imatinib approved for unresectable/metastatic DFSP — dramatic responses. Test with FISH or RT-PCR.
  • FS-DFSP: CD34 may be lost in the fibrosarcomatous component — do not use CD34 loss to exclude DFSP if classic areas are present.
  • Margins: wide local excision (2-3cm) or Mohs surgery for facial/acral sites. High local recurrence (up to 50%) with narrow margins — always comment on margin status.
  • Exam trap: honeycomb fat infiltration is a key low-power feature — without this, DFSP may be mistaken for dermatofibroma. Always assess deep margin.
  • Myxoid variant: CD34+ diffuse storiform pattern persists despite myxoid change — MUC4 negative (distinguishes from LGFMS).
Related / See Also
↗ NEUROFIBROMA↗ NODULAR FASCIITIS↗ LOW GRADE FIBROMYXOID SARCOMA (LGFMS / EVANS TUMOUR)+ Add: Dermatofibroma (fibrous histiocytoma)↗ Neurofibroma↗ Nodular Fasciitis↗ Solitary Fibrous Tumour
NODULAR FASCIITIS
Soft Tissue Fibroblastic Spindle cell Benign
Reactive myofibroblastic proliferation — the most important benign spindle cell mimic of sarcoma. Rapid growth over days to weeks (history is diagnostic); typically <3cm; spontaneously regresses.
Clinical Context
  • Reactive myofibroblastic proliferation — the most important benign spindle cell mimic of sarcoma. Rapid growth over days to weeks (history is diagnostic); typically <3cm; spontaneously regresses.
  • Young adults; upper extremity (forearm/arm) most common, then trunk and head/neck. Subcutaneous, rarely intramuscular or intravascular (intravascular fasciitis — do not misdiagnose as angiosarcoma).
  • Trigger: rapidly enlarging subcutaneous nodule in a young adult — ALWAYS consider nodular fasciitis before diagnosing sarcoma. History of rapid growth is the single most useful clinical feature.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Loosely arranged plump myofibroblasts in a myxoid stroma with feathery 'tissue culture'-like appearance — characteristic at scanning magnification.
  • Short irregular fascicles; cells with vesicular nuclei and prominent nucleoli; abundant pale cytoplasm with indistinct borders.
  • Mitoses can be brisk (up to 10+/10HPF) but atypical mitoses absent — high mitotic rate without atypia is a key reassuring feature.
  • Extravasated red blood cells, lymphocytes, and occasional osteoclast-like giant cells scattered throughout.
  • Variable collagenisation ('keloid-like' hyaline collagen at periphery); microcystic spaces; no necrosis.
✚ Positive
SMA – diffuse (myofibroblastic differentiation)Vimentin – diffuseMSA – positiveUSP6 rearrangement – present (~90%; FISH or RNA-seq)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
S100 – negative (excludes schwannoma/neurofibroma)CD34 – negative (excludes DFSP/SFT)Desmin – negative or focal (excludes leiomyoma)STAT6 – negative (excludes SFT)ALK – negative (excludes IMT)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Fibrosarcoma / MyxofibrosarcomaPersistent growth; herringbone fascicles; nuclear atypia; atypical mitoses; no tissue culture appearance; no USP6 rearrangement; >3cm
IMT (Inflammatory Myofibroblastic Tumour)⚡ALK+ (50-60%); plasma cell-rich inflammatory infiltrate; less myxoid; slower growth; retroperitoneal/mesenteric common; ALK rearrangement
DFSP↗CD34 diffuse+; storiform pattern; honeycomb fat infiltration; COL1A1-PDGFB fusion; slow growth over years
Spindle cell lipomaCD34+; RB1 loss; short bland spindle cells + mature fat + ropey collagen; posterior neck/shoulder; no myxoid tissue culture pattern
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • USP6 rearrangement (17p13): present in ~90% of nodular fasciitis (various partners — MYH9 most common). Diagnostic when detected; supports benign behaviour. RNA-seq or FISH on paraffin material.
  • Self-limiting: if diagnosis is certain, conservative excision or observation (some cases regress spontaneously post-biopsy). No adjuvant treatment required.
  • Cranial fasciitis: occurs in children on the skull — can erode bone, alarming imaging; same histology, same behaviour.
  • Intravascular fasciitis: within vessel walls — do not misinterpret vascular invasion as angiosarcoma. SMA+, USP6 rearrangement confirms.
  • Exam trap: brisk mitotic activity in a myxoid spindle cell tumour → do NOT diagnose sarcoma without first excluding nodular fasciitis. Clinical history (rapid growth, young adult) is mandatory.
Related / See Also
↗ INFLAMMATORY MYOFIBROBLASTIC TUMOUR (IMT)↗ DERMATOFIBROSARCOMA PROTUBERANS (DFSP)+ Add: Fibrosarcoma / Myxofibrosarcoma⚡ IMT (Inflammatory Myofibroblastic Tumour)↗ DFSP+ Add: Spindle cell lipoma
LOW GRADE FIBROMYXOID SARCOMA (LGFMS / EVANS TUMOUR)
Soft Tissue Fibroblastic Spindle cell Malignant
Deceptively bland low-grade fibroblastic sarcoma with significant long-term metastatic potential despite innocuous histology — the original 'wolf in sheep's clothing'. Young to middle-aged adults; deep soft tissue of proximal extremities and trunk.
Clinical Context
  • Deceptively bland low-grade fibroblastic sarcoma with significant long-term metastatic potential despite innocuous histology — the original 'wolf in sheep's clothing'. Young to middle-aged adults; deep soft tissue of proximal extremities and trunk.
  • Metastases occur in up to 50% of cases at 10+ years (lung most common) despite low-grade appearance — long-term surveillance mandatory. No reliable histological predictor of metastasis.
  • Trigger: deep soft tissue spindle cell tumour with alternating fibrous and myxoid zones in a young adult — always exclude LGFMS before diagnosing benign fibromatosis.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Alternating hypocellular fibrous (collagenous) and myxoid zones — characteristic low-power zoning pattern.
  • Bland uniform spindle cells with minimal atypia and low mitotic rate (<2/10HPF); deceptively benign nuclear morphology.
  • Whorling/storiform growth pattern with arcuate curvilinear blood vessels.
  • Giant rosettes of collagen (collagen rosettes with central fibrous core surrounded by epithelioid cells) — present in subset (~40%); pathognomonic when present.
  • Sclerosing epithelioid fibrosarcoma (SEF): variant with round/epithelioid cells in hyalinised collagen; same FUS-CREB3L2 fusion; more aggressive.
✚ Positive
MUC4 – diffuse strong (most sensitive/specific IHC marker; surrogate for FUS fusion)Vimentin – diffuseEMA – variable/focal (~20%)FUS-CREB3L2 or FUS-CREB3L1 fusion – diagnostic (FISH/RNA-seq)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
S100 – negative (excludes schwannoma/neurofibroma)CD34 – negative (excludes SFT/DFSP)SMA – negative (excludes desmoid/nodular fasciitis)STAT6 – negative (excludes SFT)Nuclear β-catenin – negative (excludes desmoid fibromatosis)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Desmoid FibromatosisNuclear β-catenin+; APC/CTNNB1 mutation; no myxoid zones; no MUC4; infiltrative into muscle; associated with Gardner/FAP or prior surgery
Neurofibroma (myxoid)↗S100+; SOX10+; CD34 stromal+; wavy collagen; entrapped axons; no MUC4; NF1 association
MyxofibrosarcomaOlder patients; superficial; curvilinear vessels with perivascular condensation; nuclear atypia; no alternating fibrous/myxoid zones; MUC4 negative; no FUS fusion
Solitary Fibrous Tumour↗STAT6 nuclear+; CD34+; NAB2-STAT6 fusion; hemangiopericytoma vasculature; no myxoid zoning; MUC4 negative
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • MUC4 IHC: highly sensitive and specific for LGFMS (and SEF) — nearly always negative in desmoid, SFT, neurofibroma, myxofibrosarcoma. Single best IHC screening test.
  • FUS-CREB3L2 t(7;16)(q33;p11): present in ~90% of LGFMS; minority have FUS-CREB3L1. RNA-seq is preferred for molecular confirmation (FISH may miss inversion-based variants).
  • SEF (sclerosing epithelioid fibrosarcoma): overlapping entity — EWSR1-CREB3L1/2 fusions also described; more aggressive; may have WT1 expression. Recognise by hyalinised stroma + epithelioid cells.
  • Prognosis: deceptively good short-term (low grade, slow growth) but 50% metastatic rate at 10 years — must convey long-term risk to clinicians. Wide excision with clear margins essential.
  • Exam trap: bland spindle cell tumour with myxoid areas → DO NOT report as benign without MUC4 IHC. LGFMS can be misdiagnosed as fibroma, desmoid, or low-grade fibrosarcoma.
Related / See Also
↗ DESMOID TUMOUR / INTRA-ABDOMINAL FIBROMATOSIS↗ DERMATOFIBROSARCOMA PROTUBERANS (DFSP)↗ SOLITARY FIBROUS TUMOUR (SFT)+ Add: Desmoid Fibromatosis↗ Neurofibroma (myxoid)+ Add: Myxofibrosarcoma↗ Solitary Fibrous Tumour
PERINEURIOMA
Soft Tissue Neural Spindle cell Benign
Rare benign peripheral nerve sheath tumour showing pure perineural cell differentiation. Two main subtypes: intraneural perineurioma (young adults, fusiform nerve enlargement, motor deficits) and soft tissue perineurioma (adults, subcutaneous/intramuscular mass, no nerve association).
Clinical Context
  • Rare benign peripheral nerve sheath tumour showing pure perineural cell differentiation. Two main subtypes: intraneural perineurioma (young adults, fusiform nerve enlargement, motor deficits) and soft tissue perineurioma (adults, subcutaneous/intramuscular mass, no nerve association).
  • No association with NF1 or NF2. Sclerosing perineurioma: distinct variant — digits of young adults, no nerve involvement.
  • Trigger: 'onion bulb' formations on nerve biopsy in a young patient with progressive focal weakness — consider intraneural perineurioma.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Bland spindle cells with elongated bipolar cytoplasmic processes; indistinct cell borders; parallel or whorling arrangement.
  • Intraneural: concentric layering of perineural cells around axons ('onion bulb' pattern) — expanded nerve fascicle.
  • Soft tissue: storiform or whorling architecture in collagenous stroma; no axons within lesion.
  • Sclerosing variant: hypocellular, hyalinised stroma with epithelioid/spindle cells in cords and trabeculae — hands/fingers of young adults.
✚ Positive
EMA – diffuse (most reliable marker; lost in schwannoma/neurofibroma)Claudin-1 – positive (tight junction protein; sensitive for perineural differentiation)Glut-1 – positiveGFAP – negative (key negative)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
S100 – negative (key; distinguishes from schwannoma and neurofibroma)SOX10 – negativeCD34 – negative (soft tissue type; occasional focal staining)SMA – negativeNeurofilament – negative (no axons within lesion in soft tissue type)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Neurofibroma↗S100+ (subset); SOX10+; EMA negative; entrapped axons (NF+); wavy collagen; CD34 stromal+; NF1 association
Schwannoma⚡S100 diffuse+; SOX10+; Antoni A/B; Verocay bodies; EMA in capsule only; thick-walled vessels; encapsulated
Synovial Sarcoma (monophasic)↗TLE1+; SS18-SSX fusion (FISH); BCL2+; CD99+; focal CK/EMA; no EMA whorling pattern; more aggressive
Low-grade fibromyxoid sarcoma↗MUC4+; FUS-CREB3L2 fusion; alternating fibrous/myxoid zones; EMA focal not diffuse
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • EMA + Claudin-1 + Glut-1 combined: highly specific for perineural differentiation. S100 negativity is the key discriminator from schwannoma and neurofibroma.
  • Intraneural perineurioma: clinical presentation of progressive focal weakness in young adult with nerve enlargement on MRI — biopsy shows onion bulb formations.
  • Soft tissue perineurioma: benign, complete excision curative. No malignant counterpart (malignant perineurioma extremely rare).
  • Sclerosing perineurioma: most common in fingers of young adults; hyalinised stroma; EMA+ cords and trabeculae; must be distinguished from epithelioid sarcoma (INI1 retained in perineurioma).
  • Exam relevance: a rare entity but tests knowledge of EMA in the context of peripheral nerve sheath tumours — EMA+ PNST = perineurioma (not schwannoma).
Related / See Also
↗ NEUROFIBROMA↗ SCHWANNOMA (CONVENTIONAL / PERIPHERAL)↗ MALIGNANT PERIPHERAL NERVE SHEATH TUMOUR (MPNST)↗ Neurofibroma⚡ Schwannoma↗ Synovial Sarcoma (monophasic)↗ Low-grade fibromyxoid sarcoma
ATYPICAL FIBROXANTHOMA (AFX) / SUPERFICIAL UPS
Skin / Dermatopath Fibroblastic Spindle cell Intermediate
AFX: low-grade undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma confined to dermis; superficial UPS (sUPS) is the deeper variant extending into subcutis with higher metastatic potential. Both are diagnoses of exclusion.
Clinical Context
  • AFX: low-grade undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma confined to dermis; superficial UPS (sUPS) is the deeper variant extending into subcutis with higher metastatic potential. Both are diagnoses of exclusion.
  • Elderly patients; sun-damaged skin of head and neck; strong UV association (p53 mutations, TERT promoter mutations). Rapid growth; polypoid/ulcerated nodule.
  • Trigger: rapidly growing ulcerated nodule on sun-damaged scalp in an elderly patient — extensive IHC panel required to exclude all other spindle cell malignancies before AFX diagnosis.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Dermal-based nodule (does NOT extend into subcutis in classic AFX); no epidermal involvement.
  • Highly pleomorphic spindle, epithelioid, and bizarre multinucleate giant cells; marked nuclear atypia; brisk mitotic rate including atypical mitoses.
  • Minimal stroma; haemorrhage common; overlying ulceration.
  • Paradox: extreme pleomorphism + dermal confinement = low metastatic potential. Extension into subcutis/fascia = sUPS → reclassify.
  • No necrosis in classic AFX (necrosis raises suspicion for sUPS or other high-grade sarcoma).
✚ Positive
CD10 – positive (most consistently expressed)Vimentin – diffuseProcollagen 1 – positivep53 – aberrant (TP53 mutation)TERT promoter mutation – present
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
S100 – negative (excludes desmoplastic melanoma — most critical exclusion)SOX10 – negative (excludes desmoplastic melanoma/spindle cell melanoma)MelanA/HMB45 – negativeCK (AE1/AE3, MNF116) – negative (excludes spindle cell SCC/sarcomatoid carcinoma)p63/p40 – negative (excludes spindle cell SCC)Desmin/SMA – negative (excludes leiomyosarcoma/myofibroblastic tumours)CD34 – negative (excludes DFSP/SFT)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Desmoplastic melanoma↗S100+, SOX10+; associated melanoma in situ component; perineural invasion common; NTRK fusions; clinically more indolent but deeper invasion; MelanA/HMB45 usually negative
Spindle cell / sarcomatoid SCCp63+, p40+; CK+; often arises in actinic keratosis/in situ SCC; dysplastic epidermal connection; FISH for CDKN2A
Leiomyosarcoma (cutaneous)↗SMA+, Desmin+, h-caldesmon+; fascicular arrangement; less pleomorphic than AFX; cigar-shaped nuclei
Pleomorphic Dermal Sarcoma (PDS/sUPS)⚡Subcutaneous invasion + necrosis + PNI distinguishes PDS from AFX — same IHC profile; AFX is diagnosis of exclusion within dermis only
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • AFX is a diagnosis of exclusion: before diagnosing AFX, a panel must be run and be negative for: S100/SOX10 (melanoma), CK/p63/p40 (SCC), SMA/Desmin (LMS), CD34 (DFSP/SFT). CD10 is the only consistently positive marker.
  • AFX vs PDS/sUPS: the distinction is anatomical and prognostic. AFX = dermis only, no necrosis, no PNI → low metastatic risk. PDS = subcutaneous extension OR necrosis OR PNI → intermediate/high risk.
  • TERT promoter and TP53 mutations: present in both AFX and PDS — not discriminatory between the two but support the diagnosis in the spindle cell pleomorphic context.
  • UV mutational signature: characteristic C>T transitions at dipyrimidine sites — supports UV pathogenesis.
  • Exam trap: S100 is the most important exclusion — desmoplastic melanoma and spindle cell melanoma can be markedly pleomorphic on sun-damaged skin. Never diagnose AFX without S100 and SOX10.
Related / See Also
↗ PLEOMORPHIC DERMAL SARCOMA (PDS) / UNDIFFERENTIATED PLEOMORPHIC SARCOMA OF SKIN↗ MELANOMA (CUTANEOUS)↗ CUTANEOUS SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA (cSCC)↗ Desmoplastic melanoma+ Add: Spindle cell / sarcomatoid SCC↗ Leiomyosarcoma (cutaneous)⚡ Pleomorphic Dermal Sarcoma (PDS/sUPS)
DESMOPLASTIC MELANOMA
Skin / Dermatopath Melanocytic Spindle cell Malignant
Rare variant of melanoma (~4% of all melanomas) with predominantly spindle cell, fibroblast-like morphology in a desmoplastic (sclerosing) stroma. Sun-damaged skin of head and neck; elderly patients; male predominance.
Clinical Context
  • Rare variant of melanoma (~4% of all melanomas) with predominantly spindle cell, fibroblast-like morphology in a desmoplastic (sclerosing) stroma. Sun-damaged skin of head and neck; elderly patients; male predominance.
  • Pure desmoplastic melanoma (>90% desmoplastic component): paradoxically lower nodal metastasis rate (~5%) but high local recurrence. Mixed desmoplastic melanoma (>10% non-desmoplastic component): nodal risk similar to conventional melanoma.
  • Trigger: poorly marginated indurated scar-like lesion on sun-damaged face/scalp in elderly patient — always consider desmoplastic melanoma. Often clinically underestimated or missed.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Spindled melanocytes embedded in dense desmoplastic (collagenised/sclerosing) stroma — low cellularity, deceptively bland at first glance.
  • Perineural invasion: characteristic and common — infiltrating spindle cells tracking along nerve twigs; key diagnostic feature.
  • Overlying epidermal component: melanoma in situ or lentigo maligna-type change present in ~50-70% — critical clue; always examine full epidermis.
  • Lymphoid aggregates at periphery (Breslow depth assessed from granular cell layer to deepest invasive cell — same as conventional melanoma).
  • Nuclear atypia variable; mitoses may be sparse in pure desmoplastic variant.
✚ Positive
S100 – diffuse strong (most reliable; the critical positive marker)SOX10 – diffuse nuclearp75 (NGFR) – positiveVimentin – diffuse
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
HMB45 – negative (key; positive in conventional melanoma)Melan-A (MART-1) – negative (key; positive in conventional melanoma)MiTF – variable/weak (usually negative in pure desmoplastic)CK – negative (excludes spindle cell SCC/sarcomatoid carcinoma)CD34 – negative (excludes DFSP/SFT)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Spindle cell / sarcomatoid SCCp63+, p40+, CK+; S100 negative; SOX10 negative; dysplastic epidermal connection; no lentigo maligna in situ component; no perineural tracking
AFX / Pleomorphic Dermal Sarcoma⚡S100 negative (key); SOX10 negative; CD10+; diagnosis of exclusion; no in situ melanocytic component; highly pleomorphic (AFX more so than DM)
Neurofibroma (desmoplastic/sclerotic)↗S100 patchy (not diffuse); SOX10+; CD34 stromal+; entrapped axons; no lentigo maligna; NF1 association
Conventional Schwannoma↗S100 diffuse+ (shared); Antoni A/B; Verocay bodies; encapsulated; no epidermal melanocytic in situ component; HMB45/Melan-A negative (shared)
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • S100 + SOX10 are the critical positive markers — HMB45 and Melan-A are typically NEGATIVE in pure desmoplastic melanoma (unlike conventional melanoma). Failure to recognise this leads to false negatives.
  • NTRK1/2/3 fusions: present in ~5-10% of desmoplastic melanomas — targetable with larotrectinib/entrectinib. Test in metastatic disease.
  • NF1 mutation: most common driver (~50%) in desmoplastic melanoma — distinct from BRAF V600E-driven conventional melanoma. BRAF V600E rare in desmoplastic melanoma.
  • Pure vs mixed desmoplastic melanoma: pure = sentinel node biopsy may be omitted (low nodal risk ~5%); mixed = sentinel node biopsy as per conventional melanoma. Report percentage desmoplastic component.
  • Exam trap: HMB45/Melan-A negative spindle cell tumour on sun-damaged skin = do NOT exclude melanoma — always include S100 and SOX10. Desmoplastic melanoma is the classic false negative for the conventional melanoma panel.
Related / See Also
↗ MELANOMA (CUTANEOUS)↗ ATYPICAL FIBROXANTHOMA (AFX) / SUPERFICIAL UPS↗ NEUROFIBROMA+ Add: Spindle cell / sarcomatoid SCC⚡ AFX / Pleomorphic Dermal Sarcoma↗ Neurofibroma (desmoplastic/sclerotic)↗ Conventional Schwannoma
GCNIS / ITGCN (TESTIS)
Urological Germ Cell Round cell Malignant 📋 3×
Germ cell neoplasia in situ — obligate precursor to all post-pubertal GCTs except spermatocytic tumour. Prevalence ~0.4–1% in cryptorchid testes.
Clinical Context
  • Germ cell neoplasia in situ — obligate precursor to all post-pubertal GCTs except spermatocytic tumour. Prevalence ~0.4–1% in cryptorchid testes.
  • Discovered incidentally in contralateral testis biopsy, infertile males, or adjacent to invasive GCT.
  • Risk of progression to invasive GCT: ~70% within 7 years — surveillance or low-dose RT (16–20 Gy) to affected testis.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Large atypical cells with clear glycogen-rich cytoplasm and irregular nuclei along seminiferous tubule basement membrane; Sertoli cells displaced to lumen.
  • Spermatogenesis absent or markedly reduced. Tubular diameter reduced.
  • PAS-positive cytoplasmic glycogen. Intratubular growth only — invasion absent by definition.
✚ Positive
PLAP – positiveOCT3/4 – diffuse nuclear (most sensitive/specific)CD117 (cKIT) – positiveD2-40 (podoplanin) – positive
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CD30 – negative (embryonal CA positive)AFP – negativeInhibin – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Intratubular large cell hyalinising Sertoli cell tumour↗PAS-negative; Inhibin+; no OCT3/4; associated with Peutz-Jeghers/CNC
Spermatocyte with maturation arrestNo cytological atypia; spermatocyte nuclear features; OCT3/4 negative
Pagetoid spread of lymphomaCD45+, PAX5+; OCT3/4 negative
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • OCT3/4 is the most reliable single marker — diffuse nuclear in GCNIS and all OCT3/4-positive GCTs (seminoma, embryonal CA).
  • Biopsy contralateral testis in: cryptorchidism, atrophic testis <12mL, prior GCT, infertility with testicular microlithiasis.
  • Management: surveillance (if only testis), low-dose RT 16–20 Gy (spares Leydig cells, sterilises), or orchidectomy.
Related / See Also
↗ SEMINOMA (TESTIS)↗ EMBRYONAL CARCINOMA (TESTIS)↗ YOLK SAC TUMOUR (TESTIS)↗ Intratubular large cell hyalinising Sertoli cell tumour+ Add: Spermatocyte with maturation arrest+ Add: Pagetoid spread of lymphoma
SEMINOMA (TESTIS)
Urological Germ Cell Round cell Malignant 📋 7×
Most common testicular GCT (~50%). Peak 30–40 years. Painless testicular enlargement. Pure seminoma — βhCG may be mildly elevated (~10–15%); AFP always normal.
Clinical Context
  • Most common testicular GCT (~50%). Peak 30–40 years. Painless testicular enlargement. Pure seminoma — βhCG may be mildly elevated (~10–15%); AFP always normal.
  • Cryptorchidism strongest risk factor (4–10× risk). Bilateral in 2–3%.
  • Trigger: painless testicular mass, normal AFP, mildly elevated βhCG — seminoma until proven otherwise.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Sheets and nests of uniform large polygonal cells; clear glycogen-rich cytoplasm; prominent central nucleoli; distinct cell membranes.
  • Fibrous septa with lymphocytic infiltrate — characteristic. Granulomatous reaction in ~20%. Syncytiotrophoblast giant cells in ~10% (βhCG positive — does not upstage).
  • GCNIS invariably present at periphery. No necrosis in pure seminoma.
✚ Positive
PLAP – diffuseOCT3/4 – diffuse nuclearCD117 – diffuse membranousD2-40 – positiveSALL4 – nuclear
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CD30 – negative (key embryonal CA distinction)AFP – negativeCK – negative (or focal only)Inhibin – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Embryonal Carcinoma↗CD30+, CK diffuse+; marked pleomorphism; necrosis; glandular/papillary architecture; OCT3/4+ but CD117−
Spermatocytic tumourOlder men (>50); three cell populations (small/medium/large); no GCNIS; OCT3/4−; CD117−; excellent prognosis
Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma↗CD45+, CD20+, PAX5+; older men; bilateral in 40%; OCT3/4−
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Stage I pure seminoma (organ-confined): surveillance preferred (5-year relapse-free ~82%) or single-dose carboplatin AUC7.
  • Seminoma highly radiosensitive and chemosensitive — excellent prognosis at all stages.
  • Spermatocytic tumour (formerly spermatocytic seminoma): completely distinct entity — WHO 2022 renamed. No GCNIS, no metastasis, no treatment needed.
  • AFP elevation in apparent pure seminoma → must assume non-seminomatous GCT component (even if not seen on histology) → treat as NSGCT.
Related / See Also
↗ GCNIS / ITGCN (TESTIS)↗ EMBRYONAL CARCINOMA (TESTIS)↗ YOLK SAC TUMOUR (TESTIS)↗ Embryonal Carcinoma+ Add: Spermatocytic tumour↗ Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma
EMBRYONAL CARCINOMA (TESTIS)
Urological Germ Cell Epithelioid Malignant 📋 2×
Pure embryonal CA ~3% of GCTs; more often component of mixed NSGCT (~80% mixed GCTs contain EC). Peak 20–30 years.
Clinical Context
  • Pure embryonal CA ~3% of GCTs; more often component of mixed NSGCT (~80% mixed GCTs contain EC). Peak 20–30 years.
  • AFP may be elevated; βhCG elevated if syncytiotrophoblast present. Aggressive — LVI common; early haematogenous spread.
  • Trigger: young man, testicular mass, elevated AFP ± βhCG — non-seminomatous GCT.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Sheets, nests, papillary or glandular/tubular architecture; large pleomorphic cells; amphophilic cytoplasm; prominent nucleoli; indistinct cell borders; extensive necrosis.
  • High mitotic rate; atypical mitoses; LVI common. Necrosis prominent — unlike seminoma.
  • GCNIS at periphery. Admixed with other GCT elements in mixed tumours.
✚ Positive
OCT3/4 – diffuse nuclearCD30 – diffuse strong (key marker)SALL4 – nuclearCK – diffuse (AE1/AE3)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
AFP – negative (YST positive)CD117 – negative (seminoma positive)D2-40 – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Seminoma↗CD30−, CK focal/negative; uniform cells; clear cytoplasm; fibrous septa with lymphocytes; no necrosis
Yolk Sac Tumour↗AFP+; Schiller-Duval bodies; myxoid stroma; CD30−; reticular/microcystic architecture
Poorly differentiated carcinoma (metastatic)⚡Clinical context; OCT3/4−; SALL4−; site-specific markers
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • CD30 is the most specific single marker for embryonal carcinoma — diffuse strong; absent in all other GCT elements.
  • LVI on orchidectomy specimen = pT2 — critical staging element driving adjuvant decision in stage I.
  • Stage I NSGCT with EC-predominant tumour + LVI: ~50% relapse risk without treatment — BEP ×1 cycle or surveillance.
  • BEP chemotherapy (bleomycin, etoposide, cisplatin) — gold standard for metastatic NSGCT.
Related / See Also
↗ GCNIS / ITGCN (TESTIS)↗ SEMINOMA (TESTIS)↗ YOLK SAC TUMOUR (TESTIS)↗ Seminoma↗ Yolk Sac Tumour⚡ Poorly differentiated carcinoma (metastatic)
YOLK SAC TUMOUR (TESTIS)
Urological Germ Cell Glandular/mucinous Malignant 📋 9×
Pure YST: most common GCT in pre-pubertal boys (<3 years); AFP markedly elevated (physiological in neonates — interpret age-adjusted).
Clinical Context
  • Pure YST: most common GCT in pre-pubertal boys (<3 years); AFP markedly elevated (physiological in neonates — interpret age-adjusted).
  • Post-pubertal: almost always component of mixed NSGCT (~40%). AFP elevation correlates with YST component.
  • Post-treatment AFP monitoring: half-life ~5–7 days — failure to normalise indicates residual/relapsed disease.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Reticular/microcystic pattern most common (anastomosing spaces lined by flat cells in myxoid stroma). Schiller-Duval bodies (glomeruloid structures with central fibrovascular core) — pathognomonic but present in only ~50%.
  • Other patterns: solid, papillary, macrocystic, hepatoid, glandular/enteric. Hyaline globules — PAS+, AFP+.
  • Pale eosinophilic extracellular basement membrane material. Myxoid stroma characteristic.
✚ Positive
AFP – positive (most specific; may be focal)SALL4 – diffuse nuclearGlypican-3 – positiveCK – positivePLAP – variable
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
OCT3/4 – negative (key distinction from seminoma/EC)CD30 – negativeCD117 – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Embryonal Carcinoma↗CD30+, OCT3/4+; no Schiller-Duval bodies; no AFP production; marked pleomorphism
Clear cell carcinoma (metastatic)⚡Clinical context; OCT3/4−; SALL4−; Glypican-3 may be positive in HCC
Hepatoid carcinomaAFP may be positive; Hep Par1+; SALL4−; no GCT context; reticular pattern absent
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Schiller-Duval bodies are pathognomonic but inconsistently present — AFP immunopositivity is more reliable.
  • Pre-pubertal YST: biologically distinct (no GCNIS, isochromosome 12p absent, excellent prognosis with surgery alone for stage I).
  • Post-pubertal YST: i(12p) present; treated as NSGCT with BEP.
  • AFP half-life monitoring post-treatment: must halve every 5–7 days. Plateau = persistent disease.
Related / See Also
↗ GCNIS / ITGCN (TESTIS)↗ SEMINOMA (TESTIS)↗ EMBRYONAL CARCINOMA (TESTIS)↗ Embryonal Carcinoma⚡ Clear cell carcinoma (metastatic)+ Add: Hepatoid carcinoma
LEYDIG CELL TUMOUR (TESTIS)
Urological Sex Cord-Stromal Epithelioid Intermediate 📋 2×
Most common sex cord-stromal tumour (SCST) of testis (~3% of testicular tumours). Any age; peak 30–60 years. Often presents with gynaecomastia (oestrogen) or precocious puberty (androgens in boys).
Clinical Context
  • Most common sex cord-stromal tumour (SCST) of testis (~3% of testicular tumours). Any age; peak 30–60 years. Often presents with gynaecomastia (oestrogen) or precocious puberty (androgens in boys).
  • 90% benign. Malignant criteria: size >5cm, infiltrative margins, LVI, necrosis, mitoses >3/10HPF, nuclear atypia, DNA aneuploidy.
  • Trigger: testicular mass + hormonal symptoms (gynaecomastia/precocious puberty) — Leydig cell tumour.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Diffuse or nodular growth of polygonal cells; abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm; round uniform nuclei; prominent nucleoli.
  • Reinke crystals (rod-shaped eosinophilic cytoplasmic crystalloids) — pathognomonic but present in only ~35%.
  • Lipochrome pigment; lipid vacuoles. Stroma may be fibrous or oedematous. No GCNIS at periphery.
✚ Positive
Inhibin A – diffuseCalretinin – diffuseMelan-A (MART-1) – positiveSF1 – nuclearAR – positive
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
PLAP – negativeOCT3/4 – negativeCK – negative (or focal only)CD30 – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Sertoli cell tumourTubular/cord architecture; Inhibin+, Calretinin+; no Reinke crystals; different hormonal profile
Adrenal cortical rest tumourBilateral; associated with CAH; same IHC profile; responds to steroid suppression
Metastatic renal cell carcinoma↗PAX8+, CA9+; Inhibin−; no Reinke crystals; clinical context
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Reinke crystals: pathognomonic but seen in only ~35% — IHC with Inhibin + Calretinin + Melan-A + SF1 is the diagnostic panel.
  • 10% malignant: staging by pathological criteria (size, LVI, mitoses, necrosis) — no reliable IHC marker of malignancy.
  • Bilateral in ~3%; association with Klinefelter syndrome and cryptorchidism.
  • Conservative surgery (partial orchidectomy) if contralateral testis viable — functional preservation priority.
Related / See Also
↗ GCNIS / ITGCN (TESTIS)+ Add: Sertoli cell tumour+ Add: Adrenal cortical rest tumour↗ Metastatic renal cell carcinoma
CHROMOPHOBE RCC
Urological Renal Epithelioid Malignant 📋 8×
~5% of RCC. No sex predilection; mean age 60. Sporadic or BHD-associated (FLCN germline mutation — also causes fibrofolliculomas, pneumothorax).
Clinical Context
  • ~5% of RCC. No sex predilection; mean age 60. Sporadic or BHD-associated (FLCN germline mutation — also causes fibrofolliculomas, pneumothorax).
  • Generally excellent prognosis — distant metastasis rare (~6%). Sarcomatoid change (~9%) dramatically worsens prognosis.
  • Trigger: renal mass with pale cells on CT — ChRCC if no clear cell features.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Large polygonal cells with abundant pale flocculent cytoplasm ('koilocyte-like'); prominent cell membranes ('plant cell' appearance); perinuclear cytoplasmic clearing/halo — key feature.
  • Binucleation common; irregular wrinkled ('raisinoid') nuclei. Two cell populations: large pale cells + smaller eosinophilic cells.
  • Hale's colloidal iron: diffuse reticular cytoplasmic staining — characteristic. Eosinophilic variant: oncocytic cells throughout.
✚ Positive
CK7 – diffuse (unlike ccRCC)CD117 – diffuse membranousEpCAM – positiveHale's colloidal iron – diffuse cytoplasmic reticular
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CAIX (CA9) – negative (ccRCC positive)Vimentin – negative (ccRCC positive)CD10 – negative/weakTFE3 – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
OncocytomaCK7 negative/focal; CD117+; no perinuclear halo; nests in oedematous stroma; Hale's negative/focal; FLCN not associated
Clear cell RCC↗CA9 diffuse+, Vimentin+, CD10+; CK7 negative; VHL mutation/3p deletion; no perinuclear halo
Eosinophilic solid and cystic RCC (ESC RCC)↗TSC1/2 mutation; diffuse CK20+; CK7 negative; stippled cytoplasm; cystic component
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • BHD (Birt-Hogg-Dubé) syndrome: FLCN mutation — bilateral multifocal oncocytic tumours including ChRCC and oncocytoma; fibrofolliculomas; pneumothorax. Screen all young/bilateral ChRCC.
  • Hale's colloidal iron: gold standard stain — diffuse reticular cytoplasmic pattern in ChRCC; focal/negative in oncocytoma.
  • Sarcomatoid dedifferentiation (~9%): aggressive; treat as high-grade; immunotherapy (nivolumab) emerging.
  • Molecular: chromosome losses (1, 2, 6, 10, 13, 17, 21) — multiplex loss pattern characteristic; no VHL/3p deletion.
Related / See Also
↗ PAPILLARY RCC (TYPES 1 & 2)↗ TRANSLOCATION RCC (MiT FAMILY — TFE3/TFEB)+ Add: Oncocytoma↗ Clear cell RCC↗ Eosinophilic solid and cystic RCC (ESC RCC)
PAPILLARY RCC (TYPES 1 & 2)
Urological Renal Glandular/mucinous Malignant 📋 2×
~15% of RCC. Type 1 (low grade): bilateral/multifocal, MET mutation/amplification (germline = hereditary papillary RCC). Type 2 (high grade): FH germline mutation → HLRCC syndrome; aggressive.
Clinical Context
  • ~15% of RCC. Type 1 (low grade): bilateral/multifocal, MET mutation/amplification (germline = hereditary papillary RCC). Type 2 (high grade): FH germline mutation → HLRCC syndrome; aggressive.
  • Hereditary: MET (type 1), FH/HLRCC (type 2), SDHB. Screen young patients or bilateral/multifocal pRCC for germline mutations.
  • Type 1 excellent prognosis; Type 2 poor — FH-deficient pRCC metastasises early.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Papillary/tubulopapillary architecture; fibrovascular cores with foamy macrophages and psammoma bodies — characteristic.
  • Type 1: single layer of small cells with scant pale cytoplasm, small nuclei (low grade). Type 2: pseudostratified columnar cells with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm, large nuclei with prominent nucleoli (high grade).
  • Haemosiderin deposits; necrosis common in type 2.
✚ Positive
CK7 – diffuse (type 1) / variable (type 2)AMACR (racemase) – diffuse cytoplasmic (strong positive — very useful)CD10 – positiveVimentin – positive
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CA9 – negative (ccRCC positive)CD117 – negative (ChRCC positive)TFE3 – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Clear cell RCC with papillary areas⚡CA9+, Vimentin+; VHL mutation/3p loss; no foamy macrophages in papillary cores
TFE3-rearranged RCCTFE3 nuclear+; AMACR may be positive; young patients; voluminous clear/eosinophilic cells; Cathepsin K+
Mucinous tubular and spindle cell carcinoma (MTSCC)Mucinous stroma; spindle cell areas; AMACR+; CK7+; FH retained
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • AMACR (alpha-methylacyl-CoA racemase): most reliable marker for pRCC — diffuse cytoplasmic staining; also positive in some oncocytic tumours.
  • FH-deficient pRCC (HLRCC-associated type 2): aggressive — metastasises at small size; FH IHC loss surrogate for FH mutation; 2SC IHC+.
  • Type 1 and 2 distinction: prognostic and molecular — not graded by Fuhrman/ISUP in isolation; FH status mandatory for type 2.
  • Treatment of metastatic pRCC: MET inhibitors (type 1/MET-driven); bevacizumab + erlotinib for FH-deficient. Immunotherapy emerging.
Related / See Also
↗ CHROMOPHOBE RCC↗ TRANSLOCATION RCC (MiT FAMILY — TFE3/TFEB)⚡ Clear cell RCC with papillary areas+ Add: TFE3-rearranged RCC+ Add: Mucinous tubular and spindle cell carcinoma (MTSCC)
CLEAR CELL PAPILLARY RENAL CELL CARCINOMA (CCPRCC)
Urological Renal Glandular/mucinous Intermediate 📋 1×
Now reclassified as 'Clear Cell Papillary Renal Cell Tumour' (WHO 2022) — considered low malignant potential. No metastases reported to date.
Clinical Context
  • Now reclassified as 'Clear Cell Papillary Renal Cell Tumour' (WHO 2022) — considered low malignant potential. No metastases reported to date.
  • Association with end-stage renal disease (acquired cystic kidney disease). Also sporadic.
  • Trigger: renal tumour in dialysis patient / ESRD — CCPRCC high in differential.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Papillary, tubular, and cystic architecture in combination; lining cells with clear cytoplasm; nuclei oriented away from basement membrane (apical/suprabasal nuclear orientation) — characteristic.
  • Low nuclear grade (ISUP 1–2); no necrosis; no sarcomatoid change. Fibromuscular stroma.
  • Subnuclear vacuolation (row of clear cells with nuclei polarised to apex).
✚ Positive
CK7 – diffuse strong (key positive)CA9 – cup-shaped/basolateral (not diffuse circumferential — unlike ccRCC)HMWCK (34βE12) – positive
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CD10 – negativeVimentin – negative (ccRCC positive)AMACR – negativeTFE3 – negativeVHL mutation/3p deletion – absent
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Clear cell RCCCA9 diffuse circumferential+; CD10+; Vimentin+; VHL/3p loss; CK7 negative; aggressive
Papillary RCC type 1⚡AMACR+; CK7 variable; foamy macrophages; no apical nuclear orientation; CA9 negative
OncocytomaCK7 negative/focal; CD117+; granular eosinophilic cytoplasm; no papillary architecture
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Apical nuclear orientation + CK7 diffuse + CA9 cup-shaped = essentially diagnostic combination.
  • No VHL mutation or 3p deletion — molecularly distinct from ccRCC despite morphological overlap.
  • Excellent prognosis: no documented metastasis even in large series — partial nephronectomy adequate.
  • Exam trap: CA9 is positive but shows cup-shaped/basolateral pattern, not the diffuse circumferential pattern of ccRCC.
Related / See Also
↗ CHROMOPHOBE RCC↗ PAPILLARY RCC (TYPES 1 & 2)+ Add: Clear cell RCC⚡ Papillary RCC type 1+ Add: Oncocytoma
TRANSLOCATION RCC (MiT FAMILY — TFE3/TFEB)
Urological Renal Epithelioid Malignant 📋 2×
MiT family translocation RCC. TFE3 (Xp11.2) more common; TFEB (6p21) less common. Young patients (peak childhood/young adulthood) but also adults.
Clinical Context
  • MiT family translocation RCC. TFE3 (Xp11.2) more common; TFEB (6p21) less common. Young patients (peak childhood/young adulthood) but also adults.
  • Prior cytotoxic chemotherapy in childhood is risk factor for TFE3 RCC. Indolent course in children; more aggressive in adults.
  • Trigger: young patient with RCC — always consider TFE3/TFEB translocation; IHC and FISH mandatory.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Papillary or nested architecture; voluminous clear-to-eosinophilic cytoplasm; psammoma bodies frequent (especially TFE3); biphasic appearance with smaller cells.
  • Cathepsin K positive granular cytoplasmic deposits. Hyaline nodules in TFEB RCC.
  • TFEB ('melanotic'): biphasic small and large cells; melanin pigment in some cases; nested pattern.
✚ Positive
TFE3 – diffuse nuclear (Xp11)TFEB – diffuse nuclear (6p21, distinct antibody needed)Cathepsin K – diffuseAMACR – positiveMelan-A – variable (TFEB)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CK7 – negativeVimentin – negativeCA9 – negativeEpCAM – weak/negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Papillary RCC type 2⚡TFE3 negative; AMACR+; CK7 variable; FH deficiency in aggressive cases; no Cathepsin K
Clear cell RCC↗CA9+, Vimentin+, CD10+; VHL/3p; no TFE3/TFEB nuclear staining; older patients
PEComa (AML)HMB45+, SMA+; TFE3 may be positive in subset; TSC mutation; fat/smooth muscle/vessels
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • TFE3 IHC: nuclear positivity in translocation RCC but also expressed in many normal tissues at low level — diffuse strong nuclear expression is significant. Confirm with FISH (break-apart).
  • Common TFE3 fusions: ASPSCR1-TFE3 (formerly ASPL-TFE3); PRCC-TFE3; NONO-TFE3 — each with slightly different morphology.
  • Adults with TFE3 RCC: worse prognosis than children; lymph node and bone metastases more common.
  • mTOR pathway activation: rapalogs (everolimus/temsirolimus) may have activity; immunotherapy response data emerging.
Related / See Also
↗ PAPILLARY RCC (TYPES 1 & 2)↗ CHROMOPHOBE RCC⚡ Papillary RCC type 2↗ Clear cell RCC+ Add: PEComa (AML)
ANGIOMYOLIPOMA (KIDNEY)
Urological Renal Mixed Intermediate 📋 1×
PEComa — triphasic tumour of adipose tissue, smooth muscle, and thick-walled dysmorphic vessels. Sporadic (80%, usually solitary) or TSC-associated (20%, bilateral/multifocal).
Clinical Context
  • PEComa — triphasic tumour of adipose tissue, smooth muscle, and thick-walled dysmorphic vessels. Sporadic (80%, usually solitary) or TSC-associated (20%, bilateral/multifocal).
  • Retroperitoneal haemorrhage (Wunderlich syndrome) risk with size >4cm — prophylactic embolisation/nephron-sparing surgery.
  • Trigger: fat-containing renal mass on CT — AML. TSC: hamartins screen bilateral multifocal lesions.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Variable proportions of mature adipose tissue, smooth muscle (often epithelioid), and dysmorphic thick-walled vessels lacking elastic lamina.
  • Epithelioid AML: predominantly epithelioid smooth muscle cells, minimal fat, perinuclear clearing — malignant potential; TSC2 mutation predominant.
  • Extramedullary haematopoiesis may be present. No capsule.
✚ Positive
HMB45 – positive (most specific — marks smooth muscle component)Melan-A – positiveSMA – smooth muscle componentTFE3 – variable (~50% of epithelioid AML)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
S100 – negativeDesmin – variableCK – negative (distinguishes from RCC)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Retroperitoneal liposarcoma↗MDM2+, CDK4+; MDM2 amplification; no HMB45; no vascular component; no TSC association
Epithelioid AML vs RCCHMB45+, CK−; CA9−; epithelioid AML: perinuclear clearing, TFE3 may be positive
Leiomyosarcoma↗Marked atypia; h-caldesmon+; HMB45−; Melan-A−
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Epithelioid AML: malignant variant — multifocal/bilateral, TSC2 mutation, retroperitoneal spread, metastasis possible. HMB45+ but morphologically alarming.
  • TSC association: TSC1 (hamartin) or TSC2 (tuberin) mutation. TSC2 more severe. mTOR pathway activated — everolimus shrinks AML in TSC.
  • Fat-poor AML: no macroscopic fat on CT; may mimic RCC. IHC (HMB45) diagnostic.
  • Pregnancy increases haemorrhage risk — prophylactic embolisation if >4cm in women of childbearing age.
Related / See Also
↗ TRANSLOCATION RCC (MiT FAMILY — TFE3/TFEB)↗ Retroperitoneal liposarcoma+ Add: Epithelioid AML vs RCC↗ Leiomyosarcoma
XANTHOGRANULOMATOUS PYELONEPHRITIS (XGP)
Urological Renal Granulomatous Variable 📋 3×
Chronic destructive renal inflammation — xanthomatous macrophages replacing renal parenchyma. F>M (3:1); obstructing calculus (staghorn calculus) in ~75%.
Clinical Context
  • Chronic destructive renal inflammation — xanthomatous macrophages replacing renal parenchyma. F>M (3:1); obstructing calculus (staghorn calculus) in ~75%.
  • Presents with flank pain, fever, weight loss, palpable mass — mimics renal malignancy on imaging.
  • Organisms: E. coli, Proteus mirabilis most common. May extend into perinephric fat and form fistulae.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Zonal architecture: inner zone — necrosis + bacterial colonies + neutrophils; middle zone — foamy lipid-laden macrophages (xanthoma cells) + Touton giant cells + cholesterol clefts; outer zone — chronic inflammation + fibrous tissue.
  • CD68+ foamy macrophages — defining cell. Necrosis prominent. Granulation tissue at advancing edge.
  • Renal architecture obliterated. No granuloma formation (epithelioid granulomas suggest TB/fungal).
✚ Positive
CD68 – xanthoma cells diffuse positive
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CK – negative in xanthoma cells (distinguishes from clear cell RCC)PAX8 – negative in xanthoma cells
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Clear cell RCC↗CK+, PAX8+, CA9+; CD68−; no zonal pattern; no calculus association; no inflammatory history
Malakoplakia↗Michaelis-Guttmann bodies (PAS+, von Kossa+); Gram-negative organism; CD68+ histiocytes; no Touton giant cells/cholesterol clefts
Renal TBEpithelioid granulomas with central caseation; ZN+/culture; no xanthoma cells; systemic TB features
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Staghorn calculus is the classic radiological/macroscopic association — XGP essentially always associated with obstruction.
  • Clinically and radiologically mimics RCC — nephrectomy is curative and diagnostic.
  • Diffuse (most common) vs focal (segmental) forms — focal may be amenable to partial nephrectomy.
  • Perinephric extension can form psoas abscess or enteric/cutaneous fistulae — check staging CT for extent.
Related / See Also
↗ Clear cell RCC↗ Malakoplakia+ Add: Renal TB
MALAKOPLAKIA
Urological Inflammatory Granulomatous Variable 📋 3×
Rare chronic inflammatory condition reflecting defective macrophage lysosomal function — inability to fully degrade phagocytosed bacteria (most commonly E. coli).
Clinical Context
  • Rare chronic inflammatory condition reflecting defective macrophage lysosomal function — inability to fully degrade phagocytosed bacteria (most commonly E. coli).
  • Bladder most common site (also kidney, colon, prostate). Middle-aged women; immunosuppression (transplant, steroid use) predisposes.
  • Soft yellow/brown plaques on mucosal surface. Clinically mimics carcinoma.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Dense infiltrate of large histiocytes (von Hansemann cells) with abundant granular eosinophilic cytoplasm containing Michaelis-Guttmann bodies.
  • Michaelis-Guttmann bodies: concentric laminated calcospherites (targetoid basophilic inclusions) within or outside histiocytes — pathognomonic.
  • PAS+, von Kossa+, Perls+ (iron), Alizarin red+ — Michaelis-Guttmann bodies. Admixed lymphocytes and plasma cells.
✚ Positive
CD68 – von Hansemann cells positivePAS – Michaelis-Guttmann bodiesVon Kossa – calcification in MG bodies
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CK – negative in histiocytesPAX8 – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis⚡Foamy xanthoma cells + Touton giants + cholesterol clefts; NO Michaelis-Guttmann bodies; calculus/obstruction
Granular cell tumourS100+, SOX10+; PAS+ granules but no Michaelis-Guttmann bodies; no inflammatory context
Signet ring cell carcinomaCK+, mucin+; no CD68; no MG bodies; malignant cytology
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Michaelis-Guttmann bodies are pathognomonic — must recognise on section and on PAS/von Kossa stains.
  • Pathogenesis: defective cGMP-mediated macrophage lysosomal function → incomplete bacterial digestion → calcium phosphate deposits around undigested bacteria.
  • Treatment: bethanechol (cholinergic — enhances cGMP) + long-term antibiotics (fluoroquinolones). Surgery for isolated lesions.
  • Important to distinguish from carcinoma clinically and macroscopically — biopsy is diagnostic.
Related / See Also
↗ XANTHOGRANULOMATOUS PYELONEPHRITIS (XGP)⚡ Xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis+ Add: Granular cell tumour+ Add: Signet ring cell carcinoma
NEPHROGENIC ADENOMA / METAPLASIA
Urological Epithelial Glandular/mucinous Benign 📋 7×
Benign urothelial metaplasia — tubular/papillary proliferation resembling renal tubules. Bladder most common; also urethra, ureter.
Clinical Context
  • Benign urothelial metaplasia — tubular/papillary proliferation resembling renal tubules. Bladder most common; also urethra, ureter.
  • Associated with trauma, instrumentation, calculi, radiation, renal transplant (shed renal tubular cells implant — explains PAX8 positivity).
  • Trigger: tubular/papillary lesion in bladder biopsy in context of instrumentation or transplant — nephrogenic adenoma.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Tubules and cysts lined by cuboidal-to-hobnail cells; intraluminal mucin; fibromyxoid stroma. Flat, papillary, tubular and cystic patterns — often mixed.
  • Hobnail cells with apical nuclei protruding into lumen — characteristic. No atypia; no mitoses.
  • Clear cell variant: can mimic clear cell adenocarcinoma — PAX8 and AMACR help.
✚ Positive
PAX8 – nuclear (key — renal tubular phenotype)AMACR – positiveCK7 – positive
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
p53 – wild-typeKi67 – lowp504s – negative in most (AMACR positive — see above — used to distinguish from clear cell adeno in some protocols)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Clear cell adenocarcinoma of bladder↗Marked atypia; high Ki67; p53 mutant; AMACR+; tubulocystic/papillary; CK7+; PAX8 negative or weak — overlap significant; clinical correlation essential
Urothelial carcinoma with glandular differentiation↗Urothelial origin; CK20+, GATA3+; no hobnail cells; PAX8 negative
Cystitis glandularisVon Brunn nests with glandular metaplasia; intestinal type (goblet cells); CDX2+; no hobnail morphology; PAX8 negative
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • PAX8 positivity supports renal transplant-origin theory — shed donor tubular cells implant in urothelium.
  • Flat nephrogenic adenoma: subtle — single layer of cuboidal cells; easily overlooked or misinterpreted as reactive.
  • Benign: no treatment needed. Recurrence common if precipitating cause persists.
  • Exam trap: AMACR positive does NOT indicate malignancy here — nephrogenic adenoma is a recognised AMACR-positive benign mimic.
Related / See Also
↗ Clear cell adenocarcinoma of bladder↗ Urothelial carcinoma with glandular differentiation+ Add: Cystitis glandularis
ADENOMATOID TUMOUR (EPIDIDYMIS / GENITAL TRACT)
Urological Mesothelial Glandular/mucinous Benign 📋 1×
Most common paratesticular tumour. Mesothelial origin. Epididymis (head/tail) in men; uterine myometrium, fallopian tube in women.
Clinical Context
  • Most common paratesticular tumour. Mesothelial origin. Epididymis (head/tail) in men; uterine myometrium, fallopian tube in women.
  • Incidental finding or small painless paratesticular nodule. No malignant potential.
  • Trigger: small well-circumscribed paratesticular mass — adenomatoid tumour (most common paratesticular benign lesion).
Key Microscopic Features
  • Anastomosing tubules, cords, and gland-like spaces lined by flat-to-cuboidal cells with bland nuclei and vacuolated cytoplasm. Fibromuscular stroma.
  • Signet-ring-like cells (cytoplasmic vacuolation displacing nucleus) — do NOT misinterpret as signet ring carcinoma.
  • No atypia, no mitoses, no necrosis.
✚ Positive
Calretinin – diffuse (mesothelial marker)CK5/6 – positiveD2-40 – positiveWT1 – nuclear positiveCK7 – positive
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CEA – negativeCD15 – negativeMOC31 – negative (excludes adenocarcinoma)CK20 – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Metastatic signet ring adenocarcinoma↗CK7+, CK20 variable, CEA+, MOC31+; Calretinin−; marked atypia; clinical history
Mesothelioma (malignant)↗Same IHC profile — distinguish by morphology, invasion, atypia; clinical context; paratesticular mesothelioma rare
Yolk sac tumour (Sertoli-Leydig cell tumour)⚡AFP+, SALL4+; OCT3/4+; Calretinin−; young patient; GCT markers positive
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Mesothelial origin proven by EM and IHC — calretinin, CK5/6, D2-40, WT1 all positive.
  • Signet-ring morphology is a trap — vacuolated cells lack atypia and CEA/mucin markers are negative.
  • Conservative excision curative — no recurrence. Diagnosis usually made at time of surgery for other indication.
  • Can rarely arise in uterine serosa, ovary, adrenal, lymph node, omentum — same IHC profile.
Related / See Also
↗ Metastatic signet ring adenocarcinoma↗ Mesothelioma (malignant)⚡ Yolk sac tumour (Sertoli-Leydig cell tumour)
PARAGANGLIOMA (BLADDER)
Urological Neuroendocrine Well-differentiated Intermediate 📋 2×
Rare (<0.1% of bladder tumours) — extra-adrenal paraganglioma arising from bladder wall (parasympathetic). Classic triad: haematuria + hypertensive crisis during micturition + headache/palpitations.
Clinical Context
  • Rare (<0.1% of bladder tumours) — extra-adrenal paraganglioma arising from bladder wall (parasympathetic). Classic triad: haematuria + hypertensive crisis during micturition + headache/palpitations.
  • Submucosal location; urothelium intact. ~10–15% malignant (metastasis only reliable criterion).
  • Trigger: hypertensive crisis on voiding + haematuria — pathognomonic presentation for bladder paraganglioma.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Zellballen (cell balls) — nests of chief cells surrounded by sustentacular cells in delicate fibrovascular stroma.
  • Chief cells: abundant granular amphophilic/eosinophilic cytoplasm; round nuclei; 'salt-and-pepper' chromatin. Sustentacular cells: flattened cells at periphery of nests.
  • No reliable histological criteria for malignancy — only metastasis confirms. Vascular invasion common even in benign tumours.
✚ Positive
Synaptophysin – chief cells positiveChromogranin A – chief cells positiveS100 – sustentacular cells (peripheral nest cells)GATA3 – positive (~50%)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CK – negative (key exclusion of carcinoma)GATA3 – variable (urothelial CA GATA3+, paraganglioma variable)INSM1 – positive (NE marker)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Urothelial carcinoma with neuroendocrine differentiation↗CK+, GATA3+; no Zellballen; no sustentacular cells (S100−); urothelial morphology
Well-differentiated NET (metastatic)⚡S100 sustentacular cells absent in NET; clinical/radiological context; site-specific markers (TTF1, CDX2); no Zellballen
Alveolar soft part sarcomaTFE3+; PAS+ crystals; no NE markers; Zellballen pattern but different IHC
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Pre-operative alpha-blockade (phenoxybenzamine) mandatory before any biopsy or surgery — hypertensive crisis risk.
  • Germline testing: SDH (B/C/D/A), VHL, RET, NF1, TMEM127, MAX — ~30–40% of paragangliomas have germline mutation.
  • SDHB IHC loss: surrogate for SDHB/C/D/A mutations — diffuse cytoplasmic granular loss. High metastatic potential.
  • 123I-MIBG scan/68Ga-DOTATATE PET: staging and detection of multifocal/metastatic disease.
Related / See Also
↗ Urothelial carcinoma with neuroendocrine differentiation⚡ Well-differentiated NET (metastatic)+ Add: Alveolar soft part sarcoma
PLASMACYTOID UROTHELIAL CARCINOMA
Urological Epithelial Epithelioid Malignant 📋 1×
Rare aggressive variant of urothelial carcinoma (~1%). Discohesive single cells mimic plasma cells or signet ring cells — diagnosis frequently delayed.
Clinical Context
  • Rare aggressive variant of urothelial carcinoma (~1%). Discohesive single cells mimic plasma cells or signet ring cells — diagnosis frequently delayed.
  • Diffuse infiltration of bladder wall; peritoneal spread common (mimics peritoneal carcinomatosis). Advanced stage at presentation typical.
  • CDH1 (E-cadherin) loss — somatic in most; germline CDH1 mutation reported — consider HDGC screen in young patients.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Discohesive single cells with eccentric nuclei and abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm resembling plasma cells; intracytoplasmic mucin in some ('pseudo-signet ring').
  • Infiltrative growth through muscularis propria; no cohesive nests; no conventional urothelial architecture.
  • Often associated with conventional high-grade UCC elsewhere in bladder — check all areas of resection.
✚ Positive
CK7 – positiveGATA3 – positive (key — distinguishes from plasma cell dyscrasia and adenocarcinoma)p63 – variableUroplakin II – variable
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
E-cadherin – lost/reduced (CDH1 mutation/loss)CK20 – variableCD138 – negative (plasma cells CD138+)Synaptophysin – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Plasmacytoma/multiple myelomaCD138+, CD38+, monoclonal Ig light chain; GATA3−; CK−; serum/urine protein electrophoresis
Lobular carcinoma of breast (metastatic)↗ER/PR+, GATA3+, mammaglobulin+; CK7+; clinical history; E-cadherin also lost — site context critical
Signet ring cell adenocarcinoma (gastric/colorectal metastasis)↗CDX2+/CK20+ (colorectal); SATB2+; HER2 (gastric); GATA3−
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • E-cadherin loss distinguishes from most UCC variants — CDH1 somatic mutation (~90%) or promoter methylation.
  • Prognosis extremely poor: most patients have pT3b/pT4 or M1 at diagnosis. Radical cystectomy rarely curative.
  • Peritoneal dissemination common — systemic chemotherapy (gemcitabine/cisplatin or MVAC) with or without cystectomy.
  • Exam trap: discohesive single cell infiltrate in bladder → do not diagnose as plasmacytoma without GATA3/CK panel.
Related / See Also
+ Add: Plasmacytoma/multiple myeloma↗ Lobular carcinoma of breast (metastatic)↗ Signet ring cell adenocarcinoma (gastric/colorectal metastasis)
OVARIAN FIBROMA / FIBROTHECOMA
Gynaecological Sex Cord-Stromal Fibrous/collagenous Benign 📋 7×
Most common benign solid ovarian tumour. Any age; peak perimenopausal. Unilateral in 90%. Meigs syndrome: fibroma + ascites + pleural effusion (resolves on resection).
Clinical Context
  • Most common benign solid ovarian tumour. Any age; peak perimenopausal. Unilateral in 90%. Meigs syndrome: fibroma + ascites + pleural effusion (resolves on resection).
  • Gorlin syndrome (PTCH1 germline): bilateral, calcified, multinodular fibromas — in young women, always consider.
  • Fibrothecoma: overlapping entity with thecoma component; may be weakly oestrogenic.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Intersecting fascicles of bland spindle cells with scant pale cytoplasm and oval nuclei embedded in collagenous stroma — 'herringbone' or storiform pattern.
  • Oedematous variant: stromal oedema separating cell bundles — can mimic other entities on frozen section.
  • Thecoma component: lipid-rich rounded/polygonal cells with pale cytoplasm (oil-red O positive) intermixed with fibrous areas.
✚ Positive
Inhibin – variable (thecoma > fibroma)Calretinin – positiveSF1 – nuclearCD56 – positiveWT1 – variable
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
EMA – negativeCK – negative (excludes Brenner, Krukenberg)CD117 – negativeSTAT6 – negative (excludes SFT)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Solitary Fibrous Tumour (ovary)↗STAT6 nuclear+; CD34+; NAB2-STAT6 fusion; hemangiopericytoma vasculature; Inhibin/Calretinin negative
Cellular fibroma / low-grade fibrosarcoma↗Mitoses ≥4/10HPF (cellular fibroma); ≥4/10HPF + atypia (fibrosarcoma); same IHC; recurrence risk increases with mitotic count
Sclerosing stromal tumour↗Young women; pseudolobular architecture; dilated 'staghorn' vessels; Inhibin+; oestrogenic symptoms
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Frozen section: classic FRCPath case — benign solid ovarian mass. Key message: oophorectomy curative; no staging required.
  • Cellular fibroma (mitoses 1–3/10HPF): low recurrence risk; complete excision adequate.
  • Mitoses ≥4/10HPF with atypia = fibrosarcoma — rare, aggressive, requires staging.
