  • Meigs syndrome resolves completely after tumour removal — do not over-investigate ascites/effusion pre-operatively.
Related / See Also
↗ Solitary Fibrous Tumour (ovary)↗ Cellular fibroma / low-grade fibrosarcoma↗ Sclerosing stromal tumour
ENDOMETRIAL CARCINOMA (ENDOMETRIOID TYPE)
Gynaecological Epithelial Glandular/mucinous Malignant 📋 7×
Most common gynaecological malignancy in developed world. Postmenopausal bleeding in ~75%. Risk factors: unopposed oestrogen, obesity, PCOS, tamoxifen, Lynch syndrome (MLH1/MSH2/MSH6/PMS2).
Clinical Context
  • Most common gynaecological malignancy in developed world. Postmenopausal bleeding in ~75%. Risk factors: unopposed oestrogen, obesity, PCOS, tamoxifen, Lynch syndrome (MLH1/MSH2/MSH6/PMS2).
  • Two pathways: Type I (endometrioid, low-grade, oestrogen-driven, EIN/complex atypical hyperplasia precursor) vs Type II (serous/clear cell, high-grade, p53-mutant, atrophic endometrium).
  • Trigger: postmenopausal bleeding → pipelle/hysteroscopy. Staging: FIGO 2023 (surgical-pathological). Lynch: MSI/MMR testing mandatory on all endometrial carcinomas.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Grade 1: well-formed confluent glands, ≤5% solid non-squamous growth; Grade 2: 6–50% solid; Grade 3: >50% solid. Squamous differentiation common (morules — benign squamous metaplasia within glands).
  • EIN (endometrial intraepithelial neoplasia): crowded glands >50% volume ratio, cytological abnormality, >1mm — precursor lesion.
  • Villoglandular pattern: papillary fronds with fibrovascular cores; Grade 1 behaviour despite architecture — do not over-grade.
✚ Positive
PAX8 – diffuseCK7 – diffuseER/PR – positive (Grade 1/2)Vimentin – positiveβ-catenin – nuclear (CTNNB1 mutation, ~25%)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
p16 – negative/patchy (diffuse block p16 = serous carcinoma)p53 – wild-type (Grade 1/2; aberrant in Grade 3/serous)WT1 – negative (positive in serous)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Endometrial Serous Carcinomap53 aberrant (diffuse/null); p16 diffuse block+; WT1+; ER/PR−; marked nuclear atypia; psammoma bodies; atrophic background
Endometrioid carcinoma of ovary (metastatic)⚡PAX8+; clinical context; bilateral ovarian involvement; surface implants; CK7+/CK20−
Endometrial clear cell carcinoma↗HNF1β+; napsin A+; tubulocystic/papillary/solid architecture; hobnail cells; ER/PR−
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • TCGA/ProMisE molecular classification (mandatory WHO 2020): POLE-ultramutated (best prognosis), MMR-deficient (Lynch/sporadic MLH1 methylation), p53-mutant (worst), NSMP (no specific molecular profile).
  • MMR IHC (MLH1/PMS2/MSH2/MSH6) on all cases: loss → MSI-high → reflex MLH1 methylation (sporadic) vs germline Lynch testing.
  • FIGO 2023: Stage IA now split by grade/LVSI; Stage IC = deep myometrial invasion + aggressive histology. Staging integrates molecular subtype.
  • POLE mutation: ultramutated, paradoxically excellent prognosis despite high-grade morphology — do not over-treat.
  • Exam trap: morules in endometrioid carcinoma are benign squamous metaplasia — do not grade as solid component.
Related / See Also
↗ HIGH GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA (OVARY / PERITONEUM / FALLOPIAN TUBE)+ Add: Endometrial Serous Carcinoma⚡ Endometrioid carcinoma of ovary (metastatic)↗ Endometrial clear cell carcinoma
HIGH GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA (OVARY / PERITONEUM / FALLOPIAN TUBE)
Gynaecological Epithelial Glandular/mucinous Malignant 📋 5×
Most common and lethal ovarian malignancy. Origin: fimbrial end of fallopian tube (STIC — serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma) → peritoneal dissemination. 70% FIGO III/IV at diagnosis.
Clinical Context
  • Most common and lethal ovarian malignancy. Origin: fimbrial end of fallopian tube (STIC — serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma) → peritoneal dissemination. 70% FIGO III/IV at diagnosis.
  • BRCA1/2 germline in ~15–20%; somatic BRCA alteration additional ~10%; HRD (homologous recombination deficiency) overall ~50%. All HGSC require BRCA/HRD testing for PARP inhibitor eligibility.
  • Trigger: bilateral ovarian/peritoneal masses + elevated CA125 in peri/postmenopausal woman.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Papillary, slit-like glandular, and solid architecture; marked nuclear pleomorphism and atypia; prominent nucleoli; high mitotic rate; psammoma bodies (concentric calcifications) — characteristic.
  • STIC in fallopian tube: p53 signature → STIC (TP53 mutation, loss of p63, high Ki67, loss of normal ciliated cells). Always sample fimbria in SEE-FIM protocol.
  • Transitional cell-like/SET patterns: less papillary; can mimic urothelial carcinoma — WT1/PAX8 confirm Müllerian.
✚ Positive
WT1 – diffuse nuclear (most specific ovarian serous marker)PAX8 – diffusep53 – aberrant (diffuse strong OR complete null — mutant pattern)p16 – diffuse block positiveCK7 – diffuse
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CK20 – negativeCDX2 – negativeER – negative/weak (unlike endometrioid)Calretinin – negative (unlike mesothelioma)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Endometrial serous carcinoma (metastatic)⚡Morphologically identical — WT1 negative in endometrial serous (key distinction); PAX8+; clinical/imaging context
Malignant mesothelioma (peritoneal)⚡Calretinin+, CK5/6+; WT1+ (shared); MOC31−, BerEP4−, PAX8−; BAP1 loss; CDKN2A deletion
Low grade serous carcinoma (LGSC)↗KRAS/BRAF/NRAS/ERBB2 mutation; p53 wild-type; WT1+; indolent; micropapillary; psammoma bodies; MEK inhibitor target
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • SEE-FIM protocol (Sectioning and Extensively Examining the FIMbria): mandatory for all salpingo-oophorectomy specimens — STIC identification changes staging.
  • BRCA1/2 and HRD testing: guides PARP inhibitor (olaparib/niraparib/rucaparib) maintenance therapy. BRCA-mutant HGSC: best response.
  • Bevacizumab (anti-VEGF) + platinum/paclitaxel: standard first-line for advanced HGSC.
  • p53 aberrant IHC pattern: diffuse strong (missense mutation) OR complete null (nonsense/frameshift). Wild-type pattern (variable, moderate) = NOT HGSC.
  • Exam trap: WT1 is positive in both HGSC and mesothelioma — always run full panel. WT1 negative in endometrial serous carcinoma.
🧫 Cytology Counterpart
↗ HIGH GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA — EFFUSION CYTOLOGY (IAC Harrogate)
Related / See Also
↗ ENDOMETRIAL CARCINOMA (ENDOMETRIOID TYPE)↗ HIGH GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA — EFFUSION CYTOLOGY (IAC Harrogate)⚡ Endometrial serous carcinoma (metastatic)⚡ Malignant mesothelioma (peritoneal)↗ Low grade serous carcinoma (LGSC)
CERVICAL CARCINOMA (SCC AND ADENOCARCINOMA)
Gynaecological Epithelial Epithelioid Malignant 📋 3×
HPV-driven (99%): high-risk HPV16/18/31/33/45. SCC ~70%; adenocarcinoma ~25%; adenosquamous/others ~5%. Adenocarcinoma increasing (less amenable to cytological screening).
Clinical Context
  • HPV-driven (99%): high-risk HPV16/18/31/33/45. SCC ~70%; adenocarcinoma ~25%; adenosquamous/others ~5%. Adenocarcinoma increasing (less amenable to cytological screening).
  • UK cervical screening: LBC (liquid-based cytology) + HPV primary testing. Colposcopy if HPV+ with cytological abnormality.
  • Trigger: abnormal screening/post-coital bleeding → colposcopy → biopsy → LLETZ. FIGO 2018 staging now includes imaging.
Key Microscopic Features
  • SCC: nests and sheets of squamous cells with intercellular bridges; keratin pearls (keratinising SCC) or non-keratinising; CIN3 at surface margin common.
  • Endocervical adenocarcinoma: glands with pseudostratification, mitotic figures, apoptoses, and luminal debris ('dirty necrosis' in gastric-type). Usual-type (HPV-driven) vs gastric-type (HPV-independent, SPEM origin, worse prognosis).
  • HSIL/CIN3 at transformation zone — precursor. CIN grading: CIN1 (LSIL) → CIN2 (HSIL) → CIN3 (HSIL) → invasion.
✚ Positive
p16 – diffuse block (HPV-driven SCC and usual-type adeno)CK5/6, p40, p63 – SCCCK7 – adenocarcinomaHPV ISH – confirmatory
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
Gastric-type adeno: p16 negative/patchy; HER2 amplified in ~30%; MUC6+PAX8 – negative (distinguishes from endometrial origin)WT1 – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Endocervical vs endometrial adenocarcinoma↗Endocervical: p16 diffuse+, PAX8−/weak, CK7+, ER−; Endometrial: p16 patchy, PAX8+, ER+, vimentin+
Gastric-type adenocarcinoma (GAS)p16 negative; HER2 amplification; MUC6+; deeper invasion; minimal deviation (adenoma malignum) variant; HPV-independent
Metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma⚡CDX2+, CK20+, SATB2+; p16 negative; CK7−; clinical history
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Gastric-type adenocarcinoma (GAS): HPV-independent; p16 negative; associated with Peutz-Jeghers (STK11); worst prognosis; minimal deviation variant (adenoma malignum) — deceptively bland glands infiltrating deeply.
  • FIGO 2018 staging: Stage IA1 = ≤3mm depth, no LVSI (cone biopsy sufficient); IA2 = 3–5mm; IB1 = ≥5mm and <2cm. LVSI does not upstage but must be reported.
  • Radical hysterectomy (Wertheim): Stages IA2–IB2. Concurrent chemoradiotherapy: ≥IB3/IIA2 or node-positive.
  • HPV vaccination (Gardasil 9): targets HPV 16/18/31/33/45/52/58 — virtually eliminates SCC and most adenocarcinoma.
  • p16 diffuse block positivity = surrogate for transcriptionally active high-risk HPV — not a cancer marker per se (CIN2/3 also p16+).
Related / See Also
↗ ENDOMETRIAL CARCINOMA (ENDOMETRIOID TYPE)↗ Endocervical vs endometrial adenocarcinoma+ Add: Gastric-type adenocarcinoma (GAS)⚡ Metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma
BORDERLINE MUCINOUS OVARIAN TUMOUR (MBOT)
Gynaecological Epithelial Glandular/mucinous Intermediate 📋 4×
~10% of borderline ovarian tumours. Unilateral in >95%; large (mean 15–20cm); multilocular cystic. Intestinal-type most common; endocervical-type (seromucinous) distinct entity.
Clinical Context
  • ~10% of borderline ovarian tumours. Unilateral in >95%; large (mean 15–20cm); multilocular cystic. Intestinal-type most common; endocervical-type (seromucinous) distinct entity.
  • Excellent prognosis if confined to ovary — 5-year OS >95%. Bilateral tumours should raise suspicion for metastatic mucinous adenocarcinoma (colorectal, gastric, pancreatic, appendiceal).
  • Trigger: large unilateral multilocular cystic ovarian mass on frozen section — if mucinous, assess for pseudomyxoma/peritoneal deposits.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Gastrointestinal-type epithelium: columnar cells with goblet cells in stratified/tufted epithelium; nuclear stratification ≤3 layers; no destructive stromal invasion.
  • Intraepithelial carcinoma foci: areas of severe atypia within MBOT (does not upstage if no invasion). Microinvasion (<5mm): does not worsen prognosis.
  • Mural nodules: sarcoma-like vs carcinomatous — sarcoma-like (anaplastic giant cells, osteoclast-like): benign behaviour. Carcinomatous: malignant — upstages.
✚ Positive
CK7 – positiveCK20 – positive (intestinal-type)CDX2 – variableMUC5AC – positive
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
WT1 – negative (unlike serous)p53 – wild-typePAX8 – negative or weak
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Metastatic mucinous adenocarcinoma (colorectal/appendiceal)⚡Bilateral; smaller ovaries; surface involvement; SATB2+, CDX2+; CK7−/CK20+ (colorectal); infiltrative rather than expansile invasion; clinical history
Mucinous cystadenomaNo epithelial stratification or atypia; single layer of bland mucinous cells; no tufting
Mucinous carcinoma (primary ovarian)Confluent glandular/expansile invasion >5mm; marked atypia; KRAS mutation; same IHC
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Frozen section challenge: must sample extensively (1 section per cm); distinguish from metastatic mucinous carcinoma (critical intraoperative decision).
  • KRAS mutation: ~75% of MBOT and mucinous carcinoma — not diagnostically useful but confirms mucinous lineage.
  • Pseudomyxoma peritonei (PMP) from MBOT is extremely rare — if PMP present, recheck for appendiceal primary (LAMN).
  • Seromucinous MBOT: now classified separately as borderline seromucinous tumour — mixed Müllerian epithelium; ARID1A mutation; endometriosis-associated; p16 patchy; PAX8+.
  • Exam trap: bilateral mucinous ovarian tumours = metastasis until proven otherwise. SATB2 is the best marker for colorectal/appendiceal primary.
Related / See Also
↗ LOW-GRADE APPENDICEAL MUCINOUS NEOPLASM (LAMN)↗ METASTATIC MUCINOUS / SIGNET RING CELL ADENOCARCINOMA⚡ Metastatic mucinous adenocarcinoma (colorectal/appendiceal)+ Add: Mucinous cystadenoma+ Add: Mucinous carcinoma (primary ovarian)
MATURE CYSTIC TERATOMA (DERMOID CYST)
Gynaecological Germ Cell Mixed Benign 📋 4×
Most common ovarian tumour in women <45 years. Contains mature tissues from all three germ layers (ecto/meso/endoderm). Bilateral in 10–15%. Usually asymptomatic; risk of torsion.
Clinical Context
  • Most common ovarian tumour in women <45 years. Contains mature tissues from all three germ layers (ecto/meso/endoderm). Bilateral in 10–15%. Usually asymptomatic; risk of torsion.
  • Malignant transformation in <2% (usually SCC from squamous epithelium) — suspect if solid component/rapid growth/postmenopausal.
  • Trigger: cystic ovarian mass with fat/calcification on CT (teeth/hair on imaging) — pathognomonic for dermoid.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Cyst lined by stratified squamous epithelium with adnexal structures (hair follicles, sebaceous glands, sweat glands). Rokitansky nodule (dermoid plug): solid mural nodule containing teeth, cartilage, bone, neural tissue.
  • Any mature tissue may be present: thyroid (struma ovarii — can cause hyperthyroidism), respiratory epithelium, bowel mucosa, brain, muscle.
  • Immature teratoma: neuroepithelial (neural rosettes/tubules) — grade by amount of immature neural tissue (Grade 1: <1 LPF/slide; Grade 2: 1–3 LPF/slide; Grade 3: >3 LPF/slide).
✚ Positive
Various — depends on tissue type presentTTF1 – struma ovarii (thyroid tissue)Synaptophysin/GFAP – neuroepithelial component
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
OCT3/4 – negative (positive in GCNIS/seminoma)SALL4 – negativeAFP – negative (pure mature; positive in immature/YST)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Immature teratomaImmature neuroepithelial tissue (Homer-Wright rosettes, neural tubules); AFP elevated; grade by immature neural tissue; malignant — requires staging
Struma ovariiDominant thyroid tissue (>50% of teratoma); TTF1+; thyroglobulin+; may cause hyperthyroidism; malignant struma ovarii rare
Monodermal teratoma (carcinoid)Insular/trabecular NE architecture; Synaptophysin+, CgA+; may cause carcinoid syndrome without mets (direct ovarian venous drainage)
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Macroscopy: unilocular cyst filled with sebaceous material and hair; Rokitansky nodule (solid mural nodule) — slice carefully and sample.
  • Grading immature teratoma: critical — grade determines adjuvant chemotherapy (BEP) requirement. Grade 3 → chemotherapy.
  • Malignant transformation: SCC most common (~80% of malignant transformation); treated as SCC at that site.
  • Peritoneal gliomatosis: peritoneal implants of mature glial tissue — benign; no chemotherapy; distinguish from immature implants (immature neural tissue → grade → treat).
  • Struma ovarii: treat as thyroid carcinoma at that site if malignant transformation identified — thyroidectomy + RAI.
Related / See Also
↗ YOLK SAC TUMOUR (TESTIS)+ Add: Immature teratoma+ Add: Struma ovarii+ Add: Monodermal teratoma (carcinoid)
ENDOMETRIOID CARCINOMA (OVARY)
Gynaecological Epithelial Glandular/mucinous Malignant 📋 3×
~10% of epithelial ovarian carcinomas. Strongly associated with endometriosis (~40%) and synchronous endometrial carcinoma (~15–20%). Usually FIGO Stage I/II at diagnosis — better prognosis than HGSC.
Clinical Context
  • ~10% of epithelial ovarian carcinomas. Strongly associated with endometriosis (~40%) and synchronous endometrial carcinoma (~15–20%). Usually FIGO Stage I/II at diagnosis — better prognosis than HGSC.
  • ARID1A, PIK3CA, PTEN, CTNNB1, MMR mutations — molecular overlap with endometrial carcinoma. Lynch syndrome association.
  • Trigger: ovarian mass + endometriosis + younger age → endometrioid carcinoma. Always sample uterus if synchronous uterine disease suspected.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Confluent/cribriform glands lined by columnar cells resembling endometrioid endometrial carcinoma; squamous morules common; villoglandular pattern.
  • Arises from endometriosis: atypical endometriosis → endometrioid carcinoma transition (look for haemosiderin-laden macrophages and glandular crowding at margin).
  • FIGO grading (1–3) same as endometrial carcinoma: based on solid non-squamous component percentage.
✚ Positive
CK7 – positivePAX8 – positiveER/PR – positiveVimentin – positiveβ-catenin – nuclear (~50%, CTNNB1 mutation)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
WT1 – negative (key — HGSC WT1 positive)p53 – wild-type (Grade 1/2)CK20 – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
High grade serous carcinoma⚡WT1+, p53 aberrant, p16 diffuse block+; ER−; psammoma bodies; no squamous morules; no endometriosis background
Synchronous endometrial primary vs ovarian primary↗Molecular clonality testing if discordant morphology; WT1−, PAX8+, ER+ in both — site-context, FIGO staging criteria (independent primaries vs metastasis)
Metastatic endometrial endometrioid carcinoma⚡Bilateral ovarian; surface involvement; smaller ovaries; CK7+, PAX8+, ER+ — morphologically identical; staging and imaging critical
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Synchronous endometrial and ovarian endometrioid carcinoma (~15%): usually two independent primaries (different CTNNB1 mutations) — stage each independently; generally good prognosis.
  • ARID1A loss (IHC): associated with endometriosis-related carcinomas (endometrioid and clear cell); marker of endometriosis-driven carcinogenesis.
  • MMR testing: Lynch syndrome screen — same protocol as endometrial carcinoma; germline testing if MMR IHC loss.
  • Exam trap: WT1 negative in ovarian endometrioid carcinoma is the single most useful discriminator from HGSC.
Related / See Also
↗ HIGH GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA (OVARY / PERITONEUM / FALLOPIAN TUBE)↗ ENDOMETRIAL CARCINOMA (ENDOMETRIOID TYPE)⚡ High grade serous carcinoma↗ Synchronous endometrial primary vs ovarian primary⚡ Metastatic endometrial endometrioid carcinoma
DIFFUSE LARGE B-CELL LYMPHOMA (DLBCL, NOS)
Haempath Lymphoma Round cell Malignant 📋 3×
Most common aggressive lymphoma (~30% of NHL). Any age; peak 60–70s. Rapidly enlarging nodal or extranodal mass — testis (2×), CNS, GI tract, mediastinum common extranodal sites.
Clinical Context
  • Most common aggressive lymphoma (~30% of NHL). Any age; peak 60–70s. Rapidly enlarging nodal or extranodal mass — testis (2×), CNS, GI tract, mediastinum common extranodal sites.
  • Testicular DLBCL: elderly men; bilateral risk ~15%; CNS relapse common → CNS prophylaxis. Treat with R-CHOP + intrathecal MTX ± RT to contralateral testis.
  • Staging: Ann Arbor + IPI (International Prognostic Index: age, stage, LDH, PS, extranodal sites). PET-CT for staging and response assessment.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Diffuse sheet-like growth of large lymphoid cells effacing nodal architecture; prominent nucleoli; scant-to-moderate cytoplasm; high mitotic rate.
  • Morphological variants: centroblastic (most common — large cells, vesicular nuclei, 2–3 nucleoli); immunoblastic (single central nucleolus, plasmacytoid cytoplasm); anaplastic (CD30+, bizarre giant cells).
  • GCB vs ABC/non-GCB subtype by Hans algorithm (CD10, BCL6, MUM1) — prognostic and therapeutic implications.
✚ Positive
CD20 – diffuse strong (pan-B marker; target of rituximab)PAX5 – nuclearCD79a – positiveBCL2 – variable (poor prognosis if positive with MYC)BCL6 – GCB subtypeMUM1/IRF4 – ABC/non-GCB subtypeKi67 – high (>40%, often >80%)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CD3 – negative (T-cell exclusion)CD138 – negative (plasma cell exclusion — but positive in plasmablastic variant)CD10 – GCB subtype (negative in ABC)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Burkitt lymphoma↗Ki67 ~100%; starry-sky pattern; MYC translocation t(8;14); CD10+, BCL6+, BCL2−; TdT−; younger patients
Classic Hodgkin lymphomaRS cells in inflammatory background; CD30+, CD15+; CD20− (weak/partial); PAX5 weak; CD45−
Plasmablastic lymphomaCD138+, MUM1+, CD20−; HIV/immunosuppression context; EBV+; MYC rearrangement; plasmacytoid morphology
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Hans algorithm: GCB = CD10+ OR (CD10− + BCL6+ + MUM1−). Non-GCB/ABC = everything else. GCB better prognosis with R-CHOP.
  • Double-hit lymphoma (DHL): MYC + BCL2 and/or BCL6 rearrangement → high-grade B-cell lymphoma, NOS — R-CHOP insufficient; R-EPOCH or clinical trial.
  • Double-expressor (DEL): MYC protein >40% + BCL2 protein >50% by IHC without rearrangement — poor prognosis but not DHL; R-CHOP standard.
  • MYC rearrangement: FISH mandatory if morphology high-grade or Ki67 >90%. Partners: BCL2 (t(14;18)) → DHL; BCL6 → DHL.
  • Testicular DLBCL: always bilateral testicular RT post-chemotherapy (contralateral risk ~15%); CNS prophylaxis mandatory.
Related / See Also
↗ BURKITT LYMPHOMA↗ CLASSICAL HODGKIN LYMPHOMA (cHL)↗ Burkitt lymphoma+ Add: Classic Hodgkin lymphoma+ Add: Plasmablastic lymphoma
BURKITT LYMPHOMA
Haempath Lymphoma Round cell Malignant 📋 1×
Three variants: Endemic (Africa; EBV-driven; jaw/orbit; children); Sporadic (ileum/caecum/ovary; children/young adults; EBV ~30%); Immunodeficiency-associated (HIV; EBV ~40%).
Clinical Context
  • Three variants: Endemic (Africa; EBV-driven; jaw/orbit; children); Sporadic (ileum/caecum/ovary; children/young adults; EBV ~30%); Immunodeficiency-associated (HIV; EBV ~40%).
  • Fastest-growing human tumour — doubling time ~24–48 hours. Tumour lysis syndrome (TLS) risk on treatment — aggressive prophylaxis mandatory.
  • Trigger: rapidly enlarging abdominal mass in child/young adult + elevated LDH + starry-sky pattern on biopsy.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Monotonous medium-sized lymphoid cells with round nuclei, multiple small nucleoli, and deeply basophilic cytoplasm with lipid vacuoles (Diff-Quik/Giemsa).
  • Starry-sky pattern: tingible body macrophages (pale histiocytes) scattered among monotonous dark lymphoma cells — due to high apoptotic rate.
  • Ki67 ~100% — virtually all cells in cycle. No mitotic activity not in cycle.
✚ Positive
CD20 – positiveCD10 – positiveBCL6 – positiveMYC – positive (>40% nuclear; IHC surrogate)Ki67 – ~100%
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
BCL2 – negative (key distinction from DLBCL/DHL)TdT – negative (distinguishes from B-ALL)CD5 – negativeMUM1 – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
DLBCL / High-grade B-cell lymphoma with MYC+BCL2↗BCL2+ in DHL; larger cells; Ki67 <100%; MYC IHC <40% possible; FISH for MYC/BCL2 rearrangement essential
B-lymphoblastic leukaemia/lymphoma (B-ALL)TdT+; CD34+; CD10+; immature chromatin; marrow involvement; no starry-sky; younger age
Mantle cell lymphoma (blastoid)Cyclin D1+; SOX11+; CD5+; t(11;14); CD10−; BCL2+; CD23−
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • MYC translocation t(8;14)(q24;q32): IGH-MYC (~80%); also t(2;8) and t(8;22) — all partner MYC with Ig loci. FISH mandatory.
  • Treatment: intensive short-course regimens (CODOX-M/IVAC or DA-EPOCH-R) — NOT R-CHOP. Cure rate >90% in localised; ~60–80% advanced.
  • CNS prophylaxis: intrathecal chemotherapy in all cases. CNS involvement at diagnosis: ~15% — LP at staging.
  • EBV association: latency I (EBNA1 only) in endemic; variable in sporadic.
  • Exam trap: BCL2 negative is required for Burkitt — if BCL2 positive, reclassify as high-grade B-cell lymphoma with MYC+BCL2 (DHL) → different treatment.
Related / See Also
↗ DIFFUSE LARGE B-CELL LYMPHOMA (DLBCL, NOS)↗ DLBCL / High-grade B-cell lymphoma with MYC+BCL2+ Add: B-lymphoblastic leukaemia/lymphoma (B-ALL)+ Add: Mantle cell lymphoma (blastoid)
CLASSICAL HODGKIN LYMPHOMA (cHL)
Haempath Lymphoma Mixed Malignant
Bimodal age distribution: 15–35 years and >55 years. Painless cervical/mediastinal lymphadenopathy; B symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss >10%); Pel-Ebstein fever; alcohol-induced pain (rare but classic).
Clinical Context
  • Bimodal age distribution: 15–35 years and >55 years. Painless cervical/mediastinal lymphadenopathy; B symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss >10%); Pel-Ebstein fever; alcohol-induced pain (rare but classic).
  • Subtypes: Nodular sclerosis (most common, ~70%; young women; mediastinal); Mixed cellularity (~25%; HIV/EBV; older); Lymphocyte-rich; Lymphocyte-depleted (AIDS-defining).
  • Staging: Lugano (Ann Arbor modification) + PET-CT. Early favourable → ABVD ×2–4 + RT; advanced → ABVD ×6 or BrEACADD/BEACOPP.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Pathognomonic Reed-Sternberg (RS) cells: large binucleate/multinucleate cells with prominent eosinophilic 'owl-eye' nucleoli; abundant pale cytoplasm — in reactive inflammatory background (lymphocytes, eosinophils, plasma cells, neutrophils, fibroblasts).
  • Lacunar cells (nodular sclerosis variant): RS cell variants with retraction artefact creating space around nucleus — artefact of formalin fixation.
  • Nodular sclerosis: fibrous bands dividing lymph node into nodules; lacunar cells.
✚ Positive
CD30 – diffuse strong membranous/Golgi (hallmark)CD15 – positive (~75%; Golgi pattern)PAX5 – weak nuclear (retained but dim)MUM1 – positiveEBV LMP1 – variable by subtype (MC >NS)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CD45 (LCA) – negativeCD20 – negative (or very weak/partial)CD3 – negativeALK – negative (distinguishes from ALCL)OCT2/BOB1 – negative/weak (unlike NLPHL)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Nodular lymphocyte predominant HL (NLPHL)↗LP cells ('popcorn'): CD20+, CD45+, OCT2+, BOB1+; CD30−, CD15−; EBV−; B-cell-rich nodules; better prognosis
Anaplastic Large Cell Lymphoma (ALCL)⚡CD30+ (diffuse strong); ALK+ (in ALK+ALCL); CD45+; T-cell markers; no RS cell morphology; EMA+
T-cell/histiocyte-rich large B-cell lymphoma (THRLBCL)CD20+ large B cells in T-cell/histiocyte background; CD30−; CD15−; aggressive; mimics cHL morphologically
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • RS cell origin: crippled germinal centre B cell — failed somatic hypermutation, should have undergone apoptosis; rescued by EBV (MC/LD) or unknown mechanisms (NS).
  • PD-L1 overexpression: 9p24.1 amplification → PD-L1 on RS cells → immune evasion. Pembrolizumab/nivolumab highly active in relapsed/refractory cHL.
  • Brentuximab vedotin (anti-CD30 ADC): active in relapsed/refractory cHL; consolidation post-ASCT.
  • Interim PET (iPET) response-adapted therapy: Deauville score ≤3 = complete metabolic response; escalate/de-escalate based on iPET.
  • Exam trap: CD20 weak/partial in cHL (RS cells) — do not classify as DLBCL. PAX5 weak positive (dim) + CD30 strong + CD15 = cHL.
🧫 Cytology Counterpart
↗ CLASSICAL HODGKIN LYMPHOMA — LN FNA
Related / See Also
↗ NODULAR LYMPHOCYTE PREDOMINANT HODGKIN LYMPHOMA (NLPHL)↗ DIFFUSE LARGE B-CELL LYMPHOMA (DLBCL, NOS)↗ Nodular lymphocyte predominant HL (NLPHL)⚡ Anaplastic Large Cell Lymphoma (ALCL)+ Add: T-cell/histiocyte-rich large B-cell lymphoma (THRLBCL)
NODULAR LYMPHOCYTE PREDOMINANT HODGKIN LYMPHOMA (NLPHL)
Haempath Lymphoma Mixed Intermediate 📋 2×
Distinct entity from cHL — indolent course, excellent prognosis. Young-to-middle aged men; peripheral lymphadenopathy (cervical/axillary/inguinal); mediastinal involvement rare.
Clinical Context
  • Distinct entity from cHL — indolent course, excellent prognosis. Young-to-middle aged men; peripheral lymphadenopathy (cervical/axillary/inguinal); mediastinal involvement rare.
  • Indolent: 10-year OS >90% for Stage I/II. Rare transformation to DLBCL (~5%) — biopsy any rapidly enlarging node.
  • Stage I/II: RT alone or R-CHOP; advanced: R-CHOP. Rituximab effective (CD20 positive LP cells).
Key Microscopic Features
  • Nodular architecture with large pale nodules; LP cells ('lymphocyte predominant' or 'popcorn' cells): large cells with multilobated nuclei and inconspicuous nucleoli, embedded in meshwork of reactive lymphocytes and histiocytes.
  • Nodules are B-cell follicle-like: CD21+ follicular dendritic cell meshwork; CD57+ T-cell rosettes around LP cells (characteristic).
  • No eosinophils, no plasma cells, no fibrous bands — unlike cHL.
✚ Positive
CD20 – strong (LP cells)CD45 – positiveOCT2 – positiveBOB1 – positiveBCL6 – positiveCD57 – T-cell rosettes around LP cells
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CD30 – negative (key cHL distinction)CD15 – negativeEBV – negativeALK – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Classical HL⚡RS cells: CD30+, CD15+, CD45−, CD20 weak/−, OCT2−; EBV+; eosinophils/plasma cells in background
T-cell/histiocyte-rich LBCL (THRLBCL)Diffuse architecture; CD20+ large B cells; no nodular pattern; no follicular dendritic cell meshwork; CD57 rosettes absent; aggressive
Follicular lymphoma (grade 3)↗Neoplastic follicles; BCL2+ (t(14;18)); CD10+; no LP cells; CD45+
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • LP cells are clonal germinal centre B cells with intact B-cell programme (OCT2, BOB1, CD20 all retained) — biologically distinct from RS cells.
  • CD57+ T-cell rosettes around LP cells: highly characteristic — present in ~50%; aids distinction from THRLBCL.
  • Transformation to DLBCL: usually GCB type. Biopsy any rapidly enlarging node in NLPHL patient.
  • Rituximab highly active (CD20+) — R-CVP or R-CHOP for systemic disease; RT alone for Stage IA.
Related / See Also
↗ CLASSICAL HODGKIN LYMPHOMA (cHL)↗ DIFFUSE LARGE B-CELL LYMPHOMA (DLBCL, NOS)⚡ Classical HL+ Add: T-cell/histiocyte-rich LBCL (THRLBCL)↗ Follicular lymphoma (grade 3)
MELANOMA (CUTANEOUS)
Haempath Melanocytic Epithelioid Malignant 📋 1×
Most lethal skin cancer. UV radiation primary risk factor; CDKN2A/CDK4 germline (familial); giant congenital naevus. ABCDE: Asymmetry, Border, Colour, Diameter >6mm, Evolution.
Clinical Context
  • Most lethal skin cancer. UV radiation primary risk factor; CDKN2A/CDK4 germline (familial); giant congenital naevus. ABCDE: Asymmetry, Border, Colour, Diameter >6mm, Evolution.
  • Breslow thickness (mm) most important prognostic factor. Staging: AJCC 8th (T1a <0.8mm no ulceration; T4b >4mm + ulceration). Sentinel lymph node biopsy for ≥T1b.
  • Subtype: superficial spreading (most common); nodular (vertical growth from outset, poorest prognosis); lentigo maligna (elderly, sun-damaged skin, slow-growing); acral lentiginous (palms/soles/subungual).
Key Microscopic Features
  • Pagetoid spread: atypical melanocytes ascending through epidermis singly and in nests — characteristic of radial growth phase. Confluent junctional nests with bridging.
  • Cytology: epithelioid/spindle cells with abundant cytoplasm, vesicular nuclei, prominent eosinophilic macronucleoli; melanin pigment (may be absent — amelanotic).
  • Breslow thickness: measured from granular layer (or ulcer base) to deepest tumour cell. Ulceration: full-thickness epidermal loss over tumour. Mitotic rate: per mm².
✚ Positive
S100 – diffuse strong (most sensitive, ~97%)SOX10 – diffuse nuclear (excellent sensitivity + specificity)Melan-A (MART-1) – positive (~85%)HMB45 – positive (~80%; lost in deep/spindle areas)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CK – negative (excludes carcinoma)CD45 – negative (excludes lymphoma)CD34 – negative (excludes SFT/dermatofibrosarcoma)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Pigmented Spitz naevus / Spitzoid melanoma↗FISH (6p25/RREB1, 6q23/MYB, 11q13/CCND1, CEP6): gain/loss patterns; p16 loss; ALK/ROS1/NTRK fusion in Spitz naevus; compound architecture; maturation
Clear cell sarcoma (melanoma of soft parts)↗Deep soft tissue; EWSR1-ATF1 or EWSR1-CREB1 fusion (FISH/RNA-seq); S100+, HMB45+; no junctional component; young adults
Desmoplastic melanoma↗Spindle cells in fibrous stroma; S100+/SOX10+ but HMB45−/Melan-A−; perineural invasion; sun-damaged skin; head/neck
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • BRAF V600E (~50%): dabrafenib + trametinib (targeted). NRAS (~20%): MEK inhibitor trials. NF1 (~15%): immunotherapy. Acral/mucosal: KIT mutations — imatinib.
  • Immunotherapy: anti-PD1 (pembrolizumab/nivolumab) ± anti-CTLA4 (ipilimumab) — first-line advanced melanoma regardless of BRAF status.
  • Desmoplastic melanoma: S100+/SOX10+ but HMB45/Melan-A negative — do not miss by incomplete panel.
  • SLN biopsy: for Breslow ≥0.8mm or T1b (any ulceration/mitosis). Positive SLN: completion LND no longer standard (DeCOG-SLT/MSLT-II) — observation vs adjuvant therapy.
  • Breslow and ulceration are the two most critical prognostic variables in the report — must be measured and reported.
🧫 Cytology Counterpart
↗ MELANOMA — LYMPH NODE / SKIN MASS FNA
Related / See Also
↗ Pigmented Spitz naevus / Spitzoid melanoma↗ Clear cell sarcoma (melanoma of soft parts)↗ Desmoplastic melanoma
PARATHYROID ADENOMA
Endocrine Endocrine Epithelioid Benign 📋 7×
Most common cause of primary hyperparathyroidism (~85%). Single gland adenoma in 80%; double adenoma 5%; multiglandular disease 15% (hyperplasia/MEN1/MEN2A).
Clinical Context
  • Most common cause of primary hyperparathyroidism (~85%). Single gland adenoma in 80%; double adenoma 5%; multiglandular disease 15% (hyperplasia/MEN1/MEN2A).
  • Presents with hypercalcaemia: 'bones, stones, groans, moans' — nephrolithiasis, osteoporosis, GI symptoms, neuropsychiatric. Often asymptomatic (incidental hypercalcaemia).
  • Trigger at frozen section: 'lymph node' sent intraoperatively → small tan/brown nodule → parathyroid tissue confirmed. PTH drop >50% post-excision = cure.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Compressed rim of normal parathyroid tissue at periphery (absent in hyperplasia) — the single most important diagnostic feature distinguishing adenoma from hyperplasia.
  • Chief cell proliferation (most common): sheets and nests of small cells with central round nuclei and pale cytoplasm; interspersed fat cells absent (normal parathyroid has abundant fat).
  • Water-clear cell adenoma: large cells with optically clear cytoplasm (glycogen). Oxyphil cell adenoma: large eosinophilic mitochondria-rich cells.
✚ Positive
PTH – positive (diagnostic; confirms parathyroid origin)CK – positive (low molecular weight)GATA3 – positiveChromogranin A – positive
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
Thyroglobulin – negative (distinguishes from thyroid)TTF1 – negativeCD45 – negative (distinguishes from lymph node)Rb – retained (lost in parathyroid carcinoma)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Parathyroid hyperplasia (MEN1/MEN2A)All four glands enlarged; no compressed normal rim; MEN1 (MENIN loss by IHC) or MEN2A (RET mutation) context
Parathyroid carcinomaThick fibrous bands; vascular/capsular invasion; mitoses; Rb loss (IHC); PGP9.5+; recurrent/metastatic disease; markedly elevated PTH
Thyroid nodule / lymph node (intraoperative)PTH IHC/needle washout; thyroglobulin/TTF1 for thyroid; CD45/CD20 for lymph node; small brown mobile nodule ≠ LN
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Intraoperative PTH: drop >50% from baseline within 10 minutes of excision = biochemical cure (Miami criterion). Guides extent of surgery.
  • Fat stain (oil red O): normal parathyroid ~50% fat; adenoma — fat absent or minimal. Useful if frozen section diagnosis uncertain.
  • Parathyroid carcinoma: Rb IHC loss most useful marker; thick fibrous bands; vascular/capsular invasion. PTH very high (>3× ULN). Rare (~1%).
  • MEN1: parathyroid hyperplasia + pancreatic NET + pituitary adenoma. MENIN IHC loss supports MEN1. Screen young patients with hyperparathyroidism.
  • Exam trap: frozen section of 'lymph node' → always consider parathyroid if small tan nodule. PTH IHC is the decisive stain.
Related / See Also
+ Add: Parathyroid hyperplasia (MEN1/MEN2A)+ Add: Parathyroid carcinoma+ Add: Thyroid nodule / lymph node (intraoperative)
VON MEYENBURG COMPLEX (BILE DUCT HAMARTOMA)
Hepatobiliary Medical Liver Cholestatic Benign 📋 6×
Benign biliary malformation — clusters of dilated bile ductules embedded in fibrous stroma, arising from ductal plate remnants. Incidental finding on imaging (hypoechoic liver nodules) or at surgery/autopsy.
Clinical Context
  • Benign biliary malformation — clusters of dilated bile ductules embedded in fibrous stroma, arising from ductal plate remnants. Incidental finding on imaging (hypoechoic liver nodules) or at surgery/autopsy.
  • Association with autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD), autosomal recessive PKD, and Caroli disease. Solitary or multiple (microhamartomatosis).
  • Frozen section trap: submitted as 'liver lesion' or 'suspicious nodule' — must distinguish from cholangiocarcinoma and metastatic adenocarcinoma intraoperatively.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Irregular clusters of small-to-medium bile ductules with angulated/compressed lumina containing inspissated bile; embedded in abundant fibrous stroma.
  • Ductules lined by bland cuboidal/flattened biliary epithelium — no atypia, no mitoses, no perineural/vascular invasion.
  • Portal tract-based distribution; may be subcapsular. Fibrous stroma is hyalinised/collagenous — distinguishes from desmoplastic carcinoma.
✚ Positive
CK7 – biliary epithelium positiveCK19 – biliary epithelium positive
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
Ki67 – very lowp53 – wild-typeMUC5AC – negative (positive in cholangiocarcinoma/IPMN)CEA – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Cholangiocarcinoma (intrahepatic)Nuclear atypia; irregular infiltrative glands; desmoplastic stroma; perineural invasion; MUC5AC+, CEA+; Ki67 elevated; p53 mutant pattern
Metastatic adenocarcinoma↗Cytological atypia; CK7/CK20 profile per primary; loss of biliary phenotype; clinical history
Bile duct adenoma (peribiliary gland hamartoma)↗More uniform small ducts; periportal; no inspissated bile; pale stroma; may have gastric/pyloric gland metaplasia
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Classic frozen section scenario: surgeon submits 'suspicious white nodule' on liver surface during cholecystectomy/Whipple's — benign; no further resection.
  • Malignant transformation to cholangiocarcinoma: very rare (~1%) but reported — any atypical/solid areas within VMC warrant additional sampling.
  • Microhamartomatosis: innumerable VMC throughout liver — rare; associated with portal hypertension.
  • Exam trap: do NOT diagnose as cholangiocarcinoma based on bland tubular structures in fibrotic stroma — atypia and invasion are mandatory for malignancy.
Related / See Also
↗ PRIMARY SCLEROSING CHOLANGITIS (PSC)+ Add: Cholangiocarcinoma (intrahepatic)↗ Metastatic adenocarcinoma↗ Bile duct adenoma (peribiliary gland hamartoma)
BILE DUCT ADENOMA (PERIBILIARY GLAND HAMARTOMA)
Hepatobiliary Medical Liver Cholestatic Benign 📋 2×
Benign hepatic nodule — proliferation of small uniform bile ductules with peribiliary gland differentiation. Incidental finding at laparoscopy/laparotomy; firm white subcapsular nodule <1cm.
Clinical Context
  • Benign hepatic nodule — proliferation of small uniform bile ductules with peribiliary gland differentiation. Incidental finding at laparoscopy/laparotomy; firm white subcapsular nodule <1cm.
  • No malignant potential. Clinical importance: mimics metastatic carcinoma macroscopically and on intraoperative frozen section.
  • Trigger: small white subcapsular liver nodule in patient with known primary malignancy — bile duct adenoma before metastasis.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Densely packed uniform small tubules/acini with pyloric/gastric gland-type epithelium; cuboidal cells with pale mucinous cytoplasm; round uniform nuclei; no atypia.
  • Stroma: fibrous/myxoid; inflammatory infiltrate (neutrophils, lymphocytes) common — may be florid.
  • Subcapsular location; pushing border; no invasion.
✚ Positive
CK7 – positiveCK19 – positiveMUC6 – positive (pyloric/gastric gland type)EMA – positive
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
MUC5AC – negativeCEA – negativeKi67 – very lowp53 – wild-type
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Von Meyenburg complex↗Angulated dilated ductules with inspissated bile; hyalinised fibrous stroma; no pyloric metaplasia; portal-based
Metastatic adenocarcinoma↗Nuclear atypia; infiltrative growth; CK7/CK20 per primary; Ki67 elevated; no bland pyloric gland morphology
CholangiocarcinomaAtypia; invasion; MUC5AC+; CEA+; desmoplastic stroma; perineural invasion
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Synonyms: peribiliary gland hamartoma (WHO 2019 preferred term). Some overlap with VMC — both are ductal plate malformations.
  • Frozen section: benign — avoid unnecessary hepatic resection. Report as 'benign biliary hamartoma/bile duct adenoma — no evidence of malignancy.'
  • Inflammatory infiltrate can be alarming — inflammatory pseudotumour differential; CK7/CK19 + bland cytology confirm biliary origin.
  • Association with oral contraceptive use proposed but weak.
Related / See Also
↗ VON MEYENBURG COMPLEX (BILE DUCT HAMARTOMA)↗ Von Meyenburg complex↗ Metastatic adenocarcinoma+ Add: Cholangiocarcinoma
FOCAL NODULAR HYPERPLASIA (FNH)
Hepatobiliary Medical Liver Vascular Benign 📋 1×
Benign hepatic mass — polyclonal hyperplastic response to vascular anomaly (arteriovenous malformation). F>M (8:1); reproductive-age women; OCP association (growth, not causation).
Clinical Context
  • Benign hepatic mass — polyclonal hyperplastic response to vascular anomaly (arteriovenous malformation). F>M (8:1); reproductive-age women; OCP association (growth, not causation).
  • Classic imaging: central stellate scar + spoke-wheel enhancement on arterial phase MRI (gadoxetate — specific for FNH). No malignant potential.
  • Trigger: incidental liver mass in young woman — FNH vs hepatic adenoma is the key clinical distinction (management differs).
Key Microscopic Features
  • Nodular architecture with fibrous septa radiating from central scar; thick-walled dystrophic arteries within fibrous septa — pathognomonic.
  • Hepatocyte plates of normal thickness (≤2 cells); no atypia; no fatty change; no globules. Bile ductular reaction at fibrous septa margin.
  • Central scar: fibrous tissue with thick-walled vessels + ductular reaction + inflammatory infiltrate (lymphocytes, plasma cells). Kupffer cells normal/increased.
✚ Positive
GS (glutamine synthetase) – map-like/geographic pattern (irregular strong zones throughout nodule)CK7/CK19 – ductular reaction at septaCD34 – sinusoidal (diffuse, as in all hepatocellular lesions)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
GS – not diffuse (diffuse GS = HCC; no GS = hepatic adenoma, normal liver)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Hepatocellular adenoma (HCA)↗No central scar; no ductular reaction; GS focal/negative (except β-cat mutated HCA: diffuse GS); unpaired arteries only; subtype-specific IHC (SAA, LFABP, β-catenin)
Well-differentiated HCC⚡Thickened hepatocyte plates (>2 cells); GS diffuse; reticulin loss; CD34 diffuse; nuclear atypia; GPC3+; AFP elevated
Cirrhotic nodule / large regenerative noduleBackground cirrhosis; no central scar; GS focal perivenular (normal pattern); fibrous septa are bridging not stellate
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • GS (glutamine synthetase) IHC: FNH = map-like geographic pattern (strongest around central veins, irregular extensions). Normal liver = perivenular only. HCC = diffuse. HCA = focal or absent.
  • Hepatic adenoma subtypes (WHO 2019): HNF1α-inactivated (LFABP loss, steatotic, low malignant risk); Inflammatory (SAA+, CRP+, peliosis, CRP elevated); β-catenin activated (GS diffuse, highest malignant risk, men); Unclassified.
  • Management: FNH — no resection needed; stop OCP; follow-up MRI at 1 year. HCA — resect if >5cm, β-catenin activated, or male.
  • Exam trap: map-like GS = FNH; diffuse GS = HCC. Do not confuse the two patterns.
Related / See Also
↗ HEPATOCELLULAR CARCINOMA (HCC)↗ Hepatocellular adenoma (HCA)⚡ Well-differentiated HCC+ Add: Cirrhotic nodule / large regenerative nodule
HEPATOCELLULAR CARCINOMA (HCC)
Hepatobiliary Medical Liver Hepatitic Malignant 📋 5×
Most common primary liver cancer; 5th most common cancer worldwide. Cirrhosis in ~90% (HBV, HCV, ALD, MASLD, haemochromatosis, A1ATD). HBV: HCC without cirrhosis possible.
Clinical Context
  • Most common primary liver cancer; 5th most common cancer worldwide. Cirrhosis in ~90% (HBV, HCV, ALD, MASLD, haemochromatosis, A1ATD). HBV: HCC without cirrhosis possible.
  • Surveillance: 6-monthly USS ± AFP in all cirrhotic patients. Diagnosis by imaging (LI-RADS 5 on MRI/CT: arterial enhancement + washout + capsule = HCC).
  • BCLC staging guides treatment: BCLC 0/A → resection/ablation/transplant (Milan criteria); B → TACE; C → sorafenib/lenvatinib/atezolizumab+bevacizumab; D → best supportive care.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Trabecular/pseudoglandular/solid growth; thickened hepatocyte plates (>2 cells wide); sinusoidal dilatation; unpaired arteries (no portal tracts).
  • Hepatocytic cytology: polygonal cells with abundant granular eosinophilic cytoplasm; round nuclei with prominent nucleoli; bile production (intracanalicular bile); Mallory-Denk bodies in some.
  • Well-differentiated HCC: reticulin loss (reticulin stain essential — collapse of sinusoidal framework); CD34 diffuse sinusoidal staining. High-grade: pleomorphism, giant cells, sarcomatoid transformation.
✚ Positive
Hep Par1 – positive (hepatocyte-specific mitochondrial antigen, ~90%)GPC3 (glypican-3) – positive (~80%; excellent specificity for HCC vs benign)GS – diffuse (aberrant activation)AFP – focal (~40%)CD34 – diffuse sinusoidal
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CK7 – negative (or focal)CK19 – negative (if positive → worse prognosis, stem cell phenotype)MOC31/BerEP4 – negative (positive in cholangiocarcinoma/metastatic adeno)CEA – negative (polyclonal CEA canalicular pattern is normal)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinomaCK7/CK19 diffuse+; MOC31+; MUC1/MUC5AC+; GPC3−; Hep Par1−; desmoplastic stroma; glandular differentiation
Metastatic adenocarcinoma to liver↗CK7/CK20 profile per primary; CEA+; MOC31+; Hep Par1−; GPC3−; clinical history/imaging
Hepatic adenoma (well-differentiated HCC vs HCA)⚡GPC3 negative in HCA; reticulin preserved in HCA; no thickened plates; GS map-like (FNH) or focal (HCA) not diffuse
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Reticulin stain: collapse/loss of sinusoidal reticulin framework in HCC — essential for diagnosis of well-differentiated HCC on biopsy.
  • GPC3: most useful positive marker for HCC — negative in hepatic adenoma, cirrhotic nodules, FNH. Positive in HCC, yolk sac tumour, clear cell RCC (variable).
  • Fibrolamellar HCC: young adults, no cirrhosis; DNAJB1-PRKACA fusion; CK7+, CD68 (PGM1)+; eosinophilic cells + fibrous lamellae; better prognosis.
  • Steatohepatitic HCC: MASLD background; steatosis, ballooning, Mallory-Denk bodies in tumour — can mimic steatohepatitis; GPC3+ confirms.
  • Milan criteria for transplant: single ≤5cm OR ≤3 nodules each ≤3cm, no macrovascular invasion, no extrahepatic disease.
Related / See Also
↗ FOCAL NODULAR HYPERPLASIA (FNH)↗ MASLD / MASH↗ AUTOIMMUNE HEPATITIS (AIH)+ Add: Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma↗ Metastatic adenocarcinoma to liver⚡ Hepatic adenoma (well-differentiated HCC vs HCA)
XANTHOGRANULOMATOUS CHOLECYSTITIS (XGC)
Hepatobiliary Medical Liver Granulomatous Variable 📋 2×
Severe form of chronic cholecystitis — lipid-laden macrophages (xanthoma cells) within gallbladder wall, often transmural. Associated with gallstones (>90%), Rokitansky-Aschoff sinuses, and mural perforation.
Clinical Context
  • Severe form of chronic cholecystitis — lipid-laden macrophages (xanthoma cells) within gallbladder wall, often transmural. Associated with gallstones (>90%), Rokitansky-Aschoff sinuses, and mural perforation.
  • Mimics gallbladder carcinoma clinically and on imaging — thickened gallbladder wall with mural nodules. Frozen section critical to exclude carcinoma intraoperatively.
  • Trigger: thickened gallbladder wall on imaging/macroscopy — XGC vs carcinoma distinction requires histology.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Transmural infiltration of foamy lipid-laden macrophages (CD68+); Touton giant cells; cholesterol clefts; haemosiderin; acute and chronic inflammatory cells.
  • Rokitansky-Aschoff sinuses: pseudodiverticula of gallbladder mucosa into muscularis — common in gallstone disease; may rupture → xanthogranulomatous reaction.
  • Reactive epithelial atypia overlying XGC can be severe — do not over-call as carcinoma. True carcinoma: invasive glands with desmoplasia, away from inflamed areas.
✚ Positive
CD68 – xanthoma cells diffuse positiveCK7 – reactive biliary epithelium
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
Ki67 – low in reactive epithelium (high in carcinoma)p53 – wild-type (mutant pattern in carcinoma)CEA – negative (canalicular pattern only in reactive)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Gallbladder adenocarcinomaInvasive glands with desmoplasia; nuclear atypia away from inflammation; perineural/vascular invasion; p53 mutant; Ki67 elevated; CK7 diffuse in malignant glands
Xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis (XGP)⚡Same pathological process, different organ — renal XGP associated with staghorn calculus; gallbladder XGC with cholecystolithiasis
Malakoplakia (gallbladder — rare)↗Michaelis-Guttmann bodies (PAS+); E. coli; no Touton giants/cholesterol clefts
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Frozen section role: confirm benign XGC vs unsuspected gallbladder carcinoma — carcinoma changes operative management (extended resection vs cholecystectomy alone).
  • Reactive atypia trap: inflamed mucosa overlying XGC can show severe epithelial atypia — sample away from acute inflammation; assess invasion, desmoplasia, and mitoses.
  • Fistula formation: XGC can erode into duodenum/colon/liver — Mirizzi syndrome (extrinsic compression of CBD by impacted cystic duct stone) overlap.
  • Incidental gallbladder carcinoma post-cholecystectomy: T1a — no further surgery; T1b — re-resection of cystic duct stump + LN sampling; T2+ — formal hepatic resection + LN dissection.
Related / See Also
↗ XANTHOGRANULOMATOUS PYELONEPHRITIS (XGP)+ Add: Gallbladder adenocarcinoma⚡ Xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis (XGP)↗ Malakoplakia (gallbladder — rare)
INTRADUCTAL PAPILLARY MUCINOUS NEOPLASM (IPMN)
Hepatobiliary Pancreatic Glandular/mucinous Intermediate 📋 3×
Cystic pancreatic neoplasm communicating with main pancreatic duct (MPD) or branch ducts. Main duct type (MD-IPMN): higher malignant risk (~70%); branch duct (BD-IPMN): lower risk (~25%); mixed type.
Clinical Context
  • Cystic pancreatic neoplasm communicating with main pancreatic duct (MPD) or branch ducts. Main duct type (MD-IPMN): higher malignant risk (~70%); branch duct (BD-IPMN): lower risk (~25%); mixed type.
  • Worrisome features (Fukuoka guidelines): cyst >3cm, mural nodule, MPD dilation 5–9mm, rapid growth, pancreatitis. High-risk stigmata (resect): obstructive jaundice, enhancing mural nodule, MPD ≥10mm.
  • EUS-FNA of cyst fluid: CEA >192ng/mL = mucinous cyst; amylase elevated (communicates with duct); KRAS/GNAS mutation = IPMN.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Papillary proliferation of columnar mucinous epithelium within pancreatic ducts; four subtypes: gastric (BD-IPMN, low-grade, GNAS mut); intestinal (MD-IPMN, high-grade, CDX2+, MUC2+, GNAS mut); pancreatobiliary (MD-IPMN, aggressive); oncocytic (rare, indolent).
  • Grading: low-grade dysplasia (well-formed papillae, mild atypia) → high-grade dysplasia (complex architecture, significant atypia) → invasive carcinoma.
  • Invasive carcinoma from IPMN: tubular (pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma-type) or colloid (mucinous non-cystic carcinoma) — colloid has better prognosis.
✚ Positive
MUC5AC – gastric and intestinal subtypesMUC2 – intestinal subtype (goblet cells)CDX2 – intestinal subtypeMUC1 – pancreatobiliary subtype (aggressive)CK7 – all
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
MUC1 – negative in gastric/intestinal (low-risk) subtypes
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Mucinous cystic neoplasm (MCN)No duct communication; ovarian-type stroma (PR+, inhibin+); women only; body/tail; KRAS not GNAS
Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC)Infiltrative irregular glands; desmoplastic stroma; loss of SMAD4; no papillary intraductal component; DPC4/SMAD4 loss
Serous cystadenomaGlycogen-rich cuboidal cells; no mucin; no duct communication; VHL mutation; GLUT1+; honeycomb microcysts
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • GNAS mutation (~60%): near-pathognomonic for IPMN (absent in MCN, PDAC); codon 201. KRAS mutation (~80%): shared with PDAC — not discriminating.
  • Frozen section at Whipple's: pancreatic neck margin — assess for IPMN dysplasia grade. High-grade dysplasia at margin → extend resection.
  • Subtype determines prognosis of invasive carcinoma: colloid (intestinal-derived) — median OS ~131 months; tubular (pancreatobiliary-derived) — ~20 months.
  • PanIN vs IPMN: PanIN <1cm, in small ducts, flat/micropapillary; IPMN ≥1cm, in larger ducts, grossly visible papillary lesion.
  • Surveillance of unresected BD-IPMN: per Fukuoka/European guidelines; MRI/EUS 6–12 monthly if worrisome features develop.
Related / See Also
↗ LOW-GRADE APPENDICEAL MUCINOUS NEOPLASM (LAMN)+ Add: Mucinous cystic neoplasm (MCN)+ Add: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC)+ Add: Serous cystadenoma
INSULINOMA (PANCREATIC NET — INSULIN-SECRETING)
Hepatobiliary Neuroendocrine Well-differentiated Intermediate 📋 1×
Most common functional pancreatic NET (~40%). Whipple's triad: hypoglycaemia symptoms + fasting glucose <2.2mmol/L + relief with glucose. Insulin:glucose ratio elevated; C-peptide elevated (excludes exogenous insulin).
Clinical Context
  • Most common functional pancreatic NET (~40%). Whipple's triad: hypoglycaemia symptoms + fasting glucose <2.2mmol/L + relief with glucose. Insulin:glucose ratio elevated; C-peptide elevated (excludes exogenous insulin).
  • 90% solitary, benign, <2cm; 10% malignant; ~5% MEN1 (multiple). Body/tail predominance.
  • Localisation: EUS most sensitive (~90%); 68Ga-DOTATATE PET (SSTR2 positive); intraoperative USS.
Key Microscopic Features
  • WD-NET morphology: organoid/trabecular/gyriform architecture; uniform round nuclei; salt-and-pepper chromatin; inconspicuous nucleoli; moderate amphophilic cytoplasm.
  • Amyloid deposition within tumour stroma (~50%) — islet amyloid polypeptide (IAPP/amylin); Congo red positive; characteristic of insulinoma.
  • Hyalinised fibrous bands common. Ki67 typically <2% (G1). Mitoses rare.
✚ Positive
Synaptophysin – diffuseChromogranin A – diffuseInsulin – positive (tumour cells)IAPP – positive (amyloid stroma)SSTR2 – positiveKi67 – <5%
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
Glucagon – negativeSomatostatin – negativeGastrin – negativeKi67 – <5% (G1)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Non-functioning pancreatic NETNo clinical syndrome; insulin negative; otherwise identical morphology — diagnosis requires clinical correlation
NesidioblastosisDiffuse islet hypertrophy (not a discrete mass); neonates/adults post-Roux-en-Y; focal form (PET-guided partial pancreatectomy)
Solid pseudopapillary neoplasm (SPN)⚡Young women; solid + cystic with pseudopapillary fronds; nuclear β-catenin+; CD10+; TFE3+; NE markers variable/focal; low malignant potential
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Amyloid (IAPP/Congo red+): highly characteristic of insulinoma — not present in other pancreatic NETs.
  • Malignant insulinoma criteria: metastasis is the only definitive criterion — histological features alone unreliable. Size >2cm, mitoses >2/2mm², Ki67 >5%, perineural/vascular invasion = higher risk.
  • Surgical cure rate >90% for localised insulinoma — enucleation for small tumours; distal pancreatectomy for body/tail.
  • Diazoxide: medical management of hypoglycaemia pre-operatively (inhibits insulin secretion).
  • MEN1: multiple insulinomas possible — screen all with insulinoma <40 years or multiple tumours.
Related / See Also
↗ WELL-DIFFERENTIATED NET G1/G2 (GI)+ Add: Non-functioning pancreatic NET+ Add: Nesidioblastosis⚡ Solid pseudopapillary neoplasm (SPN)
CHRONIC PANCREATITIS
Hepatobiliary Pancreatic Fibrous/collagenous Variable 📋 5×
Irreversible fibroinflammatory destruction of pancreatic parenchyma. Causes: alcohol (most common, ~70%); CFTR mutation (CF); SPINK1/PRSS1 (hereditary); autoimmune (IgG4-related); obstructive; tropical.
Clinical Context
  • Irreversible fibroinflammatory destruction of pancreatic parenchyma. Causes: alcohol (most common, ~70%); CFTR mutation (CF); SPINK1/PRSS1 (hereditary); autoimmune (IgG4-related); obstructive; tropical.
  • Presents with chronic epigastric pain ± steatorrhoea (exocrine failure) ± diabetes (endocrine failure). Pancreatic calcification on imaging (alcohol-associated).
  • Frozen section context: intraoperative mass — chronic pancreatitis vs PDAC. Critical distinction — do not diagnose PDAC without unequivocal malignant criteria.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Interlobular and intralobular fibrosis with acinar atrophy; ductal dilatation and stricturing; chronic inflammatory infiltrate (lymphocytes, plasma cells); islets of Langerhans relatively preserved until late.
  • Periductal fibrosis — 'onion-skin' pattern in obstructive chronic pancreatitis. Ductal epithelium may show reactive atypia — do not over-call.
  • IgG4-related autoimmune pancreatitis (AIP type 1): storiform fibrosis, obliterative phlebitis, IgG4+ plasma cells >10/HPF — dramatic steroid response.
✚ Positive
IgG4 – >10/HPF in AIP type 1 (must perform in all cases of unexplained chronic pancreatitis/mass)CK7/CK19 – reactive ductal epithelium
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
SMAD4/DPC4 – retained (lost in ~55% PDAC — useful if lost, not interpretable if retained)Ki67 – low in reactive epithelium
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC)Infiltrative irregular glands with nuclear atypia; haphazard arrangement; abortive lumina; perineural invasion; SMAD4 loss (~55%); incomplete glands; necrotic luminal debris
Autoimmune pancreatitis (AIP type 2)↗Granulocytic epithelial lesions (GEL — neutrophils within ductal epithelium); IgG4 <10/HPF; younger; ulcerative colitis association; ICDC criteria
Groove pancreatitisFibrous mass between pancreatic head, duodenum, and CBD; myoid spindle cells; Brunner gland hyperplasia; alcohol; mimics pancreatic head carcinoma
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • IgG4 IHC: mandatory in all chronic pancreatitis cases — AIP type 1 responds dramatically to steroids; avoid Whipple's in steroid-responsive AIP.
  • SMAD4/DPC4 IHC: loss in ~55% PDAC; retained in pancreatitis. If lost → strongly supports PDAC. If retained → does NOT exclude PDAC.
  • Frozen section: the most treacherous frozen section in pathology — reactive ductal atypia in chronic pancreatitis mimics PDAC. Requirement: unequivocal invasive malignant glands for PDAC diagnosis on frozen.
  • Hereditary pancreatitis: PRSS1 gain-of-function (gain of trypsin activity) — autosomal dominant; lifetime pancreatic cancer risk ~40%. SPINK1: modifier gene.
  • Pancreatic exocrine insufficiency: faecal elastase <200µg/g; treat with pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT).
Related / See Also
↗ INTRADUCTAL PAPILLARY MUCINOUS NEOPLASM (IPMN)+ Add: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC)↗ Autoimmune pancreatitis (AIP type 2)+ Add: Groove pancreatitis
LUNG ADENOCARCINOMA
Thoracic Epithelial Glandular/mucinous Malignant 📋 15×
Most common lung cancer (~40%); peripheral predominance; F > M; non-smoker/light smoker; rising incidence. Driver mutations: EGFR (~15% Western, ~50% Asian), ALK (~5%), ROS1 (~2%), KRAS (~30%), MET exon 14, BRAF V600E, RET, NTRK.
Clinical Context
  • Most common lung cancer (~40%); peripheral predominance; F > M; non-smoker/light smoker; rising incidence. Driver mutations: EGFR (~15% Western, ~50% Asian), ALK (~5%), ROS1 (~2%), KRAS (~30%), MET exon 14, BRAF V600E, RET, NTRK.
  • Molecular testing mandatory on all advanced non-squamous NSCLC: EGFR/ALK/ROS1/KRAS/BRAF/MET/RET/NTRK + PD-L1. Small biopsy conservation strategy essential.
  • Trigger: peripheral lung mass in non/light smoker — adenocarcinoma; request molecular panel before treatment.
Key Microscopic Features
  • WHO 2021 patterns (report % of each): lepidic (tumour cells along pre-existing alveolar walls — no stromal/vascular/pleural invasion); acinar; papillary; micropapillary (worst prognosis); solid (with mucin).
  • Adenocarcinoma in situ (AIS): pure lepidic, ≤3cm, no invasion — 100% 5-year OS if resected. Minimally invasive adenocarcinoma (MIA): lepidic + ≤5mm invasion. Invasive: >5mm or any high-grade pattern.
  • Predominant pattern determines grade: Grade 1 (lepidic predominant, no high-grade); Grade 2 (acinar/papillary predominant, no high-grade); Grade 3 (solid/micropapillary predominant OR any high-grade pattern).
✚ Positive
TTF1 – diffuse nuclear (~75–80%; most useful positive marker)Napsin A – granular cytoplasmic (~70%)CK7 – diffuseCK8/18 – positive
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CK20 – negativep40 – negative (key SCC distinction)p63 – negative/weakCK5/6 – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Squamous cell carcinoma (lung)⚡p40+, p63+, CK5/6+; TTF1−, Napsin A−; central/endobronchial; keratinisation/intercellular bridges; smoker
Metastatic adenocarcinoma to lung↗TTF1−; CK7/CK20 profile per primary; PAX8 (renal/thyroid/gynaecological); SATB2 (colorectal); clinical history; bilateral/multiple lesions
Pleural mesothelioma (epithelioid)↗Calretinin+, CK5/6+, WT1+; TTF1−, Napsin A−, BerEP4−; pleural rind; asbestos exposure
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • EGFR mutation (exons 18–21): TKI therapy — osimertinib (3rd gen) first-line for exon 19 del/L858R. Exon 20 insertion: amivantamab/mobocertinib.
  • ALK rearrangement (EML4-ALK): alectinib first-line (superior to crizotinib). ROS1: entrectinib/crizotinib. RET: selpercatinib/pralsetinib. NTRK: larotrectinib/entrectinib.
  • PD-L1 (22C3): ≥50% → pembrolizumab monotherapy first-line (no driver mutation). <50% → chemotherapy + immunotherapy.
  • Lepidic pattern on frozen section: do NOT call 'carcinoma' if pure lepidic — may be AIS or MIA; assess invasion after full resection with permanent sections.
  • Small biopsy triage: if squamous morphology not obvious → TTF1/p40 panel; preserve tissue for molecular — avoid exhausting biopsy with IHC.
🧫 Cytology Counterpart
↗ LUNG ADENOCARCINOMA — BRONCHIAL BRUSHINGS / FNA
Related / See Also
↗ SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA (LUNG)↗ SMALL CELL LUNG CARCINOMA (SCLC)⚡ Squamous cell carcinoma (lung)↗ Metastatic adenocarcinoma to lung↗ Pleural mesothelioma (epithelioid)
SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA (LUNG)
Thoracic Epithelial Epithelioid Malignant 📋 9×
~25% of lung cancers. Central/hilar origin from bronchial epithelium; strong smoking association. Presents with haemoptysis, obstructive pneumonia, post-obstructive collapse.
Clinical Context
  • ~25% of lung cancers. Central/hilar origin from bronchial epithelium; strong smoking association. Presents with haemoptysis, obstructive pneumonia, post-obstructive collapse.
  • Paraneoplastic: hypercalcaemia (PTHrP production — most common paraneoplastic of SCC). Rarely causes SIADH (commoner with SCLC).
  • Molecular: PIK3CA, FGFR1 amplification, SOX2 amplification, CDKN2A loss. EGFR/ALK/ROS1 rarely mutated — targeted therapy rarely applicable; PD-L1 + chemotherapy first-line.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Keratinising SCC: intercellular bridges (desmosomes — 'prickles'); keratin pearls (concentric whorls of keratinising cells); individual cell keratinisation (dense eosinophilic cytoplasm).
  • Non-keratinising SCC: sheets of polygonal cells with squamoid morphology but no overt keratinisation — requires IHC (p40, CK5/6) for diagnosis on small biopsy.
  • Basaloid variant: peripheral palisading; high N:C ratio; minimal keratinisation; aggressive behaviour — p40+.
✚ Positive
p40 – diffuse nuclear (most specific squamous marker; ΔNp63 isoform)p63 – diffuse nuclearCK5/6 – diffuseCK14 – positive
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
TTF1 – negativeNapsin A – negativeCK7 – negative/focalSynaptophysin/Chromogranin – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Lung adenocarcinoma (poorly differentiated)⚡TTF1+, Napsin A+; p40−, CK5/6−; glandular/papillary areas; mucin; no keratinisation
Metastatic SCC to lung↗Clinical history; p40+, CK5/6+ (shared); site-specific markers (CK20/SATB2 colorectal; GATA3 urothelial/breast; HPV ISH cervical); bilateral/multiple lesions
Large cell carcinoma↗Diagnosis of exclusion: no squamous/glandular/NE morphology and negative IHC panel; TTF1−, p40−, NE markers−
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • p40 vs p63: p40 is more specific for squamous differentiation (p63 can be focally positive in adenocarcinoma and SCLC); use p40 as primary squamous marker on small biopsies.
  • Keratinising SCC on frozen: diagnosis can be made morphologically — keratin pearls/intercellular bridges are diagnostic; no IHC needed.
  • Paraneoplastic hypercalcaemia: PTHrP (not PTH) — PTH suppressed; treat underlying tumour; bisphosphonates/denosumab for symptomatic hypercalcaemia.
  • No routine molecular testing for SCC (no targetable drivers); however, PD-L1 mandatory for immunotherapy stratification.
  • Cavitating SCC: central necrosis creates cavity — mimics abscess/TB on imaging; biopsy from cavity wall.
🧫 Cytology Counterpart
↗ SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA (LUNG) — BRONCHIAL BRUSHINGS / FNA
Related / See Also
↗ LUNG ADENOCARCINOMA↗ SMALL CELL LUNG CARCINOMA (SCLC)⚡ Lung adenocarcinoma (poorly differentiated)↗ Metastatic SCC to lung↗ Large cell carcinoma
PULMONARY HAMARTOMA
Thoracic Mesenchymal Mixed Benign 📋 4×
Most common benign lung tumour (~75% of benign pulmonary tumours). Adults; slight male predominance; usually asymptomatic — incidental 'coin lesion' on CXR/CT.
Clinical Context
  • Most common benign lung tumour (~75% of benign pulmonary tumours). Adults; slight male predominance; usually asymptomatic — incidental 'coin lesion' on CXR/CT.
  • Classic CT: popcorn calcification (chondroid) + fat attenuation — pathognomonic. If both features present, resection unnecessary.
  • Trigger: well-circumscribed peripheral lung nodule in adult — if CT popcorn calcification + fat → diagnose radiologically; if indeterminate → resection/CT-guided biopsy.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Admixture of mature hyaline cartilage (predominant), fibromyxoid stroma, fat (adipose tissue), smooth muscle, and entrapped respiratory epithelial clefts — the combination is diagnostic.
  • Lobulated architecture; sharp pushing border; no invasion. Epithelial clefts lined by normal respiratory epithelium (ciliated columnar cells).
  • Endobronchial hamartoma: same composition but polypoid; causes obstructive symptoms; more common in children.
✚ Positive
S100 – cartilaginous componentSMA – smooth muscle componentCK – entrapped respiratory epithelium only
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
TTF1 – negative in mesenchymal componentsp40 – negativeCD117 – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Carcinoid tumour (lung)Synaptophysin+, CgA+, INSM1+; OTP+; no cartilage/fat; organoid architecture; endobronchial presentation
Chondroma (Carney triad)Pure cartilage without other mesenchymal elements; young women; associated with GIST + paraganglioma; SDH-deficient
Carcinosarcoma / pulmonary blastomaMalignant epithelial + malignant mesenchymal components; nuclear atypia; mitoses; invasion
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Frozen section: classic scenario — surgeon submits 'coin lesion'; benign mixture of cartilage + fibromyxoid stroma + fat + entrapped epithelial clefts = hamartoma. No malignancy; no further resection.
  • Carney triad: pulmonary chondroma (NOT hamartoma) + GIST + paraganglioma; SDH-deficient; young women; non-hereditary somatic SDHA epigenetic silencing.
  • HMGA2 rearrangement: present in subset of pulmonary hamartomas — same gene rearranged in lipoma and pleomorphic adenoma.
  • No malignant potential; recurrence after enucleation rare. Conservative resection (wedge) or enucleation curative.
Related / See Also
↗ TYPICAL CARCINOID (LUNG)+ Add: Carcinoid tumour (lung)+ Add: Chondroma (Carney triad)+ Add: Carcinosarcoma / pulmonary blastoma
SOLITARY FIBROUS TUMOUR (SFT)
Thoracic Mesenchymal Spindle cell Intermediate 📋 3×
Ubiquitous mesenchymal tumour — pleura most common site (visceral pleura); also lung, meninges, orbit, pelvis/retroperitoneum, liver. NAB2-STAT6 fusion (chromosome 12 inversion) defines the entity at all sites.
Clinical Context
  • Ubiquitous mesenchymal tumour — pleura most common site (visceral pleura); also lung, meninges, orbit, pelvis/retroperitoneum, liver. NAB2-STAT6 fusion (chromosome 12 inversion) defines the entity at all sites.
  • Pleural SFT: often large, pedunculated; Doege-Potter syndrome (hypoglycaemia from IGF-2 secretion — rare) or Pierre-Marie-Bamberger (hypertrophic pulmonary osteoarthropathy).
  • ~20% behave malignantly (metastasis) — risk stratified by Demicco or England criteria (size, cellularity, mitoses, necrosis, pleomorphism).
Key Microscopic Features
  • 'Patternless pattern': haphazard arrangement of bland spindle cells in alternating hypercellular and hypocellular zones; staghorn/hemangiopericytoma-like branching vessels — characteristic.
  • Collagenous/myxoid stroma; no significant atypia in benign SFT; no necrosis.
  • Malignant SFT: hypercellularity, nuclear atypia, mitoses >4/10HPF, necrosis, size >10cm — predict aggressive behaviour.
✚ Positive
STAT6 – diffuse nuclear (surrogate for NAB2-STAT6 fusion; ~97% sensitive/specific)CD34 – diffuse (~90%)CD99 – positiveBCL2 – positive
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
S100 – negativeDesmin – negativeCK – negative (or focal)SMA – negative or focal
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Synovial sarcoma (monophasic)↗TLE1 diffuse+; SS18-SSX fusion; STAT6−; CD34−; younger patients; hemangiopericytoma pattern (overlap)
Mesothelioma (sarcomatoid)↗CK+, Calretinin+; STAT6−; CD34−; BAP1 loss; diffuse pleural involvement; asbestos exposure
Desmoid fibromatosis (if deep)Nuclear β-catenin+; STAT6−; CD34−; CTNNB1/APC mutation; no hemangiopericytoma vessels
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • STAT6 IHC: nuclear positivity is specific for NAB2-STAT6 fusion — cytoplasmic STAT6 staining is non-specific and should not be interpreted as positive.
  • NAB2-STAT6 fusion variants: NAB2ex4-STAT6ex2 (more aggressive) vs NAB2ex6-STAT6ex16/17 (indolent) — fusion variant correlates with prognosis.
  • Demicco risk stratification: low risk (0 points) — observe; intermediate/high risk — surgery + adjuvant RT; metastatic — sunitinib/pazopanib.
  • Recurrence risk: positive margins and malignant features → recurrence even after complete resection. Long-term follow-up mandatory (recurrence up to 20 years later).
  • Doege-Potter syndrome: paraneoplastic hypoglycaemia from IGF-2 (big IGF-2) production — tumour resection curative.
Related / See Also
↗ SYNOVIAL SARCOMA (GI)↗ DESMOID TUMOUR / INTRA-ABDOMINAL FIBROMATOSIS↗ Synovial sarcoma (monophasic)↗ Mesothelioma (sarcomatoid)+ Add: Desmoid fibromatosis (if deep)
INVASIVE BREAST CARCINOMA OF NO SPECIAL TYPE (NST / IDC)
Breast Epithelial Epithelioid Malignant 📋 10×
Most common breast malignancy (~75%). Palpable mass ± skin/nipple changes. Risk factors: BRCA1/2, age, OCP, HRT, nulliparity, early menarche/late menopause, alcohol.
Clinical Context
  • Most common breast malignancy (~75%). Palpable mass ± skin/nipple changes. Risk factors: BRCA1/2, age, OCP, HRT, nulliparity, early menarche/late menopause, alcohol.
  • Molecular subtypes (surrogate IHC): Luminal A (ER+/PR+/HER2−/low Ki67) — best prognosis; Luminal B (ER+/HER2+ or high Ki67); HER2-enriched (ER−/HER2+); Triple negative (ER−/PR−/HER2−) — BRCA1 association, worst prognosis.
  • Staging: TNM8. Sentinel lymph node biopsy standard for clinically N0. Multidisciplinary management: surgery + RT ± systemic therapy per receptor status.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Infiltrating nests, cords, trabeculae, and tubules of epithelial cells in desmoplastic stroma; no specific architectural pattern (NST). Tubule formation, nuclear grade (1–3), and mitotic count → Nottingham histological grade (1–3).
  • Nottingham grade: tubules (>75%=1, 10–75%=2, <10%=3) + nuclear pleomorphism (mild=1, moderate=2, severe=3) + mitoses per mm² (site-dependent) → Grade 1 (3–5), Grade 2 (6–7), Grade 3 (8–9).
  • DCIS component: in situ disease at periphery — grade, architecture (comedo/cribriform/micropapillary/solid), extent, and margins must be reported.
✚ Positive
ER – Allred score (proportion + intensity; ≥1% = positive by ASCO/CAP)PR – positive (Allred)CK7 – positiveGATA3 – positive (~90%; breast lineage marker)Mammaglobulin – variableGCDFP-15 – variable
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CK20 – negativeCDX2 – negativeTTF1 – negativePAX8 – negative (distinguishes from gynaecological/renal primary)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Invasive lobular carcinoma (ILC)↗Single-file infiltration; loss of E-cadherin (CDH1 mutation/methylation); no tubule formation; targetoid growth; ER+; CK7+
Metastatic carcinoma to breast↗No DCIS; bilateral; clinical history; site-specific IHC (TTF1 lung, ER/PAX8 gynaecological, PSA prostate); no desmoplastic stroma
Phyllodes tumour (malignant)Biphasic — epithelial + stromal components; stromal overgrowth; leaf-like architecture; CD34+ stroma; no carcinoma IHC profile
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • HER2 testing: IHC 0/1+ = negative; 2+ = equivocal → FISH/ISH; 3+ = positive. HER2-low (IHC 1+ or 2+/FISH−): trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd) now approved — reclassifies previously 'negative' patients.
  • BRCA1/2 germline: triple negative in young woman (<50) → refer genetics. BRCA1: high-grade, triple negative, pushing margins. BRCA2: ER+, pleomorphic lobular.
  • Neoadjuvant chemotherapy: pathological complete response (pCR = ypT0/is ypN0) is surrogate endpoint. Residual disease → capecitabine (TNBC) or T-DM1 (HER2+).
  • Oncotype DX (Recurrence Score): ER+/HER2−/N0 — score 0–25 → endocrine therapy alone; score ≥26 → chemotherapy + endocrine.
  • Paget disease of nipple: intraepidermal spread of carcinoma cells (Paget cells: large pale cells with prominent nucleoli); HER2+ in >90%; associated with underlying DCIS or invasive carcinoma.
🧫 Cytology Counterpart
↗ BREAST FNA — FIVE-CATEGORY REPORTING (C1–C5)
Related / See Also
↗ INVASIVE LOBULAR CARCINOMA (ILC)↗ DUCTAL CARCINOMA IN SITU (DCIS)↗ Invasive lobular carcinoma (ILC)↗ Metastatic carcinoma to breast+ Add: Phyllodes tumour (malignant)
INVASIVE LOBULAR CARCINOMA (ILC)
Breast Epithelial Epithelioid Malignant
~10–15% of invasive breast carcinomas. Clinically and radiologically occult — diffuse infiltration without mass; mammographic density change rather than discrete mass; bilateral in ~20%.
Clinical Context
  • ~10–15% of invasive breast carcinomas. Clinically and radiologically occult — diffuse infiltration without mass; mammographic density change rather than discrete mass; bilateral in ~20%.
  • CDH1 (E-cadherin) germline mutation → hereditary diffuse gastric cancer (HDGC) + ILC syndrome — screen ILC patients with strong family history.
  • Metastatic pattern distinct from IDC: peritoneal/retroperitoneal, meninges, GI tract (mimics linitis plastica), ovary — late recurrences (>10 years).
Key Microscopic Features
  • Single-file ('Indian file') infiltration through desmoplastic stroma; targetoid growth around normal structures. Cells small and uniform with intracytoplasmic vacuoles (intracytoplasmic lumina — signet-ring-like).
  • Classic: small cells, low grade nuclei, absent tubules, no mitoses → Grade 1/2. Pleomorphic variant: large cells, Grade 3 nuclei, prominent nucleoli — worse prognosis.
  • No tubule formation; loose desmoplasia; tumour cells in single file around ducts and lobules.
✚ Positive
ER – diffuse strong (>95%)PR – positiveGATA3 – positiveCK7 – positiveE-cadherin – LOST (complete absence — CDH1 loss)p120 catenin – cytoplasmic (normally membranous; redistributes when E-cadherin lost)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
E-cadherin – absent (key diagnostic marker)HER2 – usually negative (except pleomorphic variant)CK20 – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
IDC (NST)E-cadherin retained; tubule formation; nests/cords not single file; DCIS often present; no intracytoplasmic lumina
Metastatic gastric signet ring cell carcinoma↗CDX2+, CK20+, MUC5AC+; E-cadherin may also be lost; GATA3−; clinical context; no DCIS
Lymphoma⚡CD45+; lymphoid markers; no CK/GATA3; discohesive single cells in different pattern
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • E-cadherin loss: CDH1 somatic mutation + LOH (~50%); promoter methylation (~20%); β-catenin loss secondary. p120 catenin cytoplasmic redistribution is a useful surrogate.
  • Pleomorphic ILC: aggressive; apocrine/signet-ring features; Grade 3; HER2+ in subset; treat as high-grade carcinoma.
  • Peritoneal metastasis from ILC: mimics primary peritoneal carcinoma or appendiceal mucinous neoplasm — GATA3+ distinguishes.
  • Bilateral/multifocal: ILC more often bilateral than IDC; contralateral breast surveillance essential.
  • Endocrine therapy backbone: ER+ ILC responds well to aromatase inhibitors. CDK4/6 inhibitors (palbociclib/ribociclib/abemaciclib) for metastatic ER+/HER2−.
Related / See Also
↗ INVASIVE BREAST CARCINOMA OF NO SPECIAL TYPE (NST / IDC)↗ DUCTAL CARCINOMA IN SITU (DCIS)+ Add: IDC (NST)↗ Metastatic gastric signet ring cell carcinoma⚡ Lymphoma
DUCTAL CARCINOMA IN SITU (DCIS)
Breast Epithelial Glandular/mucinous Intermediate
Non-invasive malignancy — malignant epithelial cells confined to ducts/lobules without basement membrane breach. Detected on screening mammography (microcalcification) in >80%.
Clinical Context
  • Non-invasive malignancy — malignant epithelial cells confined to ducts/lobules without basement membrane breach. Detected on screening mammography (microcalcification) in >80%.
  • Risk of progression to invasive carcinoma: ~1–2% per year; lifetime risk ~30% untreated. Grade and necrosis are key determinants.
  • Treatment: wide local excision to clear margins (≥2mm for DCIS alone) ± adjuvant RT ± endocrine therapy (ER+ DCIS). Mastectomy for extensive DCIS.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Architectures: comedo (central necrosis + microcalcification — Grade 3); cribriform (punched-out spaces — Grade 1/2); micropapillary (intraluminal fronds without fibrovascular cores); solid; papillary (fibrovascular cores present).
  • Nuclear grade: Grade 1 (monomorphic, small, low N:C); Grade 2 (intermediate); Grade 3 (pleomorphic, large nuclei, prominent nucleoli). Grade is more important than architecture.
  • Microcalcification: laminated psammoma-type (low-grade) or amorphous (high-grade comedo necrosis). Van Nuys Prognostic Index: grade + necrosis + size + margins.
✚ Positive
ER – usually positive (low/intermediate grade)CK5/6 – negative (positive in usual ductal hyperplasia — key distinction)E-cadherin – positive (retained — distinguishes from lobular CIS)CK7 – positive
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CK5/6 – absent (UDH positive; ADH/DCIS negative — key benign vs malignant distinction)p63 – myoepithelial layer present (absent → invasion)SMA – myoepithelial layer present
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Usual ductal hyperplasia (UDH)CK5/6 positive (mosaic/streaming pattern); no rigid geometric spaces; irregular slit-like lumina; E-cadherin+; benign
Atypical ductal hyperplasia (ADH)Criteria for DCIS present but <2mm OR <2 complete duct spaces — same morphology, size threshold
Lobular carcinoma in situ (LCIS)E-cadherin lost; p120 cytoplasmic; cells fill/distend acini; no necrosis; Pagetoid spread; benign management (risk marker)
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • CK5/6 (and CK14): positive in UDH (streaming, mosaic) — negative in DCIS and ADH. Most useful single IHC for benign vs malignant in situ lesion.
  • Myoepithelial layer (p63/SMA/CK14/SMMHC): present in DCIS; absent → invasion. Crucial on small biopsies where invasion difficult to assess.
  • Margin reporting: distance of DCIS from inked margin in mm. ≥2mm = adequate for DCIS-only specimens (NICE/ASCO).
