  • Oncotype DX DCIS score: predicts 10-year risk of local recurrence — guides adjuvant RT decision for low-grade DCIS.
  • Paget disease: DCIS/invasive carcinoma extending along lactiferous ducts to nipple skin → Paget cells (large clear cells in epidermis). HER2+ >90%.
Related / See Also
↗ INVASIVE BREAST CARCINOMA OF NO SPECIAL TYPE (NST / IDC)↗ INVASIVE LOBULAR CARCINOMA (ILC)+ Add: Usual ductal hyperplasia (UDH)+ Add: Atypical ductal hyperplasia (ADH)+ Add: Lobular carcinoma in situ (LCIS)
FIBROADENOMA
Breast Epithelial Mixed Benign 📋 2×
Most common benign breast tumour; peak 15–35 years. Firm, rubbery, well-circumscribed, mobile ('breast mouse'). Bilateral/multiple in 10–20%. Regresses after menopause.
Clinical Context
  • Most common benign breast tumour; peak 15–35 years. Firm, rubbery, well-circumscribed, mobile ('breast mouse'). Bilateral/multiple in 10–20%. Regresses after menopause.
  • Complex fibroadenoma (cysts >3mm, sclerosing adenosis, papillary apocrine change, epithelial calcification): slight increased RR for breast cancer (~1.5×) vs simple FA.
  • Trigger: young woman, well-circumscribed mobile breast mass — U2 on USS; B2 on core biopsy = fibroadenoma.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Biphasic tumour: benign epithelial component (ducts/acini) + stromal component (hypercellular, myxoid/collagenous fibroblasts).
  • Two growth patterns: intracanalicular (stroma compresses ducts into slit-like spaces — 'leaf-like') and pericanalicular (stroma grows around open round ducts). Often mixed.
  • Epithelium: two cell layers (luminal + myoepithelial); no atypia; no mitoses. Stroma: bland spindle cells; ± myxoid change; ± hyalinisation.
✚ Positive
ER/PR – epithelial component positiveCK7 – epithelial componentp63/SMA – myoepithelial layer intactCD34 – stromal component
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
Ki67 – very lowp53 – wild-type
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Phyllodes tumour (benign)Stromal hypercellularity; stromal mitoses ≥2/10HPF; leaf-like architecture with fronds protruding into dilated ducts; stromal overgrowth; size usually >3cm
Tubular adenomaDensely packed tubules; minimal stroma; no biphasic intracanalicular/pericanalicular pattern; young women
Hamartoma (fibroadenolipoma)Admixture of fat, fibrous tissue and breast ducts; no increased stroma; pseudocapsule; US characteristic
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Phyllodes vs fibroadenoma distinction: stromal cellularity, mitoses, stromal overgrowth, and frond architecture are key. MED12 mutation in ~65% of fibroadenoma and phyllodes — not discriminating.
  • Juvenile fibroadenoma (giant FA): adolescent girls; rapid growth; >5cm; hypercellular stroma; gynecomastia-like epithelial hyperplasia. Benign.
  • Frozen section: not appropriate for breast lesions — deferred to permanent sections. B2 on core biopsy = no excision for typical FA in young woman.
  • Malignancy in fibroadenoma: very rare — LCIS/DCIS within FA not uncommon but incidental; invasive carcinoma within FA extremely rare.
  • Management: observe if U2/B2/C2 consistent. Excision if >3cm, rapidly growing, or patient preference. Vacuum-assisted biopsy emerging.
🧫 Cytology Counterpart
↗ BREAST FNA — FIVE-CATEGORY REPORTING (C1–C5)
Related / See Also
↗ INVASIVE BREAST CARCINOMA OF NO SPECIAL TYPE (NST / IDC)+ Add: Phyllodes tumour (benign)+ Add: Tubular adenoma+ Add: Hamartoma (fibroadenolipoma)
IgA NEPHROPATHY (IgAN)
Medical Renal Glomerulonephritis Nephritic Variable 📋 1×
Most common primary GN worldwide. Episodic macroscopic haematuria (synpharyngitic — concurrent with URTI, unlike post-streptococcal GN which has latent period) OR asymptomatic microscopic haematuria ± proteinuria.
Clinical Context
  • Most common primary GN worldwide. Episodic macroscopic haematuria (synpharyngitic — concurrent with URTI, unlike post-streptococcal GN which has latent period) OR asymptomatic microscopic haematuria ± proteinuria.
  • Secondary IgA: HSP/IgAV (systemic — purpura, arthralgia, abdominal pain, renal), liver cirrhosis, coeliac disease. MEST-C Oxford classification for biopsy scoring.
  • KDIGO 2021: treat with optimised supportive care first (RAS blockade, BP <130/80, SGLT2i); immunosuppression (steroids) if eGFR >30 and proteinuria >1g/day despite supportive care.
Key Microscopic Features
  • LM: mesangial hypercellularity (≥4 mesangial cells/mesangial area — M score); endocapillary hypercellularity (E); segmental sclerosis (S); tubular atrophy/interstitial fibrosis (T); crescents (C) — MEST-C Oxford classification.
  • Variable LM: may be near-normal to severe proliferative GN. Crescentic IgAN (rapidly progressive): >50% crescents → treat as RPGN.
  • IF: mesangial IgA deposition (dominant or codominant) — pathognomonic. IgG and C3 variable co-deposits. No C1q (distinguishes from lupus nephritis).
✚ Positive
IgA – mesangial dominant/codominant (IF; required for diagnosis)C3 – mesangial (variable)IgM – variable
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
C1q – absent (positive in lupus nephritis)IgG dominant – absent (IgA must be dominant/codominant)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Lupus nephritis (class II/III)↗Full house IF (IgA+IgG+IgM+C3+C1q); ANA/dsDNA+; subendothelial/subepithelial deposits on EM; clinical systemic lupus
Post-infectious GN (IgA-dominant)↗Staphylococcal-associated GN: IgA-dominant, C3+, exudative; hump deposits (EM); recent infection; older/diabetic patient
Thin basement membrane nephropathy / Alport syndrome↗Normal IF; diffuse GBM thinning on EM; Alport: COL4A3/4/5 mutation; sensorineural deafness; ocular abnormalities; basket-weave GBM
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Oxford MEST-C score: M1 (mesangial hypercellularity >50% glomeruli), E1 (endocapillary), S1 (segmental sclerosis), T1/T2 (tubular atrophy <25%/≥25%), C1/C2 (crescents <25%/≥25%). T and C predict outcome.
  • SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin/dapagliflozin): reduce proteinuria and slow progression — now standard of care in IgAN regardless of diabetes status.
  • Sparsentan (dual endothelin/angiotensin receptor antagonist): approved 2023 for IgAN — significant proteinuria reduction.
  • IgAN-specific therapy: targeted-release budesonide (Nefecon): mucosal IgA production; FDA/EMA approved 2022 for proteinuria ≥1g/day.
  • GBM thinning: EM shows mesangial electron-dense deposits (pathognomonic). Subepithelial humps → think post-infectious GN.
Related / See Also
↗ THROMBOTIC MICROANGIOPATHY (TMA) — RENAL↗ PAUCI-IMMUNE CRESCENTIC GN (ANCA-ASSOCIATED VASCULITIS)↗ Lupus nephritis (class II/III)↗ Post-infectious GN (IgA-dominant)↗ Thin basement membrane nephropathy / Alport syndrome
THROMBOTIC MICROANGIOPATHY (TMA) — RENAL
Medical Renal Vascular Thrombotic microangiopathy Variable 📋 1×
Microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia (MAHA) + thrombocytopaenia + end-organ damage (renal ± CNS). Causes: TTP (ADAMTS13 deficiency — severe <10%; autoimmune or congenital), HUS (STEC O157:H7 Shiga toxin; complement-mediated aHUS), complement-mediated aHUS, drug-induced, malignant hypertension, antiphospholipid syndrome, pregnancy (pre-eclampsia/HELLP).
Clinical Context
  • Microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia (MAHA) + thrombocytopaenia + end-organ damage (renal ± CNS). Causes: TTP (ADAMTS13 deficiency — severe <10%; autoimmune or congenital), HUS (STEC O157:H7 Shiga toxin; complement-mediated aHUS), complement-mediated aHUS, drug-induced, malignant hypertension, antiphospholipid syndrome, pregnancy (pre-eclampsia/HELLP).
  • TTP vs HUS: ADAMTS13 activity — <10% = TTP (treat with plasma exchange + rituximab); >10% = aHUS/other (treat with eculizumab if complement-mediated). STEC-HUS: supportive only, no antibiotics.
  • Cryoglobulinaemia-associated TMA: Type I (monoclonal — myeloma/lymphoma); Type II (mixed monoclonal IgM + polyclonal IgG — HCV most common); Type III (polyclonal — connective tissue disease).
Key Microscopic Features
  • Acute TMA: fibrin/platelet thrombi in glomerular capillaries and arterioles; endothelial swelling (glomerular capillary lumen obliteration); mesangiolysis (dissolution of mesangial matrix).
  • Chronic TMA: GBM duplication ('double contour' — new basement membrane laid down underneath); arterial intimal fibrosis ('onion-skin' — mucoid intimal expansion); obsolescent glomeruli.
  • Cryoglobulinaemic GN: membranoproliferative pattern + intraluminal cryoprecipitates ('pseudothrombi' — eosinophilic PAS-positive intraluminal deposits); wire-loop pattern; massive monocyte infiltration.
✚ Positive
IF: fibrin – thrombi in acute TMAIgM – pseudothrombi in cryoglobulinaemic GNC3 – mesangial/subendothelial in cryoglobulinaemic GNComplement factors (C4d, C1q) – complement-mediated aHUS
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
ADAMTS13 – activity assay (not IHC) — <10% = TTPAnti-GBM – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Lupus nephritis (class IV)↗Full-house IF; ANA/dsDNA+; wire loops; no mesangiolysis; no platelet thrombi; MAHA absent
Malignant hypertension↗'Onion-skin' arteriopathy; fibrinoid necrosis of arterioles; BP markedly elevated; no MAHA; TMA pattern but cause is hypertension
Anti-GBM disease↗Linear IgG on GBM (IF); crescentic GN; no thrombi; anti-GBM antibodies; ANCA may coexist (double-positive)
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • ADAMTS13 assay: <10% activity = TTP — medical emergency. PEX (plasma exchange) + rituximab; caplacizumab (anti-vWF nanobody) for acute TTP.
  • Complement-mediated aHUS: CFH/CFI/C3/CFB mutations or anti-CFH antibodies; eculizumab (anti-C5 monoclonal) — life-changing treatment; ravulizumab (long-acting).
  • STEC-HUS: no antibiotics (worsen Shiga toxin release); supportive care; dialysis if needed. Most recover renal function.
  • Cryoglobulinaemia: HCV treatment (DAAs — sofosbuvir/daclatasvir) cures Type II cryoglobulinaemia; rituximab for refractory.
  • EM in TMA: subendothelial lucent expansion (cleared deposits); endothelial swelling; fibrin tactoids in thrombi.
Related / See Also
↗ IgA NEPHROPATHY (IgAN)↗ PAUCI-IMMUNE CRESCENTIC GN (ANCA-ASSOCIATED VASCULITIS)↗ Lupus nephritis (class IV)↗ Malignant hypertension↗ Anti-GBM disease
PAUCI-IMMUNE CRESCENTIC GN (ANCA-ASSOCIATED VASCULITIS)
Medical Renal Glomerulonephritis RPGN Malignant 📋 1×
Rapidly progressive GN (RPGN): acute renal failure + active urinary sediment (RBC casts, dysmorphic RBCs, proteinuria). Most common cause of RPGN in adults. ANCA: MPO-ANCA (p-ANCA, microscopic polyangiitis/eosinophilic GPA) or PR3-ANCA (c-ANCA, GPA/Wegener's).
Clinical Context
  • Rapidly progressive GN (RPGN): acute renal failure + active urinary sediment (RBC casts, dysmorphic RBCs, proteinuria). Most common cause of RPGN in adults. ANCA: MPO-ANCA (p-ANCA, microscopic polyangiitis/eosinophilic GPA) or PR3-ANCA (c-ANCA, GPA/Wegener's).
  • GPA (granulomatosis with polyangiitis): upper/lower respiratory + renal; PR3-ANCA; c-ANCA; nasal destruction; cavitating lung lesions. MPA (microscopic polyangiitis): MPO-ANCA; renal predominant. EGPA (eosinophilic GPA/Churg-Strauss): asthma + eosinophilia + MPO-ANCA; pANCA.
  • Treat immediately: IV methylprednisolone + cyclophosphamide (or rituximab). Avacopan (C5a receptor inhibitor) adjunct — steroid-sparing. Plasma exchange if anti-GBM coexistent or severe renal failure.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Crescentic GN: >50% glomeruli with crescents (cellular → fibrocellular → fibrous). Crescents = parietal epithelial cell + monocyte/macrophage proliferation filling Bowman's space — response to fibrin leak from GBM rupture.
  • Pauci-immune: minimal or absent immune deposits on IF (<2+ staining) — the defining feature. Fibrinoid necrosis of glomerular tufts; segmental necrosis.
  • Granulomas in GPA: necrotising granulomas in lung/upper airway (NOT kidney — renal biopsy in GPA shows pauci-immune crescentic GN without granulomas).
✚ Positive
Fibrin – crescents and necrosis on IFCD68 – macrophages in crescents
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
IgG/IgA/IgM/C3/C1q – absent or trace (<2+) — pauci-immune pattern (key defining feature)Anti-GBM – linear IgG absent (coexistence possible — double-positive: ~10%)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Anti-GBM disease (Goodpasture)↗Linear IgG on GBM (IF); anti-GBM antibodies (serum); pulmonary haemorrhage; no ANCA (usually); crescentic GN identical LM
Lupus nephritis (class IV)↗Full-house IF; ANA/dsDNA+; wire loops; subendothelial deposits (EM); crescents possible but IF positive
IgA nephropathy (crescentic)↗IgA-dominant IF; mesangial deposits EM; ANCA negative; haematuria history; MEST-C score
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Three causes of crescentic GN (RPGN): (1) Pauci-immune/ANCA (most common, ~60%); (2) Anti-GBM/Goodpasture (~20%); (3) Immune complex (lupus, IgAN, post-infectious, ~20%). IF pattern discriminates.
  • PR3-ANCA (c-ANCA): GPA; relapse-prone; rituximab preferred for induction and maintenance. MPO-ANCA (p-ANCA): MPA/EGPA; more fibrotic progression; cyclophosphamide comparable to rituximab.
  • Avacopan (ADVOCATE trial): complement C5a receptor inhibitor; non-inferior to high-dose prednisolone for remission; superior for renal recovery; approved as steroid-sparing adjunct.
  • Renal biopsy in ANCA-GN: cellular crescents → reversible (treat aggressively); fibrous crescents → irreversible glomerular loss.
  • Double-positive (ANCA + anti-GBM): ~10% of anti-GBM disease; treat as anti-GBM (plasma exchange mandatory); ANCA may drive relapse after anti-GBM titres fall.
Related / See Also
↗ IgA NEPHROPATHY (IgAN)↗ THROMBOTIC MICROANGIOPATHY (TMA) — RENAL↗ Anti-GBM disease (Goodpasture)↗ Lupus nephritis (class IV)↗ IgA nephropathy (crescentic)
MINIMAL CHANGE DISEASE (MCD)
Medical Renal Glomerulonephritis Nephrotic Variable 📋 1×
Most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in children (>90%); ~20% in adults. Sudden onset: heavy proteinuria (>3.5g/day), hypoalbuminaemia, oedema, hyperlipidaemia/lipiduria.
Clinical Context
  • Most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in children (>90%); ~20% in adults. Sudden onset: heavy proteinuria (>3.5g/day), hypoalbuminaemia, oedema, hyperlipidaemia/lipiduria.
  • Secondary MCD: NSAIDs, lithium, lymphoma (Hodgkin — paraneoplastic; IL-13/IL-4 from Reed-Sternberg cells), HIV. Adults: always exclude secondary cause.
  • Exquisite steroid response (>95% children): prednisolone 60mg/m²/day. Relapsing: cyclophosphamide, calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus/ciclosporin), rituximab.
Key Microscopic Features
  • LM: essentially normal glomeruli — no hypercellularity, no GBM thickening, no deposits. Proximal tubular lipid droplets (lipoid nephrosis — lipid reabsorbed from filtered lipoproteins).
  • IF: negative — no immune deposits (pauci-immune at IF level, distinguishing from FSGS variants and membranous nephropathy).
  • EM: diffuse effacement (fusion) of podocyte foot processes — pathognomonic. Podocyte cell body swollen. No electron-dense deposits. GBM normal thickness.
✚ Positive
N/A — LM near-normal; IF negative
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
IgG/IgA/IgM/C3/C1q – negative on IFPLA2R – negative (positive in primary membranous)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS)↗Segmental sclerosis + hyalinosis in some glomeruli (LM); may require serial sections/deeper cut; EM foot process effacement (shared); steroid resistance more common
Membranous nephropathy↗GBM thickening (LM); spike pattern (silver stain); granular IgG+C3 on IF; subepithelial deposits (EM); PLA2R+ (~70%)
Diabetic nephropathy (early)↗GBM thickening; mesangial expansion; Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules later; clinical context (DM); no foot process effacement on EM early
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Podocyte foot process effacement on EM: the diagnostic finding — complete effacement (>80% of capillary loops) in MCD. Partial effacement in FSGS or other podocytopathies.
  • FSGS vs MCD on biopsy: sampling — if only a few glomeruli sampled, segmental sclerosis may be missed. Adequate biopsy: ≥10 glomeruli by cortex.
  • T-cell dysregulation hypothesis: circulating permeability factor (not yet identified) from dysregulated T cells damages podocytes — explains steroid/rituximab response.
  • Adults with MCD: higher relapse rate; more frequent steroid dependence; rituximab increasingly used for frequent relapses.
  • Hodgkin lymphoma-associated MCD: resolves with lymphoma treatment — paraneoplastic via IL-13 from RS cells.
Related / See Also
↗ FOCAL SEGMENTAL GLOMERULOSCLEROSIS (FSGS)↗ MEMBRANOUS NEPHROPATHY (MN)↗ Focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS)↗ Membranous nephropathy↗ Diabetic nephropathy (early)
FOCAL SEGMENTAL GLOMERULOSCLEROSIS (FSGS)
Medical Renal Glomerulonephritis Nephrotic Variable
Nephrotic syndrome in adults; proteinuria often >5g/day; may present with nephritic features. Primary FSGS: circulating permeability factor (FSGS factor — not yet identified); steroid-resistant in ~50%.
Clinical Context
  • Nephrotic syndrome in adults; proteinuria often >5g/day; may present with nephritic features. Primary FSGS: circulating permeability factor (FSGS factor — not yet identified); steroid-resistant in ~50%.
  • Secondary FSGS (adaptive/maladaptive): hyperfiltration (single kidney, obesity, diabetes, reflux nephropathy) — perihilar variant; HIV (collapsing variant, direct podocyte infection); heroin; anabolic steroids.
  • Genetic FSGS: podocin (NPHS2), nephrin (NPHS1), α-actinin-4 (ACTN4), WT1, INF2 — test in young onset, steroid-resistant, familial. No response to immunosuppression.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Focal (some glomeruli affected) and segmental (part of glomerular tuft): sclerosis (obliteration of capillary loops by matrix) + hyalinosis (protein/lipid deposits).
  • Columbia classification variants: NOS (most common); perihilar (maladaptive — near hilum); cellular (endocapillary hypercellularity); tip lesion (at glomerular tubular junction — best prognosis, steroid-responsive); collapsing (HIV/COVID-19/pamidronate — worst prognosis, podocyte hypertrophy + collapse).
  • Background: tubular atrophy, interstitial fibrosis in proportion to sclerosis. EM: foot process effacement (focal or diffuse per variant).
✚ Positive
IgM/C3 – non-specific trapping in sclerotic segments (IF)CD68 – foam cells in sclerotic segments
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
IgG – absent (primary FSGS; not immune complex-mediated)PLA2R – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Minimal change disease↗LM normal; no sclerosis; IF negative; EM diffuse foot process effacement; steroid-sensitive; serial sections may reveal FSGS
Diabetic nephropathy (nodular)↗Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules; diffuse GBM thickening; mesangial expansion; exudative lesions; clinical DM; no Columbia variant classification
Collapsing FSGS vs MCD in HIVHIV-associated nephropathy (HIVAN): collapsing variant + tubuloreticular inclusions (EM); HIV treatment resolves
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Collapsing FSGS: worst prognosis; APOL1 high-risk genotype (G1/G2 alleles) in African-ancestry patients — genetic predisposition to collapsing FSGS from HIV, COVID-19, IFN.
  • APOL1: G1 (S342G/I384M) and G2 (del N388/Y389) alleles — 2 risk alleles → 17× increased risk of FSGS/HIVAN. Now recommended to test in all African-ancestry patients with FSGS.
  • Tip lesion: best prognosis variant — behaves like MCD; steroid-responsive; near glomerulotubular junction.
  • Recurrence post-transplant: primary FSGS recurs in ~30% of allografts (circulating permeability factor); plasmapheresis ± rituximab. Genetic FSGS does NOT recur.
  • Columbia classification must be reported — variant determines prognosis and treatment.
Related / See Also
↗ MINIMAL CHANGE DISEASE (MCD)↗ MEMBRANOUS NEPHROPATHY (MN)↗ Minimal change disease↗ Diabetic nephropathy (nodular)+ Add: Collapsing FSGS vs MCD in HIV
MEMBRANOUS NEPHROPATHY (MN)
Medical Renal Glomerulonephritis Nephrotic Variable 📋 1×
Most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in non-diabetic Caucasian adults (>60 years, M>F). Insidious onset; heavy proteinuria; DVT/PE risk (loss of antithrombin III).
Clinical Context
  • Most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in non-diabetic Caucasian adults (>60 years, M>F). Insidious onset; heavy proteinuria; DVT/PE risk (loss of antithrombin III).
  • Primary (~75%): anti-PLA2R (phospholipase A2 receptor) antibodies — target podocyte antigen PLA2R1. Anti-THSD7A (~5% of PLA2R-negative). Secondary: malignancy (lung, colon — paraneoplastic), SLE (class V), drugs (gold, penicillamine, NSAIDs), HBV/HCV, sarcoidosis.
  • Rule of thirds (untreated): 1/3 spontaneous remission; 1/3 stable proteinuria; 1/3 progressive CKD. Treat if persistent high proteinuria, declining eGFR, or thrombosis.
Key Microscopic Features
  • LM early: normal or subtle GBM thickening. LM established: GBM thickening with 'spike' pattern on silver stain (basement membrane projecting between subepithelial deposits — 'spike and dome').
  • Stages (Jones silver): Stage I — subepithelial deposits, no spikes; II — spikes between deposits; III — deposits encorporated into GBM; IV — late, GBM sclerosis.
  • IF: granular IgG + C3 along GBM (capillary loop pattern) — IgG4 predominant (primary) vs IgG1-3 (secondary/lupus). PLA2R staining in glomeruli.
✚ Positive
IgG – granular capillary loop (IF)C3 – granular (IF)PLA2R – positive in glomeruli (primary MN; ~75%)IgG4 – predominant subclass (primary MN)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
IgA – absent or traceC1q – negative (positive in lupus MN — class V)IgG1-3 predominant – primary MN; IgG1 dominant → secondary/lupus
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Lupus nephritis class V (membranous lupus)↗Full-house IF (IgA+IgG+IgM+C3+C1q); mesangial deposits also present; IgG1/3 predominant; ANA/dsDNA+; PLA2R negative
Secondary MN (malignancy-associated)PLA2R negative; IgG1/IgG2 predominant; mesangial deposits ± on EM; older patient; new diagnosis malignancy
MPGN pattern (cryoglobulinaemia)↗Subendothelial deposits; GBM duplication; intraluminal pseudothrombi; IgM dominant; HCV association
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Anti-PLA2R titre: correlates with disease activity; monitor for treatment response (titre should fall before proteinuria improves — 6–12 month lag). Remission: undetectable PLA2R + proteinuria <0.3g/day.
  • Treatment (Kidney Disease: Improving Global Outcomes 2021): cyclophosphamide + alternating steroids (Ponticelli regimen) OR rituximab (anti-CD20). Rituximab now preferred (non-inferior, better safety).
  • Obinutuzumab: anti-CD20 (type II); superior to rituximab in Phase 2 trial (GEMRITUX2) — emerging.
  • Anti-THSD7A MN: 5% of PLA2R-negative MN; paraneoplastic association higher than PLA2R-positive — always exclude malignancy.
  • IgG subclass: IgG4 dominance = primary MN; IgG1 dominance = secondary/lupus. Subclass staining on biopsy guides aetiology.
Related / See Also
↗ MINIMAL CHANGE DISEASE (MCD)↗ FOCAL SEGMENTAL GLOMERULOSCLEROSIS (FSGS)↗ Lupus nephritis class V (membranous lupus)+ Add: Secondary MN (malignancy-associated)↗ MPGN pattern (cryoglobulinaemia)
DIABETIC NEPHROPATHY (NODULAR GLOMERULOSCLEROSIS)
Medical Renal Glomerulonephritis Nephrotic Variable 📋 1×
Most common cause of end-stage renal disease (ESRD) worldwide. Progressive: microalbuminuria (30–300mg/day) → macroalbuminuria (>300mg/day) → nephrotic syndrome → CKD → ESRD over 10–20 years.
Clinical Context
  • Most common cause of end-stage renal disease (ESRD) worldwide. Progressive: microalbuminuria (30–300mg/day) → macroalbuminuria (>300mg/day) → nephrotic syndrome → CKD → ESRD over 10–20 years.
  • Usually occurs >10 years after DM onset. Presents with proteinuria ± hypertension ± retinopathy (diabetic triopathy: nephropathy + retinopathy + neuropathy — if retinopathy present, biopsy often not needed).
  • Biopsy indicated if: atypical features (haematuria, rapid onset, no retinopathy, short DM duration, other systemic disease) — to exclude coexistent GN.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Diffuse: GBM thickening (PAS/silver); mesangial expansion (increased matrix). Nodular (Kimmelstiel-Wilson lesions): pathognomonic — PAS-positive acellular nodules expanding mesangium; peripheral GBM; 'capsular drop' and 'fibrin cap' exudative lesions.
  • Arteriolar hyalinosis: afferent AND efferent arterioles (efferent hyalinosis pathognomonic of diabetes vs other causes of hyaline arteriosclerosis which only affect afferent).
  • Armanni-Ebstein lesion (glycogen accumulation in tubular epithelium — PAS+, diastase-labile) — historical finding, rarely seen now with better glycaemic control.
✚ Positive
IgG – linear GBM staining (non-specific, collagen IV-related — not immune complex)Albumin – mesangial trapping (non-specific)Collagen IV – increased GBM and mesangial matrix
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
IF: IgA/IgM/C3/C1q – absent (non-immune complex GN)PLA2R – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Amyloidosis (AL/AA)Congo red apple-green birefringence; PAS-negative amyloid deposits (unlike Kimmelstiel-Wilson); SAA/kappa/lambda IF; EM: non-branching fibrils 8–10nm
Monoclonal immunoglobulin deposition disease (MIDD)Linear IF (kappa/lambda); GBM/TBM/mesangial deposits; no birefringence; EM: granular non-fibrillar deposits; paraprotein
MPGN pattern↗GBM duplication; subendothelial deposits; complement activation; haematuria; not mesangial nodular pattern
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules: pathognomonic for diabetic nephropathy — acellular PAS-positive mesangial nodules. May also be seen in amyloid (Congo red+) and MIDD (IF positive for immunoglobulin).
  • Efferent arteriolar hyalinosis: pathognomonic for diabetes (vs hypertension which only affects afferent arteriole).
  • SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin/dapagliflozin): reduce proteinuria, slow CKD progression, reduce cardiovascular events — first-line alongside RAS blockade.
  • Finerenone (non-steroidal MRA): reduces CKD progression and cardiovascular events in DKD — FIDELIO/FIGARO trials.
  • Renal biopsy in DM: atypical presentation is the indication — haematuria, rapid decline, absence of retinopathy all warrant biopsy to exclude IgAN, lupus, ANCA, membranous coexisting with DKD.
Related / See Also
↗ MEMBRANOUS NEPHROPATHY (MN)↗ FOCAL SEGMENTAL GLOMERULOSCLEROSIS (FSGS)+ Add: Amyloidosis (AL/AA)+ Add: Monoclonal immunoglobulin deposition disease (MIDD)↗ MPGN pattern
ACUTE TUBULOINTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS (ATIN)
Medical Renal Tubulointerstitial Tubulointerstitial Variable 📋 2×
Acute kidney injury + eosinophilia + rash + fever (classic triad — present in <30% of drug-induced ATIN). Urine: eosinophiluria (Hansel stain), WBC casts, mild proteinuria (<1g/day — distinguishes from glomerular disease).
Clinical Context
  • Acute kidney injury + eosinophilia + rash + fever (classic triad — present in <30% of drug-induced ATIN). Urine: eosinophiluria (Hansel stain), WBC casts, mild proteinuria (<1g/day — distinguishes from glomerular disease).
  • Causes: drugs (most common — NSAIDs, PPIs, beta-lactam antibiotics, sulfonamides, rifampicin, allopurinol, immune checkpoint inhibitors); infection (leptospirosis, CMV, EBV, hantavirus); immune (SLE, sarcoidosis, Sjögren, IgG4-RD, TINU syndrome).
  • Trigger: AKI in patient taking NSAIDs/PPIs/antibiotics → ATIN until proven otherwise. Withdraw offending drug before biopsy if possible.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Interstitial oedema + lymphocytic infiltrate (predominantly T cells + eosinophils); plasma cells in IgG4-related ATIN; neutrophils in infection-associated.
  • Tubulitis: lymphocytes within tubular epithelium (between tubular cells and basement membrane) — cardinal feature. Tubular epithelial injury: flattening, vacuolation, apoptosis.
  • IgG4-related TIN: storiform fibrosis + IgG4+ plasma cells >10/HPF + obliterative phlebitis (if present) + IgG4/IgG ratio >40% — hallmark.
✚ Positive
CD3/CD4/CD8 – T-lymphocytes in interstitiumIgG4 – >10/HPF in IgG4-related ATINIgG4/IgG ratio – >40% in IgG4-related
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
IgG/IgA/IgM – IF negative (no immune complex deposition)Anti-GBM – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Chronic tubulointerstitial nephritisTubular atrophy; interstitial fibrosis; mononuclear infiltrate less acute; cause: analgesic nephropathy, reflux, urate, myeloma cast nephropathy
IgG4-related TIN>10 IgG4+ plasma cells/HPF; storiform fibrosis; obliterative phlebitis; IgG4 serum elevated; steroid-responsive; often bilateral renal enlargement
Infection-associated TIN (pyelonephritis)Neutrophilic infiltrate; bacteria on culture; WBC casts + bacteriuria; fever; loin pain; ascending infection
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • ICI-associated ATIN (checkpoint inhibitor nephritis): CD8+ T-cell predominant; anti-PD1/CTLA4 therapy; steroids (prednisolone 1mg/kg) ± mycophenolate if refractory; hold or discontinue ICI.
  • TINU syndrome (tubulointerstitial nephritis + uveitis): young women; bilateral anterior uveitis; ATIN; elevated urinary β2-microglobulin; steroid-responsive.
  • Steroid treatment: withdraw offending drug first. Prednisolone 1mg/kg for 4–8 weeks if no recovery after 1–2 weeks drug withdrawal. Complete recovery in ~70%.
  • Eosinophiluria (Hansel stain): >1% eosinophils — supports drug-induced ATIN but not sensitive (~30%). Urine β2-microglobulin + NAG (tubular damage markers) more sensitive.
  • Sarcoid-associated ATIN: granulomas in interstitium (non-caseating); serum ACE elevated; hypercalcaemia; treat with steroids; screen for pulmonary/systemic sarcoid.
Related / See Also
↗ IgA NEPHROPATHY (IgAN)+ Add: Chronic tubulointerstitial nephritis+ Add: IgG4-related TIN+ Add: Infection-associated TIN (pyelonephritis)
ANTI-GBM DISEASE (GOODPASTURE SYNDROME)
Medical Renal Glomerulonephritis Crescentic Malignant
Type II hypersensitivity — autoantibodies against the α3 chain of type IV collagen (NC1 domain of COL4A3) in the GBM and alveolar basement membrane. Bimodal age distribution: young men (20s–30s) and older women (60s–70s).
Clinical Context
  • Type II hypersensitivity — autoantibodies against the α3 chain of type IV collagen (NC1 domain of COL4A3) in the GBM and alveolar basement membrane. Bimodal age distribution: young men (20s–30s) and older women (60s–70s).
  • Pulmonary-renal syndrome (Goodpasture syndrome): rapidly progressive GN + pulmonary haemorrhage. Renal-limited anti-GBM disease occurs without lung involvement (~30%).
  • Trigger: rapidly progressive renal failure + haemoptysis + linear IgG on IF → anti-GBM. Up to 30% ANCA positive (MPO > PR3) — double-positive confers worse prognosis. Serology: anti-GBM ELISA (sensitive and specific).
  • Treatment: emergency plasmapheresis (remove circulating antibody) + pulse IV methylprednisolone + cyclophosphamide. Renal outcome correlates with creatinine at presentation and % crescents on biopsy.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Necrotising crescentic glomerulonephritis: cellular crescents (parietal epithelial cells + infiltrating monocytes filling Bowman's space) ± fibrous crescents in later biopsies.
  • Glomerular tuft necrosis (fibrinoid necrosis): segmental or global — key LM finding.
  • Minimal mesangial hypercellularity and endocapillary proliferation — unlike immune complex GN.
  • Interstitial inflammation with tubulitis; tubular red cell casts. In end-stage: >50% fibrous crescents = dialysis-dependent, unlikely to recover renal function.
✚ Positive
IgG – linear along GBM (pathognomonic — differentiates from immune complex granular and pauci-immune negative patterns)C3 – linear (variable)IgA / IgM – linear (minor, variable)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
EM: NO discrete electron-dense deposits (anti-GBM antibody is GBM-bound; not visible as deposits — distinguishes from immune complex GN)ANCA – negative in pure anti-GBM (positive in ~30% double-positive disease)ANA/anti-dsDNA – negative (excludes lupus nephritis)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Pauci-immune crescentic GN (ANCA)↗IF negative (no IgG/C3); ANCA positive (MPO or PR3); same crescentic LM; no linear IgG; no anti-GBM antibody
Lupus nephritis (Class IV)↗Full house IF (IgG+IgA+IgM+C3+C1q); granular not linear deposits; anti-dsDNA+; endocapillary proliferation; wire loops; subendothelial deposits on EM
IgA Nephropathy (crescentic)↗IgA dominant mesangial deposits (not linear); granular IF; anti-GBM negative; ANCA negative; history of macroscopic haematuria post-URTI
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • IF pattern is pathognomonic: LINEAR IgG along GBM is essentially diagnostic of anti-GBM disease. Granular = immune complex. Negative = pauci-immune. This three-way IF classification of crescentic GN is core exam knowledge.
  • Lung haemorrhage trigger: smoking, pulmonary infection, cocaine, hydrocarbon exposure (all cause alveolar injury exposing alveolar BM antigen). Non-smokers rarely get pulmonary haemorrhage.
  • Creatinine threshold: if creatinine >600µmol/L or dialysis-dependent with >50% fibrous crescents at presentation — renal recovery unlikely; plasmapheresis still indicated to protect lungs.
  • Double-positive (anti-GBM + ANCA): treat both. Worse renal prognosis than ANCA alone. After anti-GBM titres clear, manage as AAV for relapse prevention.
  • Exam trap: do NOT confuse with ANCA vasculitis on IF — pauci-immune has NO IF staining. Anti-GBM has LINEAR IgG. Immune complex has GRANULAR staining.
Related / See Also
↗ PAUCI-IMMUNE CRESCENTIC GN (ANCA-ASSOCIATED VASCULITIS)↗ IgA NEPHROPATHY (IgAN)↗ Pauci-immune crescentic GN (ANCA)↗ Lupus nephritis (Class IV)↗ IgA Nephropathy (crescentic)
LUPUS NEPHRITIS (ISN/RPS CLASSIFICATION)
Medical Renal Glomerulonephritis Variable (class-dependent) Malignant
Renal involvement in SLE: present in ~50%. Most common in young women of Afro-Caribbean descent. Can present as any glomerular syndrome depending on class.
Clinical Context
  • Renal involvement in SLE: present in ~50%. Most common in young women of Afro-Caribbean descent. Can present as any glomerular syndrome depending on class.
  • ISN/RPS 2003 classification (class I–VI): guides treatment intensity. Class III and IV = proliferative → aggressive immunosuppression. Class V = membranous → nephrotic. Class VI = advanced sclerosing → no treatment benefit.
  • Serology: anti-dsDNA titres correlate with disease activity; complement consumption (↓C3/C4) = active LN. Anti-Sm, anti-Ro/La, antiphospholipid antibodies. ANA positive in >95% SLE.
  • Renal biopsy indication: new proteinuria >500mg/day, unexplained haematuria, rising creatinine, or change in clinical status. Class determines treatment: mycophenolate or cyclophosphamide for III/IV; ACEi for V.
Key Microscopic Features
  • CLASS I (Minimal mesangial): normal LM; mesangial deposits on IF/EM only.
  • CLASS II (Mesangial proliferative): mesangial hypercellularity ± matrix expansion; mesangial deposits on IF; no endocapillary proliferation.
  • CLASS III (Focal, <50% glomeruli): segmental endocapillary/extracapillary proliferation, necrosis, or sclerosis in <50% of glomeruli; subendothelial ± mesangial deposits.
  • CLASS IV (Diffuse, ≥50% glomeruli): endocapillary proliferation in ≥50% of glomeruli. Wire loops (massive subendothelial deposits thickening GBM — pathognomonic). Hyaline thrombi (intraluminal protein deposits). Fibrinoid necrosis. Cellular crescents. Global (IVG) or segmental (IVS) involvement.
  • CLASS V (Membranous LN): subepithelial deposits (like idiopathic MN) ± mesangial deposits. Concurrent Class III/IV must be recorded (III+V, IV+V).
  • CLASS VI (Advanced sclerosing): >90% glomeruli globally sclerosed; end-stage; no treatment benefit.
✚ Positive
FULL HOUSE IF — IgG + IgA + IgM + C3 + C1q + C4: pathognomonic combination; virtually diagnostic of lupus when all six presentC1q – positive (unique among GN; C1q activation indicates classic pathway activation; absent in C3G and most other GN)IgG – subclass IgG1 and IgG3 predominant
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
EM: mesangial + subendothelial deposits (Class III/IV); subepithelial deposits (Class V); fingerprint substructure (tubuloreticular inclusions in endothelial cells — characteristic of interferon-driven disease in SLE)Anti-GBM – negative (no linear IgG pattern)ANCA – negative in pure LN (positive in rare overlap with AAV)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Membranous nephropathy (Class V LN vs idiopathic MN)↗Idiopathic MN: anti-PLA2R+ (~70%), subepithelial deposits only, no mesangial deposits, no full house IF, no C1q. Class V LN: mesangial deposits present, full house IF, ANA/anti-dsDNA+
MPGN / C3 GlomerulopathyC3G: C3 dominant IF (not full house); no C1q; complement pathway mutations/antibodies; no anti-dsDNA. Immune complex MPGN: full house possible but infection/cryoglobulin history; no ANA
Anti-GBM disease↗Linear IgG (not granular); no full house; anti-GBM antibody; no anti-dsDNA; crescentic with minimal proliferation
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • 'Full house' IF (IgG+IgA+IgM+C3+C1q+C4) is virtually diagnostic of lupus nephritis — no other GN shows all six consistently. Memorise this combination.
  • Wire loops (Class IV): massive subendothelial deposits thicken capillary wall on LM without cellular hypercellularity — appear as rigid wire-like contours; PAS positive; pathognomonic for Class IV LN.
  • Tubuloreticular inclusions (TRI) on EM: endoplasmic reticulum inclusions in endothelial cells (resembling myxovirus); seen in SLE and HIV nephropathy; driven by interferon-α excess.
  • Activity vs chronicity indices (NIH): activity — endocapillary proliferation, karyorrhexis, wire loops, hyaline thrombi, fibrinoid necrosis, cellular crescents. Chronicity — fibrous crescents, tubular atrophy, interstitial fibrosis, glomerular sclerosis. Guides prognosis.
  • Exam trap: always report concurrent classes (e.g. Class IV+V = worst prognosis, most aggressive treatment). Pure Class V LN = manageable with ACEi ± mycophenolate; IV+V = treat as Class IV.
Related / See Also
↗ MEMBRANOUS NEPHROPATHY (MN)↗ PAUCI-IMMUNE CRESCENTIC GN (ANCA-ASSOCIATED VASCULITIS)↗ ANTI-GBM DISEASE (GOODPASTURE SYNDROME)↗ Membranous nephropathy (Class V LN vs idiopathic MN)+ Add: MPGN / C3 Glomerulopathy↗ Anti-GBM disease
MPGN PATTERN / C3 GLOMERULOPATHY (C3G)
Medical Renal Glomerulonephritis Membranoproliferative Malignant
MPGN is a morphological LM pattern (not a single disease). Defined by: mesangial and endocapillary hypercellularity + GBM double contours ('tram-tracks') + lobular accentuation. Classification is IF/EM-based.
Clinical Context
  • MPGN is a morphological LM pattern (not a single disease). Defined by: mesangial and endocapillary hypercellularity + GBM double contours ('tram-tracks') + lobular accentuation. Classification is IF/EM-based.
  • IF-based classification: (1) Immune complex-mediated MPGN — IgG/IgM/C3/C1q (secondary: HCV, HBV, endocarditis, cryoglobulinaemia, MGUS/paraprotein, autoimmune); (2) C3 Glomerulopathy (C3G) — alternative complement pathway dysregulation.
  • C3G subclassification by EM: Dense Deposit Disease (DDD) — ribbon-like lamina densa deposits; C3 Glomerulonephritis (C3GN) — mesangial/subendothelial/subepithelial deposits without DDD pattern. Both C3 dominant on IF (≥2+ above any Ig).
  • Investigations for C3G: complement pathway screen — C3/C4 levels (↓C3, normal C4 = alternative pathway); C3 nephritic factor (C3NeF; stabilises C3 convertase); anti-CFH antibodies; genetic panel (CFH, CFI, C3, CFB, CFHR5).
Key Microscopic Features
  • Mesangial and endocapillary hypercellularity — increased mesangial matrix and cells; endocapillary leucocyte infiltration.
  • GBM double contours ('tram-tracks') on PAS/silver stain: mesangial cell interposition into GBM creates duplicated basement membrane layers — pathognomonic for MPGN pattern.
  • Lobular accentuation: exaggerated lobular architecture of glomerular tuft.
  • Variable crescents (especially in immune complex-mediated disease). Hyaline deposits may be visible in cryoglobulinaemic MPGN (intraluminal cryoglobulin 'plugs').
✚ Positive
Immune complex MPGN: IgG + IgM + C3 + C1q (full house possible) — granular mesangial + subendothelial depositsC3G: C3 dominant (C3 ≥2+ stronger than IgG/IgM/IgA); IgG/IgM absent or traceCryoglobulinaemic MPGN: IgM κ or λ restriction (Type II cryoglobulin); rheumatoid factor positive
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
DDD on EM: highly electron-dense ribbon-like deposits within GBM lamina densa (sausage-shaped, replacing lamina densa) — pathognomonic; not seen on LM or IFC3GN on EM: mesangial + subendothelial ± subepithelial deposits (not ribbon-like)Immune complex MPGN on EM: subendothelial + mesangial deposits (electron dense but not ribbon-like)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Lupus Nephritis (Class IV)↗Full house IF (IgG+IgA+IgM+C3+C1q); ANA+/anti-dsDNA+; wire loops; tubuloreticular inclusions on EM; clinical SLE diagnosis
Diabetic Nephropathy↗No immune deposits on IF; nodular glomerulosclerosis (Kimmelstiel-Wilson); GBM thickening without double contours; clinical diabetes
Thrombotic Microangiopathy↗Double contours in TMA are due to endothelial swelling/expansion, not mesangial interposition; pauci-immune IF; fibrin/platelet thrombi; clinical TMA (MAHA + thrombocytopenia)
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • The MPGN pattern on LM always requires IF and EM to classify — 'MPGN' alone is not a complete diagnosis. Always classify as immune complex-mediated or C3G.
  • DDD (Dense Deposit Disease): the EM finding of ribbon-like lamina densa deposits is pathognomonic. Most patients have C3NeF. Recurs in ~100% of renal transplants.
  • C3G workup: always screen for underlying MGUS/paraproteinaemia (monoclonal protein activates complement), infection (HCV, endocarditis), and autoimmune disease. Genetic panel in younger patients.
  • Treatment: immune complex MPGN — treat underlying cause (antiviral for HCV; antibiotics for endocarditis; chemotherapy for MGUS-driven). C3G — complement inhibitors (eculizumab) for severe disease; mycophenolate for moderate.
  • Exam trap: C3 alone on IF does not mean C3G — normal repair after any GN can show residual C3. C3G requires C3 to be ≥2+ stronger than any immunoglobulin, AND the MPGN pattern to be present.
Related / See Also
↗ LUPUS NEPHRITIS (ISN/RPS CLASSIFICATION)↗ MEMBRANOUS NEPHROPATHY (MN)↗ THROMBOTIC MICROANGIOPATHY (TMA) — RENAL↗ Lupus Nephritis (Class IV)↗ Diabetic Nephropathy↗ Thrombotic Microangiopathy
LIGHT CHAIN CAST NEPHROPATHY (MYELOMA KIDNEY)
Medical Renal Tubulointerstitial Tubular cast Malignant
Most common cause of renal failure in multiple myeloma (~50% of myeloma-associated renal disease). Caused by tubular precipitation of monoclonal free light chains (Bence Jones protein) with Tamm-Horsfall glycoprotein.
Clinical Context
  • Most common cause of renal failure in multiple myeloma (~50% of myeloma-associated renal disease). Caused by tubular precipitation of monoclonal free light chains (Bence Jones protein) with Tamm-Horsfall glycoprotein.
  • Presentation: AKI ± CKD in context of myeloma. Serum FLC ratio markedly abnormal; SPEP/UPEP shows M-protein or Bence Jones protein. Hypercalcaemia, hyperuricaemia, and NSAIDs aggravate cast formation.
  • Biopsy indicated when: cause of AKI uncertain; to confirm diagnosis and guide treatment intensity; to exclude concurrent amyloidosis, LCDD, or other renal pathology.
  • Treatment: aggressive hydration + chemotherapy targeting plasma cell clone (VRd = bortezomib/lenalidomide/dexamethasone; daratumumab regimens). Reduce serum FLC rapidly — FLC >500mg/L with rising creatinine = urgent treatment. Plasmapheresis: no proven benefit in RCTs.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Tubular casts: large, pale eosinophilic, PAS-negative or weakly positive, hard (glassy/waxy) casts filling and distending tubular lumina — predominantly distal tubules and collecting ducts.
  • Fracture planes within casts: characteristic cracked/fractured appearance on H&E — pathognomonic in context.
  • Giant cell (histiocytic) reaction surrounding casts: multinucleated giant cells and macrophages surrounding and engulfing cast material — virtually diagnostic combination.
  • Tubular epithelial injury/necrosis around casts; variable interstitial inflammation; tubular atrophy and interstitial fibrosis in chronic cases.
  • Concurrent findings: look for amyloid (Congo red) and light chain deposition disease (LCDD — nodular GS without amyloid staining).
✚ Positive
IF — κ OR λ monotypic restriction on casts (not both): confirms monoclonal nature; κ more common than λTamm-Horsfall protein – positive (casts contain co-precipitated THP)SPEP/UPEP/serum FLC: markedly elevated monoclonal FLC ratio (>100 or <0.01)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
Congo red – NEGATIVE (distinguishes from renal amyloidosis)IF: no significant glomerular immune deposits (glomeruli usually unremarkable unless concurrent LCDD or amyloid)EM: no organised fibrils (unlike amyloid) or granular deposits (unlike immune complex GN)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Renal Amyloidosis (AL)↗Congo red positive (apple-green birefringence); EM fibrils 8–12nm; glomerular and vascular deposits; no fractured casts; no giant cell reaction; same monoclonal light chain aetiology
Light Chain Deposition Disease (LCDD)Nodular glomerulosclerosis (resembles diabetic nephropathy); Congo red negative; PAS-positive nodules; IF shows linear GBM/TBM deposits (not cast pattern); EM granular deposits along GBM/TBM
Acute Tubular Injury (other causes)↗No fractured hard casts; no giant cell reaction; no monotypic IF on casts; no M-protein on SPEP/FLC; ischaemic/toxic history
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • The combination of fracture-plane casts + giant cell reaction is virtually pathognomonic for light chain cast nephropathy — no other cast nephropathy shows this.
  • κ:λ ratio in myeloma: κ more common overall but λ light chains are more nephrotoxic and more likely to cause cast nephropathy. λ cast nephropathy = worse renal prognosis.
  • Concurrent pathology in myeloma kidney: always examine for amyloidosis (Congo red), LCDD (nodular GS, PAS positive nodules), and hypercalcaemia-related nephrocalcinosis — multiple pathologies can coexist.
  • FLC threshold for biopsy: no firm consensus, but most centres biopsy when FLC >500mg/L with AKI to exclude concurrent amyloid/LCDD that would change management.
  • Exam trap: Congo red negativity distinguishes from AL amyloidosis — the most important distinction since both involve monoclonal light chains. Always perform Congo red on suspected myeloma kidney biopsy.
Related / See Also
↗ RENAL AMYLOIDOSIS↗ ACUTE TUBULOINTERSTITIAL NEPHRITIS (ATIN)↗ Renal Amyloidosis (AL)+ Add: Light Chain Deposition Disease (LCDD)↗ Acute Tubular Injury (other causes)
RENAL AMYLOIDOSIS
Medical Renal Deposition Amyloid deposition Malignant
AL amyloidosis (light chain-derived; most common in developed world): plasma cell dyscrasia → misfolded Ig light chains → amyloid fibril deposition. Systemic — kidneys, heart, liver, peripheral nerves, tongue. Renal presentation: nephrotic syndrome ± AKI.
Clinical Context
  • AL amyloidosis (light chain-derived; most common in developed world): plasma cell dyscrasia → misfolded Ig light chains → amyloid fibril deposition. Systemic — kidneys, heart, liver, peripheral nerves, tongue. Renal presentation: nephrotic syndrome ± AKI.
  • AA amyloidosis (serum amyloid A; reactive): chronic inflammation — rheumatoid arthritis, IBD, chronic infection, familial Mediterranean fever, bronchiectasis. SAA protein → amyloid fibrils. Predominantly renal (nephrotic) and hepatosplenic.
  • Investigations: serum/urine electrophoresis + FLC ratio (AL); SAA level (AA); fat pad aspirate or rectal biopsy (Congo red — less invasive than renal biopsy); bone marrow biopsy. Mass spectrometry (LA-ICP-MS) on biopsy: gold standard for amyloid typing.
  • Treatment: AL — plasma cell clone-directed chemotherapy (daratumumab-VCd; autologous SCT in eligible). AA — treat underlying inflammatory disease; colchicine for FMF. Anti-SAP antibody (miridesap + dezamizumab): depletes amyloid deposits (trial).
Key Microscopic Features
  • Amorphous, homogeneous, eosinophilic, PAS-negative deposits in glomerular mesangium and capillary walls, blood vessel walls (especially arterioles), and tubular basement membranes — all three compartments may be involved.
  • Glomerular: expansion of mesangium and GBM by amyloid with effacement of normal architecture. Deposits may mimic nodular glomerulosclerosis (distinguish: Congo red positive, no Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules).
  • Vascular: arteriolar amyloid deposition with thickening and rigidity of vessel walls.
  • Special stains: Congo red → apple-green birefringence under polarised light (pathognomonic); Thioflavin T → green fluorescence (more sensitive, less specific).
  • Subtyping on biopsy: IF/IHC — AL: κ or λ restriction; AA: SAA antibody positive. EM: non-branching, randomly oriented fibrils 8–12nm diameter — pathognomonic of amyloid (all types).
✚ Positive
Congo red – apple-green birefringence under polarised light (pathognomonic for amyloid of any type)Thioflavin T/S – fluorescence positive (more sensitive than Congo red)AL amyloid: κ or λ monotypic restriction on IF/IHC (κ more common; λ associated with worse cardiac involvement)AA amyloid: SAA (serum amyloid A) IHC positive; permanganate-sensitive (AA loses Congo red reactivity after KMnO4 treatment — rarely used now)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
EM: non-branching randomly oriented fibrils 8–12nm diameter (all amyloid types share this ultrastructure)Mass spectrometry (LA-ICP-MS): gold standard typing — identifies amyloid protein definitively (AL, AA, ATTR, etc.); essential when IHC inconclusiveATTR (transthyretin amyloid): TTR IHC positive; associated with cardiomyopathy; wildtype (senile) or hereditary (Val122Ile, Val30Met); tafamidis for ATTR-CM
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Diabetic Nephropathy (nodular GS)↗Congo red NEGATIVE; PAS-positive Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules; GBM thickening; no vascular amyloid; clinical diabetes; no M-protein
Light Chain Deposition Disease (LCDD)↗Congo red NEGATIVE; PAS-positive linear GBM/TBM deposits; IF shows linear (not mesangial granular) κ or λ; EM granular (not fibrillar) deposits; resembles diabetic nodular GS
Light Chain Cast Nephropathy↗Congo red NEGATIVE; fractured casts + giant cell reaction in tubules; no glomerular amyloid deposits; same monoclonal light chain aetiology but different compartment
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Congo red under polarised light (apple-green birefringence) is the definitive histological diagnosis of amyloid — sensitivity ~80%, specificity >95%. Do not diagnose amyloid without polarised light confirmation.
  • Amyloid typing is essential before treatment: AL requires plasma cell clone therapy; AA requires treating the underlying inflammatory disease; ATTR requires tafamidis/diflunisal. Wrong treatment = patient harm.
  • Mass spectrometry (LA-ICP-MS) from paraffin block: supersedes permanganate sensitivity and immunofluorescence in difficult cases — can type any amyloid variant including rarer types (AFib, ALys, AGel). Request if IHC inconclusive.
  • Fat pad aspirate: ~70–80% sensitivity for AL/AA; quick, minimally invasive; Congo red performed at bedside. Negative fat pad does not exclude organ-specific amyloid.
  • Exam trap: amyloid can look like nodular glomerulosclerosis on H&E — Congo red is mandatory in any biopsy showing mesangial nodular expansion, especially in non-diabetic patients.
Related / See Also
↗ LIGHT CHAIN CAST NEPHROPATHY (MYELOMA KIDNEY)↗ DIABETIC NEPHROPATHY (NODULAR GLOMERULOSCLEROSIS)↗ Diabetic Nephropathy (nodular GS)↗ Light Chain Deposition Disease (LCDD)↗ Light Chain Cast Nephropathy
POLYARTERITIS NODOSA (PAN) — RENAL
Medical Renal Vascular Necrotising arteritis Malignant
PAN: necrotising arteritis of medium-sized arteries (arcuate, interlobar). Critically: NO glomerulonephritis, NO small vessel involvement. This distinguishes it from ANCA-associated vasculitis (MPA/GPA which affect small vessels and cause GN).
Clinical Context
  • PAN: necrotising arteritis of medium-sized arteries (arcuate, interlobar). Critically: NO glomerulonephritis, NO small vessel involvement. This distinguishes it from ANCA-associated vasculitis (MPA/GPA which affect small vessels and cause GN).
  • Renal features: renovascular hypertension (renal artery stenosis), renal infarcts, haematuria (from infarction, not GN), microaneurysms visible on angiography ('beading' pattern — diagnostic). Renal insufficiency from infarction, not GN.
  • HBV-associated PAN: 10–30% of PAN; treat HBV first (antiviral + short-course steroids, then taper steroids quickly to avoid viral replication). Non-HBV PAN: cyclophosphamide + glucocorticoids.
  • ANCA negative (key diagnostic criterion) — ANCA positivity redirects to MPA/GPA. Check HBsAg, anti-HCV. Angiography (CT or conventional): microaneurysms and stenoses of medium-sized arteries = characteristic.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Segmental transmural inflammation of medium-sized muscular arteries (arcuate and interlobar renal arteries): neutrophilic infiltrate → fibrinoid necrosis of vessel wall → disruption of elastic lamina.
  • Lesions at different stages in same biopsy: acute (fibrinoid necrosis + neutrophils) + healing (fibroblastic repair) + healed (fibrous occlusion ± aneurysm formation) — pathognomonic feature of PAN.
  • Renal parenchyma: wedge-shaped cortical infarcts (from artery occlusion); viable parenchyma adjacent. NO glomerulonephritis, NO glomerular necrosis, NO crescents — if crescents present, reconsider diagnosis.
  • Small vessel sparing: interlobular arteries, arterioles, and glomeruli unremarkable — key distinguishing feature from ANCA vasculitis.
✚ Positive
ANCA serology – NEGATIVE (essential diagnostic criterion; ANCA+ = reconsider MPA/GPA)HBsAg / anti-HCV – positive in HBV/HCV-associated PANIF on renal biopsy: pauci-immune (no immune deposits in vessels) — fibrinoid necrosis is non-immune mediated
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
NO glomerulonephritis (no mesangial hypercellularity, no crescents, no endocapillary proliferation) — critical distinguishing featureNO small vessel vasculitis (arterioles and glomerular capillaries spared)C-ANCA/PR3 and P-ANCA/MPO – negative (MPA has P-ANCA/MPO in ~60%; GPA has C-ANCA/PR3 in ~90%)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Microscopic Polyangiitis (MPA)Small vessel + glomerular involvement (pauci-immune crescentic GN); P-ANCA/MPO positive (~60%); NO medium artery aneurysms; no HBV association
ANCA-associated vasculitis (GPA)Upper/lower respiratory tract granulomas; C-ANCA/PR3+ (~90%); pauci-immune crescentic GN; small vessel — not medium artery only
Thrombotic Microangiopathy↗Small vessel/glomerular thrombi; MAHA + thrombocytopenia; no fibrinoid arterial necrosis; no microaneurysms on angiography
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • The cardinal rule of PAN: medium vessel vasculitis with NO GN. If there is GN (haematuria + proteinuria + casts), reconsider MPA or other ANCA vasculitis. PAN causes haematuria only from infarction.
  • Lesions at different stages (acute + healing + healed) in the same biopsy: pathognomonic of PAN. Other vasculitides tend to show synchronous lesions.
  • Microaneurysms on angiography: caused by post-necrotic dilatation of medium artery walls. Rupture → retroperitoneal haemorrhage — life-threatening. CT angiography is the diagnostic investigation of choice.
  • HBV-PAN: steroid monotherapy promotes viral replication. Protocol: short-course steroids (to control acute inflammation) + antiviral (lamivudine/tenofovir) + plasma exchange. Taper steroids rapidly.
  • Exam trap: ANCA is negative in PAN — do not call ANCA-negative MPA 'PAN'. MPA is small vessel + GN + ANCA positive in majority. PAN is medium vessel + NO GN + ANCA negative.
Related / See Also
↗ PAUCI-IMMUNE CRESCENTIC GN (ANCA-ASSOCIATED VASCULITIS)↗ THROMBOTIC MICROANGIOPATHY (TMA) — RENAL+ Add: Microscopic Polyangiitis (MPA)+ Add: ANCA-associated vasculitis (GPA)↗ Thrombotic Microangiopathy
POST-INFECTIOUS GLOMERULONEPHRITIS (PIGN)
Medical Renal Glomerulonephritis Endocapillary proliferative Variable
Immune complex GN triggered by infection — classically post-streptococcal (Group A β-haemolytic Strep; throat/skin infection), but any bacterial, viral, or parasitic infection can cause PIGN.
Clinical Context
  • Immune complex GN triggered by infection — classically post-streptococcal (Group A β-haemolytic Strep; throat/skin infection), but any bacterial, viral, or parasitic infection can cause PIGN.
  • Post-streptococcal GN (PSGN): children 5–12 years; 1–3 weeks after throat infection (type 12) or 3–6 weeks after skin infection (impetigo). Presents with nephritic syndrome — haematuria, hypertension, oedema, oliguria. Usually self-limiting.
  • IgA-dominant PIGN: staphylococcal (particularly in diabetics, elderly, IV drug users) — IgA dominant deposits but distinct from IgAN (acute onset, concurrent infection, resolves with treatment).
  • Investigations: ASOT rising titre (strep); C3 markedly depressed (alternative complement pathway activation); C4 normal (distinguishes from LN/cryoglobulin where C4 also low). Urine: haematuria + proteinuria + RBC casts.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Diffuse endocapillary hypercellularity: glomerular capillary lumina obliterated by infiltrating neutrophils and monocytes + endothelial/mesangial proliferation — 'exudative' appearance.
  • Glomerular tufts enlarged and hypercellular; Bowman's space compressed.
  • Variable mesangial expansion. Occasional small crescents (<20% of glomeruli typical in PSGN; >20% suggests more aggressive disease).
  • Interstitial oedema and mild inflammatory infiltrate. Tubular red cell casts (haemoglobinuric casts).
  • Resolution phase: hypercellularity decreases; mesangial residual deposits persist longest.
✚ Positive
C3 – dominant (C3 > IgG in acute phase); 'starry sky' pattern in capillary wallsIgG – present but less intense than C3 in classic PSGNIgA – dominant in staph-associated PIGN (IgA-dominant PIGN; concurrent staph infection)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
EM: subepithelial 'humps' — large, coarse, electron-dense deposits on epithelial side of GBM (pathognomonic in acute phase; resolve over months)C1q – negative or trace (distinguishes from LN; positive C1q with full house → LN)Anti-GBM – negative; ANCA – negative; ANA – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
IgA Nephropathy↗IgA dominant mesangial deposits (no acute onset after infection; chronic recurrent haematuria); C3 normal; ASOT negative; no subepithelial humps on EM; C3 not markedly depressed
IgA-dominant PIGN (staphylococcal)Identical IgA dominant IF — distinguished by: acute onset, concurrent staphylococcal infection (bacteraemia, skin infection, endocarditis), elderly/diabetic, resolves with infection treatment; IgAN has no concurrent infection
Lupus Nephritis Class IV↗Full house IF (IgG+IgA+IgM+C3+C1q); C4 low (SLE activates classical pathway); ANA/anti-dsDNA+; wire loops; TRIs on EM; no concurrent infection
MPGN (endocapillary pattern)GBM double contours (absent in acute PIGN); persistent complement depression; C3G: C3-dominant but no humps; no acute infection history
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Subepithelial 'humps' on EM: large, coarse, hump-shaped electron-dense deposits on epithelial (subepithelial) aspect of GBM — pathognomonic for PIGN in acute phase. Resolve over months as complement clears deposits.
  • C3 vs C4 in diagnosis: C3 markedly low + C4 normal = alternative pathway activation (PSGN, C3G, aHUS). C3 + C4 both low = classical pathway (SLE, cryoglobulin, endocarditis). Important for diagnosis.
  • Prognosis: PSGN in children — >95% full recovery. Adults and elderly: less favourable, especially with extensive crescents or pre-existing renal disease. No specific treatment — supportive (antihypertensives, diuretics, penicillin if active infection).
  • Exam trap: IgA-dominant PIGN (staph) can look identical to IgAN on IF — only clinical context (acute onset, concurrent infection, elderly/diabetic) distinguishes. IgAN is chronic; IgA-dominant PIGN resolves.
Related / See Also
↗ IgA NEPHROPATHY (IgAN)↗ MPGN PATTERN / C3 GLOMERULOPATHY (C3G)↗ LUPUS NEPHRITIS (ISN/RPS CLASSIFICATION)↗ IgA Nephropathy+ Add: IgA-dominant PIGN (staphylococcal)↗ Lupus Nephritis Class IV+ Add: MPGN (endocapillary pattern)
FIBRILLARY GLOMERULONEPHRITIS
Medical Renal Glomerulonephritis Mesangial/membranoproliferative Malignant
Rare immune complex GN (~1% of native biopsies) defined by polyclonal fibrillar deposits on EM. Adults; mean age ~50 years; slight male predominance.
Clinical Context
  • Rare immune complex GN (~1% of native biopsies) defined by polyclonal fibrillar deposits on EM. Adults; mean age ~50 years; slight male predominance.
  • Presentation: proteinuria (often nephrotic-range), haematuria, hypertension, progressive renal insufficiency. Not associated with systemic amyloidosis or cryoglobulinaemia.
  • Association with malignancy (~30%): hepatitis C, autoimmune disease, dysproteinaemia. DNAJB9 IHC (heat shock protein): highly sensitive and specific IHC marker for fibrillary GN — positive in >95%.
  • Treatment: no proven effective therapy. Rituximab used in some series. Renal survival ~50% at 10 years. Recurs in transplant.
Key Microscopic Features
  • LM: mesangial expansion ± endocapillary proliferation ± membranoproliferative pattern ± membranous change. No single LM pattern — variable.
  • Congo red NEGATIVE — critical distinction from amyloidosis (both have fibrillar deposits on EM but amyloid is Congo red positive).
  • PAS-positive mesangial and capillary wall deposits.
✚ Positive
IgG – dominant (polyclonal — both κ AND λ; distinguishes from AL amyloid which is monotypic)C3 – positiveDNAJB9 (DnaJ heat shock protein B9) – positive by IHC (>95% sensitive and specific; most useful single marker)κ AND λ – both positive (polyclonal; key distinction from amyloid/LCDD)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
Congo red – NEGATIVE (most important: distinguishes from ALL amyloid subtypes)EM: randomly oriented fibrils 12–24nm diameter (larger than amyloid 8–12nm; smaller than immunotactoid 30–50nm — size is the discriminating EM measurement)Monoclonal restriction – absent (polyclonal κ+λ; unlike AL amyloid or LCDD)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
AL Amyloidosis↗Congo red POSITIVE (apple-green birefringence) — the single most important distinction. EM: fibrils 8–12nm (smaller than fibrillary GN). Monotypic light chain restriction. DNAJB9 negative
Immunotactoid GlomerulopathyEM: large microtubules 30–50nm (much larger than fibrillary GN 12–24nm) in parallel arrays/stacked bundles. Associated with lymphoproliferative disorder/MGUS. Monoclonal IgG (usually IgGκ). Congo red negative
Diabetic Nephropathy (nodular)↗Congo red negative (shared); no fibrillar EM deposits; GBM thickening; Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules PAS+; no IgG/C3 deposits; clinical diabetes
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • DNAJB9 IHC: the key advance in fibrillary GN diagnosis. Positive in >95% of fibrillary GN, negative in amyloid, immunotactoid GP, MPGN, lupus, and diabetic nephropathy. Request on any biopsy with fibrillar deposits on EM.
  • Fibril size on EM is the key parameter: amyloid 8–12nm, fibrillary GN 12–24nm, immunotactoid 30–50nm. Random orientation (fibrillary GN/amyloid) vs parallel arrays (immunotactoid) is the other distinguishing feature.
  • Polyclonal vs monoclonal: fibrillary GN = polyclonal (IgG κ+λ both positive). AL amyloid = monotypic (κ OR λ only). LCDD = monotypic. This single IF feature largely solves the differential.
  • Exam trap: Congo red must be performed under polarised light — weakly staining amyloid can be overlooked in bright field. Never exclude amyloid without polarised light examination.
Related / See Also
↗ RENAL AMYLOIDOSIS↗ MPGN PATTERN / C3 GLOMERULOPATHY (C3G)↗ MEMBRANOUS NEPHROPATHY (MN)↗ AL Amyloidosis+ Add: Immunotactoid Glomerulopathy↗ Diabetic Nephropathy (nodular)
ALPORT SYNDROME / THIN BASEMENT MEMBRANE DISEASE (TBMD)
Medical Renal Hereditary GBM structural abnormality Variable
Alport syndrome: inherited COL4A3/COL4A4/COL4A5 mutations → defective type IV collagen (α3α4α5 network) in GBM, cochlea, and lens. X-linked (XLAS, COL4A5 — 80%; males affected, females variable); autosomal recessive (ARAS, COL4A3/4 — 15%, severe in both sexes); autosomal dominant (ADAS — milder).
Clinical Context
  • Alport syndrome: inherited COL4A3/COL4A4/COL4A5 mutations → defective type IV collagen (α3α4α5 network) in GBM, cochlea, and lens. X-linked (XLAS, COL4A5 — 80%; males affected, females variable); autosomal recessive (ARAS, COL4A3/4 — 15%, severe in both sexes); autosomal dominant (ADAS — milder).
  • Clinical triad: persistent microscopic haematuria (from birth) + sensorineural hearing loss (bilateral, high-frequency) + ocular abnormalities (anterior lenticonus, macular flecks). Progression to ESRD: males with XLAS ~50% by age 25, ~90% by age 40.
  • Thin Basement Membrane Disease (TBMD/Benign Familial Haematuria): heterozygous COL4A3/COL4A4 mutations → GBM thinning alone — isolated haematuria, no hearing loss, generally benign. Overlaps with ADAS.
  • Genetic testing: COL4A3/COL4A4/COL4A5 panel now preferred over biopsy in young patients with family history of haematuria. Biopsy required if diagnosis uncertain or to assess chronicity.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Light microscopy: usually normal or minimal change in early disease. Focal mesangial hypercellularity in later stages. Foam cells (lipid-laden interstitial cells) — non-specific but characteristic in advanced Alport.
  • Advanced disease: focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (secondary FSGS pattern), tubular atrophy, interstitial fibrosis — end-stage features.
  • TBMD on LM: entirely normal (thinning only visible on EM).
✚ Positive
EM: GBM thinning (<250nm; normal ~350nm in adults) — TBMD diagnosis. In Alport: irregular GBM with alternating thinning and thickening, lamellation (splitting), and basket-weave pattern of lamina densaCOL4A5 IHC (α5 chain): absent from GBM in X-linked Alport (males); discontinuous/absent in female XLAS carriersCOL4A3/COL4A4 IHC: absent in ARAS; present in XLAS (α3/α4 may be retained despite α5 loss)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
IF: NEGATIVE (no immune deposits — Alport/TBMD are non-immune diseases)COL4A5 on skin biopsy: absent epidermal BM staining in X-linked Alport males — less invasive than renal biopsy for initial diagnosisFSGS markers (if secondary FSGS develops): podocin/synaptopodin — may show reduction in advanced disease
Top Differentials + Discriminators
IgA Nephropathy↗IgA dominant mesangial deposits on IF (absent in Alport/TBMD); mesangial hypercellularity; no GBM thinning or lamellation on EM; no family history of ESRD/deafness
Primary FSGSNo family history of haematuria/deafness; no GBM thinning/lamellation on EM; IF negative (shared); podocyte effacement on EM (shared in nephrotic phase); COL4A5 retained
Thin GBM disease (TBMD) vs AlportTBMD: uniform diffuse thinning only (no lamellation/basket-weave); isolated haematuria; no deafness/lens; heterozygous COL4A3/4 mutation; generally benign. Alport: thinning + thickening + lamellation + basket-weave; systemic features
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • EM basket-weave/lamellation of GBM: irregular splitting and layering of the lamina densa in a 'moth-eaten' appearance — pathognomonic for established Alport syndrome. This EM finding alone is diagnostic in the right clinical context.
  • Type IV collagen network: α1α2 (ubiquitous GBM) replaced in mature GBM by α3α4α5 (glomerular-specific). Alport mutations disrupt this mature network → persistent fetal α1α2 → GBM structural weakness.
  • COL4A5 IHC on skin biopsy: α5 chain normally present in epidermal basement membrane. Absent in X-linked Alport males — allows diagnosis without renal biopsy. Carrier females show mosaic/patchy staining.
  • Prognosis modifiers in XLAS: missense mutations milder than truncating/splice site. Females with XLAS: 15% ESRD by 40 years but significant proportion develop proteinuria and CKD. Genetic counselling essential.
  • Exam trap: TBMD has entirely normal LM and IF — EM is the only diagnostic modality. Never dismiss isolated persistent haematuria in a young patient with family history without EM or genetic testing.
Related / See Also
↗ FOCAL SEGMENTAL GLOMERULOSCLEROSIS (FSGS)↗ IgA NEPHROPATHY (IgAN)↗ IgA Nephropathy+ Add: Primary FSGS+ Add: Thin GBM disease (TBMD) vs Alport
SCLEROSING PNEUMOCYTOMA (SP)
Thoracic Epithelial Mixed epithelial/stromal Benign
Benign primary lung neoplasm of type II pneumocyte differentiation — previously known as 'sclerosing haemangioma' (misnomer; not vascular). Almost exclusively in Asian women (F:M ~5:1); wide age range but peak 40–60 years.
Clinical Context
  • Benign primary lung neoplasm of type II pneumocyte differentiation — previously known as 'sclerosing haemangioma' (misnomer; not vascular). Almost exclusively in Asian women (F:M ~5:1); wide age range but peak 40–60 years.
  • Typically incidental solitary peripheral coin lesion on imaging; well-circumscribed, lobulated. Rarely symptomatic; very rarely bilateral or multifocal.
  • Trigger: solitary well-defined lung nodule in a middle-aged Asian woman — include SP in differential alongside carcinoid, hamartoma, and metastasis. Frozen section: benign — no further resection needed.
  • Excellent prognosis after resection; recurrence exceptionally rare. Lymph node metastasis reported in <5% but does NOT alter excellent prognosis — behaves as benign.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Two cell populations (both required for diagnosis): (1) surface cuboidal cells lining papillary structures — resemble type II pneumocytes (TTF1+, NapsinA+, Surfactant protein+); (2) round/polygonal stromal cells filling solid areas — bland, uniform, pale eosinophilic cytoplasm, absent/rare mitoses (TTF1+, EMA+, but NapsinA−).
  • Four architectural patterns (varying proportions in same tumour): papillary (papillary fronds lined by surface cells), sclerotic (hyalinised collagen), solid (sheets of stromal cells), and haemorrhagic (blood-filled spaces lined by surface cells).
  • Sclerosis: dense hyalinised stroma between cellular areas — low-power clue at scanning magnification.
  • Haemorrhagic areas: blood-filled cystic spaces lined by cuboidal surface cells — the original 'haemangioma' misinterpretation.
  • Cholesterol clefts and haemosiderin-laden macrophages common in haemorrhagic areas. No significant atypia, no necrosis, mitoses <1/10HPF.
✚ Positive
TTF1 – positive in BOTH cell populations (key: dual TTF1 positivity is characteristic)EMA – positive in stromal (round) cellsCK7 – positive in surface cells (cuboidal lining cells)NapsinA – positive in surface cells only (negative in stromal cells — helps distinguish the two populations)Surfactant protein A/B – positive in surface cells
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CD34 – negative (key: excludes SFT and haemangioma — original misdiagnosis)Synaptophysin / Chromogranin – negative (excludes carcinoid/NET — important DDx)p40 / p63 – negative (excludes squamous differentiation)Ki67 – very low (<5% in stromal cells; confirms benign behaviour)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Carcinoid / Well-differentiated NET⚡Synaptophysin+, Chromogranin+, INSM1+; organoid/trabecular architecture; uniform salt-and-pepper nuclei; no dual TTF1 cell populations; no haemorrhagic/sclerotic pattern
Lung Adenocarcinoma (lepidic/papillary)⚡Single malignant cell population; nuclear atypia + mitoses; invasive growth; TTF1+ but no bland dual-population architecture; NapsinA+ throughout; no sclerotic/haemorrhagic zones
Pulmonary Hamartoma↗Cartilage + fat + fibromyxoid stroma + entrapped epithelial clefts; no TTF1+ stromal cells; no dual cell populations; HMGA2 rearrangement; 'popcorn' calcification on imaging
Solitary Fibrous Tumour (pulmonary)↗STAT6 nuclear+; CD34+; NAB2-STAT6 fusion; hemangiopericytoma vasculature; no TTF1; no dual cell populations; patternless architecture
Meningothelial-like nodule (chemodectoma)Incidental finding; whorled nests of bland epithelioid cells around vessels; EMA+; small (<3mm); no papillary/haemorrhagic/sclerotic pattern; TTF1−
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • The two-cell population is the diagnostic hallmark: surface cells (type II pneumocyte-like: TTF1+/NapsinA+/CK7+) line papillary structures; stromal cells (TTF1+/EMA+/NapsinA−) fill solid areas. Dual TTF1 positivity across both populations is key.
  • Frozen section: four architectural patterns (papillary + sclerotic + solid + haemorrhagic) in a well-circumscribed lesion = SP. Reassure surgeon — benign, conservative resection adequate.
  • Lymph node metastasis: reported in <5% cases but does NOT indicate malignant behaviour — excise involved nodes, observe. Overall prognosis unaffected.
  • 'Sclerosing haemangioma' misnomer: abandoned since the tumour is of type II pneumocyte origin, not endothelial. WHO 2021 designates it Sclerosing Pneumocytoma.
  • Exam trap: the haemorrhagic pattern with blood-filled spaces can mimic vascular tumour — CD34 negativity (excludes haemangioma) and dual TTF1 positivity resolves this. Always request CD34 in any suspected SP.
Related / See Also
↗ PULMONARY HAMARTOMA↗ TYPICAL CARCINOID (LUNG)↗ LUNG ADENOCARCINOMA↗ SOLITARY FIBROUS TUMOUR (SFT)⚡ Carcinoid / Well-differentiated NET⚡ Lung Adenocarcinoma (lepidic/papillary)↗ Pulmonary Hamartoma↗ Solitary Fibrous Tumour (pulmonary)+ Add: Meningothelial-like nodule (chemodectoma)
CUTANEOUS SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA (cSCC)
Skin / Dermatopath Epithelial Epithelioid Malignant 📋 7×
Second most common skin cancer. UV-induced; sun-exposed sites (head & neck, dorsal hands); highest risk: immunosuppression (transplant recipients — 100× increased risk), chronic ulcers (Marjolin's ulcer), HPV (subungual, periungual, anogenital), arsenical keratoses, X-ray dermatitis.
Clinical Context
  • Second most common skin cancer. UV-induced; sun-exposed sites (head & neck, dorsal hands); highest risk: immunosuppression (transplant recipients — 100× increased risk), chronic ulcers (Marjolin's ulcer), HPV (subungual, periungual, anogenital), arsenical keratoses, X-ray dermatitis.
  • Subungual SCC: periungual/subungual; HPV16/18 association; mimics onychomycosis — biopsy essential. Oral SCC: buccal mucosa, tongue, floor of mouth; tobacco/alcohol; HPV-independent at most sites.
  • High-risk features (NCCN): >2cm, poor differentiation, PNI, LVI, depth >6mm, location (lip/ear/non-sun-exposed), immunosuppression, recurrent tumour.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Invasive nests and sheets of squamous cells with intercellular bridges and keratinisation; keratin pearls (whorls of eosinophilic keratinised cells) — well-differentiated. Poorly differentiated: no keratinisation, marked pleomorphism, high mitoses.
  • Precursor: actinic keratosis (full-thickness epidermal dysplasia, preserved basilar layer) → Bowen's disease/SCCIS (full-thickness epidermal involvement with basement membrane intact) → invasive SCC.
  • Desmoplastic SCC: infiltrative thin cords in desmoplastic stroma; perineural invasion common; high recurrence; p40+/CK5/6+ but sparse.
✚ Positive
p40 – diffuse nuclearp63 – diffuse nuclearCK5/6 – diffuseCK14 – positive
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
TTF1 – negativeNapsin A – negativeSOX10 – negative (excludes desmoplastic melanoma)Melan-A/HMB45 – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
KeratoacanthomaCrateriform architecture with central keratin-filled crater; pushing base; bland cytology; may spontaneously regress; well-differentiated; difficult distinction — treat as SCC
Desmoplastic melanoma↗S100+, SOX10+; HMB45/Melan-A−; no keratinisation; p40−; perineural invasion common; sun-damaged skin (head/neck)
PorocarcinomaEccrine duct origin; biphasic (poroid + cuticular/ductal cells); CEA+/EMA+ (ductal); p40+; connection to overlying epidermis; CK7+
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Margin reporting on excision: distance to closest peripheral and deep margin in mm. Oral cavity SCC: mandibular involvement requires radiological assessment (cortical erosion = bone invasion).
  • Frozen section for margins: SCC margins can be assessed on frozen — state involved/close (<1mm)/clear (≥1mm). Desmoplastic SCC: easy to under-call on frozen — defer to permanents if uncertain.
  • Perineural invasion (PNI): high-risk feature; drives adjuvant RT decision; must be explicitly reported (named nerve calibre and location if large nerve).
  • Subungual SCC: HPV16/18 driven; present as non-healing periungual lesion; may destroy nail plate. Radiograph to exclude bony involvement. Mohs or amputation depending on depth.
  • Transplant recipients: cSCC behaves aggressively — rapid growth, high metastatic risk, reduced immunosuppression ± acitretin chemoprevention.
Related / See Also
↗ BASAL CELL CARCINOMA (BCC)↗ MELANOMA (CUTANEOUS)+ Add: Keratoacanthoma↗ Desmoplastic melanoma+ Add: Porocarcinoma
BASAL CELL CARCINOMA (BCC)
Skin / Dermatopath Epithelial Epithelioid Intermediate
Most common human malignancy. UV-induced; sun-exposed sites; fair skin; rarely metastasises (<0.1%) but locally destructive. Gorlin syndrome (PTCH1 germline): multiple BCCs + jaw keratocysts + calcified falx cerebri + bifid ribs.
Clinical Context
  • Most common human malignancy. UV-induced; sun-exposed sites; fair skin; rarely metastasises (<0.1%) but locally destructive. Gorlin syndrome (PTCH1 germline): multiple BCCs + jaw keratocysts + calcified falx cerebri + bifid ribs.
  • Clinical subtypes: nodular (pearly papule + telangiectasia); superficial (erythematous plaque); morphoeic/sclerosing (firm scar-like — highest recurrence, ill-defined margins); basosquamous (aggressive — focal squamous differentiation).
  • Trigger: slow-growing pearly nodule with rolled edge and telangiectasia on sun-damaged skin — BCC.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Nests, islands, and cords of basaloid cells with peripheral palisading (nuclei aligned at periphery of nests) and retraction artefact (cleft between tumour nest and stroma) — pathognomonic combination.
  • Subtypes: nodular (large rounded nests); superficial (buds from basal layer, no deep invasion); micronodular (small irregular nests — higher recurrence); infiltrative (thin strands, desmoplastic stroma); morphoeic (sclerosing stroma, thin strands, poorly defined margins).
  • Stroma: mucinous/myxoid; tumour-specific fibrous stroma. Mitoses variable; apoptosis at periphery of nests.
✚ Positive
BerEP4 – diffuse (most useful — negative in SCC)BCL2 – diffuseCK14 – positivep63 – positive (non-specific)Androgen receptor – positive (~70%)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CEA – negative (positive in eccrine tumours)EMA – negative (positive in eccrine/sebaceous)Melan-A – negativep40 – variable/focal (diffuse in SCC)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Trichoepithelioma / trichoblastomaNo retraction artefact; no infiltration; CK20+ Merkel cells in stroma; papillary mesenchymal bodies; hair follicle differentiation; stromal induction; CYLD mutation
Merkel cell carcinomaCK20 paranuclear dot+, TTF1−, CD56+, synaptophysin+; small blue cells; no peripheral palisading; no retraction; CK20+ Merkel cell polyomavirus
Basosquamous carcinoma⚡BCC + focal squamous differentiation (abrupt keratinisation, intercellular bridges); aggressive behaviour; lymph node mets possible; treat as high-risk SCC
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • BerEP4: diffuse in BCC, negative in SCC — most useful discriminator on small biopsies where basaloid vs squamous distinction is needed.
  • Gorlin syndrome: PTCH1 (9q22) tumour suppressor — Hedgehog pathway. Vismodegib/sonidegib (SMO inhibitors): approved for locally advanced/metastatic BCC and in Gorlin syndrome.
  • Morphoeic BCC: frozen section challenging — ill-defined infiltrative strands in sclerosing stroma; Mohs surgery preferred for complete excision.
