  • Perineural invasion in BCC: rare but occurs — must report; associated with morphoeic subtype; drives adjuvant RT decision.
  • Margin reporting: same as cSCC. Complete excision rates vary by subtype — morphoeic highest recurrence (incomplete excision common even with apparent clear margins).
Related / See Also
↗ CUTANEOUS SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA (cSCC)↗ MELANOMA (CUTANEOUS)+ Add: Trichoepithelioma / trichoblastoma+ Add: Merkel cell carcinoma⚡ Basosquamous carcinoma
INFLAMMATORY SKIN DISEASE — PATTERN FRAMEWORK
Skin / Dermatopath Inflammatory Mixed Variable
Dermatopathology diagnosis driven by pattern recognition at low power. Patterns are not diagnoses — clinical correlation mandatory. Key question: where is the pathology (epidermis/DEJ/dermis/subcutis) and what is the reaction type?
Clinical Context
  • Dermatopathology diagnosis driven by pattern recognition at low power. Patterns are not diagnoses — clinical correlation mandatory. Key question: where is the pathology (epidermis/DEJ/dermis/subcutis) and what is the reaction type?
  • Trigger: any inflammatory skin biopsy — classify pattern first, then refine with high-power features and clinical correlation.
  • Key patterns: Lichenoid | Interface | Psoriasiform | Spongiotic | Bullous | Granulomatous | Vasculitic | Neutrophilic | Perivascular
Key Microscopic Features
  • LICHENOID: band-like lymphocytic infiltrate hugging DEJ; basal cell death (Civatte/colloid bodies); saw-tooth rete ridges. LP: hypergranulosis, wedge-shaped hypergranulosis. LPLK: epidermal atrophy. Drug: eosinophils, deeper infiltrate, parakeratosis. DLE: follicular plugging, basement membrane thickening.
  • INTERFACE: vacuolar degeneration of basal layer ± sparse lymphocytes at DEJ. EM/SCLE/DLE: vacuolar + thickened BM (PAS). Fixed drug: necrotic keratinocytes. Viral exanthem: sparse. Erythema multiforme: full-thickness keratinocyte necrosis + vacuolar change.
  • PSORIASIFORM: regular epidermal acanthosis (elongated rete ridges); parakeratosis; neutrophils in stratum corneum (Munro microabscesses) or spongiform pustules (Kogoj). Psoriasis: diminished granular layer, dilated tortuous dermal papillary vessels, thin suprapapillary plates, club-shaped rete ridges. Chronic eczema: irregular acanthosis, spongiosis.
✚ Positive
CD3/CD4/CD8 – T-cell infiltrate characterisationCD20 – B-cell aggregates (DLE, lymphoma mimics)CD123 – plasmacytoid DCs in lupusIgG/IgA/IgM/C3 – DIF for bullous/interface diseases
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
N/A — DIF essential for bullous diseases and lupus
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Lichenoid drug reaction vs lichen planus↗Drug: eosinophils in infiltrate; deeper mixed infiltrate; parakeratosis; less wedge hypergranulosis; temporal drug exposure. LP: pure lymphocytic; wedge hypergranulosis; no parakeratosis
Psoriasis vs chronic eczema (psoriasiform)↗Psoriasis: regular elongation, thin suprapapillary plates, Munro abscesses, diminished granular layer, neutrophils. Eczema: irregular acanthosis, spongiosis, eosinophils, preserved granular layer
Spongiotic dermatitis (eczema vs dermatophyte)↗PAS/GMS stain for fungal hyphae in stratum corneum; neutrophils in SC favour dermatophyte; eosinophils favour atopic/contact
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • SPONGIOTIC: intercellular oedema separating keratinocytes (spongiosis); Langerhans cell microvesicles; eosinophils (atopic/contact) vs lymphocytes (id reaction). Vesicle → pustule = secondary infection.
  • BULLOUS: subepidermal (PAS — BM at floor of blister) vs intraepidermal. DIF mandatory: IgG linear BMZ = bullous pemphigoid (BP180/BP230); IgG intercellular = pemphigus; IgA linear = linear IgA/dermatitis herpetiformis; granular IgA dermal papillae = DH.
  • VASCULITIC: fibrinoid necrosis of vessel walls; neutrophilic infiltrate ± nuclear dust (leukocytoclastic vasculitis); RBC extravasation. DIF: IgA vessel wall = HSP/IgAV.
  • GRANULOMATOUS: sarcoidal (naked granulomas, asteroid bodies) vs tuberculoid (caseating) vs palisaded (necrobiosis lipoidica, GA, RAN) vs suppurative (deep fungal, mycobacteria).
  • DIF always required for: bullous diseases, lupus, vasculitis. Snap-freeze perilesional skin in Michel's medium (NOT formalin) for DIF.
Related / See Also
↗ MELANOMA (CUTANEOUS)↗ CUTANEOUS SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA (cSCC)↗ Lichenoid drug reaction vs lichen planus↗ Psoriasis vs chronic eczema (psoriasiform)↗ Spongiotic dermatitis (eczema vs dermatophyte)
LICHEN PLANUS AND LICHENOID REACTIONS
Skin / Dermatopath Inflammatory Lichenoid Variable
Lichen planus (LP): T-cell-mediated autoimmune destruction of basal keratinocytes. 4Ps: pruritic, polygonal, planar (flat-topped), purple papules. Wickham's striae. Koebner phenomenon. Mucosa (erosive LP), nails, scalp (lichen planopilaris → cicatricial alopecia).
Clinical Context
  • Lichen planus (LP): T-cell-mediated autoimmune destruction of basal keratinocytes. 4Ps: pruritic, polygonal, planar (flat-topped), purple papules. Wickham's striae. Koebner phenomenon. Mucosa (erosive LP), nails, scalp (lichen planopilaris → cicatricial alopecia).
  • Lichenoid drug reactions: clinically similar but drug history; may be photodistributed. Implicated drugs: gold, antimalarials, ACE inhibitors, thiazides, NSAIDs, beta-blockers.
  • Lichen planopilaris (LPP): follicular LP; scalp; lymphocytic infiltrate around follicular isthmus; progressive scarring alopecia.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Band-like lymphocytic infiltrate (CD4 > CD8) at DEJ, obscuring DEJ; basal cell death — Civatte bodies (eosinophilic rounded apoptotic keratinocytes) at DEJ and in superficial dermis.
  • Epidermal changes: orthokeratosis; hypergranulosis (wedge-shaped — characteristic of LP); irregular acanthosis with saw-tooth rete ridges; thinning of suprapapillary epidermis.
  • Drug lichenoid: eosinophils in infiltrate; parakeratosis; deeper infiltrate extending into reticular dermis; less consistent hypergranulosis — these features favour drug over idiopathic LP.
✚ Positive
CD3/CD4 – predominant T-lymphocytes at DEJDIF: fibrinogen – linear/shaggy at DEJ (LP — non-specific but supportive)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CD20 – minimal B-cellsDIF: IgG/IgA/C3 – negative (distinguishes from lupus, bullous pemphigoid)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Lupus erythematosus (DLE/SCLE)DIF: granular IgG+IgM+C3 at DEJ ('lupus band'); CD123+ plasmacytoid DCs; follicular plugging; BM thickening (PAS); adnexal damage
Lichenoid GVHDClinically and histologically identical to LP; transplant history; satellite cell necrosis (apoptotic keratinocytes adjacent to lymphocytes); eosinophils
Fixed drug eruptionRecurrent same site; heavily pigmented dermis (melanin incontinence); full-thickness necrotic keratinocytes; eosinophils; less band-like pattern
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • DIF in LP: fibrinogen shaggy at DEJ — non-specific. Unlike lupus (granular IgG/IgM/C3). DIF negative for IgG/IgA = LP, not bullous disease.
  • Melanin incontinence: melanin-laden macrophages (melanophages) in superficial dermis — consequence of basal cell destruction; causes post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation.
  • Oral LP: white reticular pattern (Wickham's striae) or erosive; erosive oral LP has low malignant transformation risk (~1–5%); biopsy if erosive/nodular change.
  • Lichen planopilaris: key feature = lymphocytic infiltrate around upper follicular isthmus (not lower follicle as in alopecia areata). Scarring alopecia — irreversible.
  • Exam trap: lichenoid drug reaction histologically mimics LP — eosinophils + parakeratosis are the distinguishing features; always note drug history on request form.
Related / See Also
↗ INFLAMMATORY SKIN DISEASE — PATTERN FRAMEWORK+ Add: Lupus erythematosus (DLE/SCLE)+ Add: Lichenoid GVHD+ Add: Fixed drug eruption
PSORIASIS AND PSORIASIFORM DERMATITIS
Skin / Dermatopath Inflammatory Psoriasiform Variable
Chronic plaque psoriasis: well-demarcated erythematous plaques with silvery scale; extensor surfaces, scalp, nails. Auspitz sign (pinpoint bleeding on scale removal). Koebner phenomenon. IL-17/IL-23 axis driven.
Clinical Context
  • Chronic plaque psoriasis: well-demarcated erythematous plaques with silvery scale; extensor surfaces, scalp, nails. Auspitz sign (pinpoint bleeding on scale removal). Koebner phenomenon. IL-17/IL-23 axis driven.
  • Variants: guttate (post-streptococcal, teardrop lesions, HLA-Cw6), pustular (GPP — IL-36RN/CARD14 mutation — systemic; palmoplantar), erythrodermic (systemic).
  • Nail psoriasis: pitting, onycholysis, oil drop, subungual hyperkeratosis — important differential for subungual SCC (biopsy essential to exclude).
Key Microscopic Features
  • Regular psoriasiform epidermal hyperplasia: elongated club-shaped rete ridges; suprapapillary thinning (thin plates over dilated papillary dermal vessels); confluent parakeratosis; absent/diminished granular layer.
  • Munro microabscesses: neutrophilic aggregates within parakeratotic stratum corneum — pathognomonic of psoriasis. Spongiform pustules of Kogoj: neutrophils within spinous layer (pustular psoriasis).
  • Dilated tortuous capillaries in dermal papillae (Auspitz sign morphological correlate). Absence of spongiosis distinguishes from spongiotic/eczematous dermatitis.
✚ Positive
Ki67 – increased epidermal proliferationCD8 – T-cells in epidermisTNF-α / IL-17 – in situ (research)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
N/A — morphological diagnosis
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Chronic eczema (spongiotic)Irregular acanthosis (not regular club-shaped); spongiosis; eosinophils; preserved granular layer; no Munro abscesses; mast cells
Seborrhoeic dermatitis↗Shoulder parakeratosis (parakeratosis at tips of rete ridges, orthokeratosis between); perifollicular; Malassezia; less regular acanthosis; mild spongiosis
Secondary syphilis (psoriasiform)Plasma cells in infiltrate; endothelial swelling; lichenoid component; silver stain (Treponema); serology (VDRL/TPHA)
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Munro microabscesses: neutrophils in paratum corneum — pathognomonic but not always present in early/treated lesions. Spongiform pustule of Kogoj: subcorneal/spinous layer — pustular psoriasis.
  • GPP (generalised pustular psoriasis): IL-36 pathway — IL-36RN loss-of-function; spesolimab (anti-IL-36R) approved 2022 for acute flares.
  • Biologics: IL-17 inhibitors (secukinumab, ixekizumab, bimekizumab — IL-17A+F); IL-23 inhibitors (guselkumab, risankizumab, tildrakizumab — p19); IL-12/23 (ustekinumab — p40); TNF-α (adalimumab, etanercept). IL-17/IL-23 agents most effective.
  • Guttate psoriasis: throat swab + ASOT; post-streptococcal; teardrop lesions; usually self-limiting; small rete ridges, less regular than chronic plaque.
  • Biopsy site: active edge of plaque, non-treated area. Punch biopsy. Orientation important for assessment of rete ridge pattern.
Related / See Also
↗ INFLAMMATORY SKIN DISEASE — PATTERN FRAMEWORK↗ LICHEN PLANUS AND LICHENOID REACTIONS+ Add: Chronic eczema (spongiotic)↗ Seborrhoeic dermatitis+ Add: Secondary syphilis (psoriasiform)
BULLOUS PEMPHIGOID (BP) AND SUBEPIDERMAL BULLOUS DISEASES
Skin / Dermatopath Inflammatory Bullous Variable
Most common autoimmune blistering disease. Elderly (>70 years). Tense blisters on erythematous/urticarial base; flexural predominance; pruritic urticarial prodrome.
Clinical Context
  • Most common autoimmune blistering disease. Elderly (>70 years). Tense blisters on erythematous/urticarial base; flexural predominance; pruritic urticarial prodrome.
  • Autoantibodies: anti-BP180 (type XVII collagen, hemidesmosome) and anti-BP230 (hemidesmosomal plaque). BP180 titre correlates with disease activity.
  • Treatment: superpotent topical steroids (clobetasol — non-inferior to systemic); systemic prednisolone; doxycycline ± nicotinamide (steroid-sparing); rituximab for refractory.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Subepidermal blister (PAS — basement membrane at floor of blister) with eosinophilic infiltrate; eosinophils in blister cavity + dermis + epidermis (eosinophilic spongiosis in pre-bullous stage).
  • Dermal oedema; perivascular eosinophil-rich infiltrate. Eosinophilic spongiosis in urticarial pre-bullous stage — mimics eczema but eosinophil-rich.
  • PAS stain: BM at floor → subepidermal split. Salt-split skin technique: splits at lamina lucida — IgG on roof (BP180 — most BP) or floor (EBA).
✚ Positive
DIF: IgG – linear BMZ (perilesional skin)DIF: C3 – linear BMZIIF: IgG – epidermal side of salt-split skin (BP180/BP230)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
DIF: IgA – negative (linear IgA disease: IgA linear BMZ)IIF: dermal side – negative (EBA: IgG dermal = COL7 antibody)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Pemphigus vulgaris↗Intraepidermal split (suprabasal — tombstone basal cells); acantholysis; DIF: IgG intercellular ('chicken-wire'); anti-desmoglein 3 (mucosal) ± 1 (skin)
Dermatitis herpetiformis (DH)↗Subepidermal; DIF: granular IgA dermal papillae; neutrophil-rich; gluten sensitivity (coeliac); HLA-DQ2/DQ8; responds to dapsone
Epidermolysis bullosa acquisita (EBA)Subepidermal; DIF: IgG linear BMZ; IIF: dermal side of salt-split skin (COL7 = floor); trauma-induced; milia; anti-COL7 antibodies
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • DIF: perilesional skin within 1cm of blister. Snap-freeze in Michel's medium (NOT formalin). BP = IgG + C3 linear BMZ — the most important diagnostic test.
  • Salt-split skin (IIF): separates lamina lucida. IgG on epidermal side (roof) = BP (BP180/BP230). IgG on dermal side (floor) = EBA (collagen VII) or anti-laminin 332 mucous membrane pemphigoid.
  • Pemphigus vulgaris: mucosal ulceration + flaccid blisters; Nikolsky sign positive; anti-desmoglein 3 (mucosal) and/or 1 (skin). Rituximab first-line for moderate-severe PV.
  • Mucous membrane pemphigoid: IgG/IgA/C3 at BMZ; anti-BP180/laminin-332/COL7; scarring of conjunctiva/mouth/oesophagus — ophthalmology referral essential.
  • Eosinophilic spongiosis: pre-bullous BP mimics eczema histologically — DIF mandatory for any eosinophil-rich spongiotic dermatitis in elderly patient.
Related / See Also
↗ INFLAMMATORY SKIN DISEASE — PATTERN FRAMEWORK↗ Pemphigus vulgaris↗ Dermatitis herpetiformis (DH)+ Add: Epidermolysis bullosa acquisita (EBA)
PLEOMORPHIC DERMAL SARCOMA (PDS) / UNDIFFERENTIATED PLEOMORPHIC SARCOMA OF SKIN
Skin / Dermatopath Mesenchymal Spindle cell Malignant 📋 1×
Rare high-grade cutaneous sarcoma — diagnosis of exclusion (undifferentiated after extensive IHC/molecular). UV-induced; sun-damaged skin in elderly; scalp predominant. Rapidly growing ulcerated tumour.
Clinical Context
  • Rare high-grade cutaneous sarcoma — diagnosis of exclusion (undifferentiated after extensive IHC/molecular). UV-induced; sun-damaged skin in elderly; scalp predominant. Rapidly growing ulcerated tumour.
  • Historically called 'malignant fibrous histiocytoma' (MFH) of skin — term abandoned. Distinct from pleomorphic liposarcoma, leiomyosarcoma, and other differentiated pleomorphic sarcomas.
  • ~20–30% local recurrence; ~10–15% metastasis. Related to atypical fibroxanthoma (AFX) — PDS = deep AFX (invades subcutis ± PNI ± LVI ± necrosis).
Key Microscopic Features
  • Dermis and subcutis: sheets of highly pleomorphic spindle and epithelioid cells; bizarre multinucleated giant cells; atypical mitoses; tumour necrosis.
  • Atypical fibroxanthoma (AFX): superficial variant — confined to dermis; no subcutaneous invasion, no PNI, no LVI, no necrosis. AFX = low malignant potential; PDS = aggressive.
  • Inflammatory variant (formerly inflammatory MFH): prominent xanthomatous and giant cell infiltrate surrounding pleomorphic cells.
✚ Positive
CD10 – positive (~80%)Vimentin – positiveCD68 – variable (histiocytic differentiation)Prox1 – positive in subset
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CK – negative (excludes SCC, sarcomatoid carcinoma)S100/SOX10 – negative (excludes spindle cell melanoma, MPNST)Desmin/SMA/h-caldesmon – negative (excludes LMS)CD34 – negative (excludes SFT/DFSP)STAT6 – negative (excludes SFT)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Sarcomatoid/spindle cell SCCCK focal/diffuse+; p40+; epidermal connection/actinic keratosis; more common than PDS at this site
Desmoplastic melanoma↗S100+, SOX10+; HMB45/Melan-A−; perineural invasion; no epithelioid giant cells; BRAF/NRAS testing
Dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans (DFSP)↗CD34 diffuse+; storiform pattern; honeycomb infiltration of fat; COL1A1-PDGFB fusion; low-grade; Breslow-like growth
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • PDS vs AFX distinction: invasion of subcutis, tumour necrosis, PNI, or LVI = PDS. Pure dermal involvement = AFX. Margin status critical for both.
  • Diagnosis of exclusion: must exclude with IHC — SCC (CK/p40), spindle cell melanoma (S100/SOX10), LMS (Desmin/SMA/h-caldesmon), SFT (STAT6), DFSP (CD34), angiosarcoma (CD31/ERG/CD34).
  • Wide excision (≥1cm margins) primary treatment. Adjuvant RT for positive margins or PNI.
  • TP53 and CDKN2A mutations common — UV signature. No targetable driver; immunotherapy (pembrolizumab) reported responses in metastatic cases.
  • Exam trap: always exclude spindle cell SCC first (much more common on sun-damaged skin/scalp) — focal CK+ can be present in SCC; OSCAR/CAM5.2 more sensitive than MNF116 for CK in spindle cell carcinoma.
Related / See Also
↗ CUTANEOUS SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA (cSCC)↗ MELANOMA (CUTANEOUS)↗ MALIGNANT PERIPHERAL NERVE SHEATH TUMOUR (MPNST)↗ ATYPICAL FIBROXANTHOMA (AFX) / SUPERFICIAL UPS↗ DESMOPLASTIC MELANOMA+ Add: Sarcomatoid/spindle cell SCC↗ Desmoplastic melanoma↗ Dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans (DFSP)
HERPES SIMPLEX VIRUS (HSV) / HERPES ZOSTER — SKIN
Skin / Dermatopath Inflammatory Bullous Variable 📋 1×
HSV-1 (orolabial, HSV encephalitis); HSV-2 (genital, neonatal herpes). Prodrome: tingling/burning → grouped vesicles on erythematous base → pustules → crusting. Recurrent at same site (latency in sensory ganglia).
Clinical Context
  • HSV-1 (orolabial, HSV encephalitis); HSV-2 (genital, neonatal herpes). Prodrome: tingling/burning → grouped vesicles on erythematous base → pustules → crusting. Recurrent at same site (latency in sensory ganglia).
  • VZV (varicella-zoster): primary = chickenpox; reactivation = herpes zoster (shingles) — dermatomal, painful, unilateral. Ramsay Hunt: VZV reactivation in geniculate ganglion (ear pain + ipsilateral LMN facial palsy + vesicles in ear canal).
  • Immunocompromised: disseminated herpes, visceral HSV, chronic mucocutaneous HSV — IHC/PCR confirmation essential.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Intraepidermal vesicle with acantholysis; multinucleated giant cells (Tzanck cells/cells) with ground-glass nuclear chromatin margination, nuclear moulding, and intranuclear eosinophilic inclusions (Cowdry type A).
  • Balloon degeneration of keratinocytes (intracellular oedema → rounded cells); reticular degeneration (extreme oedema → multilocular vesicle with septa).
  • Dermis: perivascular lymphocytic infiltrate; oedema; haemorrhage in severe cases.
✚ Positive
HSV-1/HSV-2 IHC – nuclear and cytoplasmic positivity in infected cellsVZV IHC – if zoster suspectedPCR – most sensitive (swab from vesicle base)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
N/A — morphological + confirmatory IHC/PCR
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Varicella-zoster virusIdentical histology — IHC/PCR required for definitive HSV vs VZV distinction; VZV: dermatomal, older patient, more painful
Hand, foot and mouth disease (enterovirus)Suprabasal acantholysis; no multinucleated giant cells; no Cowdry inclusions; children; palms/soles/oral
Bullous pemphigoid (early)⚡Subepidermal; eosinophil-rich; no Cowdry inclusions; no Tzanck cells; DIF IgG+C3 linear BMZ
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Tzanck smear: scrape base of fresh vesicle; Giemsa stain; multinucleated giant cells with moulded nuclei = positive. Quick bedside test; does not distinguish HSV from VZV.
  • Cowdry type A inclusions: eosinophilic intranuclear inclusions with surrounding clear halo (chromatin margination) — diagnostic of herpesviruses.
  • Foreskin biopsy context: HSV on penile/foreskin biopsy — typical intraepidermal vesicle + Tzanck cells + Cowdry inclusions. IHC confirms type (HSV-2 most common genital).
  • Kaposi's varicelliform eruption (eczema herpeticum): disseminated HSV in patient with pre-existing dermatosis (eczema, Darier's) — widespread punched-out erosions; urgent IV aciclovir.
  • Treatment: aciclovir/valaciclovir; resistant (immunocompromised) → foscarnet.
Related / See Also
↗ INFLAMMATORY SKIN DISEASE — PATTERN FRAMEWORK+ Add: Varicella-zoster virus+ Add: Hand, foot and mouth disease (enterovirus)⚡ Bullous pemphigoid (early)
HEAD AND NECK SCC (ORAL / LARYNGEAL / OROPHARYNGEAL)
Head & Neck Epithelial Epithelioid Malignant 📋 3×
Oral cavity SCC: tobacco + alcohol synergistic; floor of mouth, lateral tongue, buccal mucosa. HPV-independent; aggressive behaviour; alveolar bone invasion must be excluded (X-ray/CT).
Clinical Context
  • Oral cavity SCC: tobacco + alcohol synergistic; floor of mouth, lateral tongue, buccal mucosa. HPV-independent; aggressive behaviour; alveolar bone invasion must be excluded (X-ray/CT).
  • Oropharyngeal SCC (OPSCC): tonsil + base of tongue; HPV16-driven in ~80% (Western); younger, non-smokers; better prognosis than HPV-negative. p16 IHC surrogate for HPV.
  • Laryngeal SCC: smoking; glottic (vocal cord) — early hoarseness; supraglottic — late presentation; subglottic — rare. Laryngectomy specimen: mandatory assessment of anterior commissure, subglottic extension, thyroid cartilage invasion, and soft tissue margins.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Keratinising SCC: nests with central keratin pearls; intercellular bridges; stromal desmoplasia. Non-keratinising (HPV-driven oropharyngeal): solid nests, basaloid morphology, central necrosis ('comedo-like'), minimal keratinisation — do NOT grade as poorly differentiated.
  • Verrucous carcinoma: well-differentiated, exophytic, pushing (not infiltrative) base; no significant atypia; no metastasis — clinically and radiologically confirmed before treatment.
  • Perineural invasion and lymphovascular invasion: must report — drives adjuvant RT/chemotherapy decisions. Depth of invasion (DOI) in oral SCC: ≥4mm → elective neck dissection.
✚ Positive
p40 – diffuse nuclearCK5/6 – diffusep63 – diffusep16 – diffuse block (surrogate HPV; oropharyngeal context)CK7 – variable
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
TTF1 – negativeNapsin A – negativeThyroglobulin – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Nasopharyngeal carcinoma (NPC)Undifferentiated (lymphoepithelioma): EBV EBER+; syncytial growth; lymphocytic infiltrate; CK+; p40 variable; nasopharynx origin; EBER ISH essential
Basaloid SCC vs HPV-driven OPSCCBoth basaloid; p16 block+ in HPV-driven; HPV ISH/PCR confirmation; HPV+ OPSCC: better prognosis, de-escalation trials (PATHOS, OPTIMA)
Small cell carcinoma (metastatic/primary)⚡Synaptophysin+, CD56+; p40−; nuclear moulding; crush artefact; TTF1+; no squamous architecture
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • DOI vs tumour thickness: DOI measured from level of adjacent normal mucosal basement membrane to deepest point of invasion. DOI ≥4mm = pT2 for oral SCC (AJCC 8th); ≥10mm = pT3. Must be measured and reported.
  • HPV testing in OPSCC: p16 IHC (≥70% strong diffuse nuclear + cytoplasmic = proxy). Confirm with HPV ISH or PCR for equivocal cases. HPV+ OPSCC = better prognosis — separate staging system (AJCC 8th).
  • Laryngectomy macroscopy: measure tumour from anterior commissure and subglottic extent. Thyroid cartilage invasion = T4a. Extralaryngeal spread = T4a. Invasion of prevertebral space/carotid = T4b (unresectable).
  • Adjuvant therapy: positive margins or pN2+ → concurrent chemoradiotherapy (cisplatin). pN1 with no adverse features → RT alone.
  • Verrucous carcinoma: benign-appearing but clinically invasive; transformation to SCC after RT (historically feared — evidence weak); wide excision first-line.
Related / See Also
↗ CUTANEOUS SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA (cSCC)↗ POLYMORPHOUS ADENOCARCINOMA (PAC)+ Add: Nasopharyngeal carcinoma (NPC)+ Add: Basaloid SCC vs HPV-driven OPSCC⚡ Small cell carcinoma (metastatic/primary)
PAPILLARY THYROID CARCINOMA — HISTOLOGY
Head & Neck Epithelial Glandular/mucinous Malignant 📋 1×
Most common thyroid malignancy (~85%). Any age; F:M 3:1; peak 30–50 years. Presents as thyroid nodule ± cervical lymphadenopathy. Lateral cervical LN metastasis does NOT worsen prognosis (unlike most carcinomas).
Clinical Context
  • Most common thyroid malignancy (~85%). Any age; F:M 3:1; peak 30–50 years. Presents as thyroid nodule ± cervical lymphadenopathy. Lateral cervical LN metastasis does NOT worsen prognosis (unlike most carcinomas).
  • Radiation exposure (Chernobyl, childhood RT): RET/PTC rearrangements; younger patients. Hashimoto's thyroiditis: modest increased PTC risk.
  • Excellent prognosis: 10-year OS >95% for classical PTC. Aggressive variants (tall cell, hobnail, columnar cell, diffuse sclerosing) significantly worse.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Papillary architecture: fibrovascular cores lined by tumour cells. Nuclear features (pathognomonic — required for diagnosis): nuclear enlargement + pale/ground-glass 'Orphan Annie eye' chromatin + nuclear grooves (longitudinal infoldings) + intranuclear pseudoinclusions (cytoplasmic invaginations — sharp, eosinophilic, round).
  • Psammoma bodies (concentrically laminated calcifications): present in ~50% — highly characteristic; if present in cervical LN without obvious tumour → search for occult PTC.
  • Follicular variant PTC (FVPTC): follicular architecture with PTC nuclei. Encapsulated FVPTC without invasion = NIFTP (non-invasive follicular thyroid neoplasm with papillary-like nuclear features) — reclassified as non-malignant (WHO 2022).
✚ Positive
CK19 – diffuse strongHBME-1 – membranousGalectin-3 – positiveBRAF V600E IHC (VE1) – ~80% classical PTCTTF1 – positiveThyroglobulin – positive
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
Calcitonin – negative (excludes MTC)Synaptophysin – negativeCD56 – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Follicular adenoma / follicular carcinoma↗No PTC nuclear features; HBME-1/CK19/Galectin-3 negative; capsular/vascular invasion distinguishes FA from FTC; NRAS/HRAS mutation
NIFTPEncapsulated FVPTC; PTC nuclear features present; NO capsular/vascular invasion; NO solid/tall cell/columnar pattern; no psammoma bodies; RAS mutation (not BRAF)
Medullary thyroid carcinoma⚡Calcitonin+, CEA+, Synaptophysin+; no PTC nuclei; amyloid stroma; C-cell origin; RET mutation
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • BRAF V600E (~80%): associated with extrathyroidal extension, LN mets, radioiodine resistance, recurrence. Test on resection; guides adjuvant therapy decisions. Dabrafenib+trametinib for BRAF+ ATC.
  • NIFTP: reclassified as non-malignant in 2016 — impacts ROM calculations for Thy3f/Thy4 cytology categories. Requires full capsule sampling (1 section per mm) to exclude invasion.
  • Tall cell variant (≥30% of cells ≥3× height as width, eosinophilic cytoplasm): BRAF V600E in >95%; extrathyroidal extension common; treat as high-risk.
  • Microcarcinoma (≤1cm): active surveillance increasingly adopted for low-risk unifocal microPTC (no LN mets, no ETE, no aggressive variant).
  • Lymph node metastasis: common and does not worsen prognosis in PTC (unlike most cancers). Central (level VI) LN dissection for cN0; lateral (levels II–V) for cN1b.
🧫 Cytology Counterpart
↗ PAPILLARY THYROID CARCINOMA — FNA (Thy5 / Thy4)
Related / See Also
↗ PAPILLARY THYROID CARCINOMA — FNA (Thy5 / Thy4)↗ THYROID FNA — RCPath THY CATEGORY OVERVIEW↗ Follicular adenoma / follicular carcinoma+ Add: NIFTP⚡ Medullary thyroid carcinoma
ADENOID CYSTIC CARCINOMA (AdCC)
Head & Neck Epithelial Biphasic Malignant
Most common malignancy of submandibular and minor salivary glands; second in parotid. Slow growing but relentless — late distant metastasis (lung, bone) 10–20 years after diagnosis.
Clinical Context
  • Most common malignancy of submandibular and minor salivary glands; second in parotid. Slow growing but relentless — late distant metastasis (lung, bone) 10–20 years after diagnosis.
  • Perineural invasion: pathognomonic feature and dominant spread mechanism — tracks along named nerves to skull base. Pain/numbness may predate mass. Skip lesions common.
  • MYB-NFIB fusion (~60%) or MYBL1-NFIB; MYB IHC surrogate. No effective systemic therapy for metastatic disease — surgery ± RT primary modality.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Three architectural patterns (must report predominant): cribriform (pseudocysts filled with basement membrane material/mucin — 'Swiss cheese'; best prognosis); tubular (true ductal lumina; best prognosis); solid (sheets of basaloid cells, no pseudocysts; worst prognosis — >30% solid = high-grade transformation).
  • Biphasic: ductal cells (inner — cuboidal, eosinophilic) + myoepithelial/abluminal cells (outer — small, hyperchromatic, angulated). Dense eosinophilic basement membrane material in pseudocysts (PAS+, laminin+, collagen IV+).
  • Perineural invasion: present in virtually all cases — intraneural, perineural, and endoneural. Must state named nerve involvement.
✚ Positive
CK7 – ductal componentp63 – myoepithelial componentp40 – myoepithelialSMA – myoepithelialCD117 (c-Kit) – diffuse (~90%)MYB – nuclear (surrogate MYB-NFIB fusion)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
p40 – negative in PAC (key distinction — p40+ in AdCC myoepithelial component)PRKD1 – mutation in PAC not AdCCSOX10 – variable (positive in PAC)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Polymorphous Adenocarcinoma (PAC)⚡Cytological uniformity + architectural diversity; p40−; MYB−; PRKD1/2/3 mutation; targetoid PNI; no pseudocysts; palate predominant
Pleomorphic adenoma (cribriform areas)⚡Chondromyxoid stroma; no cribriform pseudocysts; no angulated basaloid nuclei; PLAG1/HMGA2 rearrangement; benign dual-component architecture
Basal cell adenocarcinoma⚡Less PNI; less aggressive; peripheral palisading; CD117 may be positive; no MYB rearrangement; CYLD mutation
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Grading: Grade 1/2 (cribriform/tubular predominant); Grade 3 (>30% solid component) — high-grade transformation; significantly worse prognosis; may lose adenoid cystic morphology.
  • Perineural tracking: must perform PNI-directed frozen sections at surgical margins; positive perineural margin → adjuvant RT mandatory.
  • Distant metastasis: lung most common (~40% of cases over lifetime); bone, liver, brain. Metastasis may appear >20 years after primary — lifelong surveillance.
  • CD117 (c-Kit) expression: high (~90%) but imatinib trials largely negative. Axitinib, lenvatinib: modest activity. No approved targeted therapy.
  • Exam trap: p40 is POSITIVE in AdCC (myoepithelial component) but NEGATIVE in PAC — do not use p40 to distinguish between them alone; MYB and PRKD1 molecular testing decisive.
Related / See Also
↗ POLYMORPHOUS ADENOCARCINOMA (PAC)⚡ Polymorphous Adenocarcinoma (PAC)⚡ Pleomorphic adenoma (cribriform areas)⚡ Basal cell adenocarcinoma
MUCOEPIDERMOID CARCINOMA (MEC)
Head & Neck Epithelial Glandular/mucinous Malignant
Most common salivary gland malignancy overall. Parotid most common site; any salivary gland; also arising in jaw bones (central MEC), bronchus, and thyroid.
Clinical Context
  • Most common salivary gland malignancy overall. Parotid most common site; any salivary gland; also arising in jaw bones (central MEC), bronchus, and thyroid.
  • Wide spectrum: low-grade (cystic, indolent, >95% cure with surgery) to high-grade (solid, aggressive, ~30% 5-year survival). Grade determines treatment.
  • MAML2 rearrangement: CRTC1/3-MAML2 fusion (~75%); predicts better prognosis even in high-grade MEC — molecular testing increasingly important.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Three cell types (all required for diagnosis): mucous cells (goblet-like, mucicarmine/PAS+, pale foamy cytoplasm); epidermoid/squamoid cells (polygonal, intercellular bridges, eosinophilic); intermediate cells (small basaloid, between mucous and epidermoid).
  • Grading (AFIP/Brandwein): cystic component (%), PNI, LVI, bony invasion, necrosis, mitoses → low (0–4 pts), intermediate (5–6 pts), high (≥7 pts). Low-grade: cyst-predominant, conspicuous mucous cells. High-grade: solid, minimal mucous cells.
  • Low-grade: cysts lined by mucous cells; indolent; may be mistaken for mucoepidermoid cyst. High-grade: solid nests of epidermoid cells; minimal mucin; may mimic SCC.
✚ Positive
CK7 – diffuseCK5/6 – epidermoid componentp63 – epidermoid/intermediate cellsCEA – mucous cellsEMA – positiveMAML2 FISH – rearrangement confirmatory
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
Calponin/SMA – negative (no myoepithelial component)DOG1 – negativeSOX10 – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
High-grade MEC vs SCC↗MEC: mucous cells (mucicarmine+, PAS+) — even focal; CEA+; EMA+; MAML2 rearrangement; primary salivary site. SCC: no mucin; p40 diffuse; no MAML2
Metastatic mucinous adenocarcinoma↗No three cell types; CK7/CK20 profile; SATB2+ (colorectal); CDX2+; no MAML2; clinical history
Warthin's tumour (cystic variant)↗Oncocytic columnar epithelium + lymphoid stroma; no mucous/intermediate/epidermoid cell triad; no mucicarmine+ goblet cells; benign
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • MAML2 rearrangement: CRTC1-MAML2 t(11;19) most common. MAML2-rearranged MEC — better prognosis even when histologically high-grade. FISH or RNA-seq for confirmation.
  • Grading critical: low-grade → surgery alone (parotidectomy). High-grade → surgery + RT ± chemotherapy. Intermediate → surgery ± RT per margin/nodal status.
  • Mucicarmine stain: highlights mucous cells — essential for diagnosis of high-grade MEC (mucous cells may be very sparse). PAS-diastase also useful.
  • Jaw/central MEC: arises from mucous cells of odontogenic cysts; lower jaw predominant; MAML2 rearrangement common; treat as salivary gland MEC.
  • Exam trap: high-grade MEC with minimal mucous cells mimics SCC — mucicarmine stain essential; even a single mucicarmine+ goblet cell changes the diagnosis.
Related / See Also
↗ ADENOID CYSTIC CARCINOMA (AdCC)↗ POLYMORPHOUS ADENOCARCINOMA (PAC)↗ High-grade MEC vs SCC↗ Metastatic mucinous adenocarcinoma↗ Warthin's tumour (cystic variant)
PLEOMORPHIC ADENOMA (PA) — HISTOLOGY
Head & Neck Epithelial Mixed Intermediate 📋 1×
Most common salivary gland tumour (~65%). Parotid in 80%; submandibular ~10%; minor salivary (palate) ~10%. Slow growing; mobile; firm. Recurrence after enucleation/inadequate excision — multifocal if ruptured capsule.
Clinical Context
  • Most common salivary gland tumour (~65%). Parotid in 80%; submandibular ~10%; minor salivary (palate) ~10%. Slow growing; mobile; firm. Recurrence after enucleation/inadequate excision — multifocal if ruptured capsule.
  • Malignant transformation in longstanding PA (~5–10% of untreated PA >15 years): carcinoma ex pleomorphic adenoma (CaExPA). Rapid growth, pain, facial nerve involvement → biopsy urgently.
  • PLAG1 (8q12) or HMGA2 (12q14) rearrangement. Frozen section role: assess margin — positive → re-excision at same sitting.
Key Microscopic Features
  • True mixed tumour: biphasic — epithelial/myoepithelial component + chondromyxoid stromal component (unique to PA — not present in any other tumour).
  • Epithelial: ductal structures, tubules, solid nests, squamous/plasmacytoid/spindle/clear cell differentiation. Myoepithelial: abluminal cells, spindle/plasmacytoid/clear; p63+/SMA+.
  • Stroma: chondromyxoid (cartilaginous) — metachromatic on Giemsa; PAS+; transitions from myxoid to hyalinised in old lesions. Incomplete/partially absent capsule — focally invasive capsular protrusion (does not equate to malignancy).
✚ Positive
CK7 – ductal componentS100 – myoepithelial (diffuse)SOX10 – myoepithelialGFAP – myoepithelial (variable)p63/SMA – myoepithelial
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
MYB – negative (AdCC positive — key distinction)p40 – myoepithelial positive (shared with AdCC — not discriminating alone)Ki67 – very low (<5%)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Carcinoma ex pleomorphic adenoma (CaExPA)⚡Malignant component (usually adenocarcinoma NOS, myoepithelial Ca, or SDC) arising within PA; invasive CaExPA: invasion >1.5mm beyond PA capsule; p53 mutant; Ki67 elevated; loss of dual architecture
Adenoid cystic carcinoma↗No chondromyxoid stroma; angulated hyperchromatic nuclei; pseudocysts filled with basement membrane material; MYB+/NFIB rearrangement; PNI dominant
MyoepitheliomaPure myoepithelial cells (no ductal component); spindle/plasmacytoid/clear cell; SMA+, p63+, S100+; PLAG1 rearrangement in some; benign or low malignant potential
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Frozen section: positive margin → immediate re-excision. State distance of tumour from inked margin. Ruptured capsule intraoperatively → thorough wound washout + extended excision to reduce recurrence.
  • Recurrent PA: increasingly difficult surgical dissection; higher facial nerve injury risk; risk of multifocal seeding from previous rupture. MRI for planning.
  • CaExPA prognosis: depends on extent of invasion. Intracapsular/minimally invasive (≤1.5mm): excellent. Widely invasive: aggressive — metastasis common. Histological grade of malignant component matters.
  • PLAG1 IHC: nuclear positivity — sensitive/specific for PA (85/95%); not affected by malignant transformation. Useful in confirming PA when morphology atypical.
  • Exam trap: focally invasive capsular protrusion (satellite nodules within capsule) is NOT malignancy — this is characteristic of PA and does not change management.
🧫 Cytology Counterpart
↗ PLEOMORPHIC ADENOMA — SALIVARY FNA (Milan II)
Related / See Also
↗ ADENOID CYSTIC CARCINOMA (AdCC)↗ MUCOEPIDERMOID CARCINOMA (MEC)↗ PLEOMORPHIC ADENOMA — SALIVARY FNA (Milan II)⚡ Carcinoma ex pleomorphic adenoma (CaExPA)↗ Adenoid cystic carcinoma+ Add: Myoepithelioma
COLORECTAL ADENOCARCINOMA (CRC)
LGI Epithelial Glandular/mucinous Malignant 📋 1×
Second most common cancer death. Lynch syndrome (MLH1/MSH2/MSH6/PMS2 germline — ~3%), AFAP/FAP (APC), MUTYH-associated polyposis. Sporadic: APC → KRAS → SMAD4/TP53 (Fearon-Vogelstein sequence); serrated pathway (BRAF V600E → CpG island methylation → MLH1 silencing → MSI-H).
Clinical Context
  • Second most common cancer death. Lynch syndrome (MLH1/MSH2/MSH6/PMS2 germline — ~3%), AFAP/FAP (APC), MUTYH-associated polyposis. Sporadic: APC → KRAS → SMAD4/TP53 (Fearon-Vogelstein sequence); serrated pathway (BRAF V600E → CpG island methylation → MLH1 silencing → MSI-H).
  • Staging: TNM8/AJCC 8th. Macroscopic CRM (circumferential resection margin) assessment mandatory on LAR specimens — CRM ≤1mm = involved. Mesorectal excision quality (complete/near-complete/incomplete TME).
  • MMR/MSI testing mandatory on all CRC: loss of MLH1 → reflex BRAF V600E (somatic = sporadic methylation; wild-type = Lynch). MSI-H: pembrolizumab first-line for metastatic; immunotherapy responsive.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Moderately differentiated (most common): irregular glands with nuclear pleomorphism, necrotic luminal debris ('dirty necrosis' — characteristic of colorectal origin); desmoplastic stroma; LVI and PNI must be reported.
  • Mucinous carcinoma (>50% extracellular mucin pools): worse prognosis; more BRAF/MSI-H association. Signet ring cell carcinoma (>50% signet ring cells): poor prognosis; linitis plastica pattern.
  • Tumour budding (single cells or clusters ≤4 cells at invasive front): Bd1/2/3 per ITBCC; Bd3 = high-grade budding — independent adverse prognostic factor; must report in pT1 carcinoma (drives decision for completion surgery).
✚ Positive
CK20 – diffuse (colorectal marker)CDX2 – nuclear (intestinal differentiation)SATB2 – nuclear (most specific colorectal marker)CK7 – negative or focal
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CK7 – negativeTTF1 – negativePAX8 – negativeWT1 – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Mucinous adenocarcinoma from appendix (LAMN/HAMN-derived)SATB2+, CDX2+, CK7−/CK20+ — IHC overlaps; pushing invasion (LAMN/HAMN) vs infiltrative (mucinous adenocarcinoma); clinical/imaging context essential
Metastatic gastric adenocarcinoma to peritoneum↗CK7+, CK20 variable, MUC5AC+; SATB2−; CDX2 usually−; HER2 amplified in ~15%; clinical history
Endometrial carcinoma (metastatic)⚡PAX8+, ER+, Vimentin+; SATB2−; CDX2−; CK7+; clinical history
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • MMR IHC panel (MLH1/PMS2/MSH2/MSH6): loss of MLH1+PMS2 → reflex BRAF V600E — if BRAF mutant = sporadic (hypermethylation); if BRAF wild-type = Lynch → germline testing. MSH2/6 loss or MSH6 alone → Lynch (no BRAF reflex needed).
  • RAS/BRAF/MSI testing for systemic therapy: KRAS/NRAS wild-type → anti-EGFR (cetuximab/panitumumab, left-sided only). BRAF V600E mutant → encorafenib + cetuximab. MSI-H → pembrolizumab.
  • Tumour budding Bd3 in pT1: high-risk feature → completion resection (surgery after polypectomy). Other high-risk pT1 features: poor differentiation, LVI, deep submucosal invasion (Sm3).
  • CRM: distance of tumour/extramural venous invasion/involved LN to mesorectal fascia. ≤1mm = R1. Must be measured in mm on macroscopy and confirmed histologically.
  • Medullary carcinoma: poorly differentiated, solid growth, prominent tumour-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs), syncytial pattern — MSI-H almost universally; good prognosis despite high grade.
Related / See Also
↗ LOW-GRADE APPENDICEAL MUCINOUS NEOPLASM (LAMN)↗ METASTATIC MUCINOUS / SIGNET RING CELL ADENOCARCINOMA+ Add: Mucinous adenocarcinoma from appendix (LAMN/HAMN-derived)↗ Metastatic gastric adenocarcinoma to peritoneum⚡ Endometrial carcinoma (metastatic)
GASTRIC AND OESOPHAGEAL ADENOCARCINOMA
UGI Epithelial Glandular/mucinous Malignant 📋 5×
Gastric adenocarcinoma: H. pylori (intestinal type, Lauren classification); EBV-associated (~10%); diffuse type (CDH1 germline = HDGC; sporadic CDH1 loss). Oesophageal adenocarcinoma: Barrett's oesophagus (GORD → intestinal metaplasia → dysplasia → adenocarcinoma); GOJ/cardia tumours staged as oesophageal (AJCC 8th).
Clinical Context
  • Gastric adenocarcinoma: H. pylori (intestinal type, Lauren classification); EBV-associated (~10%); diffuse type (CDH1 germline = HDGC; sporadic CDH1 loss). Oesophageal adenocarcinoma: Barrett's oesophagus (GORD → intestinal metaplasia → dysplasia → adenocarcinoma); GOJ/cardia tumours staged as oesophageal (AJCC 8th).
  • HER2 testing mandatory on all advanced gastric/GOJ adenocarcinoma: IHC 3+ or 2+/FISH+ → trastuzumab + chemotherapy (ToGA trial). HER2-low: T-DXd emerging.
  • Gastrectomy/oesophagogastrectomy macroscopy: Siewert classification (GOJ tumours): Type I (oesophageal, >1cm above GOJ), II (true cardia, 1cm above to 2cm below GOJ), III (subcardial, >2cm below GOJ) — determines surgical approach and staging.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Lauren classification: Intestinal type (gland-forming, intestinal metaplasia background, older male, H. pylori); Diffuse type (poorly cohesive/signet ring cells, no glands, younger, CDH1, worse prognosis); Mixed.
  • Barrett's oesophagus: specialised intestinal metaplasia (goblet cells) at GOJ/tubular oesophagus — confirmed by H&E (goblet cells not always needed under UK guidelines). Low-grade dysplasia → high-grade dysplasia → intramucosal adenocarcinoma → invasive.
  • Signet ring cell carcinoma: intracytoplasmic mucin displacing nucleus; desmoplastic stroma; linitis plastica pattern (diffuse mural thickening). EBV-associated: dense lymphocytic infiltrate ('lymphoepithelioma-like'); better prognosis; PD-L1 high.
✚ Positive
CK7 – diffuseMUC5AC – gastric foveolar typeHER2 – IHC score 0–3+ (ToGA scoring — different from breast)PD-L1 CPS – combined positive score for immunotherapy eligibilityEBV EBER ISH – EBV-associated subtype
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
SATB2 – negative (positive in colorectal — useful distinction)CDX2 – variable (may be positive in intestinal-type)E-cadherin – lost in diffuse/signet ring (CDH1 mutation)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Colorectal adenocarcinoma (metastatic)⚡SATB2+, CK20+, CDX2+; dirty necrosis; CK7−; clinical/imaging context
High grade serous carcinoma (peritoneal)⚡WT1+, PAX8+, p53 aberrant; CK7+; no signet ring cells; MUC5AC−; SATB2−
Poorly cohesive lobular breast carcinomaER/PR+, GATA3+, mammaglobulin+; E-cadherin lost (shared); CDH1 mutation (shared); CK7+; MUC5AC−; clinical history
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • HER2 scoring (ToGA): IHC 3+ = complete basolateral membranous staining ≥10% of cells; 2+ = incomplete/weak basolateral ≥10% → FISH. Different thresholds from breast (complete circumferential membrane in breast; basolateral in gastric).
  • PD-L1 CPS (combined positive score): tumour cells + lymphocytes + macrophages expressing PD-L1 / total tumour cells × 100. CPS ≥5 → pembrolizumab + chemotherapy (KEYNOTE-590/811). MSI-H → pembrolizumab monotherapy.
  • FLOT regimen (perioperative): 5-FU, leucovorin, oxaliplatin, docetaxel — standard peri-operative chemotherapy for resectable gastric/GOJ. Pathological response (TRG 1/2) = improved survival.
  • Barrett's surveillance: non-dysplastic → 2–5 yearly endoscopy; LGD → 6-monthly; HGD/intramucosal carcinoma → endoscopic resection (EMR/ESD) ± ablation (RFA).
  • Siewert type II/III: total gastrectomy. Siewert type I: oesophagectomy. Proximal margins (GOJ): ≥5cm for diffuse; ≥3cm intestinal type.
Related / See Also
↗ METASTATIC MUCINOUS / SIGNET RING CELL ADENOCARCINOMA↗ COLORECTAL ADENOCARCINOMA (CRC)⚡ Colorectal adenocarcinoma (metastatic)⚡ High grade serous carcinoma (peritoneal)+ Add: Poorly cohesive lobular breast carcinoma
INFLAMMATORY BOWEL DISEASE — UC AND CROHN'S
LGI Inflammatory Mixed Variable 📋 4×
UC: continuous mucosal disease from rectum proximally; bloody diarrhoea; macroscopic proctitis/left-sided/extensive. Crohn's: transmural, skip lesions, any GI site (ileocolonic most common); fistulae, strictures, perianal disease.
Clinical Context
  • UC: continuous mucosal disease from rectum proximally; bloody diarrhoea; macroscopic proctitis/left-sided/extensive. Crohn's: transmural, skip lesions, any GI site (ileocolonic most common); fistulae, strictures, perianal disease.
  • Colectomy indications: acute severe UC unresponsive to IV steroids (Truelove & Witts Day 3 decision — Travis score); colorectal cancer prophylaxis; dysplasia surveillance. Crohn's: fistula, obstruction, perforation, malignancy.
  • Dysplasia surveillance: colonoscopy with biopsies at defined intervals. Dysplasia classification: negative/indefinite/low-grade/high-grade. HGD or multifocal LGD → colectomy discussion.
Key Microscopic Features
  • UC: mucosal/submucosal inflammation only; crypt architectural distortion (branching, shortening, atrophy — hallmarks of chronicity); cryptitis (neutrophils in crypt epithelium) and crypt abscesses (neutrophils in crypt lumen); mucin depletion; basal plasmacytosis (plasma cells below crypt bases — earliest feature of chronicity).
  • Crohn's: transmural inflammation (lymphoid aggregates through all layers); non-caseating granulomas (~50% — pathognomonic when present); skip lesions; deep fissuring ulcers; neural hyperplasia; fat wrapping (creeping fat). Cobblestone mucosa (oedematous islands between ulcers).
  • UC-dysplasia: low-grade (nuclear stratification, pencillate nuclei, loss of surface maturation) vs high-grade (complex architecture, loss of polarity, prominent nucleoli). p53 IHC: aberrant (diffuse strong or null) supports dysplasia. Ki67: elevated throughout crypt.
✚ Positive
CD3/CD4/CD8 – T-lymphocytesIgG4 – if IgG4-related colitis suspectedp53 aberrant – supports dysplasia (not diagnostic alone)Ki67 – elevated in dysplastic epithelium
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
ZN/PAS-D – negative (excludes TB/fungal in granulomatous colitis)CMV IHC – negative (important exclusion in severe UC — CMV colitis mimics/exacerbates acute severe UC)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Crohn's vs UC — macroscopyCrohn's: skip lesions, cobblestones, fissuring ulcers, perianal disease, fat wrapping, strictures. UC: continuous from rectum, flat mucosal ulceration, pseudopolyps
Crohn's vs TB colitisTB: caseating granulomas, ZN+, IGRA+, ileocaecal predominance, upper lobe CXR. Crohn's: non-caseating, ZN−, perianal disease, fistulae
IBD vs ischaemic colitisIschaemic: watershed zones (splenic flexure, sigmoid); ghost cells; superficial withered crypts; no chronic architectural change; acuteOnset
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • CMV colitis in acute severe UC: IHC for CMV mandatory on all biopsies in steroid-refractory severe UC — CMV inclusions (owl-eye nuclei) in endothelial/stromal cells. Treat with ganciclovir if found.
  • Basal plasmacytosis: earliest and most specific histological marker of IBD chronicity — plasma cells below crypt bases (absent in normal colon). Present before crypt architectural distortion.
  • Granulomas in Crohn's: present in ~50% of surgical specimens but only ~15–30% of biopsies — absence does not exclude Crohn's. Granulomas in crypt cryptolysis = rupture granuloma (not diagnostic of Crohn's).
  • Panproctocolectomy macroscopy: measure extent of disease from anus, identify strictures/fistulae/masses, sample ileocaecal valve and terminal ileum, map dysplastic areas.
  • Indeterminate colitis: ~10–15% of IBD cases — features of both UC and Crohn's; cannot be classified after full clinicopathological assessment. Pouch formation still possible.
Related / See Also
↗ COLORECTAL ADENOCARCINOMA (CRC)↗ HEPATIC GRANULOMA — DIFFERENTIAL FRAMEWORK+ Add: Crohn's vs UC — macroscopy+ Add: Crohn's vs TB colitis+ Add: IBD vs ischaemic colitis
SCHISTOSOMIASIS (UROGENITAL / INTESTINAL)
Urological Inflammatory Granulomatous Variable 📋 3×
Schistosoma haematobium: urogenital — bladder, ureter, genital tract. Endemic sub-Saharan Africa/Middle East. Presents with haematuria (painless — classic). Chronic infection → bladder squamous metaplasia → squamous cell carcinoma (most common cause of SCC bladder worldwide).
Clinical Context
  • Schistosoma haematobium: urogenital — bladder, ureter, genital tract. Endemic sub-Saharan Africa/Middle East. Presents with haematuria (painless — classic). Chronic infection → bladder squamous metaplasia → squamous cell carcinoma (most common cause of SCC bladder worldwide).
  • S. mansoni/S. japonicum: intestinal/hepatic — portal hypertension, hepatic fibrosis ('pipe-stem fibrosis'), colitis. Cercarial penetration through skin (swimmer's itch) → Katayama fever.
  • Diagnosis: urine/stool microscopy (ova with terminal/lateral spine), serology, rectal snip biopsy. Treatment: praziquantel.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Schistosome ova in tissue: S. haematobium — large oval eggs with terminal spine (bladder wall/ureter); S. mansoni — lateral spine (intestinal wall); both calcify in chronic infection.
  • Granulomatous reaction around ova: epithelioid histiocytes + giant cells + eosinophils + fibrosis (chronic). Acute: eosinophilic infiltrate around viable ova. Dead/calcified ova: 'ghost' outlines within fibrosis.
  • Bladder: mucosal squamous metaplasia (leukoplakia); submucosal ova with granulomas + calcification ('sandy patches' on cystoscopy); urothelial hyperplasia → dysplasia → SCC.
✚ Positive
N/A — morphological diagnosis; ova identified by shape and spine location
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
N/A
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Tuberculosis cystitisCaseating granulomas; ZN+; no ova; ulceration; systemic TB features; IGRA+
Xanthogranulomatous cystitis⚡CD68+ foam cells; no ova; calculus association; different clinical picture
Bladder SCC (without visible ova)Squamous carcinoma; if from endemic area → search for ova (deeper sections, PAS stain calcified ova); HPV-independent
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Terminal spine = S. haematobium (terminal = bladder); lateral spine = S. mansoni (lateral = gut). The spine location identifies the species — exam favourite.
  • Calcified ova: dead but still visible as calcified ovoid structures in fibrous stroma — PAS stains the wall; von Kossa stains calcification.
  • Bladder SCC risk: ~30–40× increased in chronic S. haematobium infection. Tends to be well-differentiated keratinising SCC, often advanced at diagnosis. p40/CK5/6+ ; HPV-negative.
  • Pipe-stem fibrosis (Symmers' fibrosis): periportal fibrosis from S. mansoni hepatic egg deposition — portal hypertension without cirrhosis (hepatic parenchyma preserved until late).
  • Praziquantel: single dose cures active infection. Does not reverse established fibrosis or malignant change.
Related / See Also
↗ XANTHOGRANULOMATOUS PYELONEPHRITIS (XGP)↗ PARAGANGLIOMA (BLADDER)+ Add: Tuberculosis cystitis⚡ Xanthogranulomatous cystitis+ Add: Bladder SCC (without visible ova)
DIAPHRAGM DISEASE (NSAID ENTEROPATHY)
LGI Inflammatory Fibrous/collagenous Variable
Characteristic complication of long-term NSAID use: multiple thin circumferential fibrous strictures (diaphragms) of the small bowel (mid-jejunum/ileum), resembling diaphragms across the lumen.
Clinical Context
  • Characteristic complication of long-term NSAID use: multiple thin circumferential fibrous strictures (diaphragms) of the small bowel (mid-jejunum/ileum), resembling diaphragms across the lumen.
  • Presents with small bowel obstruction, iron deficiency anaemia (chronic mucosal injury), or protein-losing enteropathy. Capsule endoscopy or CT enterography for diagnosis.
  • Small bowel resection confirms diagnosis — macroscopy (multiple thin web-like strictures) is characteristic.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Thin circumferential submucosal fibrosis creating shelf-like projections into the lumen ('diaphragms' — 2–4mm thick); overlying mucosa relatively preserved.
  • Submucosal fibrosis with fibroblast proliferation; minimal inflammation in established lesions; chronic mucosal ulceration/erosion over diaphragms; villous atrophy adjacent.
  • Muscularis propria intact and non-hypertrophied — distinguishes from Crohn's stricture (transmural inflammation + hypertrophy).
✚ Positive
SMA – submucosal fibroblasts/myofibroblasts
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CD117/DOG1 – negative (excludes GIST)Desmin – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Crohn's disease (small bowel stricture)Transmural inflammation; skip lesions; granulomas; fat wrapping; mucosal cobblestoning; PNI; no thin diaphragm-type fibrosis
Ischaemic strictureHaemosiderin; ghost crypts; watershed distribution; acute history of ischaemia; no NSAID use
Potassium chloride-induced strictureSame diaphragm appearance — K+ tablets (wax matrix) cause focal mucosal injury; drug history
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • NSAID mechanism: inhibition of COX-1 → reduced prostaglandin → increased mucosal permeability → bacterial translocation → submucosal fibrosis.
  • Macroscopy is diagnostic: multiple thin (≤5mm) circumferential diaphragms, usually 2–10 in number, in mid-small bowel. Proximal bowel dilated.
  • Stop NSAIDs: essential; strictures do not regress with drug withdrawal — resection required for obstruction. Topical budesonide has been tried.
  • Capsule endoscopy: diagnostic of choice for mid-small bowel diaphragm disease — shows multiple web-like strictures. Capsule retention risk if severe obstruction.
  • Exam trigger: small bowel resection specimen with multiple thin circumferential strictures in elderly patient on NSAIDs = diaphragm disease.
Related / See Also
↗ INFLAMMATORY BOWEL DISEASE — UC AND CROHN'S+ Add: Crohn's disease (small bowel stricture)+ Add: Ischaemic stricture+ Add: Potassium chloride-induced stricture
MEDULLARY COLORECTAL CARCINOMA (MSI-H)
LGI Epithelial Round cell Malignant 📋 1×
Rare morphological subtype (~5% of CRC): MSI-H in virtually 100% of cases. Right-sided colon predominance. Better prognosis than grade alone suggests — despite poor differentiation, 5-year OS ~70%.
Clinical Context
  • Rare morphological subtype (~5% of CRC): MSI-H in virtually 100% of cases. Right-sided colon predominance. Better prognosis than grade alone suggests — despite poor differentiation, 5-year OS ~70%.
  • Lynch syndrome association significant — but mostly sporadic MLH1 methylation (BRAF V600E). All medullary CRC should have MMR IHC ± BRAF testing.
  • Highly immunogenic: dense TIL infiltrate; PD-L1 expression; pembrolizumab highly effective in MSI-H metastatic CRC.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Solid sheets and nests of large polygonal cells with vesicular nuclei, prominent nucleoli, and abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm — no gland formation (despite being classified as adenocarcinoma).
  • Prominent intratumoural and peritumoural lymphocytic infiltrate (TILs — tumour-infiltrating lymphocytes): Crohn's-like peritumoral lymphoid reaction at periphery. Pushing border (not infiltrative).
  • Syncytial/sheet-like growth; rare mitoses relative to grade; no necrotic luminal debris (unlike conventional poorly differentiated CRC).
✚ Positive
CK20 – positiveCDX2 – positiveSATB2 – positiveMLH1 – LOST (with PMS2 loss — MLH1 methylation or Lynch)Ki67 – high
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
MLH1/PMS2 – absent (MMR-deficient)Synaptophysin/Chromogranin – negative (excludes NEC)TTF1 – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Poorly differentiated NEC (large cell type)⚡Synaptophysin+, INSM1+; p53 mutant; Rb lost; high mitotic rate; no TILs; no Crohn's reaction; CK20−
Undifferentiated carcinoma of colonNo lymphoid infiltrate; no MSI-H association; worse prognosis; may have rhabdoid morphology; SMARCB1 loss in some
Conventional poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma⚡Irregular glands or solid growth without TILs; dirty necrosis; infiltrative borders; usually MSS (microsatellite stable)
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • MSI-H CRC: ~15% of all CRC; 3% Lynch syndrome; 12% sporadic (MLH1 methylation + BRAF V600E). MSI-H = better prognosis in stage II (no adjuvant chemo benefit); worse relative prognosis in stage IV (historically — reversed by pembrolizumab).
  • Pembrolizumab: FDA-approved first-line for MSI-H metastatic CRC (KEYNOTE-177) — 43% ORR, dramatically improved PFS vs FOLFOX/FOLFIRI.
  • TILs: density correlates with MSI-H status. High TILs + solid growth + right-sided + pushing border = medullary CRC until proven otherwise — always check MMR.
  • BRAF V600E in MSI-H CRC: sporadic methylation; worse prognosis than BRAF wild-type MSI-H (Lynch). In MSS CRC with BRAF V600E: encorafenib + cetuximab.
  • Do not grade medullary CRC as Grade 3/poorly differentiated — it behaves better. WHO 2019: medullary carcinoma is a distinct entity, not graded by conventional criteria.
Related / See Also
↗ COLORECTAL ADENOCARCINOMA (CRC)⚡ Poorly differentiated NEC (large cell type)+ Add: Undifferentiated carcinoma of colon⚡ Conventional poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma
PROSTATIC ADENOCARCINOMA
Urological Epithelial Glandular/mucinous Malignant 📋 6×
Most common cancer in UK men. PSA screening controversial (overdiagnosis vs early detection). Most common sites: peripheral zone (70%); transition zone (20% — TURP specimen).
Clinical Context
  • Most common cancer in UK men. PSA screening controversial (overdiagnosis vs early detection). Most common sites: peripheral zone (70%); transition zone (20% — TURP specimen).
  • Grade Group (ISUP 2019) replaces Gleason: GG1 (Gl 3+3=6) — low risk, surveillance eligible; GG2 (3+4=7) — intermediate; GG3 (4+3=7); GG4 (4+4/3+5/5+3=8); GG5 (4+5/5+4/5+5=9–10) — high risk.
  • Staging: radical prostatectomy specimens — extraprostatic extension (EPE), seminal vesicle invasion (SVI = pT3b), surgical margins (positive if tumour at inked margin). Rectal adenoCA on biopsy → confirm PSA/prostatic origin.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Infiltrative small glands between benign glands; lack of basal cell layer (key feature — p63/CK5/6/HMWCK negative); single cell layer; prominent nucleoli (Grade 3: moderately prominent; Grade 4: macronucleoli = Grade 4/5).
  • Gleason pattern: Pattern 3 = discrete well-formed glands; Pattern 4 = fused/cribriform/poorly-formed glands; Pattern 5 = solid sheets/single cells/comedonecrosis. Cribriform pattern = Pattern 4 (minimum) — always Grade 4.
  • Perineural invasion: common and characteristic; not a staging criterion but must be reported. Collagenous micronodules, glomeruloid structures, intraluminal crystalloids, blue mucin = supportive features.
✚ Positive
PSA – positiveNKX3.1 – nuclear (most sensitive prostate marker)PSAP – positiveAR – nuclearERG – nuclear positive (~50%, TMPRSS2-ERG fusion)AMACR (P504S) – luminal cytoplasmic (racemase; positive in carcinoma, negative in benign)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
p63 – absent (present in benign basal cells)HMWCK (34βE12/CK5/6) – absent (basal layer absent)CK7 – negativeCK20 – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
High-grade PIN (HGPIN)↗Basal cell layer retained (p63+/HMWCK+); architectural distortion within pre-existing large glands; no infiltrative growth; AMACR may be positive
Adenosis (atypical adenomatous hyperplasia)Lobular architecture; basal cells present (p63+ focally); pale cytoplasm; no prominent nucleoli; AMACR negative
Rectal adenocarcinoma invading prostateCK7 variable, CK20+, CDX2+, SATB2+; PSA−, NKX3.1−; glandular dirty necrosis; clinical context — rectal primary
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • AMACR (P504S/racemase): positive in carcinoma + HGPIN, negative in most benign mimics — combine with p63/HMWCK for triple cocktail (AMACR+/p63−/HMWCK− = carcinoma).
  • ISUP Grade Group 1 (Gleason 3+3=6): active surveillance eligible (NICE/EAU) — no worse than Gleason 6 anywhere; no Gleason 1 or 2 reported on biopsy.
  • Cribriform pattern: always Gleason Pattern 4 minimum; cribriform = worse prognosis even within GG2; intraductal carcinoma (IDC-P) = high-grade intraluminal proliferation, not invasive but marker of aggressive disease.
  • PSMA PET-CT: superior to conventional imaging for biochemical recurrence and staging; lutetium-177-PSMA (Pluvicto): approved for PSMA-positive mCRPC post-docetaxel + ARPI.
  • Rectal adenoCA on prostate biopsy: direct extension or biopsy needle tracking; PSA−, CDX2+, SATB2+ confirms rectal origin — do not diagnose prostate primary without confirmatory IHC.
Related / See Also
↗ PARAGANGLIOMA (BLADDER)↗ High-grade PIN (HGPIN)+ Add: Adenosis (atypical adenomatous hyperplasia)+ Add: Rectal adenocarcinoma invading prostate
UROTHELIAL CARCINOMA (INVASIVE) AND CIS
Urological Epithelial Epithelioid Malignant 📋 5×
Most common bladder malignancy (~90%). Risk: smoking (3× risk), aromatic amines (industrial), cyclophosphamide, chronic catheterisation, schistosomiasis. Haematuria (painless) is the cardinal presenting symptom.
Clinical Context
  • Most common bladder malignancy (~90%). Risk: smoking (3× risk), aromatic amines (industrial), cyclophosphamide, chronic catheterisation, schistosomiasis. Haematuria (painless) is the cardinal presenting symptom.
  • CIS (flat high-grade urothelial carcinoma): high-grade dysplasia confined to urothelium; no invasion; erythematous flat lesion; high progression risk (~50% → invasion without treatment). BCG intravesical therapy first-line.
  • Staging: NMIBC (pTa/T1/CIS) vs MIBC (≥pT2). MIBC: radical cystectomy ± neoadjuvant cisplatin (MVAC/GC) or chemoradiotherapy (Trimodal). FGFR3 mutation: erdafitinib for metastatic.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Papillary urothelial carcinoma: papillary fronds with true fibrovascular cores lined by urothelium — LG (low-grade): orderly, mild atypia; HG (high-grade): disorderly, pleomorphic, mitoses.
  • CIS: flat full-thickness replacement of urothelium by high-grade dysplastic cells — discohesive, pleomorphic nuclei, prominent nucleoli; denuded urothelium (Pagetoid spread or complete denudation).
  • Lamina propria invasion (pT1): identify muscularis mucosae (thin, discontinuous — unlike muscularis propria); substaging T1a (above MM) vs T1b (below MM) — important but not mandatory in UK. Muscularis propria (detrusor): thick smooth muscle bundles — must be present in biopsy to stage pT2.
✚ Positive
CK7 – diffuseGATA3 – diffuse nuclear (~90% — most useful marker)Uroplakin II – variable (~60%, specific)CK20 – variablep63 – positiveTERT promoter mutation – ~80% (molecular)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
PSA – negative (prostate origin)CDX2 – negative (glandular origin)CK5/6 – variable (positive in squamous differentiation)p40 – negative unless squamous differentiation
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Prostatic adenocarcinoma invading bladder↗PSA+, NKX3.1+, AMACR+; GATA3 variable (positive in both — not discriminating); p63− (basal cells absent); clinical context
Squamous cell carcinoma (bladder)⚡p40+, CK5/6+; GATA3−/weak; keratinisation; associated with schistosomiasis/chronic catheterisation; HPV-independent
Plasmacytoid variant UCC↗GATA3+, CK7+; E-cadherin lost; discohesive single cells; CDH1 mutation; peritoneal spread
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • GATA3: most reliable urothelial marker — positive in ~90% UCC including CIS, plasmacytoid, micropapillary, sarcomatoid variants. Also positive in breast (use in combination with other markers).
  • Muscularis propria in biopsy: mandatory for staging — without MP, cannot confirm pT2. If absent, repeat TURBT requested. Report: 'Muscularis propria present/absent in specimen.'
  • Variant histologies: micropapillary (aggressive, early lymphovascular invasion, upstage), nested (bland morphology, deceptive — GATA3+), plasmacytoid (CDH1 loss, peritoneal spread), sarcomatoid (vimentin+, CK focally+, GATA3 may be lost).
  • BCG immunotherapy: intravesical — for CIS and high-risk NMIBC; mechanism T-cell mediated anti-tumour response; failure → radical cystectomy or nadofaragene firadenovec (gene therapy, FDA approved 2023).
  • Upper tract UCC (UTUC): ureter/renal pelvis — ureteric washings cytology; nephroureterectomy; Lynch syndrome association (MSH2 especially) — MSI testing on all UTUC.
🧫 Cytology Counterpart
↗ HIGH GRADE UROTHELIAL CARCINOMA — URINE CYTOLOGY (Paris V)
Related / See Also
↗ PLASMACYTOID UROTHELIAL CARCINOMA↗ SCHISTOSOMIASIS (UROGENITAL / INTESTINAL)↗ Prostatic adenocarcinoma invading bladder⚡ Squamous cell carcinoma (bladder)↗ Plasmacytoid variant UCC
OSTEOSARCOMA
Soft Tissue / Bone Bone Mixed Malignant 📋 2×
Most common primary malignant bone tumour. Bimodal: peak 10–20 years (adolescent — metaphysis of long bones, distal femur/proximal tibia/proximal humerus) and >65 years (secondary to Paget's disease/radiation/other bone lesion).
Clinical Context
  • Most common primary malignant bone tumour. Bimodal: peak 10–20 years (adolescent — metaphysis of long bones, distal femur/proximal tibia/proximal humerus) and >65 years (secondary to Paget's disease/radiation/other bone lesion).
  • Presents with pain ± soft tissue mass; Codman's triangle (periosteal reaction) + 'sunburst' pattern on X-ray. Alkaline phosphatase elevated. LDH correlates with tumour burden.
  • Macroscopy on bone resection: measure tumour, relationship to growth plate/epiphysis/soft tissue, joint involvement. Neoadjuvant chemotherapy response (% necrosis): >90% necrosis = good response (Huvos Grade III/IV) → better prognosis.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Malignant spindle/polygonal cells producing osteoid (pink amorphous extracellular matrix mineralising directly from tumour cells — without lamellar bone architecture). Osteoid production by tumour cells is the defining criterion.
  • Subtypes: osteoblastic (most common — abundant osteoid); chondroblastic (cartilaginous matrix dominant — may mimic chondrosarcoma); fibroblastic (fibrous pattern, minimal osteoid — worst response to chemo); telangiectatic (blood-filled spaces, minimal osteoid — DDx ABC); small cell (mimics Ewing).
  • Neoadjuvant response: % viable tumour at resection. Huvos: Grade I (<50% necrosis); II (50–90%); III (>90%); IV (100% necrosis). Grade III/IV = good responder.
✚ Positive
SATB2 – diffuse nuclear (osteoblastic differentiation marker; most useful)Vimentin – positiveALP – positive (alkaline phosphatase)MDM2/CDK4 – negative (parosteal osteosarcoma exception — secondary OS may show)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CD99 – negative/focal (Ewing positive)FLI1/NKX2.2 – negative (Ewing positive)Desmin – negativeCK – negative (excludes metaplastic carcinoma)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Ewing sarcoma / PNETCD99 diffuse membranous; NKX2.2+, FLI1+; EWSR1-FLI1 or EWSR1-ERG (FISH/RNA-seq); no osteoid; round cell; diaphysis/flat bones
Chondrosarcoma (conventional)Cartilaginous matrix (not osteoid); lobular architecture; S100+, SOX9+; IDH1/2 mutation (central chondrosarcoma); SATB2 negative
Malignant fibrous histiocytoma of bone / undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma⚡Diagnosis of exclusion — no osteoid production; SATB2 negative or focal; extensive IHC panel required
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • SATB2 IHC: diffuse nuclear positivity = osteoblastic differentiation. Present in osteosarcoma, giant cell tumour of bone, and some colorectal carcinoma — contextualise with site and morphology.
  • Chemotherapy (MAP regimen): methotrexate + doxorubicin (Adriamycin) + cisplatin (Platinol). 5-year OS ~70% for localised; ~20–30% for metastatic.
  • Macroscopy protocol: bone resections require radiological-pathological correlation. Mark inked margins, confirm neoadjuvant treatment history, assess extent of necrosis (% viable tumour). Bivalve and photograph.
  • Paget's disease-associated OS: older patients; polyostotic Paget's; aggressive; poor prognosis. Radiation-associated OS: long latency (5–20 years); any bone in radiation field.
  • Telangiectatic OS: blood-filled cystic spaces; minimal osteoid; mimics aneurysmal bone cyst (ABC) on imaging — biopsy always needed for diagnosis. Good response to chemotherapy.
Related / See Also
+ Add: Ewing sarcoma / PNET+ Add: Chondrosarcoma (conventional)⚡ Malignant fibrous histiocytoma of bone / undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma
PITUITARY ADENOMA (PITUITARY NEUROENDOCRINE TUMOUR — PitNET)
Endocrine Neuroendocrine Well-differentiated Intermediate 📋 1×
WHO 2022: renamed PitNET (pituitary neuroendocrine tumour). Classified by cell lineage (pit-1/SF-1/TPIT) not solely hormone secretion. Microadenoma <10mm; macroadenoma ≥10mm.
Clinical Context
  • WHO 2022: renamed PitNET (pituitary neuroendocrine tumour). Classified by cell lineage (pit-1/SF-1/TPIT) not solely hormone secretion. Microadenoma <10mm; macroadenoma ≥10mm.
  • Functional: prolactinoma (most common — hyperprolactinaemia, amenorrhoea/galactorrhoea, infertility; treat with dopamine agonists cabergoline first); GH-secreting (acromegaly — IGF-1 elevated); ACTH-secreting (Cushing's disease); TSH/FSH/LH-secreting (rare).
  • Frozen section context: transsphenoidal hypophysectomy — confirm pituitary tissue/adenoma present vs fibrous tissue/normal gland. Intraoperative smear effective.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Monomorphic population of cells with uniform round nuclei, stippled 'salt-and-pepper' chromatin, absent/small nucleoli, and moderate cytoplasm arranged in sheets, trabecular, or pseudorosette patterns — NE morphology.
  • Loss of normal acinar architecture (reticulin stain: normal pituitary — acinar pattern; adenoma — reticulin-poor, no acini). Sinusoidal vascular pattern. No mitoses in typical adenoma.
  • Invasion: into cavernous sinus, bone, dura — 'invasive' behaviour. Pituitary carcinoma (rare): proven CSF/systemic metastasis only — morphology alone insufficient for carcinoma diagnosis.
✚ Positive
Synaptophysin – diffuseChromogranin A – positiveINSM1 – diffuse nuclearPit-1 – GH/PRL/TSH lineageSF-1 – gonadotroph lineageTPIT – corticotroph lineage (ACTH)Specific hormone IHC (GH/PRL/ACTH/TSH/FSH/LH) – lineage classification
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
Ki67 – <3% in typical adenoma (>3% = higher proliferation — not malignant alone)p53 – wild-type typically
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Craniopharyngioma (adamantinomatous)Squamous epithelium + 'wet keratin' nodules + palisading; calcification; CTNNB1 mutation (nuclear β-catenin+); non-NE; cholesterol clefts
Meningioma (sphenoid wing)Whorls; psammoma bodies; EMA+; SSTR2+; PR+ (~70%); not NE markers positive
Normal anterior pituitary vs adenomaNormal: reticulin-rich acinar pattern; mixed cell population; adenoma: reticulin-poor, monomorphic cell population — reticulin stain essential on frozen
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Reticulin stain: mandatory on pituitary adenoma — shows loss of normal acinar pattern. Most useful single stain for confirming adenoma on frozen section.
  • PitNET classification (WHO 2022): lineage-based (Pit-1/SF-1/TPIT/immature PIT-1/double negative) is prognostically superior to hormone-based classification.
  • High-risk PitNET ('aggressive pituitary adenoma'): Ki67 >3%, p53 immunoreactivity, invasion — increased recurrence risk; requires closer follow-up.
  • Temozolomide: treatment for aggressive/recurrent PitNET — MGMT promoter methylation predicts response.
  • Frozen section report: 'Fragments of pituitary neuroendocrine tumour/adenoma — reticulin stain shows loss of acinar pattern. No normal pituitary parenchyma in sampled tissue / Adjacent normal pituitary present.'
Related / See Also
+ Add: Craniopharyngioma (adamantinomatous)+ Add: Meningioma (sphenoid wing)+ Add: Normal anterior pituitary vs adenoma
GRANULOMATOUS PROSTATITIS (INCLUDING BCG-INDUCED)
Urological Inflammatory Granulomatous Variable 📋 4×
BCG-induced (most common specific form): 4–6 weeks post-intravesical BCG therapy for bladder NMIBC; granulomatous prostatic inflammation from BCG tracking down prostatic ducts. Often asymptomatic; PSA may be elevated (mimics carcinoma clinically).
Clinical Context
  • BCG-induced (most common specific form): 4–6 weeks post-intravesical BCG therapy for bladder NMIBC; granulomatous prostatic inflammation from BCG tracking down prostatic ducts. Often asymptomatic; PSA may be elevated (mimics carcinoma clinically).
  • Non-specific granulomatous prostatitis: related to ruptured prostatic ducts (extravasated secretions); foreign body-type giant cells. Infectious: TB (ZN stain, culture), fungal (GMS/PAS), parasitic.
  • Xanthomatous prostatitis: CD68+ foam cells; associated with high cholesterol; may mimic high-grade prostate carcinoma on biopsy (low N:C, pale cytoplasm).
Key Microscopic Features
  • BCG: non-caseating epithelioid granulomas with Langhans giant cells in and around prostatic ducts/acini; variable central necrosis; lymphocytic infiltrate; no significant atypia in adjacent epithelium.
  • Non-specific: granulomas centred on ruptured ducts; foreign body giant cells around secretions/calcifications; no organisms on special stains.
  • Infectious TB: caseating necrosis; ZN stain positive (confirm with culture/PCR); systemic TB features.
✚ Positive
CD68 – epithelioid histiocytes and giant cellsZN – Mycobacteria (TB)GMS/PAS – fungi
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
PSA – negative in granulomas (confirms non-neoplastic)AMACR – negativep63 absent in areas — adjacent benign glands retain p63
Top Differentials + Discriminators
High-grade prostate carcinoma↗PSA+, NKX3.1+, AMACR+, p63− (basal cells absent); no granulomas; prominent nucleoli; infiltrative glands
Xanthomatous prostatitisCD68+ foam cells without giant cells; no granulomas; PSA−, AMACR−; statin-associated; clinical context
Malakoplakia (prostate)↗CD68+ von Hansemann cells with Michaelis-Guttmann bodies (PAS+, von Kossa+); E. coli; no granulomas
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • BCG granulomatous prostatitis: clinical context essential — always ask about BCG treatment history. PSA may be markedly elevated — do not diagnose carcinoma based on PSA alone in BCG-treated patient.
  • Systemic BCG infection (BCG-osis): rare severe complication — disseminated granulomas (liver, lung, bone marrow); treat with anti-TB therapy (isoniazid + rifampicin ± ethambutol).
  • ZN stain in BCG prostatitis: BCG organisms usually not identifiable (very few viable bacilli by the time of TURP biopsy) — granulomas present but ZN often negative. Clinical history is the discriminating factor.
  • Florid granulomatous prostatitis with PSA elevation: clinical trap — biopsy confirms benign aetiology; avoid radical treatment.
  • Non-BCG specific granulomatous prostatitis: 'allergic'/eosinophilic subtype (eosinophil-rich, associated with asthma/systemic eosinophilia) — distinct from BCG-induced.
Related / See Also
↗ HEPATIC GRANULOMA — DIFFERENTIAL FRAMEWORK↗ High-grade prostate carcinoma+ Add: Xanthomatous prostatitis↗ Malakoplakia (prostate)
GRANULOSA CELL TUMOUR (OVARY)
Gynaecological Sex Cord-Stromal Epithelioid Malignant 📋 1×
Adult type (AGCT, ~95%): perimenopausal/postmenopausal; oestrogenic symptoms (endometrial hyperplasia/carcinoma — always sample endometrium); postmenopausal bleeding. Juvenile type (JGCT, ~5%): <30 years; isosexual precocious puberty.
Clinical Context
  • Adult type (AGCT, ~95%): perimenopausal/postmenopausal; oestrogenic symptoms (endometrial hyperplasia/carcinoma — always sample endometrium); postmenopausal bleeding. Juvenile type (JGCT, ~5%): <30 years; isosexual precocious puberty.
  • FOXL2 C134W (402 C→G) mutation: present in ~97% of AGCT — essentially pathognomonic. JGCT: AKT1 E17K mutation.
  • Indolent but relapse after >10 years common — lifelong surveillance. Inhibin A/B and AMH (anti-Müllerian hormone) as serum markers for monitoring.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Adult type: microfollicular pattern with Call-Exner bodies (small rosette-like structures with eosinophilic central material — pathognomonic); diffuse, macrofollicular, insular, trabecular, watered-silk/moiré patterns.
  • Cells: pale/grooved ('coffee-bean') nuclei — longitudinal nuclear groove is characteristic. Scant cytoplasm. Mitoses variable.
  • Juvenile type: follicles with basophilic mucinous fluid; cells with abundant cytoplasm and rounder nuclei (less grooved); luteinisation; higher mitotic rate.
✚ Positive
Inhibin A – diffuse (most sensitive)Calretinin – diffuseSF1 – nuclearFOXL2 – nuclearCD56 – positiveWT1 – positive
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
EMA – negativeCK7 – negative/focalCK20 – negativePAX8 – negative (gynaecological carcinoma positive)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Endometrioid carcinoma of ovary (low-grade)↗CK7+, PAX8+, ER+, EMA+; Inhibin−, Calretinin−; tubular/glandular architecture; no Call-Exner bodies; squamous morules
Carcinoid tumour (primary ovarian)Synaptophysin/CgA diffuse+; Inhibin−; OTP+ (lung/carcinoid); no grooved nuclei; no Call-Exner bodies
Small cell carcinoma of ovary (hypercalcaemic type)↗SMARCA4-deficient (BRG1 IHC loss); aggressive; young women; hypercalcaemia; SMARCA4+/− cocktail discriminates
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • FOXL2 C134W mutation: present in ~97% AGCT — almost pathognomonic. Can be tested on FFPE tissue by PCR/Sanger sequencing. JGCT: FOXL2 wild-type.
  • Call-Exner bodies: small rosettes with central eosinophilic material (basement membrane) — pathognomonic but not always present. Nuclear grooves ('coffee-bean') are more consistently identified.
  • Endometrium: always curettage — oestrogen secretion → endometrial hyperplasia in 25–50%; synchronous endometrial carcinoma in ~5%. FIGO staging for AGCT.
  • Inhibin A: serum marker for monitoring recurrence. AMH (anti-Müllerian hormone): highly sensitive and specific — rising AMH precedes clinical relapse.
  • Late recurrence: AGCT can recur 5–30 years after initial diagnosis — lifelong follow-up essential. Surgery ± platinum-based chemotherapy for recurrence.
Related / See Also
↗ OVARIAN FIBROMA / FIBROTHECOMA↗ HIGH GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA (OVARY / PERITONEUM / FALLOPIAN TUBE)↗ Endometrioid carcinoma of ovary (low-grade)+ Add: Carcinoid tumour (primary ovarian)↗ Small cell carcinoma of ovary (hypercalcaemic type)
CASTLEMAN'S DISEASE (GIANT LYMPH NODE HYPERPLASIA)
Haempath Lymphoma Mixed Variable 📋 1×
