Unicentric (UCD): single enlarged LN/mass; usually hyaline-vascular type; mediastinum most common; young adults; surgical excision curative.
Clinical Context
  • Unicentric (UCD): single enlarged LN/mass; usually hyaline-vascular type; mediastinum most common; young adults; surgical excision curative.
  • Multicentric (MCD): systemic — lymphadenopathy + splenomegaly + systemic inflammation (fever, weight loss, elevated CRP/IL-6). HHV8-associated MCD (HIV+/immunocompromised): plasmablastic variant, HHV8 LANA-1+; treat with rituximab ± chemotherapy.
  • idiopathic MCD (iMCD): HHV8-negative; IL-6 driven; TAFRO syndrome subtype (thrombocytopaenia, anasarca, fever, reticulin fibrosis, organomegaly); siltuximab (anti-IL-6) or tocilizumab.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Hyaline-vascular type (UCD): small atrophic follicles with 'lollipop' vessels penetrating germinal centres (dysplastic GC); expanded mantle zones with concentric 'onion-skin' arrangement; interfollicular vascular proliferation; hyalinised vessels.
  • Plasma cell type (MCD): large follicles with prominent germinal centres; marked interfollicular plasmacytosis (sheets of mature plasma cells); HHV8+ plasmablasts in HHV8-MCD (LANA-1 nuclear).
  • Mixed type: features of both. Stromal changes: prominent vessels; clusters of follicular dendritic cells (FDC); CD21+/CD23+ FDC meshwork.
✚ Positive
CD20 – follicular B cellsCD138 – plasma cells (plasma cell type)HHV8 LANA-1 – HHV8-MCD plasmablastsCD21/CD23 – FDC meshwork (hyaline-vascular)IgG4 – must assess (IgG4-RD mimic)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
IgG4 – negative (IgG4-RD has >10 IgG4+/HPF)EBV EBER – negative (HHV8-MCD: HHV8+, EBV−; lymphoma-associated: EBV may be positive)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
IgG4-related lymphadenopathyIgG4/IgG ratio >40%; storiform fibrosis; IgG4+ plasma cells >10/HPF; serum IgG4 elevated; steroid-responsive; multiorgan involvement
Follicular lymphoma (interfollicular)↗CD10+, BCL2+; clonal B cells; BCL2/IgH rearrangement t(14;18); disrupted FDC meshwork; no onion-skin mantle zones
Reactive follicular hyperplasia↗Preserved LN architecture; polarised GCs (light/dark zones); no onion-skin mantle; no lollipop vessels; clinical context
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Siltuximab (anti-IL-6 chimeric antibody): approved for HHV8-negative iMCD. Tocilizumab (anti-IL-6R): off-label but widely used. Both target the IL-6 pathway driving MCD.
  • TAFRO syndrome: severe iMCD subtype — thrombocytopaenia, anasarca (fluid overload), fever, reticulin fibrosis (marrow), organomegaly. High mortality; aggressive immunosuppression required.
  • HHV8-MCD: always consider in HIV+ patient with systemic lymphadenopathy. LANA-1 IHC on biopsy confirms. Rituximab highly effective. Risk of Kaposi sarcoma and PEL.
  • Plasma cell dyscrasia: POEMS syndrome (polyneuropathy, organomegaly, endocrinopathy, M-protein, skin changes) — associated with plasma cell type Castleman's; vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) elevated.
  • Exam trap: hyaline-vascular Castleman's — 'lollipop' vessels penetrating GCs are pathognomonic; do not diagnose as follicular lymphoma (no BCL2, polyclonal plasma cells, FDC meshwork preserved).
Related / See Also
↗ DIFFUSE LARGE B-CELL LYMPHOMA (DLBCL, NOS)↗ NODULAR LYMPHOCYTE PREDOMINANT HODGKIN LYMPHOMA (NLPHL)+ Add: IgG4-related lymphadenopathy↗ Follicular lymphoma (interfollicular)↗ Reactive follicular hyperplasia
ANAPLASTIC LARGE CELL LYMPHOMA (ALCL)
Haempath Lymphoma Round cell Malignant 📋 1×
T-cell lymphoma defined by strong CD30 expression + ALK protein (ALK+ ALCL) or ALK-negative. ALK+ ALCL: young adults (median 30 years); excellent prognosis (~80% 5-year OS). ALK− ALCL: older; worse prognosis (~50% 5-year OS).
Clinical Context
  • T-cell lymphoma defined by strong CD30 expression + ALK protein (ALK+ ALCL) or ALK-negative. ALK+ ALCL: young adults (median 30 years); excellent prognosis (~80% 5-year OS). ALK− ALCL: older; worse prognosis (~50% 5-year OS).
  • ALK+ ALCL: NPM-ALK fusion t(2;5)(p23;q35) in ~75%; other ALK fusions. Brentuximab vedotin (anti-CD30 ADC) highly active — first-line BV+CHP in ALK− and relapsed ALK+.
  • Breast implant-associated ALCL (BIA-ALCL): ALK−, CD30+; EFS within capsule; indolent — capsulectomy often curative. ALK− systemic ALCL: aggressive — chemotherapy.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Hallmark cells: large pleomorphic cells with eccentric horseshoe/kidney-shaped nuclei, prominent nucleoli, abundant cytoplasm — 'hallmark cells' are pathognomonic.
  • Sinusoidal/paracortical growth pattern; cohesive sheets; often invades subcapsular sinus. Common pattern: large cell with variable small cell admixture.
  • Small cell variant: predominance of small cells; scattered hallmark cells — easily missed; requires CD30 IHC for diagnosis. Lymphohistiocytic variant: many reactive histiocytes obscuring tumour cells.
✚ Positive
CD30 – diffuse strong (defining marker)EMA – positive (~70%)ALK – nuclear+cytoplasmic (NPM-ALK); cytoplasmic only (other fusions)Perforin/TIA-1/Granzyme B – cytotoxic T-cell markers
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CD20 – negativePAX5 – negative (B-cell exclusion)CD3 – negative or partial (aberrant loss common)ALK – negative in ALK− ALCL
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Classical Hodgkin lymphoma⚡RS cells: CD30+, CD15+, CD45−, PAX5 weak+; ALK−; CD20 weak/−; no hallmark cells; EMA−; inflammatory background
DLBCL (anaplastic variant)↗CD20+, PAX5+, BCL6+; CD30 variable; ALK−; EMA−; B-cell clonality; MYC/BCL2 rearrangements
Peripheral T-cell lymphoma, NOSCD30 negative or weak; no hallmark cells; ALK−; CD2/3/5/7 aberrant expression; TCR clonality
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • ALK IHC pattern: NPM-ALK t(2;5) → nuclear + cytoplasmic (both compartments). Other ALK fusions (TPM3, CLTC) → cytoplasmic only. Pattern indicates fusion partner.
  • Brentuximab vedotin (BV): anti-CD30 ADC delivering MMAE. BV+CHP (cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, prednisone): standard first-line for CD30+ peripheral T-cell lymphomas including ALK− ALCL and PTCL.
  • BIA-ALCL (breast implant): WHO 2022 — in situ (within seroma/capsule): excellent prognosis with capsulectomy alone. Invasive (beyond capsule): treat as systemic ALCL. Textured implants highest risk.
  • Sarcomatoid/anaplastic ALCL: hallmark cells in a sarcomatoid spindle cell background — may mimic carcinoma or sarcoma. CD30 + EMA + ALK panel essential in any undifferentiated pleomorphic tumour.
  • ALK inhibitors (crizotinib, ceritinib): active in relapsed/refractory ALK+ ALCL — same pathway as ALK+ NSCLC.
Related / See Also
↗ CLASSICAL HODGKIN LYMPHOMA (cHL)↗ DIFFUSE LARGE B-CELL LYMPHOMA (DLBCL, NOS)⚡ Classical Hodgkin lymphoma↗ DLBCL (anaplastic variant)+ Add: Peripheral T-cell lymphoma, NOS
MARGINAL ZONE LYMPHOMA (MALT / NODAL / SPLENIC)
Haempath Lymphoma Mixed Intermediate 📋 1×
Three types: extranodal MALT lymphoma (most common); nodal MZL; splenic MZL. MALT: stomach (H. pylori-driven — eradication therapy curative in localised gastric MALT); lung, ocular adnexa, salivary gland (Sjögren), thyroid (Hashimoto's), skin.
Clinical Context
  • Three types: extranodal MALT lymphoma (most common); nodal MZL; splenic MZL. MALT: stomach (H. pylori-driven — eradication therapy curative in localised gastric MALT); lung, ocular adnexa, salivary gland (Sjögren), thyroid (Hashimoto's), skin.
  • Gastric MALT: H. pylori eradication cures ~75% of localised (Stage IE) disease. Non-responders: RT (30Gy) or rituximab. API2-MALT1 t(11;18): associated with H. pylori-independent MALT — no eradication response.
  • Pulmonary MALT (lung case in past exams): indolent; consolidation on CT; can be watched; rituximab or RT for symptomatic disease.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Marginal zone/monocytoid B cells: medium-sized cells with moderate pale cytoplasm, small irregular nuclei, inconspicuous nucleoli; centrocyte-like cells. Lymphoepithelial lesions (LELs): tumour cells infiltrating and destroying glandular epithelium — characteristic of MALT.
  • Follicular colonisation: tumour cells filling and expanding pre-existing reactive follicles — mimics follicular lymphoma but CD10−, BCL2 variable.
  • Plasma cell differentiation (20–30%): Dutcher bodies (intranuclear pseudoinclusions containing Ig) — characteristic. Dutcher bodies + monocytoid cells = MALT.
✚ Positive
CD20 – positiveCD79a – positiveBCL2 – variableIgM – surface (usually)IRTA1 – marginal zone marker
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CD5 – negative (positive in MCL/CLL)CD10 – negative (positive in FL)CD23 – negativeCyclin D1 – negative (positive in MCL)BCL6 – negative (positive in FL/DLBCL)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Follicular lymphoma↗CD10+, BCL6+, BCL2+; t(14;18)/BCL2-IgH; no LELs; nodular pattern with neoplastic follicles; no monocytoid cells
Mantle cell lymphomaCD5+, Cyclin D1+, SOX11+; t(11;14)/CCND1-IgH; blastoid variant aggressive; no LELs
Reactive lymphoid hyperplasia (mucosa-associated)No LELs; polyclonal (kappa/lambda mix); no Dutcher bodies; no clonal B cells on flow/PCR
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • API2-MALT1 t(11;18): H. pylori eradication-resistant — identify before treating; RT or chemotherapy required.
  • Other MALT translocations: t(14;18) BCL10-IgH; t(3;14) FOXP1-IgH; t(1;14) BCL10-IgH — all activate NF-κB pathway.
  • Sjögren-associated MALT: parotid gland; 5% lifetime risk of lymphoma in Sjögren's; MZL most common. Monitor with ultrasound and biopsy if gland enlargement.
  • Pulmonary MALT: consolidation pattern on CT; often incidental; bronchoscopy + BAL + transbronchial biopsy or VATS for diagnosis; excellent prognosis.
  • Transformation to DLBCL: ~10% of MALT lymphomas; associated with large cell areas on biopsy; treat as DLBCL.
Related / See Also
↗ DIFFUSE LARGE B-CELL LYMPHOMA (DLBCL, NOS)↗ BURKITT LYMPHOMA↗ Follicular lymphoma+ Add: Mantle cell lymphoma+ Add: Reactive lymphoid hyperplasia (mucosa-associated)
CARDIAC VALVE VEGETATIONS — DIFFERENTIAL FRAMEWORK
Haempath Cardiovascular Mixed Variable 📋 1×
Infective endocarditis (IE): fever + new murmur + bacteraemia (Duke criteria). Most common organisms: Staph. aureus (acute, destructive, native and prosthetic valves); viridans streptococci (subacute, pre-existing valvular disease); Enterococcus; HACEK group; Coxiella burnetii (Q fever); fungi (Candida — prosthetic, immunocompromised).
Clinical Context
  • Infective endocarditis (IE): fever + new murmur + bacteraemia (Duke criteria). Most common organisms: Staph. aureus (acute, destructive, native and prosthetic valves); viridans streptococci (subacute, pre-existing valvular disease); Enterococcus; HACEK group; Coxiella burnetii (Q fever); fungi (Candida — prosthetic, immunocompromised).
  • Non-bacterial thrombotic endocarditis (NBTE/Libman-Sacks): SLE (mitral, both surfaces); antiphospholipid syndrome; marantic endocarditis (sterile thrombus in cachexia/malignancy — tricuspid/mitral, atrial surface).
  • Mitral valve resection: measure vegetation size, describe cusps, identify perforation/destruction.
Key Microscopic Features
  • IE vegetations: fibrin-platelet thrombus with bacterial colonies (Gram stain) ± acute inflammatory cells; underlying valve destruction; granulation tissue in subacute. Gram+ cocci in clusters = Staph; Gram+ cocci in chains = Strep.
  • NBTE/marantic: sterile fibrin-platelet thrombus; no organisms; no inflammation; bland; adherent to valve closure line (atrial surface of mitral/tricuspid).
  • Libman-Sacks (SLE): small, irregular, warty vegetations on both surfaces of mitral valve; fibrin + mild inflammation; immune complex deposition on valve; often sterile.
✚ Positive
Gram stain – bacteria identificationGMS/PAS – fungiFibrin – PTAH/Martius Scarlet BlueIgG/IgM/C3 – Libman-Sacks valve (lupus)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
N/A — morphological + microbiological diagnosis
Top Differentials + Discriminators
IE vs NBTE vs Libman-SacksIE: Gram+ organisms; acute inflammation; valve destruction. NBTE: sterile; no inflammation; atrial surface; malignancy/cachexia. Libman-Sacks: both surfaces; SLE; immune complex IF
Rheumatic valvular diseaseChronic — fibrosis, calcification, commissural fusion; Aschoff nodules (acute) — fibrinoid necrosis + Anitschkow cells ('caterpillar' nuclei); chronic rheumatic changes
Papillary fibroelastoma⚡Benign; aortic valve; papillary fronds with elastic cores; no inflammation; no organisms; incidental finding on echo/surgery
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Gram stain mandatory on all valve vegetation specimens — essential for organism identification and guides antibiotic de-escalation.
  • Culture of valve tissue: always submit fresh tissue for microbiology (aerobic + anaerobic + fungal + Coxiella/Brucella serology). Molecular (16S rRNA PCR) for culture-negative IE.
  • Libman-Sacks: antiphospholipid antibodies (lupus anticoagulant/anti-cardiolipin/anti-β2GPI) — anticoagulation to prevent systemic emboli.
  • Marantic endocarditis: associated with advanced malignancy, hypercoagulable states; systemic emboli common; no antibiotic therapy needed (sterile).
  • Pathologist's role: report — vegetation size/location, organism Gram stain morphology, valve structural damage (perforation, cusp involvement), presence/absence of abscess.
Related / See Also
+ Add: IE vs NBTE vs Libman-Sacks+ Add: Rheumatic valvular disease⚡ Papillary fibroelastoma
BASAL CELL CARCINOMA (BCC)
Skin / Dermatopath Epithelial Basaloid Malignant
Most common malignancy worldwide. UV-driven; sun-exposed skin; head and neck >80%. Locally destructive; metastasis exceptionally rare (<0.1%).
Clinical Context
  • Most common malignancy worldwide. UV-driven; sun-exposed skin; head and neck >80%. Locally destructive; metastasis exceptionally rare (<0.1%).
  • Risk factors: fair skin, chronic UV, immunosuppression, Gorlin syndrome (PTCH1 germline — multiple BCCs in young, jaw keratocysts, medulloblastoma, calcified falx).
  • Subtypes (prognostically relevant): nodular (most common, ~60%); superficial (~25%); infiltrative/morphoeic (~10%, high recurrence); basosquamous (aggressive, metastatic potential).
Key Microscopic Features
  • Basaloid lobules/nests/trabeculae arising from epidermis; peripheral nuclear palisading; retraction artefact (clefting between tumour and stroma) — characteristic.
  • Nodular: large well-circumscribed lobules, mucin-rich stroma. Superficial: buds from underside of epidermis, multifocal. Infiltrative/morphoeic: thin cords and strands in dense sclerotic stroma, no palisading — subtle, difficult margins.
  • Basosquamous (metatypical): mixed BCC and SCC features; keratinisation within basaloid nests; no peripheral palisading in squamoid areas — behaves aggressively, must be identified.
✚ Positive
BerEP4 – diffuse (most useful BCC marker; negative in SCC)BCL2 – diffuse in BCC (patchy in SCC)CK14 – positivep63 – positive
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
EMA – negative (positive in SCC)CK20 – negative in BCC (positive in Merkel cell carcinoma)High molecular weight CK (34βE12) – negative/weak in BCC (strong in SCC)Androgen receptor – negative in most BCC (positive in trichoepithelioma)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
TrichoepitheliomaBerEP4 negative/focal; CK20+ Merkel cells at periphery; papillary mesenchymal bodies; fibrotic stroma without clefting; AR positive
Basosquamous carcinoma vs SCC⚡Basosquamous: BerEP4+ in basaloid areas; transition zones; both keratinisation and basaloid palisading. SCC: EMA+, BerEP4−
Merkel cell carcinomaCK20 paranuclear dot+; neuroendocrine markers (Synaptophysin, CgA, INSM1); BerEP4 negative; MCPyV in ~80%
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Gorlin syndrome (PTCH1): autosomal dominant; PTCH1 loss-of-function → constitutive Hedgehog pathway activation. Multiple BCCs (often >5 by age 30), jaw keratocysts (odontogenic), medulloblastoma (desmoplastic), calcified falx, rib anomalies.
  • Hedgehog pathway inhibitors: vismodegib/sonidegib for locally advanced/metastatic BCC. SMO mutation = resistance mechanism.
  • Reporting: subtype is mandatory — infiltrative/morphoeic changes management (wider margins, Mohs). Basosquamous must be identified and communicated to MDT.
  • Excision margins: nodular/superficial = 4mm peripheral margin. Morphoeic/infiltrative = Mohs micrographic surgery or wider margins (≥5mm).
  • Exam trap: fibrotic stroma + thin cords without palisading = morphoeic BCC — easily missed at low power; sclerotic dermis mimics scar.
Related / See Also
↗ CUTANEOUS SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA (cSCC)↗ MELANOMA (CUTANEOUS)↗ INFLAMMATORY SKIN DISEASE — PATTERN FRAMEWORK+ Add: Trichoepithelioma⚡ Basosquamous carcinoma vs SCC+ Add: Merkel cell carcinoma
SPITZ NAEVUS AND ATYPICAL SPITZ TUMOUR
Skin / Dermatopath Melanocytic Spindle/epithelioid Variable
Spitz naevus: benign melanocytic proliferation; children/young adults (peak <20 years). Dome-shaped pink/red papule; face and extremities. Classical Spitz naevus in children is benign.
Clinical Context
  • Spitz naevus: benign melanocytic proliferation; children/young adults (peak <20 years). Dome-shaped pink/red papule; face and extremities. Classical Spitz naevus in children is benign.
  • Atypical Spitz tumour (AST): morphologically between Spitz naevus and spitzoid melanoma; uncertain biological potential. Adults >puberty, larger (>1cm), asymmetry. Sentinel LN positivity ~30% but does NOT predict outcome like in conventional melanoma.
  • Spitzoid melanoma: true melanoma with spitzoid morphology; aggressive. Adults; large; ulcerated; deep. Must distinguish from AST — molecular testing increasingly essential.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Classic Spitz: symmetrical, well-circumscribed, wedge-shaped junctional/compound proliferation of large epithelioid and/or spindled melanocytes; nests 'rain down' from epidermis.
  • Kamino bodies: dull pink eosinophilic globules at DEJ — characteristic of Spitz naevus (PAS+). Maturation with depth (nests become smaller, cells become smaller). No deep mitoses. Clefting around junctional nests.
  • Atypical features (AST/spitzoid melanoma): asymmetry, poor circumscription, ulceration, deep mitoses (>2/mm²), lack of maturation, pagetoid spread beyond lesion, deep extension into subcutis, high Ki67 in deep component, sheet-like growth.
✚ Positive
S100 – diffuse (all melanocytic)SOX10 – diffuseMelan-A – diffuse (stronger in Spitz than melanoma)HMB45 – gradient: strong superficial, lost deep (maturation) in benign Spitz; diffuse/lost maturation in melanomaALK – positive in ~30% Spitz naevi (ALK fusions); not diagnostic of malignancy
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
p16 (CDKN2A) – retained in Spitz naevus; LOST in spitzoid melanoma (homozygous 9p21 deletion — most useful discriminator)PRAME – negative in Spitz naevus; positive/diffuse in spitzoid melanoma (~75%)Ki67 – low (<2%) in deep component of benign Spitz; high in deep component = worrying
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Spitzoid melanomaAsymmetry; ulceration; deep mitoses; no maturation; p16 loss (FISH 9p21 homozygous deletion); PRAME diffuse+; TERT promoter mutation
Conventional melanomaPagetoid spread; consumption of epidermis; regression; sheet-like growth; BRAF V600E (rarely in Spitz); atypical mitoses
Dysplastic naevus↗Smaller melanocytes; lentiginous junctional growth; bridging of rete ridges; lamellar/concentric eosinophilic fibroplasia; no Kamino bodies; no spindle/epithelioid cells
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • FISH panel (Gerami): 6p25 gain, 6q23 loss, 11q13 gain, 8q24 gain, 9p21 homozygous loss. 9p21 homozygous deletion (CDKN2A) = most specific for spitzoid melanoma vs Spitz naevus.
  • Kinase fusions define Spitz tumours: HRAS (11p15) in classic Spitz; ALK, ROS1, NTRK1/2/3, RET, MET, BRAF non-V600E fusions. These fusions are NOT seen in conventional melanoma.
  • PRAME IHC: diffuse nuclear positivity in melanoma; negative in benign naevi. Increasingly used as first-line discriminator. Not 100% specific — interpret in context.
  • AST management: complete excision with margins; SLNB is controversial (positive SLN does NOT upstage to melanoma in spitzoid lesions — different biology). MDT discussion mandatory.
  • Exam trap: Spitz naevus in a child = benign; Spitz-like lesion in an adult (>40 years) = treat with suspicion; molecular workup essential. Never diagnose 'Spitz naevus' in adults without excluding spitzoid melanoma.
Related / See Also
↗ MELANOMA (CUTANEOUS)↗ INFLAMMATORY SKIN DISEASE — PATTERN FRAMEWORK+ Add: Spitzoid melanoma+ Add: Conventional melanoma↗ Dysplastic naevus
BULLOUS PEMPHIGOID (BP)
Skin / Dermatopath Inflammatory Subepidermal blistering Variable
Most common autoimmune blistering disease. Elderly (>70 years); tense blisters on erythematous/urticarial base; flexural and trunk distribution; pruritus often severe; Nikolsky sign NEGATIVE (unlike pemphigus).
Clinical Context
  • Most common autoimmune blistering disease. Elderly (>70 years); tense blisters on erythematous/urticarial base; flexural and trunk distribution; pruritus often severe; Nikolsky sign NEGATIVE (unlike pemphigus).
  • Drug-associated: DPP-4 inhibitors (gliptins), PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint inhibitors, diuretics. Drug history essential.
  • Mucosal involvement in ~10–30% (unlike pemphigus vulgaris where mucosal is predominant). Mortality ~25% at 1 year in elderly — systemic steroids carry significant morbidity.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Subepidermal blister with intact roof (epidermis); eosinophil-rich infiltrate in blister cavity and upper dermis — hallmark. Perivascular eosinophils in pre-bullous (urticarial) phase.
  • Dermal papillary oedema; eosinophilic spongiosis in early/pre-bullous lesions (eosinophils infiltrating epidermis — mimics drug reaction/eczema).
  • PAS stain: BM at FLOOR of blister (epidermis is the roof) — confirms subepidermal split. Contrast pemphigus where BM is at the base (intraepidermal split).
✚ Positive
DIF (perilesional skin): linear IgG + C3 at BMZ (basement membrane zone) — diagnostic patternSalt-split skin: IgG on EPIDERMAL (roof) side — distinguishes from EBA (dermal/floor side)BP180 (NC16A domain) ELISA – positive (titre correlates with disease activity)BP230 ELISA – positive (~60%)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
DIF: IgA linear at BMZ – absent (present in linear IgA disease)DIF: intercellular IgG – absent (present in pemphigus)Salt-split skin: IgG on dermal (floor) side – absent in BP (present in EBA, anti-laminin-332 pemphigoid)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Pemphigus vulgaris↗Intraepidermal (suprabasal) blister; acantholysis; DIF intercellular IgG ('chicken wire'); Dsg3 ± Dsg1 antibodies; Nikolsky positive
Epidermolysis bullosa acquisita (EBA)Subepidermal blister; DIF linear IgG BMZ (same as BP); salt-split: dermal (floor) side — key distinction; collagen VII target; scarring/milia
Dermatitis herpetiformis (DH)↗Granular IgA deposits at dermal papillae on DIF (NOT linear); neutrophilic microabscesses at dermal papillae; coeliac association; anti-tTG antibodies
Linear IgA diseaseLinear IgA (not IgG) at BMZ on DIF; heterogeneous blister contents; drug-associated (vancomycin); crown of jewels pattern
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • DIF is MANDATORY for all blistering diseases — snap-freeze perilesional skin in Michel's medium (NOT formalin). Take perilesional skin (not within blister) for DIF.
  • Pre-bullous BP: urticarial plaques/eczematous changes WITHOUT blisters — eosinophilic spongiosis on biopsy; DIF still positive. Easily misdiagnosed as eczema/drug reaction.
  • Salt-split skin technique: distinguishes BP (epidermal side = BP180/230 targets at hemidesmosomes) from EBA (dermal side = collagen VII at anchoring fibrils).
  • Treatment: potent topical steroids (clobetasol — superior to systemic steroids in RCTs); doxycycline; systemic prednisolone for severe/refractory; rituximab emerging.
  • Checkpoint inhibitor-associated BP: PD-1/PD-L1 therapy; may persist after drug cessation; manage with topical ± systemic steroids; does not always require stopping immunotherapy.
  • Exam trap: eosinophilic spongiosis without blister formation = pre-bullous BP until proven otherwise. Always request DIF. Do not report as 'eczema' without DIF if clinical suspicion of blistering disease.
Related / See Also
↗ INFLAMMATORY SKIN DISEASE — PATTERN FRAMEWORK↗ LICHEN PLANUS AND LICHENOID REACTIONS↗ Pemphigus vulgaris+ Add: Epidermolysis bullosa acquisita (EBA)↗ Dermatitis herpetiformis (DH)+ Add: Linear IgA disease
DYSPLASTIC NAEVUS (CLARK NAEVUS)
Skin / Dermatopath Melanocytic Junctional/compound Benign
Common acquired melanocytic naevus with architectural disorder and cytological atypia. 'Bridge' lesion between common naevi and melanoma — debated entity but high-yield for exam.
Clinical Context
  • Common acquired melanocytic naevus with architectural disorder and cytological atypia. 'Bridge' lesion between common naevi and melanoma — debated entity but high-yield for exam.
  • Dysplastic naevus syndrome (DNS/FAMMM): multiple (>50) dysplastic naevi + family history melanoma + CDKN2A germline mutation. Lifetime melanoma risk ~80%. Annual dermatological surveillance.
  • Clinically: >5mm; irregular border; variegated colour (tan/brown/pink); 'fried egg' appearance (central papular, peripheral macular). Trunk/proximal extremities.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Architectural disorder: lentiginous melanocytic hyperplasia with bridging of rete ridges (nests span between rete); irregular spacing; junctional shouldering (junctional component extends beyond dermal component).
  • Cytological atypia: enlarged nuclei; dusty melanin; prominent nucleoli; nuclear pleomorphism (mild/moderate/severe — grade of atypia must be reported).
  • Stromal response: lamellar (concentric eosinophilic) fibroplasia around rete ridges — characteristic; papillary dermal fibrosis. Melanin incontinence. Host response lymphocytic.
✚ Positive
S100 – diffuseSOX10 – diffuseMelan-A – diffuse (highlights junctional nests for architecture assessment)HMB45 – gradient: lost with maturation into dermis (same as benign naevi)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
PRAME – negative (positive in melanoma)Ki67 – low (<5%); high Ki67 in dermal component = concern for melanomap16 – retained (lost in melanoma)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Melanoma in situ↗Contiguous single-cell pagetoid spread; loss of nesting pattern; PRAME diffuse+; confluent growth; no maturation; consumption of epidermis
Common compound naevusSymmetrical; no junctional shouldering; uniform nests; no bridging; no lamellar fibroplasia; no cytological atypia
Spitz naevus↗Large epithelioid/spindle melanocytes; Kamino bodies; symmetrical wedge; no bridging of rete; no lamellar fibroplasia
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Grading of cytological atypia: mild / moderate / severe — must be reported. Severe atypia approaches melanoma in situ and requires narrower re-excision margins and MDT discussion.
  • Margin status must be reported — if severe atypia extends to margin, re-excise. Mild/moderate at margin: clinical follow-up acceptable.
  • Melan-A/MART-1 staining: essential for assessing junctional component — highlights single melanocytes and nesting pattern. Can overcall by staining background dendritic melanocytes — interpret with H&E.
  • CDKN2A (9p21): germline mutation in FAMMM. Same locus lost (homozygous deletion) in progression to melanoma. CDKN2A/p16 IHC retained in dysplastic naevus; lost in melanoma.
  • Exam trap: lamellar fibroplasia + bridging rete + shoulder phenomenon = dysplastic naevus. Do NOT diagnose melanoma in situ without pagetoid spread and loss of orderly nesting.
Related / See Also
↗ MELANOMA (CUTANEOUS)↗ SPITZ NAEVUS AND ATYPICAL SPITZ TUMOUR↗ INFLAMMATORY SKIN DISEASE — PATTERN FRAMEWORK↗ Melanoma in situ+ Add: Common compound naevus↗ Spitz naevus
MELANOMA — STAGING, BRESLOW, AND MELANOMA IN SITU
Skin / Dermatopath Melanocytic Variable Malignant
Melanoma in situ (MIS): confined to epidermis; no dermal invasion. Most common subtype: lentigo maligna (chronically sun-damaged skin, face/neck, elderly). SSM-in-situ (younger, trunk/limbs).
Clinical Context
  • Melanoma in situ (MIS): confined to epidermis; no dermal invasion. Most common subtype: lentigo maligna (chronically sun-damaged skin, face/neck, elderly). SSM-in-situ (younger, trunk/limbs).
  • Invasive melanoma: Breslow thickness (mm from granular layer to deepest tumour cell) is the single most important prognostic factor. AJCC 8th edition T-staging: T1a ≤0.8mm no ulceration; T1b ≤0.8mm with ulceration OR 0.8–1.0mm; T2 1.01–2.0mm; T3 2.01–4.0mm; T4 >4.0mm.
  • SLNB threshold: offered for T1b and above (Breslow >0.8mm or ulcerated). SLN positivity most powerful predictor of survival after Breslow thickness.
Key Microscopic Features
  • MIS/lentigo maligna: atypical melanocytes along DEJ in single-cell and nested pattern; confluence of melanocytes along basal layer; pagetoid upward migration; atypical melanocytes extending down adnexal structures (hair follicle involvement — important for margin assessment).
  • Invasive: asymmetry; pagetoid spread; sheet-like growth; no maturation; deep mitoses; ulceration (full-thickness epidermal loss with fibrin); regression (fibrosis replacing tumour; grey-blue melanophages; inflammatory infiltrate).
  • Report MUST include: Breslow thickness (0.1mm increments); ulceration (Y/N); mitotic rate (per mm²); Clark level (if T1); microsatellitosis; LVSI; neurotropism; regression; TILs (brisk/non-brisk/absent); predominant cell type; margins (peripheral and deep).
✚ Positive
S100 – diffuse (most sensitive; ~95%)SOX10 – diffuse nuclear (highly sensitive and specific)Melan-A/MART-1 – positive (caution: overcalls dendritic melanocytes in sun-damaged skin)HMB45 – positive but lost in deep component of benign naevi; retained in deep component of melanoma = loss of maturationPRAME – diffuse nuclear in melanoma (~75%); negative in naevi (increasingly used as first-line)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
p16 – lost in melanoma (retained in naevi — key discriminator for ambiguous lesions)BAP1 – lost in BAP1-inactivated melanocytic tumours (epithelioid, BAPI-inactivated naevus → uveal melanoma predisposition)CK – negative (excludes carcinoma)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Severely dysplastic naevus↗Retains nesting; no contiguous pagetoid spread; lamellar fibroplasia; PRAME negative; p16 retained; maturation present
Desmoplastic melanoma↗Spindle cell; S100+ diffuse; SOX10+; Melan-A/HMB45 often NEGATIVE; paucicellular collagenous dermis; neurotropism; high local recurrence; low SLN positivity
Lentigo maligna vs solar lentigo with melanocyte hyperplasiaLM: nuclear atypia, nesting, confluence, adnexal extension, PRAME+. Solar lentigo: no atypia, scattered basal melanocytes, no nesting, PRAME−
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • BRAF V600E: ~50% of cutaneous melanoma (younger patients, intermittent sun exposure). Targeted therapy: dabrafenib + trametinib (BRAF + MEK inhibition). Predictive testing mandatory for Stage III/IV.
  • NRAS mutation: ~20%; MEK inhibitor alone (binimetinib). KIT mutation: ~15% acral/mucosal; imatinib/dasatinib.
  • Immunotherapy: pembrolizumab/nivolumab (anti-PD-1) ± ipilimumab (anti-CTLA-4) — adjuvant for Stage III; first-line Stage IV. PD-L1 IHC not required for treatment eligibility (unlike NSCLC).
  • Desmoplastic melanoma: pure desmoplastic has low SLN positivity (~5%) despite often thick Breslow; radiation-sensitive. Mixed desmoplastic behaves like conventional melanoma — distinguish on biopsy.
  • Regression pitfall: if extensive regression present, true Breslow may be underestimated — report regression extent and discuss SLNB with clinician even if measured Breslow is thin.
  • Excision margins (BAD/RCPath): MIS = 5mm clinical; T1 (<1mm) = 1cm; T1.01–2mm = 1–2cm; T2.01–4mm = 2cm; T4 (>4mm) = 2cm. Wider margins do not improve survival beyond these.
  • Exam trap: Melan-A overcalls in sun-damaged skin — always interpret alongside H&E. SOX10 does not overcall dendritic cells and is preferred for MIS/LM margin assessment.
Related / See Also
↗ MELANOMA (CUTANEOUS)↗ DYSPLASTIC NAEVUS (CLARK NAEVUS)↗ SPITZ NAEVUS AND ATYPICAL SPITZ TUMOUR↗ INFLAMMATORY SKIN DISEASE — PATTERN FRAMEWORK↗ Severely dysplastic naevus↗ Desmoplastic melanoma+ Add: Lentigo maligna vs solar lentigo with melanocyte hyperplasia
CUTANEOUS SCC AND BOWEN'S DISEASE (SCC IN SITU)
Skin / Dermatopath Epithelial Squamous Malignant
Bowen's disease (SCC in situ): full-thickness epidermal dysplasia confined to epidermis. Sun-exposed and non-sun-exposed sites. Arsenic exposure: multiple Bowen's on non-sun-exposed skin (exam classic). Pinkish scaly plaque.
Clinical Context
  • Bowen's disease (SCC in situ): full-thickness epidermal dysplasia confined to epidermis. Sun-exposed and non-sun-exposed sites. Arsenic exposure: multiple Bowen's on non-sun-exposed skin (exam classic). Pinkish scaly plaque.
  • Invasive cSCC: second most common skin malignancy. UV (cumulative); immunosuppression (SOTRs — 65-250× increased risk); chronic wounds/scars (Marjolin ulcer). Head and neck, dorsum of hands.
  • High-risk features (RCPath): depth >4mm or beyond subcutaneous fat; poor differentiation; perineural invasion (PNI); LVSI; immunosuppression. High-risk cSCC has ~5% metastatic rate (regional LN).
Key Microscopic Features
  • Bowen's: full-thickness dysmaturation of epidermis; windblown appearance (disordered keratinocytes); atypical mitoses; intact basement membrane; no dermal invasion. May extend down follicular epithelium (important for margin assessment — similar issue to lentigo maligna).
  • Invasive SCC: nests and sheets of squamous cells invading dermis; intercellular bridges (desmosomes); keratin pearls (well-differentiated); nuclear pleomorphism; mitoses. Grade: well → moderately → poorly differentiated.
  • Variants: keratoacanthoma-type (crateriform with keratinous plug, rapid growth, potential regression — controversial if variant or distinct entity); spindle cell/sarcomatoid (aggressive, loss of CK expression); desmoplastic (sclerotic stroma, PNI common); adenosquamous (glandular + squamous); verrucous (low-grade, broad pushing base, no conventional invasion).
✚ Positive
p40 – diffuse (most specific squamous marker)p63 – diffuseCK5/6 – positiveEMA – positive (distinguishes from BCC which is EMA−)34βE12 (high MW CK) – positive
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
BerEP4 – negative (positive in BCC — most useful discriminator in poorly differentiated cases)S100/SOX10 – negative (excludes melanoma)CD34 – negative (excludes DFSP, AFX)CK7 – negative (if positive, consider metastatic SCC from internal site or mucoepidermoid)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Basal cell carcinoma⚡BerEP4+; BCL2 diffuse; EMA−; peripheral palisading; retraction artefact; no keratinisation (except basosquamous)
Keratoacanthoma (KA)Crateriform symmetrical architecture; central keratin plug; overhanging lip of epidermis; ground-glass cytoplasm; minimal atypia; rapid growth history — many consider KA a well-differentiated SCC variant
Atypical fibroxanthoma (AFX) / Pleomorphic dermal sarcoma (PDS)⚡AFX: CK−, S100−, CD10+; diagnosis of exclusion (extensive IHC panel). PDS: same phenotype as AFX but subcutaneous invasion, necrosis, LVSI, PNI
Merkel cell carcinomaCK20 paranuclear dot+; neuroendocrine markers; BerEP4−; p40−; small blue cell tumour; MCPyV
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • PNI: must be actively sought and reported — presence upgrades to high-risk cSCC; consider adjuvant radiotherapy. Large calibre nerve PNI (≥0.1mm) particularly adverse.
  • Immunosuppressed patients (SOTRs): SCC is the most common malignancy post-transplant; more aggressive behaviour; consider reducing immunosuppression; increased surveillance.
  • Keratoacanthoma controversy: RCPath recommends reporting KA as 'well-differentiated SCC, keratoacanthoma type' — manages the lesion appropriately regardless of biological debate.
  • Marjolin ulcer: SCC arising in chronic wound/scar/burn; aggressive; may not have typical UV mutational signature; often diagnosed late.
  • Staging: AJCC 8th (head and neck cSCC has own staging). High-risk features: >4mm depth, PNI, poor differentiation, invasion beyond subcutis. BWH (Brigham and Women's) alternative staging increasingly adopted.
  • Exam trap: spindle cell SCC can be CK-negative on small panels — use p40 (most specific), broad CK panel, and exclude melanoma/sarcoma before diagnosing sarcomatoid SCC.
Related / See Also
↗ CUTANEOUS SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA (cSCC)↗ BASAL CELL CARCINOMA (BCC)↗ MELANOMA (CUTANEOUS)↗ INFLAMMATORY SKIN DISEASE — PATTERN FRAMEWORK⚡ Basal cell carcinoma+ Add: Keratoacanthoma (KA)⚡ Atypical fibroxanthoma (AFX) / Pleomorphic dermal sarcoma (PDS)+ Add: Merkel cell carcinoma
PEMPHIGUS VULGARIS (PV)
Skin / Dermatopath Inflammatory Intraepidermal blistering Variable
Autoimmune intraepidermal blistering disease targeting desmoglein 3 (mucosal dominant) ± desmoglein 1 (mucocutaneous). Flaccid blisters that rupture easily; positive Nikolsky sign. Painful erosions.
Clinical Context
  • Autoimmune intraepidermal blistering disease targeting desmoglein 3 (mucosal dominant) ± desmoglein 1 (mucocutaneous). Flaccid blisters that rupture easily; positive Nikolsky sign. Painful erosions.
  • Mucosal involvement predominant (>80%): oral erosions often first presentation; can affect pharynx, larynx, oesophagus, conjunctivae. Mucosal-dominant PV: anti-Dsg3 only. Mucocutaneous: anti-Dsg3 + anti-Dsg1.
  • Peak 40–60 years; Ashkenazi Jewish predisposition; HLA-DR4/DR14. Untreated mortality ~75% (was near-universally fatal pre-steroids). Paraneoplastic pemphigus (PNP): associated with lymphoma/Castleman; lichenoid interface + acantholysis.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Suprabasal acantholysis: loss of intercellular adhesion above basal layer → intraepidermal cleft. Basal keratinocytes remain attached to BM like 'tombstones' (row of tombstones).
  • Acantholytic (Tzanck) cells in blister cavity: round, detached keratinocytes with condensed cytoplasm and large hyperchromatic nuclei. Eosinophils in blister fluid.
  • PAS stain: BM at BASE of blister (not floor, as in BP) — epidermis splits above BM. Early lesion: eosinophilic spongiosis → progresses to frank acantholysis.
✚ Positive
DIF (perilesional skin): intercellular IgG + C3 in 'chicken wire' or 'fishnet' pattern — DIAGNOSTIC. Full-thickness epidermis.Anti-Dsg3 ELISA – positive (mucosal dominant PV)Anti-Dsg1 ELISA – positive (mucocutaneous PV; also positive in pemphigus foliaceus as sole target)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
DIF: linear BMZ pattern – absent (present in BP/linear IgA)DIF: IgA intercellular – absent in PV (present in IgA pemphigus)BP180/BP230 ELISA – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Pemphigus foliaceus (PF)Subcorneal/granular layer acantholysis (NOT suprabasal); anti-Dsg1 only (no Dsg3); superficial crusted erosions; no mucosal involvement; DIF same pattern but more superficial
Bullous pemphigoid⚡Subepidermal blister; eosinophil-rich; linear IgG at BMZ (not intercellular); Nikolsky negative; tense blisters; elderly
Hailey-Hailey disease (familial benign pemphigus)Acantholysis throughout entire epidermis ('dilapidated brick wall'); autosomal dominant ATP2C1 mutation; DIF NEGATIVE; flexural; no circulating antibodies
Paraneoplastic pemphigus (PNP)Combined interface dermatitis (lichenoid) + acantholysis; DIF: intercellular + BMZ pattern; multiple antibodies (Dsg3, envoplakin, periplakin, desmoplakin); associated neoplasm (NHL, CLL, Castleman)
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Desmoglein compensation theory: Dsg1 expressed throughout epidermis but predominant superficially; Dsg3 predominant basally and in mucosa. Anti-Dsg3 alone → mucosal only (skin compensated by Dsg1). Anti-Dsg3 + Dsg1 → mucocutaneous.
  • Treatment: rituximab now first-line (RITUX 3 trial); systemic corticosteroids ± azathioprine/mycophenolate. Dsg3 titre correlates with disease activity — monitor for treatment response.
  • Paraneoplastic pemphigus: suspect if lichenoid + acantholytic features on biopsy; respiratory involvement (bronchiolitis obliterans) is distinctive and often fatal. Screen for underlying neoplasm.
  • DIF mandatory: perilesional skin in Michel's medium. Negative DIF essentially excludes pemphigus. Serum indirect IF also useful (monkey oesophagus substrate for PV; guinea pig lip for PF).
  • Exam trap: eosinophilic spongiosis in early PV mimics drug reaction/eczema/pre-bullous BP — DIF distinguishes. Always request DIF if acantholysis suspected clinically.
Related / See Also
↗ BULLOUS PEMPHIGOID (BP)↗ INFLAMMATORY SKIN DISEASE — PATTERN FRAMEWORK↗ LICHEN PLANUS AND LICHENOID REACTIONS+ Add: Pemphigus foliaceus (PF)⚡ Bullous pemphigoid+ Add: Hailey-Hailey disease (familial benign pemphigus)+ Add: Paraneoplastic pemphigus (PNP)
DERMATITIS HERPETIFORMIS (DH)
Skin / Dermatopath Inflammatory Subepidermal blistering Variable
Cutaneous manifestation of coeliac disease (gluten-sensitive enteropathy). IgA autoimmunity against epidermal transglutaminase (eTG). All DH patients have coeliac on duodenal biopsy (may be subclinical).
Clinical Context
  • Cutaneous manifestation of coeliac disease (gluten-sensitive enteropathy). IgA autoimmunity against epidermal transglutaminase (eTG). All DH patients have coeliac on duodenal biopsy (may be subclinical).
  • Intensely pruritic grouped vesicles on extensor surfaces: elbows, knees, buttocks, scalp, upper back. Symmetrical. Often excoriated (intact vesicles rare clinically — scratched off). M>F; 20–40 years; HLA-DQ2/DQ8.
  • Associated with increased lymphoma risk (enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma, EATL) — same as coeliac. Gluten-free diet (GFD) is primary treatment; dapsone for rapid symptom control.
Key Microscopic Features
  • Neutrophilic microabscesses at tips of dermal papillae (papillary tip neutrophils) — highly characteristic. Eosinophils also present. Papillary oedema; early subepidermal vesicle formation.
  • Established blister: subepidermal cleft at dermal papillary tips; multiloculated (papillary microabscesses coalesce). Sparse lymphocytic perivascular infiltrate.
  • Biopsy site: perilesional skin for DIF; lesional skin for H&E. In excoriated lesions, papillary neutrophils may be the only clue — vesicle destroyed by scratching.
✚ Positive
DIF (perilesional skin): GRANULAR IgA deposits at dermal papillae — PATHOGNOMONIC. This is the defining diagnostic feature.Anti-tTG IgA (serum) – positive (~95%); coeliac markerAnti-eTG IgA (serum) – most specific for DH (>90%)Anti-endomysial IgA – positive
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
DIF: linear IgA at BMZ – absent (present in linear IgA disease — key distinction)DIF: intercellular IgG – absent (present in pemphigus)DIF: linear IgG at BMZ – absent (present in BP)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Linear IgA disease (LAD)LINEAR IgA at BMZ on DIF (NOT granular at papillae); drug-associated (vancomycin classic); 'crown of jewels' clinical pattern; heterogeneous blisters; children (chronic bullous disease of childhood)
Bullous pemphigoid⚡Linear IgG + C3 at BMZ; eosinophil-predominant; no papillary microabscesses; tense blisters; elderly; no coeliac association
IgA vasculitis (HSP)IgA deposits in vessel walls (not papillae); leukocytoclastic vasculitis; palpable purpura; abdominal pain; nephritis
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • DIF is diagnostic: granular IgA at dermal papillae = DH. Linear IgA at BMZ = linear IgA disease. This distinction is the single most important exam point in blistering disease DIF.
  • All DH patients have coeliac: duodenal biopsy mandatory (Marsh classification). GI symptoms may be absent — subclinical enteropathy. GFD treats both skin and gut disease.
  • Dapsone: rapid symptom control (within 48 hours); mechanism = neutrophil inhibition. Side effects: haemolysis (check G6PD first), methaemoglobinaemia, agranulocytosis.
  • GFD: only definitive treatment — reduces EATL risk. Dapsone controls symptoms but does not treat underlying coeliac or reduce lymphoma risk.
  • Exam trap: granular IgA at papillae = DH. Linear IgA at BMZ = linear IgA disease. If the question gives you DIF with IgA — the PATTERN determines the diagnosis.
Related / See Also
↗ BULLOUS PEMPHIGOID (BP)↗ PEMPHIGUS VULGARIS (PV)↗ INFLAMMATORY SKIN DISEASE — PATTERN FRAMEWORK+ Add: Linear IgA disease (LAD)⚡ Bullous pemphigoid+ Add: IgA vasculitis (HSP)
POLYMORPHOUS ADENOCARCINOMA
Head & Neck Epithelial Trabecular; Cribriform; Intermediate
Minor salivary glands
Clinical Context
  • Minor salivary glands
  • 2:1 F:M
  • Mean age 50-60s
  • Second most common SG Ca of palate and oral cavity.
  • Presents as palpable mass
Key Microscopic Features
  • Cytologic uniformity: monotonous pale nuclei with marked chromatin clearing resemlbing PTC
  • Architectural diversity: single filing, trabecular, tubular, reticular, papillary, solid, cribriform.
  • Targetoid arrangement around nerves and vessels.
  • Perineural invasion frequent.
  • Cribriform carcinoma of salivary gland considered a variant - lobulated architecture, fibrous septae, cribriform/microcystic.
✚ Positive
S100 - diffuse, strongCK7 - diffuse, strongSOX10 - diffuse, strongp63 - variable (78-100%)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
p40 - negativeEMA - less than 20% positiveSMA - less than 20% positiveGATA3 - less than 20% positive
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Adenoid cystic carcinoma↗Biphasic, dark angulated basaloid nuclei, typically p63+/p40+
Secretory carcinomaGCDFP-15+, mammaglobulin+, ETV6-NTRK3 fusion
Myoepithelial carcinomalacks PRKD gene alterations, difficult on IHC
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Indolent salivary gland carcinoma with cytologic uniformity and architectural diversity.
  • PRKD1 E710D hotspot mutation (73-89%)
  • PRKD1/2/3 fusion (6-11%)
Related / See Also
↗ Adenoid cystic carcinoma+ Add: Secretory carcinoma+ Add: Myoepithelial carcinoma
KAPOSI'S SARCOMA
Soft Tissue / Bone Mesenchymal Spindle cell Intermediate
4 clinical variants:
Clinical Context
  • 4 clinical variants:
  • Classic/sporadic (Mediterranean) - Middle aged/Elderly, M>F, areas of high HHV8 prevalence
  • Endemic (African) - M adults, M&F children, not HIV related, mirrors HHV8 seropositivity
  • Iatrogenic (transplant related) - M>F, >50yo, solid organ transx, risk corr. w/ immunosuppression
  • Epidemic (HIV/AIDS assoc.) - HIV+, risk inc. w/ declining CD4 cell counts
  • KS in MSM w/o HIV+ - v. rare, MSM immunocompetent, favourable progn.
  • Progressive, enlarging red to violaceous skin lesions - patch -> plaque -> tumor
Key Microscopic Features
  • Slit-like vascular spaces formed by spindled endothelial cells.
  • Minimal to moderate atypia.
  • Haemorrhage, haemosiderin deposition and plasma cells common.
✚ Positive
HHV8ERGCD31CD34PAS - hyaline globules
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
SMADesminAE1/3S100MelanA
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Angiosarcoma
Severe vascular stasis
Hobnail haemangioma
Spindle cell haemangioma
Kaposiform haemangioendothelioma
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Malignant vascular neoplasm of intermediate behaviour.
  • Aetiological agent is human herpesvirus 8 (HHV8) - other diseases assoc. primary effusion lymphoma, multicentric Castleman disease (MCD)
Related / See Also
+ Add: Angiosarcoma+ Add: Severe vascular stasis+ Add: Hobnail haemangioma+ Add: Spindle cell haemangioma+ Add: Kaposiform haemangioendothelioma

Filter
Specimen Type
Reporting System
Behaviour
26 entities
BENIGN COLLOID NODULE / COLLOID GOITRE (Thy2)
Head & Neck FNA Thy2 — Non-neoplastic Benign
Abundant colloid — watery/thin (diluted in liquid prep) or thick 'cracked mud/crazy paving' pattern on air-dried smears; may form dense pink sheets.
Adequacy Criteria
  • RCPath minimum: 6 groups of ≥10 well-preserved follicular cells on ≥2 slides. Colloid-only aspirates from macrofollicular/colloid nodules: report as Thy2 even if scant follicular cells — colloid is informative.
  • Cystic lesions with only macrophages and colloid: Thy1c (non-diagnostic cystic) if no follicular cells. If clinical/US features consistent with benign cyst, correlate.
Reporting System

RCPath Thyroid (Thy1–5) — Thy2 — Non-neoplastic

Cytological Features
  • Abundant colloid — watery/thin (diluted in liquid prep) or thick 'cracked mud/crazy paving' pattern on air-dried smears; may form dense pink sheets.
  • Follicular cells: flat honeycomb sheets; uniform small nuclei; regular nuclear spacing; inconspicuous nucleoli; no nuclear enlargement, grooves, or pseudoinclusions.
  • Macrofollicular architecture (large follicles with central colloid) at low power. Bare nuclei in background (stripped follicular cells).
  • Background: colloid dominant; occasional macrophages (if cystic degeneration); no necrosis; no inflammation unless co-existent thyroiditis.
✚ Positive
N/A — morphological diagnosis
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
N/A
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Follicular Neoplasm (Thy3f)Microfollicular/trabecular architecture; scant colloid; cellular aspirate; follicular cells with mild nuclear enlargement — cannot exclude on cytology alone; needs histology
Hashimoto's Thyroiditis (Thy2)Lymphocytic infiltrate; Hürthle cell change; lymphoid aggregates; no dominant colloid; plasma cells; clinical context (hypothyroidism, elevated TPO antibodies)
Papillary Thyroid Carcinoma (Thy4/5)PTC nuclear features (grooves, pseudoinclusions, powdery chromatin, nuclear overlap); papillary fronds; dense colloid ('bubblegum')
⚠ Pitfalls
  • Liquid-based cytology (LBC): colloid appears pale/watery — less dramatic than smear; do not underestimate colloid content.
  • Cystic degeneration: haemosiderin-laden macrophages dominate; follicular cells scarce → Thy1c if <6 groups. Re-aspirate solid component.
  • Hürthle cell change in benign nodule: abundant granular cytoplasm, prominent nucleoli — do not over-call as Hürthle cell neoplasm unless dominant cell population in a cellular aspirate.
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Thy2 = ~60-70% of all thyroid FNAs. Risk of malignancy: ~0-3%.
  • Management: clinical/US follow-up. Repeat FNA if nodule grows or US features change (TIRADS 4-5).
  • Thy2 does NOT exclude malignancy — sampling error is the main limitation. Correlate with US (TIRADS category).
  • Macrofollicular pattern on US (spongiform nodule) + Thy2 = very low malignancy risk (<1%) — conservative management appropriate.
  • Colloid 'cracked mud' pattern on Diff-Quik/Giemsa: highly characteristic; less apparent on Papanicolaou stain.
HASHIMOTO'S THYROIDITIS (Thy2)
Head & Neck FNA Thy2 — Non-neoplastic Benign
Hürthle (oxyphilic/oncocytic) cell change — follicular cells with abundant granular eosinophilic cytoplasm, large nuclei, prominent nucleoli; arranged in sheets or clusters. Hallmark of Hashimoto's.
Adequacy Criteria
  • Adequacy assessment modified: lymphocyte-rich aspirates from Hashimoto's may lack follicular cells — report as Thy2 if features are diagnostic of lymphocytic thyroiditis.
  • If dominant nodule in Hashimoto's background: standard adequacy criteria apply — exclude neoplasm within nodule.
Reporting System

RCPath Thyroid (Thy1–5) — Thy2 — Non-neoplastic

Cytological Features
  • Hürthle (oxyphilic/oncocytic) cell change — follicular cells with abundant granular eosinophilic cytoplasm, large nuclei, prominent nucleoli; arranged in sheets or clusters. Hallmark of Hashimoto's.
  • Lymphocytic infiltrate — mixed T and B lymphocytes permeating follicular cell groups; lymphoid tangles; tingible body macrophages. Plasma cells present.
  • Colloid scanty or absent; lymphoid aggregates with germinal centre formation in severe disease.
  • Background: abundant lymphocytes; occasional lymphoid follicle fragments; granular/inflammatory debris.
✚ Positive
N/A — morphological + clinical diagnosis (TPO antibodies, TSH)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
N/A
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Hürthle Cell Neoplasm (Thy3f/Thy4)Pure Hürthle cell population without lymphocytes; cellular; microfollicular/trabecular; no lymphocytic background — if dominant Hürthle cell nodule in Hashimoto's, Thy3f appropriate
Lymphoma (primary thyroid)Monomorphic lymphoid population; large atypical lymphocytes (DLBCL) or small cleaved cells (MALToma); loss of follicular cell component; flow cytometry on aspirate
Subacute (De Quervain's) ThyroiditisGranulomatous inflammation; multinucleated giant cells; no Hürthle cell change; painful tender thyroid; elevated ESR; viral prodrome
⚠ Pitfalls
  • Hürthle cell atypia in Hashimoto's: nuclear enlargement and prominent nucleoli are reactive — do not over-call as malignancy. Lymphocytic background is reassuring.
  • PTC arising in Hashimoto's: Hashimoto's is a risk factor for PTC. Assess nuclear features carefully in any dominant nodule — PTC nuclear changes may be focal.
  • Primary thyroid lymphoma: always suspect in elderly woman with rapid thyroid enlargement on background of Hashimoto's — flow cytometry on FNA material is essential.
  • LBC: lymphocytes dispersed; may appear less dramatic — Hürthle cells still recognisable.
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Hashimoto's thyroiditis = most common cause of hypothyroidism in iodine-sufficient populations. TPO antibodies elevated in >90%.
  • Risk of malignancy in Thy2 Hashimoto's: ~0-3% (same as other Thy2). However PTC and primary thyroid lymphoma risk modestly increased.
  • Primary thyroid lymphoma: predominantly MALT lymphoma (low-grade) or DLBCL (high-grade). Virtually always on background of Hashimoto's. Flow cytometry + clonality studies essential.
  • Management: thyroid function tests; levothyroxine if hypothyroid. FNA of dominant nodule to exclude neoplasm.
  • Riedel's thyroiditis (fibrous thyroiditis): distinct — dense fibrosis invading adjacent structures; IgG4-related in some cases; rare cause of thyroid enlargement.
SUBACUTE GRANULOMATOUS THYROIDITIS (DE QUERVAIN'S) (Thy2)
Head & Neck FNA Thy2 — Non-neoplastic Benign
Granulomatous inflammation: epithelioid histiocytes in loose clusters; multinucleated giant cells (Langhans and foreign-body type) — key feature.
Adequacy Criteria
  • Often paucicellular — inflammatory background may predominate. Report as Thy2 if granulomatous features present even with limited follicular cells.
Reporting System

RCPath Thyroid (Thy1–5) — Thy2 — Non-neoplastic

Cytological Features
  • Granulomatous inflammation: epithelioid histiocytes in loose clusters; multinucleated giant cells (Langhans and foreign-body type) — key feature.
  • Degenerating/disrupted follicular cells; colloid fragments engulfed by giant cells; follicular cells may show reactive atypia.
  • Inflammatory background: mixed acute and chronic inflammation; neutrophils, lymphocytes, histiocytes; necrotic debris.
  • Background: granular/necrotic debris; no lymphoid follicles (distinguishes from Hashimoto's).
✚ Positive
N/A — morphological + clinical diagnosis
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
N/A
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Hashimoto's ThyroiditisLymphocytic infiltrate; Hürthle cell change; lymphoid follicles; no giant cells; no granulomas; chronic course; elevated TPO antibodies
Palpation ThyroiditisFocal giant cells after vigorous palpation; no clinical thyroiditis; incidental finding
Tuberculous ThyroiditisCaseating granulomas (rare); ZN stain positive; systemic TB features; immunocompromised patient
⚠ Pitfalls
  • Reactive atypia in inflamed follicular cells may mimic PTC — clinical context (painful thyroid, elevated ESR, viral prodrome) essential.
  • Paucicellular aspirates: granulomatous thyroiditis can yield sparse material — do not report as Thy1 if granulomatous features present.
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Clinical diagnosis: painful tender thyroid + elevated ESR + raised CRP + transient hyperthyroidism (early) → hypothyroidism → recovery.
  • Viral aetiology presumed (post-viral); often follows URTI.
  • Treatment: NSAIDs; corticosteroids for severe cases. Self-limiting — most recover full thyroid function.
  • Permanent hypothyroidism in ~5-10%.
  • FNA rarely needed — clinical diagnosis usually sufficient. Biopsy if diagnostic uncertainty.
ATYPIA OF UNDETERMINED SIGNIFICANCE (Thy3a)
Head & Neck FNA Thy3a — Neoplasm possible, atypia Variable
Mild nuclear atypia not meeting threshold for Thy3f or Thy4: nuclear enlargement, mild anisonucleosis, occasional nuclear grooves — focal and not convincing for PTC.
Adequacy Criteria
  • Thy3a implies the specimen IS adequate — atypia has been identified. If truly inadequate, report as Thy1.
  • Thy3a is a category of diagnostic uncertainty, not inadequacy.
Reporting System

RCPath Thyroid (Thy1–5) — Thy3a — Neoplasm possible, atypia

Cytological Features
  • Mild nuclear atypia not meeting threshold for Thy3f or Thy4: nuclear enlargement, mild anisonucleosis, occasional nuclear grooves — focal and not convincing for PTC.
  • Architectural atypia without nuclear atypia: focal microfollicular pattern insufficient for Thy3f; crowding/overlapping without PTC features.
  • Hürthle cell change: focal or limited Hürthle cells insufficient for Hürthle cell neoplasm (Thy3f) — not dominant population.
  • Obscuring factors: blood/clot, poor fixation, air-drying artefact, scant cellularity causing interpretive difficulty — when these prevent definitive benign or neoplastic classification.
✚ Positive
N/A — morphological category; cell block IHC (HBME-1, CK19, Galectin-3) may assist in selected cases but not routinely recommended
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
N/A
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Thy2 (Benign)No atypia; adequate colloid; regular honeycomb sheets; repeat FNA if persistent concern
Thy3f (Follicular Neoplasm)Dominant microfollicular/trabecular architecture; scant colloid; cellular; consistent atypia throughout — Thy3f when architecture and cellularity clearly neoplastic
Thy4 (Suspicious)More convincing but incomplete PTC nuclear features; Thy4 when nuclear changes focal but unequivocal
⚠ Pitfalls
  • Thy3a is a category of last resort — should represent <7% of all thyroid FNAs. Overuse indicates diagnostic imprecision.
  • Cystic lesion with atypical cells lining cyst wall: may represent cystic PTC — Thy3a minimum; Thy4 if nuclear features convincing. US correlation essential.
  • Repeat FNA resolves Thy3a in ~50% of cases — recommend 3-6 month interval.
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Risk of malignancy: ~10-30% (RCPath/BTA data). Bethesda AUS/FLUS equivalent.
  • Management: repeat FNA at 3–6 months (preferred) OR molecular testing (ThyroSeq, Afirma) if available OR diagnostic hemithyroidectomy based on US/clinical risk.
  • Molecular testing: Afirma GSC (gene expression classifier) — high NPV; used to avoid surgery in low-risk Thy3a. ThyroSeq v3 — mutation/fusion panel; positive result guides surgical extent.
  • Persistent Thy3a on repeat FNA: hemithyroidectomy generally recommended.
  • UK practice: molecular testing less established than USA — clinical MDT decision predominates.
FOLLICULAR NEOPLASM (Thy3f)
Head & Neck FNA Thy3f — Neoplasm possible, follicular Variable 📋 1×
Cellular aspirate — high cellularity relative to colloid (cellularity-to-colloid ratio inverted compared to benign nodule).
Adequacy Criteria
  • Standard RCPath adequacy criteria apply. Thy3f requires a cellular aspirate with consistent microfollicular/trabecular architecture — not a diagnosis made on a scant specimen.
Reporting System

RCPath Thyroid (Thy1–5) — Thy3f — Neoplasm possible, follicular

Cytological Features
  • Cellular aspirate — high cellularity relative to colloid (cellularity-to-colloid ratio inverted compared to benign nodule).
  • Microfollicular/trabecular architecture: small follicles (<15 cells); follicular cells arranged in ring-like structures around small lumen; trabecular cords. Scant or absent colloid.
  • Follicular cells: mild nuclear enlargement; round-to-oval nuclei; fine chromatin; inconspicuous nucleoli. NO convincing PTC nuclear features (no grooves, pseudoinclusions, powdery chromatin).
  • Background: scant colloid; bare nuclei; no lymphocytes; no giant cells.
✚ Positive
N/A — cytology cannot distinguish follicular adenoma from follicular carcinoma (capsular/vascular invasion requires histology)HBME-1, CK19, Galectin-3: positive panel increases probability of malignancy on cell block but not diagnostic
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
N/A
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Benign Colloid Nodule (Thy2)Macrofollicular architecture; abundant colloid; low cellularity; regular honeycomb sheets; Thy2 when colloid dominant and architecture macrofollicular
Papillary Thyroid Carcinoma — follicular variant (Thy4)PTC nuclear features present (grooves, pseudoinclusions, chromatin changes) even in follicular architecture → Thy4/Thy5; BRAF V600E if present
Hürthle Cell Neoplasm (Thy3f)Dominant Hürthle cell population; same Thy3f category but distinct entity; oncocytic cytoplasm; prominent nucleoli
⚠ Pitfalls
  • Critical limitation: cytology CANNOT distinguish follicular adenoma (benign) from follicular carcinoma (malignant) — both show identical cytological features. Capsular and vascular invasion can only be assessed on histological sections of the entire nodule.
  • NIFTP (Non-Invasive Follicular Thyroid neoplasm with Papillary-like nuclear features): RAS-mutant; borderline entity; previously classified as encapsulated follicular variant PTC; cytology may show intermediate features (Thy3a/Thy3f/Thy4 — variable).
  • Multinodular goitre with adenomatoid nodules: can appear microfollicular on aspiration — clinical/US context essential.
  • Parathyroid adenoma on FNA: microfollicular pattern mimicking thyroid; PTH washout from needle confirms parathyroid origin.
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Risk of malignancy: ~25-40% for Thy3f. Equivalent to Bethesda IV (follicular neoplasm/suspicious for FN).
  • Management: diagnostic hemithyroidectomy (standard UK practice). Completion thyroidectomy if malignancy confirmed on histology.
  • Molecular testing: RAS mutation (NRAS/HRAS/KRAS) — most common in follicular neoplasm; does not distinguish adenoma from carcinoma. PPARG rearrangement — specific for follicular carcinoma.
  • Hürthle cell (oncocytic) neoplasm: now classified separately in WHO 2022 — distinct molecular profile (mitochondrial DNA mutations, chromosome 22 loss); higher malignancy rate; less radioiodine avid.
  • Minimally invasive follicular carcinoma: excellent prognosis after complete excision. Widely invasive follicular carcinoma: aggressive; haematogenous spread (lung, bone).
PAPILLARY THYROID CARCINOMA — FNA (Thy5 / Thy4)
Head & Neck FNA Thy5 — Malignant (or Thy4 — Suspicious if nuclear features incomplete) Malignant 📋 1×
Architecture: papillary fronds with fibrovascular cores; flat monolayer sheets with nuclear crowding and overlap; syncytial fragments. Papillary architecture best appreciated on smears.
Adequacy Criteria
  • Standard RCPath adequacy criteria. PTC aspirates are often cellular — adequacy rarely an issue.
  • Cystic PTC: cystic fluid may be paucicellular — sample solid mural component. Thy1c if only macrophages obtained.
Reporting System

RCPath Thyroid (Thy1–5) — Thy5 — Malignant (or Thy4 — Suspicious if nuclear features incomplete)

Cytological Features
  • Architecture: papillary fronds with fibrovascular cores; flat monolayer sheets with nuclear crowding and overlap; syncytial fragments. Papillary architecture best appreciated on smears.
  • Nuclear features (most important): nuclear enlargement with irregular nuclear membranes; nuclear grooves (longitudinal infoldings — best on Papanicolaou); intranuclear pseudoinclusions (cytoplasmic invaginations — round, sharp-bordered, eosinophilic); fine powdery/ground-glass chromatin ('Orphan Annie eyes' — better on histology); nuclear overlap and crowding.
  • Cytoplasmic features: pale/dense cytoplasm; squamoid metaplasia in some variants; multinucleated giant cells.
  • Background: dense 'bubblegum' colloid (thick, stringy — differs from watery colloid of benign nodule); psammoma bodies (concentric calcifications — pathognomonic when present); foamy/haemosiderin macrophages in cystic cases.
✚ Positive
TTF1 – positiveThyroglobulin – positiveCK19 – diffuse strong (cell block)HBME-1 – positive (cell block)BRAF V600E IHC (VE1) – ~80% classical PTC (cell block)Galectin-3 – positive
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
Calcitonin – negative (excludes MTC)Synaptophysin / Chromogranin – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Follicular Neoplasm (Thy3f)No PTC nuclear features; microfollicular not papillary architecture; scant colloid; nuclear grooves/pseudoinclusions absent
Medullary Thyroid CarcinomaCalcitonin+; plasmacytoid/spindle cells; amyloid in background (Congo red); salt-and-pepper chromatin; no papillary architecture; no PTC nuclei
Hashimoto's with reactive atypiaLymphocytic background; Hürthle cell change; reactive nuclear grooves (less convincing); TPO antibodies elevated; no papillary architecture; no psammoma bodies
⚠ Pitfalls
  • Nuclear pseudoinclusions less reliable in FNA than histology — cytoplasmic invagination artefact during preparation. Nuclear grooves are more diagnostically useful in cytological material.
  • Cystic PTC: paucicellular cyst fluid with macrophages only — report Thy1c, re-aspirate solid component. Cystic change does not exclude PTC.
  • Tall cell variant: cells ≥3× as tall as wide; intense eosinophilic cytoplasm; more aggressive — may be Thy4 if nuclear features incomplete.
  • Follicular variant PTC: no papillary architecture; diagnosis rests entirely on nuclear features — Thy3f/Thy4 on cytology; NIFTP overlaps significantly.
  • Graves' disease: 'Orphan Annie eyes' can be seen in non-neoplastic thyrocytes in Graves' — correlate clinically; nuclear grooves and pseudoinclusions should be absent or minimal.
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Most common thyroid malignancy (~85%). Excellent prognosis: 10-year OS >95% for classical PTC.
  • BRAF V600E (~80% classical PTC): associated with extrathyroidal extension, LN metastasis, radioiodine resistance, recurrence. Test on cell block or molecular.
  • RET/PTC rearrangements: radiation-associated; younger patients; papillary architecture predominant.
  • Thy4 vs Thy5: Thy5 = unequivocal malignancy; Thy4 = suspicious but incomplete features (e.g. focal/subtle nuclear changes, paucicellular). ROM: Thy4 ~60-75%; Thy5 >95%.
  • Aggressive variants (tall cell, hobnail, diffuse sclerosing, columnar cell): poorer prognosis — note on report if suspected.
  • Management: hemithyroidectomy (low-risk, <4cm, no LN mets) or total thyroidectomy (high-risk, >4cm, bilateral, LN mets) ± RAI ± TSH suppression.
  • Active surveillance: selected papillary microcarcinoma (<1cm, no high-risk features) — increasingly adopted.
🔬 Histology Counterpart
↗ PAPILLARY THYROID CARCINOMA — HISTOLOGY
MEDULLARY THYROID CARCINOMA — FNA (Thy5)
Head & Neck FNA Thy5 — Malignant Malignant
Cellular aspirate; discohesive single cells and loose clusters — not cohesive sheets. Highly variable morphology: plasmacytoid (most common); spindle cell; epithelioid; small cell; giant cell; mixed.
Adequacy Criteria
  • Standard RCPath criteria. MTC aspirates are usually cellular. If calcitonin measurement from needle washout is available — highly diagnostic (elevated calcitonin in FNA washout fluid).
Reporting System

RCPath Thyroid (Thy1–5) — Thy5 — Malignant

Cytological Features
  • Cellular aspirate; discohesive single cells and loose clusters — not cohesive sheets. Highly variable morphology: plasmacytoid (most common); spindle cell; epithelioid; small cell; giant cell; mixed.
  • Nuclear features: eccentric nuclei ('plasmacytoid'); binucleation and multinucleation common; salt-and-pepper neuroendocrine chromatin; intranuclear pseudoinclusions may occur. Nucleoli variable.
  • Cytoplasm: abundant granular cytoplasm; red neurosecretory granules on Diff-Quik/Giemsa. Cytoplasmic processes in spindle cell variant.
  • Background: amyloid — grey-green amorphous material on Papanicolaou; red/orange on Diff-Quik; positive Congo red (apple-green birefringence). Amyloid present in ~50-80% of cases.
✚ Positive
Calcitonin – diffuse strong (most specific marker)CEA – positive (serum and IHC)Synaptophysin – diffuseChromogranin A – positiveINSM1 – diffuse nuclearTTF1 – positive (~70%)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
Thyroglobulin – negative (key exclusion — distinguishes from follicular-derived tumours)PAX8 – variable/focal (usually negative in MTC, positive in follicular-derived)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Papillary Thyroid CarcinomaThyroglobulin+; no calcitonin; no amyloid; PTC nuclear features; papillary/follicular architecture
Hürthle Cell Neoplasm / Oncocytic tumourThyroglobulin+; Calcitonin−; abundant mitochondria-rich cytoplasm; no amyloid; no neuroendocrine markers
Metastatic Neuroendocrine TumourClinical history of primary NET; TTF1 may be positive (lung); Thyroglobulin−; Calcitonin−; site-specific markers
⚠ Pitfalls
  • MTC is highly variable morphologically — spindle cell variant mimics sarcoma; small cell variant mimics lymphoma; plasmacytoid mimics plasmacytoma. Calcitonin IHC on cell block is essential.
  • Amyloid may be absent (~20-50%) — do not exclude MTC on absence of amyloid alone.
  • Congo red staining on cell block: apple-green birefringence under polarised light confirms amyloid.
  • Serum calcitonin: if MTC suspected clinically, measure preoperatively — extremely elevated in MTC; also measure CEA. Calcitonin washout from FNA needle highly sensitive.
  • Sporadic vs hereditary: 25% of MTC is hereditary (MEN2A, MEN2B, FMTC) — RET mutation testing mandatory in ALL MTC.
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Arises from parafollicular C cells; accounts for ~5% of thyroid malignancies.
  • RET proto-oncogene mutations: somatic (sporadic, ~50%) or germline (hereditary — MEN2A/MEN2B/FMTC). Screen ALL MTC patients for germline RET — if positive, family screening and prophylactic thyroidectomy in carriers.
  • MEN2A (RET codon 634): MTC + phaeochromocytoma + primary hyperparathyroidism. MEN2B (RET codon 918): MTC + phaeochromocytoma + marfanoid habitus + mucosal neuromas — most aggressive.
  • Serum markers: calcitonin (staging, surveillance) + CEA (elevated CEA with normal calcitonin = dedifferentiation — worse prognosis).
  • Surgery: total thyroidectomy + central neck dissection (all cases) ± lateral neck dissection (if LN positive). Prophylactic thyroidectomy in RET carriers by age/mutation risk category (ATA guidelines).
  • Systemic therapy: vandetanib (RET/VEGFR inhibitor) or cabozantinib for progressive metastatic MTC. Selpercatinib/pralsetinib for RET-mutant (highly selective RET inhibitors).
  • Prognosis: 10-year OS ~70-80% overall; worse with distant metastases (bone, lung, liver).
ANAPLASTIC (UNDIFFERENTIATED) THYROID CARCINOMA — FNA (Thy5)
Head & Neck FNA Thy5 — Malignant Malignant
Highly cellular; markedly pleomorphic — three main patterns: spindle cell (sarcomatoid); giant cell (pleomorphic multinucleated giant cells — osteoclastic or pleomorphic type); epithelioid (squamoid/carcinomatous). Mixed patterns common.
Adequacy Criteria
  • Necrotic aspirates from anaplastic carcinoma may be paucicellular — sample viable peripheral tumour. Core needle biopsy preferable for histological diagnosis and molecular profiling.
Reporting System

RCPath Thyroid (Thy1–5) — Thy5 — Malignant

Cytological Features
  • Highly cellular; markedly pleomorphic — three main patterns: spindle cell (sarcomatoid); giant cell (pleomorphic multinucleated giant cells — osteoclastic or pleomorphic type); epithelioid (squamoid/carcinomatous). Mixed patterns common.
  • Extreme nuclear pleomorphism: bizarre nuclei; irregular chromatin; prominent nucleoli; atypical mitoses; tumour giant cells.
  • Necrosis — often extensive; inflammatory background (neutrophils); haemorrhage.
  • No colloid; no follicular architecture; no amyloid.
✚ Positive
CK – focal/variable (often retained, confirms epithelial origin despite undifferentiated morphology)PAX8 – positive (~80% — confirms thyroid origin, most useful marker)p53 – mutant pattern (strong diffuse or complete null)Ki67 – very high (>50-80%)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
Thyroglobulin – negative or focal only (lost with dedifferentiation)TTF1 – negative (lost with dedifferentiation)Calcitonin – negativeSOX10 – negative (excludes spindle cell melanoma)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
High-grade sarcoma (primary or metastatic)CK−, PAX8−; sarcoma-specific markers (SMA, Desmin, S100); clinical context; no thyroid origin
Poorly differentiated thyroid carcinoma (PDTC)Insular/trabecular/solid architecture; convoluted nuclei; mitoses >3/2mm²; necrosis; but less pleomorphic than ATC; PAX8+; TTF1 variable; Thyroglobulin focally positive
Metastatic carcinoma to thyroidClinical history; site-specific IHC (TTF1 for lung adenocarcinoma, CDX2 for colorectal, PAP/PSA for prostate); PAX8 negative in most metastases
⚠ Pitfalls
  • ATC is a morphological and clinical emergency — rapid clinical progression (weeks). Immediate MDT referral on cytology report.
  • Squamoid/epithelioid variant: may mimic squamous cell carcinoma metastasis — PAX8 positivity confirms thyroid origin.
  • Core needle biopsy preferred over FNA: provides better material for BRAF/RAS/TERT testing which guides targeted therapy eligibility.
  • CK negativity does not exclude ATC — IHC panel including PAX8 essential.
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Rarest thyroid malignancy (<2%) but most aggressive — median survival 3-5 months; 1-year OS ~20%.
  • All ATC = pT4 by definition (WHO/AJCC) regardless of size — always Stage IVA-C.
  • Molecular: BRAF V600E (~40%); RAS mutations (~20%); TERT promoter mutations (>70%); TP53 (>70%); often arises from dedifferentiation of pre-existing PTC or PDTC.
  • BRAF V600E-positive ATC: dabrafenib + trametinib (BRAF+MEK inhibition) — dramatic responses reported; FDA-approved. Test BRAF in ALL ATC.
  • Surgery: rarely curative; R0 resection if possible without major morbidity. Palliative debulking + EBRT + systemic therapy.
  • Immunotherapy: pembrolizumab ± targeted therapy under investigation.
  • MDT with high-volume centre essential. Palliation/hospice discussion early given prognosis.
POORLY DIFFERENTIATED THYROID CARCINOMA — FNA (Thy4/Thy5)
Head & Neck FNA Thy4 — Suspicious / Thy5 — Malignant Malignant
Cellular aspirate; insular, trabecular, or solid growth pattern — small to medium follicular cells without colloid formation.
Adequacy Criteria
  • Standard RCPath criteria. Cellular aspirate usually obtained — necrosis may limit interpretable material.
Reporting System

RCPath Thyroid (Thy1–5) — Thy4 — Suspicious / Thy5 — Malignant

Cytological Features
  • Cellular aspirate; insular, trabecular, or solid growth pattern — small to medium follicular cells without colloid formation.
  • Convoluted (raisinoid) nuclei — nuclear membrane irregularity without full PTC nuclear features; small nucleoli; fine chromatin. Less pleomorphic than ATC.
  • Mitoses present; necrosis (single cell or geographic); no well-formed follicles.
  • Scant/absent colloid; no amyloid; no psammoma bodies.
✚ Positive
TTF1 – positive (retained, unlike ATC)PAX8 – positiveThyroglobulin – variable/focal (reduced)Ki67 – elevated (>10-20%)p53 – mutant pattern in some
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
Calcitonin – negativeFull PTC nuclear features absent on cytology
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Well-differentiated NET G3 (metastatic)Synaptophysin+, Chromogranin+; TTF1+ (lung primary); Thyroglobulin−; clinical context; organoid architecture
Anaplastic Thyroid CarcinomaMore extreme pleomorphism; bizarre giant cells; TTF1 lost; Thyroglobulin lost; clinically more rapid
Medullary Thyroid CarcinomaCalcitonin+; amyloid; plasmacytoid morphology; Thyroglobulin−
⚠ Pitfalls
  • PDTC is a diagnosis of exclusion on cytology — Turin criteria require histological assessment (mitotic count, necrosis, convoluted nuclei). Cytology may only suggest 'high-grade follicular-derived carcinoma'.
  • Insular pattern can be focal in otherwise well-differentiated PTC — overall grade depends on dominant pattern on resection.
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • WHO 2022 Turin criteria (histological): solid/trabecular/insular architecture PLUS ≥1 of: necrosis, convoluted (raisinoid) nuclei, or mitoses ≥3/2mm².
  • Intermediate prognosis between well-differentiated PTC/FTC and ATC. 5-year OS ~50-60%.
  • TERT promoter mutations + RAS/BRAF: TERT + driver mutation = higher risk of dedifferentiation and mortality.
  • Surgery: total thyroidectomy + central neck dissection. RAI less effective than well-differentiated tumours but attempt if Tg-producing.
  • Lenvatinib/sorafenib: radioiodine-refractory PDTC — same systemic therapy as differentiated thyroid carcinoma.
THYROID FNA — RCPath THY CATEGORY OVERVIEW
Head & Neck FNA Thy1–Thy5 framework Variable 📋 1×
Thy1 / Thy1c: non-diagnostic. Thy1c = cystic, acellular. Repeat FNA with USS guidance; sample solid areas.
Adequacy Criteria
  • Adequate (Thy2–5): ≥6 groups of ≥10 well-preserved follicular cells from ≥2 slides.
  • Thy1: non-diagnostic — insufficient cells, poor preparation, or obscured by blood/cyst fluid.
  • Thy1c: cystic lesion — non-diagnostic; acellular or only macrophages; sample solid component.
Reporting System

RCPath Thyroid (Thy1–5) — Thy1–Thy5 framework

Cytological Features
  • Thy1 / Thy1c: non-diagnostic. Thy1c = cystic, acellular. Repeat FNA with USS guidance; sample solid areas.
  • Thy2: benign/non-neoplastic. Colloid goitre, Hashimoto's, De Quervain's, simple cyst. Risk of malignancy <3%.
  • Thy3a: atypia of undetermined significance (AUS). Architectural or nuclear atypia insufficient for Thy3f/Thy4. ROM ~10-30%. Repeat FNA or molecular testing. Includes: nuclear atypia in Hashimoto's; scant follicular cells; Hürthle cell change.
  • Thy3f: follicular neoplasm. Cellular smear; microfollicular pattern; scant colloid; may be FA or FTC — cannot distinguish on cytology. ROM ~25-40%. Hemithyroidectomy standard.
  • Thy4: suspicious of malignancy. Features suggest but insufficient for definitive malignancy. ROM ~60-75%. Usually PTC features short of Thy5; MTC possible.
  • Thy5: malignant. Definitive malignant diagnosis. ROM >95%. PTC most common; MTC; ATC; lymphoma; metastasis.
✚ Positive
N/A — category framework card
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
N/A
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Thy3a vs Thy3fThy3a = atypia (nuclear/architectural) not qualifying as follicular neoplasm; Thy3f = cellular, microfollicular, scant colloid — distinct management pathways
Thy4 vs Thy5Thy4 = suspicious but not definitive; Thy5 = diagnostic confidence for malignancy. Clinical impact: both usually proceed to surgery but Thy5 may direct more radical initial surgery
Thy2 vs Thy3aThy2 = clearly benign; Thy3a = atypical features creating genuine diagnostic uncertainty. Key: Thy3a triggers active management (repeat/molecular); Thy2 does not
⚠ Pitfalls
  • Hashimoto's thyroiditis: lymphocytic background + Hürthle cell change can mimic Thy3a or even Thy4 — clinical context (TPO antibodies, USS) essential.
  • Cystic PTC: heavily cystic aspirate may yield only macrophages (Thy1c) — USS-guided repeat targeting solid mural nodule.
  • Follicular variant PTC (FVPTC): nuclear features may be subtle on cytology; may be reported as Thy3f; NIFTP (non-invasive follicular thyroid neoplasm with papillary-like nuclei) now recognised as low-risk — molecular testing increasingly used.
  • Hürthle cell (oncocytic) neoplasm: cellular aspirate with Hürthle cells ± microfollicular pattern — reported as Thy3f; Hürthle cell carcinoma cannot be excluded on cytology alone.
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
