  • Risk of malignancy (ROM) by category: Thy1 ~5-10% (if excised); Thy1c ~5%; Thy2 <3%; Thy3a ~10-30%; Thy3f ~25-40%; Thy4 ~60-75%; Thy5 >95%.
  • Molecular testing: ThyroSeq v3, Afirma GSC — increasing use for Thy3a/Thy3f to refine ROM and guide surgery vs surveillance.
  • BRAF V600E: ~80% classical PTC. If detected in Thy3a/Thy3f → effectively reclassifies to Thy5 clinically.
  • RET/PTC rearrangements: radiation-associated PTC; younger patients. RAS mutations: follicular-patterned lesions (FA, FVPTC, FTC).
  • NIFTP: encapsulated FVPTC with no invasion — indolent; reclassified as non-malignant (2016). Impacts ROM calculations for Thy3f/Thy4 categories.
  • Repeat FNA guidance: Thy1 → repeat at 3 months. Thy1c → USS-guided repeat targeting solid component. Thy3a → repeat at 3 months or molecular testing.
  • MDT discussion for all Thy3 and above. Thy5 ATC: urgent MDT — prognosis dismal, BRAF/RAS testing for targeted therapy (dabrafenib + trametinib if BRAF V600E+).
MESOTHELIOMA — EFFUSION CYTOLOGY (IAC Harrogate)
Thoracic Effusion Positive for malignancy — Mesothelioma Malignant 📋 1×
Large cohesive clusters/papillary groups and single cells; mesothelial cells with abundant dense cytoplasm, two-tone cytoplasm (pale inner, dense outer rim), and 'windows' (intercellular spaces) between adjacent cells.
Adequacy Criteria
  • IAC Harrogate minimum: adequate cellularity for evaluation — no fixed cell count. Presence of mesothelial cells + clinical/radiological context.
  • Cytological diagnosis of mesothelioma now accepted for clinical management (revised 2019 IMIG/IAC guidelines) — biopsy for histological subtype still preferred where feasible.
Reporting System

IAC Harrogate (Effusion) — Positive for malignancy — Mesothelioma

Cytological Features
  • Large cohesive clusters/papillary groups and single cells; mesothelial cells with abundant dense cytoplasm, two-tone cytoplasm (pale inner, dense outer rim), and 'windows' (intercellular spaces) between adjacent cells.
  • Multinucleation and macronucleoli; cell-in-cell engulfment; scalloped cell borders; large nuclei with prominent nucleoli. Morula/berry-like clusters.
  • Background: bloody/proteinaceous; no mucin (distinguishes from adenocarcinoma); may be paucicellular in fibrous mesothelioma.
✚ Positive
Calretinin – diffuse nuclear+cytoplasmic (most sensitive/specific)CK5/6 – positiveWT1 – nuclearD2-40 – positiveHBME-1 – positiveBAP1 – LOSS (nuclear loss in ~60% of epithelioid mesothelioma — confirms malignancy)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CEA – negativeMOC31 (EpCAM) – negativeBerEP4 – negativeTTF1 – negativeGATA3 – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Metastatic adenocarcinoma (lung)TTF1+, BerEP4+, CEA+, MOC31+; Calretinin−; no intercellular windows; mucin+
Reactive mesothelial hyperplasiaBAP1 retained; CDKN2A (p16) deletion absent by FISH; clinical/radiological context; no significant atypia
High grade serous carcinoma (ovarian)PAX8+, WT1+ (but also in mesothelioma — WT1 not specific); ER+; MOC31+; BerEP4+; Calretinin−
⚠ Pitfalls
  • Reactive mesothelial cells can show significant atypia — BAP1 IHC loss and CDKN2A FISH deletion are essential to confirm malignancy on cytology.
  • High grade serous carcinoma: WT1 positive in both — always run full panel including Calretinin, BerEP4, MOC31, PAX8.
  • Paucicellular effusion in fibrous/desmoplastic mesothelioma — biopsy required for diagnosis.
  • Cell block essential for IHC — cytospin alone insufficient for mesothelioma diagnosis.
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • BAP1 IHC loss: confirms malignant transformation in mesothelial cells — ~60% sensitivity for epithelioid mesothelioma, nearly 100% specific. Retained in reactive cells.
  • CDKN2A (p16) FISH homozygous deletion: complementary to BAP1 — ~75% sensitive, highly specific. Covers BAP1-retained mesotheliomas.
  • IAC Harrogate 2019: cytological diagnosis of mesothelioma acceptable if BAP1 loss OR p16 deletion confirmed on cell block/cytospin.
  • Asbestos exposure history: crocidolite (blue asbestos) highest risk. Latency 30–40 years. Occupational/environmental exposure key trigger.
  • Prognosis dismal (median OS ~12–18 months for pleural). Immunotherapy (ipilimumab + nivolumab) first-line in unresectable — CheckMate 743.
PLEOMORPHIC ADENOMA — SALIVARY FNA (Milan II)
Head & Neck FNA Milan II — Non-neoplastic Benign 📋 2×
Characteristic chondromyxoid (fibrillary/metachromatic) stroma — magenta on Diff-Quik, pale green/grey on Papanicolaou; forms 'tails' or pools around epithelial cells.
Adequacy Criteria
  • Milan: no strict cell number requirement for salivary FNA. Adequacy judged by presence of diagnostic material. Fibrillary matrix alone (in absence of cells) can be diagnostic if characteristic.
  • Hypocellular aspirates acceptable for PA diagnosis if metachromatic fibrillar stroma + epithelial component present.
Reporting System

Milan (Salivary) — Milan II — Non-neoplastic

Cytological Features
  • Characteristic chondromyxoid (fibrillary/metachromatic) stroma — magenta on Diff-Quik, pale green/grey on Papanicolaou; forms 'tails' or pools around epithelial cells.
  • Epithelial/myoepithelial cells: small bland cells in cohesive groups, tubular arrangements, or dispersed; plasmacytoid myoepithelial cells; squamous/spindle/clear cell variants.
  • Background: clean; no inflammatory infiltrate. Combination of stroma + dual epithelial/myoepithelial population is diagnostic.
✚ Positive
CK7 – ductal epithelial componentS100 – myoepithelial componentSOX10 – myoepithelialGFAP – myoepithelial (variable)p63 – myoepithelial
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
Ki67 – lowp40 – myoepithelial (same as p63)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Adenoid Cystic Carcinoma (Milan V/VI)Cylindromatous matrix (spherical globules with palisading cells); dark angulated basaloid nuclei; basement membrane material; MYB rearrangement; no myxoid stroma tails
Cellular PA vs Carcinoma ex PACytological atypia, necrosis, high Ki67 raise suspicion — requires histology; Milan IVb if suspicious
Basal cell adenomaBasaloid cells without chondromyxoid stroma; palisading; solid nests; CK7+, SMA+; CYLD mutation
⚠ Pitfalls
  • Hypercellular PA without stroma: can mimic carcinoma — correlate with clinical/US; Milan IVb if truly suspicious.
  • Stroma may be scarce in recurrent PA — use clinical context (prior surgery).
  • Carcinoma ex PA: sudden growth in longstanding lump; atypia in otherwise bland PA background → report as Milan V/VI with comment.
  • FNCLCC grading not applicable on cytology — histology needed for carcinoma ex PA grading.
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Most common salivary gland tumour (~65%). Parotid in 80%. PLAG1 (8q12) or HMGA2 (12q14) rearrangement.
  • Milan II (non-neoplastic) for unequivocal PA on FNA — ROM approximately 0%.
  • Recurrence rate post-superficial parotidectomy ~2–5% (increased with extracapsular dissection/incomplete excision); malignant transformation in longstanding PA ~5–10%.
  • Chondromyxoid stroma on Diff-Quik is the single most discriminating cytological feature — magenta metachromatic material.
🔬 Histology Counterpart
↗ PLEOMORPHIC ADENOMA (PA) — HISTOLOGY
WARTHIN'S TUMOUR (PAPILLARY CYSTADENOMA LYMPHOMATOSUM) — SALIVARY FNA (Milan II)
Head & Neck FNA Milan II — Non-neoplastic Benign 📋 1×
Oncocytic (Hürthle) cells: columnar/cuboidal cells with abundant granular eosinophilic cytoplasm, round uniform nuclei; arranged in flat sheets or papillary fronds.
Adequacy Criteria
  • Cystic component may dominate — aspirate of cyst fluid alone is non-diagnostic (Milan I). Sample any solid/mural component.
  • Diagnostic cytology: oncocytic cells + lymphoid background + cystic fluid. Any two of three is supportive.
Reporting System

Milan (Salivary) — Milan II — Non-neoplastic

Cytological Features
  • Oncocytic (Hürthle) cells: columnar/cuboidal cells with abundant granular eosinophilic cytoplasm, round uniform nuclei; arranged in flat sheets or papillary fronds.
  • Lymphoid background: mature lymphocytes and plasma cells intimately mixed with oncocytic cells — essential component.
  • Cystic contents: turbid/dark (motor oil-like) fluid; cholesterol crystals; mucoid material; foamy macrophages.
✚ Positive
CK7 – oncocytic cells positiveCK8/18 – positiveGCDFP-15 – variable
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
S100 – negative (distinguishes from PA myoepithelial component)SOX10 – negativeKi67 – very low
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Oncocytic carcinoma (Milan V/VI)Marked nuclear atypia; high Ki67; necrosis; invasion on biopsy; no lymphoid background
Hürthle cell neoplasm in ectopic thyroid tissueTTF1+, Thyroglobulin+; no lymphoid background; clinical context
Low-grade mucoepidermoid carcinomaMucous cells + intermediate cells + squamous cells; mucin+; no oncocytic cells; no lymphoid background; MAML2 fusion
⚠ Pitfalls
  • Infarcted Warthin's (post-FNA): haemorrhagic necrosis, ghost cells, histiocytes — can mimic carcinoma on repeat FNA. Clinical history of prior aspiration critical.
  • Bilateral parotid Warthin's in ~10% — do not diagnose as malignancy based on bilaterality alone.
  • Lymphoma in Warthin's background: rare but reported — atypical lymphoid cells warrant flow cytometry.
  • Mucoepidermoid carcinoma (low-grade) can be cystic with mucous cells — careful search for intermediate/squamous cells.
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Second most common parotid tumour (~5–10%). Virtually exclusive to parotid. Strong smoking association.
  • M>F, older adults (50–70). Bilateral in ~10%. No malignant potential in pure Warthin's.
  • BRAF V600E mutation reported in ~40–80% — not clinically actionable but of molecular interest.
  • Milan II — benign: ROM <1%. Management: observation (small, asymptomatic) or superficial parotidectomy.
HIGH GRADE UROTHELIAL CARCINOMA — URINE CYTOLOGY (Paris V)
Urological Urine Paris V — Positive for high grade urothelial carcinoma Malignant 📋 7×
High N:C ratio (>0.7); hyperchromatic irregular nuclei; nuclear membrane irregularity (angular/notched); coarse chromatin; prominent nucleoli.
Adequacy Criteria
  • Paris system: voided urine adequate if ≥10 well-preserved urothelial cells. Catheterised/instrumented specimens more cellular — lower threshold for cellularity concerns.
  • Low grade UCC: Paris explicitly states cytology has LOW sensitivity for low-grade papillary UCC — these are often Paris II/III.
Reporting System

Paris (Urine) — Paris V — Positive for high grade urothelial carcinoma

Cytological Features
  • High N:C ratio (>0.7); hyperchromatic irregular nuclei; nuclear membrane irregularity (angular/notched); coarse chromatin; prominent nucleoli.
  • Cells singly dispersed or in small irregular clusters; necrotic background (tumour diathesis); macronucleoli in some.
  • CIS: smaller cells, high N:C, eccentrically placed nucleus, 'cercariform' (tadpole) cells in some cases. Cytoplasm often scant.
✚ Positive
GATA3 – positive (confirmatory on cell block)CK7 – positiveUroplakin II – variablep53 – mutant pattern in HGUC
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CK20 – variableCD44 – negative (positive in reactive cells)CK5/6 – negative (positive in squamous differentiation)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Reactive urothelial atypia / instrumentation effectParis II/III; N:C ratio <0.7; smooth nuclear membranes; degenerative vacuolation; clinical history of recent instrumentation
High grade urothelial carcinoma in situCannot reliably distinguish CIS from invasive HGUC on urine cytology — both Paris V; clinical/cystoscopic correlation
Squamous cell carcinoma (bladder)Squamous pearls; intercellular bridges; CK5/6+, p40+; GATA3 often negative/weak
⚠ Pitfalls
  • Instrumentation effect: catheterisation causes cellular damage mimicking atypia — Paris III at most; correlate with instrumentation history.
  • Polyoma virus (decoy cells): large homogeneous ground-glass nuclei; smudged chromatin; no irregular nuclear membrane — Paris II; HPyVs IHC/ISH if in doubt.
  • Low grade papillary UCC: cytology has very low sensitivity (~20–30%) — negative urine cytology does NOT exclude low-grade UCC; cystoscopy required.
  • LBC vs voided urine: LBC preserves cells better but changes morphological appearances — calibrate N:C ratio interpretation per preparation.
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Paris system 2016 (updated 2022): Negative (I) → Atypical (II) → AUC (III) → SHGUC (IV) → Positive HGUC (V) → Malignant other (VI).
  • Sensitivity of urine cytology for HGUC: ~70–85%; specificity ~97% for Paris V. Sensitivity for low-grade: ~20%.
  • FISH (UroVysion): polysomy 3, 7, 17 and 9p21 deletion — increases sensitivity for HGUC; useful for Paris III/IV cases.
  • CIS: flat intraluminal shedding of CIS cells → Paris V. Management: BCG intravesical therapy ± radical cystectomy.
  • Monitoring post-TURBT/cystectomy: urine cytology + ureteric washings (upper tract surveillance).
🔬 Histology Counterpart
↗ UROTHELIAL CARCINOMA (INVASIVE) AND CIS
SALIVARY GLAND FNA — MILAN SYSTEM OVERVIEW
Head & Neck FNA Milan I–VI framework Variable
Milan I — Non-diagnostic: insufficient/obscured material. ROM ~25%. Repeat FNA recommended.
Adequacy Criteria
  • No minimum cell count defined — adequacy based on presence of diagnostic material for the clinical question.
  • Milan I: non-diagnostic — acellular, obscured by blood, air-drying artefact, or insufficient material.
Reporting System

Milan (Salivary) — Milan I–VI framework

Cytological Features
  • Milan I — Non-diagnostic: insufficient/obscured material. ROM ~25%. Repeat FNA recommended.
  • Milan II — Non-neoplastic: reactive, infectious, inflammatory, cysts. Includes PA, Warthin's (when diagnostic). ROM ~10%.
  • Milan III — Atypia of undetermined significance (AUS): low-grade atypia not meeting neoplasm criteria. ROM ~20%. Clinical/US review; repeat or excision.
  • Milan IVa — Benign neoplasm: PA with diagnostic features, Warthin's (unequivocal), basal cell adenoma. ROM ~5%.
  • Milan IVb — Neoplasm of uncertain malignant potential: features of neoplasm but cannot exclude malignancy — hypercellular PA, oncocytic tumour. ROM ~35%.
  • Milan V — Suspicious for malignancy: strong features but not definitive. ROM ~60%. Surgical excision.
  • Milan VI — Malignant: definitive malignancy — mucoepidermoid, AdCC, carcinoma ex PA, lymphoma, metastasis. ROM >90%.
✚ Positive
N/A — framework card
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
N/A
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Milan IVa vs IVbIVa = PA with unequivocal chondromyxoid stroma + dual cell population; IVb = neoplastic features present but uncertain malignant potential — hypercellularity, atypia, absent stroma
Milan II vs IVaIVa reserved for clearly neoplastic lesions; Milan II includes non-neoplastic cysts and inflammatory lesions
Milan V vs VIV = suspicious but not diagnostic; VI = sufficient cytological evidence for definitive malignant diagnosis
⚠ Pitfalls
  • PA without stroma: hypercellular PA may be reported Milan IVb or V — histology needed.
  • Infarcted Warthin's (post-aspiration): necrosis + ghost cells → Milan III/V — always obtain history of prior FNA.
  • Low-grade mucoepidermoid CA: mucous cells + cystic content → may be reported Milan II or III — low threshold for excision of cystic parotid lesions.
  • Lymphoma: lymphoid-predominant aspirate → flow cytometry essential before Milan VI designation.
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • ROM by category: I ~25%, II ~10%, III ~20%, IVa ~5%, IVb ~35%, V ~60%, VI >90% (MSRSGC 2018 data).
  • Parotid ~80% of salivary gland tumours; submandibular ~10%; sublingual/minor ~10%.
  • Most common salivary gland tumour: PA (~65%). Most common malignancy: mucoepidermoid carcinoma (adults), acinic cell carcinoma (children).
  • Molecular testing increasingly available: MAML2 (mucoepidermoid), MYB/MYBL1 (AdCC), PRKD1 (PAC), ETV6 (secretory carcinoma), PLAG1/HMGA2 (PA).
HIGH GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA — EFFUSION CYTOLOGY (IAC Harrogate)
Gynaecological Effusion Positive for malignancy — Adenocarcinoma (HGSC) Malignant
Large cohesive papillary clusters and three-dimensional spheres ('morula/cannon-ball clusters'); cells with moderate-to-abundant cytoplasm; marked nuclear pleomorphism; prominent nucleoli.
Adequacy Criteria
  • IAC Harrogate: no fixed cell count — diagnostic material required. Cell block essential for IHC confirmation.
  • Pleural/peritoneal/pericardial — HGSC most common malignant effusion in women.
Reporting System

IAC Harrogate (Effusion) — Positive for malignancy — Adenocarcinoma (HGSC)

Cytological Features
  • Large cohesive papillary clusters and three-dimensional spheres ('morula/cannon-ball clusters'); cells with moderate-to-abundant cytoplasm; marked nuclear pleomorphism; prominent nucleoli.
  • Psammoma bodies (concentric calcifications within cell clusters) — when present, highly characteristic of serous carcinoma.
  • Background: bloody/proteinaceous; no intercellular windows (distinguishes from mesothelioma); single malignant cells with high N:C ratio.
✚ Positive
WT1 – diffuse nuclearPAX8 – diffuseBerEP4 – positiveMOC31 – positiveCK7 – diffusep53 – aberrant pattern
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
Calretinin – negativeCK5/6 – negativeMesothelin – negative (positive in mesothelioma)CK20 – negativeER – negative/weak
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Reactive/malignant mesotheliomaCalretinin+, CK5/6+, WT1+ (shared); BerEP4−, MOC31−, PAX8−; intercellular windows; BAP1 loss
Metastatic breast carcinomaER/PR+, GATA3+, mammaglobulin+; WT1−; PAX8−; clinical history
Endometrial carcinomaWT1 negative (key); PAX8+; ER+; CK7+; clinical context
⚠ Pitfalls
  • WT1 is positive in both HGSC and mesothelioma — never rely on WT1 alone; always include BerEP4/MOC31 and Calretinin/CK5/6.
  • Psammoma bodies can be seen in reactive mesothelium — do not diagnose malignancy on psammoma bodies alone.
  • Peritoneal washings at staging: even single convincing malignant cells = positive. Reactive mesothelial atypia mimics carcinoma — cell block + IHC mandatory.
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Most common cause of malignant effusion in women. Peritoneal washings are part of FIGO staging for ovarian/endometrial carcinoma.
  • IAC Harrogate category: Positive (V) for malignancy — specify 'adenocarcinoma, favour HGSC' with IHC panel.
  • BRCA status should be flagged in report if known — impacts systemic therapy selection.
  • Peritoneal washing cytology: positive washes = FIGO IIIA1 for ovarian, Stage IIIA for endometrial — critical staging information.
🔬 Histology Counterpart
↗ HIGH GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA (OVARY / PERITONEUM / FALLOPIAN TUBE)
MELANOMA — LYMPH NODE / SKIN MASS FNA
Haempath FNA Positive for malignancy — Melanoma Malignant 📋 5×
Dyscohesive large epithelioid or spindle cells; abundant cytoplasm; prominent eosinophilic macronucleoli ('cherry-red' nucleolus) — hallmark.
Adequacy Criteria
  • Descriptive reporting for LN FNA / skin mass. No formal adequacy criteria — cell block for IHC.
  • LN FNA for melanoma metastasis: highly cellular aspirates typical; cell block essential for S100/SOX10/Melan-A/HMB45.
Reporting System

Descriptive — Positive for malignancy — Melanoma

Cytological Features
  • Dyscohesive large epithelioid or spindle cells; abundant cytoplasm; prominent eosinophilic macronucleoli ('cherry-red' nucleolus) — hallmark.
  • Intracytoplasmic melanin pigment (brown granules, Fontana-Masson+) — may be absent (amelanotic). Binucleation and multinucleation.
  • Plasmacytoid morphology in some variants. Intranuclear pseudoinclusions (cytoplasmic invaginations — also seen in PTC/MTC).
✚ Positive
S100 – diffuse strongSOX10 – diffuse nuclearMelan-A – positiveHMB45 – positive (may be lost in metastatic)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CK – negativeCD45 – negativeCD30 – negative (ALCL)TTF1 – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Metastatic carcinomaCK diffuse+; S100/SOX10−; site-specific markers (TTF1 lung, GATA3 breast/urothelial, PSA prostate)
DLBCL / large cell lymphomaCD45+, CD20+, PAX5+; S100−; Melan-A−; lymphoglandular bodies in background
Granular cell tumourS100+; SOX10+; HMB45−, Melan-A−; PAS+ granules; no atypia; benign
⚠ Pitfalls
  • Amelanotic melanoma: no pigment — relies entirely on IHC. Always include S100/SOX10/Melan-A/HMB45 on cell block for any pleomorphic LN FNA.
  • Desmoplastic melanoma: HMB45 and Melan-A often negative — S100/SOX10 only positive markers. Spindled cells in fibrous background.
  • Pseudoinclusions in melanoma nuclei can mimic PTC — site, clinical context, and full IHC panel essential.
  • Intranuclear inclusions: present in melanoma AND PTC AND MTC — do not use as discriminating feature in isolation.
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • BRAF V600E IHC (VE1 clone): if positive on cell block → reflexes to molecular confirmation for targeted therapy eligibility.
  • LN FNA for staging: sensitivity ~90% for metastatic melanoma; specificity ~100%. Avoids formal LN dissection if positive.
  • Molecular: request BRAF/NRAS/NF1/KIT on cell block material — guides systemic therapy. PDL1 IHC for immunotherapy (less critical as IO now first-line regardless).
  • Report format: 'Positive for malignancy. Morphology and IHC profile consistent with metastatic melanoma (S100+, SOX10+, Melan-A+, HMB45+, CK−).'
🔬 Histology Counterpart
↗ MELANOMA (CUTANEOUS)
CLASSICAL HODGKIN LYMPHOMA — LN FNA
Haempath FNA Suspicious/positive for lymphoma — Classical Hodgkin Malignant 📋 2×
Reed-Sternberg cells: large binucleate cells with prominent eosinophilic 'owl-eye' nucleoli; abundant pale cytoplasm — in mixed inflammatory background.
Adequacy Criteria
  • LN FNA adequacy: representative material with lymphoid cells. Cell block essential for IHC confirmation.
  • Core needle biopsy preferred over FNA for primary lymphoma diagnosis — FNA adequate for known relapse or if patient cannot tolerate biopsy.
Reporting System

Descriptive — Suspicious/positive for lymphoma — Classical Hodgkin

Cytological Features
  • Reed-Sternberg cells: large binucleate cells with prominent eosinophilic 'owl-eye' nucleoli; abundant pale cytoplasm — in mixed inflammatory background.
  • Mononuclear variants (Hodgkin cells, lacunar cells). RS cells often sparse — majority of cells are reactive (lymphocytes, eosinophils, plasma cells, histiocytes).
  • Background: polymorphous mixed inflammation; eosinophilia characteristic; no lymphoglandular bodies (unlike NHL).
✚ Positive
CD30 – RS cells strong membranous/GolgiCD15 – RS cells positivePAX5 – RS cells weak nuclear
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
CD45 – RS cells negativeCD20 – RS cells negative (or very weak)ALK – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
NLPHLLP 'popcorn' cells: CD20+, CD45+, CD30−, CD15−; B-cell-rich background; no eosinophils
ALCL (T-cell)CD30+ large cells but CD45+, T-cell markers (CD3/CD5/CD4/TIA1), ALK+ in systemic; no RS morphology
Reactive lymphadenopathy with immunoblastsNo RS cells; CD30 weak/focal; CD15−; CD45+; clinical context (EBV/CMV)
⚠ Pitfalls
  • RS cells are rare — scanning at low power essential; majority of aspirate is reactive background.
  • Infectious mononucleosis: immunoblasts can mimic RS cells on FNA — EBV serology; CD30 may be weakly positive; CD15 negative; EBER ISH on cell block.
  • Core biopsy always preferred for primary lymphoma diagnosis — FNA may miss architecture-dependent diagnoses (follicular lymphoma, NLPHL).
  • LMP1 (EBV): positive in ~40% of cHL (mixed cellularity > nodular sclerosis) — useful supportive IHC.
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • FNA of cervical/mediastinal LN: suspicious smear → core biopsy for architectural assessment and definitive subtyping.
  • Reporting: 'Atypical large cells morphologically consistent with Reed-Sternberg cells in a mixed inflammatory background. IHC profile (CD30+, CD15+, CD45−) supports classical Hodgkin lymphoma. Core biopsy recommended for subtype classification.'
  • PET-CT: Hodgkin is uniformly FDG-avid — essential for staging and response assessment.
  • EBV association: mixed cellularity (~75%) > nodular sclerosis (~25%). EBV-positive cHL has better outcome with ABVD.
🔬 Histology Counterpart
↗ CLASSICAL HODGKIN LYMPHOMA (cHL)
LYMPHOCYTIC EFFUSION — DIFFERENTIAL FRAMEWORK (IAC Harrogate)
Haempath Effusion Negative for malignancy / Atypical Variable 📋 21×
Lymphocyte-predominant: >50% small mature lymphocytes; occasional large reactive lymphocytes/immunoblasts; mesothelial cells; macrophages.
Adequacy Criteria
  • IAC Harrogate: lymphocyte-predominant effusion — adequate if sufficient cells for differential cell count. Flow cytometry on fresh fluid if lymphoma suspected.
  • Always send fresh unfixed fluid for flow cytometry simultaneously with cytology — cannot retrospectively perform clonality on fixed material.
Reporting System

IAC Harrogate (Effusion) — Negative for malignancy / Atypical

Cytological Features
  • Lymphocyte-predominant: >50% small mature lymphocytes; occasional large reactive lymphocytes/immunoblasts; mesothelial cells; macrophages.
  • Reactive/transudative causes (most common): cardiac failure (bilateral), TB (exudate, unilateral), viral pleuritis, rheumatoid pleuritis, chylothorax, post-cardiac injury syndrome.
  • Atypical lymphocytes raising lymphoma concern: monotonous large lymphoid cells; cleaved/irregular nuclei; lymphoglandular bodies (smeared nuclear material — NHL hallmark on Diff-Quik).
✚ Positive
CD45 – all lymphocytesCD3/CD5 – T-cells (reactive effusion: T-cell predominant)CD20/PAX5 – B-cellsFlow cytometry: κ/λ light chain ratio (>3:1 or <0.3 = clonal B-cell)
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
Calretinin – mesothelial cells (not lymphocytes)CK – negative in lymphocytesBerEP4 – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
TB pleuritisLymphocyte-predominant exudate; ADA (adenosine deaminase) elevated >40 U/L; granulomas on pleural biopsy; ZN/culture; IGRA/Mantoux
Primary effusion lymphoma (PEL)HIV+; HHV8+; EBV+; large anaplastic cells; CD45+, CD30+/−; B-cell markers negative; body cavity only; no solid mass
CLL/SLL in effusionMonotonous small lymphocytes; CD5+, CD23+, CD20 dim, CD10−; flow cytometry clonal B-cell; FMC7−; low-level surface Ig
⚠ Pitfalls
  • Always send fresh fluid for flow cytometry — retrospective clonality testing on fixed Cytospin not possible.
  • TB: ADA >40 U/L in lymphocytic exudate = TB until proven otherwise; pleural biopsy for granulomas/culture more sensitive than cytology.
  • Chylothorax: milky fluid; triglycerides >110mg/dL; lymphocyte-predominant; caused by thoracic duct obstruction (lymphoma, trauma, surgery).
  • Reactive large lymphocytes post-cardiac surgery (Dressler syndrome): immunoblasts can mimic lymphoma — clinical context essential.
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • IAC Harrogate reporting: lymphocyte-predominant effusion → 'Negative for malignancy' if reactive, OR 'Atypical lymphoid cells, flow cytometry recommended' if clonal/suspicious.
  • TB effusion: most common cause of lymphocytic exudate worldwide. ADA >40 U/L: 93% sensitive, 90% specific for TB pleuritis. Cultures positive only ~30%.
  • Chylothorax diagnosis: triglycerides >110mg/dL (specific); lymphocyte predominant; milky appearance; Sudan III+ fat globules.
  • Flow cytometry interpretation: κ:λ ratio >3:1 → B-cell clonality. T-cell clonality: TCR Vβ repertoire or PCR.
BILIARY BRUSHINGS — FRAMEWORK (Paris / Descriptive)
Hepatobiliary Bile duct Negative / Atypical / Suspicious / Malignant Variable 📋 5×
Normal/negative: flat honeycomb sheets of bland columnar biliary cells; uniform nuclei with smooth membranes; small nucleoli; abundant cytoplasm. Background: bile pigment, mucus, inflammatory cells.
Adequacy Criteria
  • Biliary brushing adequacy: ≥10 well-preserved biliary epithelial cells. Inadequate specimens are common (~20–30%) — notify endoscopist immediately for re-brushing.
  • ERCP brushing: single pass gives ~30–40% sensitivity for cholangiocarcinoma. Multiple passes, endobiliary FNA, or cholangioscopy (Spyglass) increase yield.
Reporting System

Descriptive — Negative / Atypical / Suspicious / Malignant

Cytological Features
  • Normal/negative: flat honeycomb sheets of bland columnar biliary cells; uniform nuclei with smooth membranes; small nucleoli; abundant cytoplasm. Background: bile pigment, mucus, inflammatory cells.
  • Atypical: mild nuclear enlargement or anisonucleosis insufficient for malignancy — reactive (stent, stones, PSC, IgG4-SC). Report as 'Atypical biliary cells — clinical correlation required.'
  • Suspicious/malignant: nuclear crowding; loss of polarity; irregular nuclear membranes; prominent nucleoli; three-dimensional clusters; single malignant cells; necrotic background.
✚ Positive
KRAS mutation (molecular) – ~50% cholangiocarcinoma (liquid biopsy or brushing DNA)MUC5AC – adenocarcinomaCEA – malignant
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
N/A — morphological diagnosis primarily; FISH p16 deletion for malignancy in atypical
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Reactive atypia (stent/stone/PSC)Clinical history of stent placement or calculi; mild uniform atypia; no loss of polarity; honeycomb architecture preserved; resolves on clinical follow-up
Cholangiocarcinoma (hilar/distal)Three-dimensional clusters; nuclear pleomorphism; irregular membranes; prominent nucleoli; malignant single cells; FISH p16 deletion
Extrinsic compression by pancreatic adenocarcinomaBrushings may be negative/atypical — cells from reactive biliary epithelium; pancreatic FNA (EUS) more informative
⚠ Pitfalls
  • Sensitivity for cholangiocarcinoma only ~30–40% — negative brushings do NOT exclude malignancy. Report: 'Negative for malignancy — clinical/radiological correlation mandatory.'
  • PSC and IgG4-SC cause reactive atypia mimicking malignancy — always flag PSC history; FISH (p16/CEP9) increases specificity.
  • Stent effect: epithelial denudation, reactive changes, granulation tissue — artefact mimics atypia. Note stent in situ on request form.
  • Hilar cholangiocarcinoma (Klatskin tumour): brushings at bifurcation technically difficult — cholangioscopy-directed biopsy preferred.
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • FISH (fluorescence in situ hybridisation): p16 (9p21) homozygous deletion or polysomy 3/7/17 increases sensitivity to ~50–60% for malignancy in atypical brushings.
  • Reporting categories: Negative (C1) / Inadequate (C1i) / Atypical (C3) / Suspicious (C4) / Malignant (C5) — analogous to breast cytology.
  • Cholangioscopy-directed biopsy (Spyglass): sensitivity ~75–80% for malignancy — superior to standard brushings; reserved for indeterminate strictures.
  • IgG4-related cholangiopathy: can mimic hilar cholangiocarcinoma radiologically — IgG4 serum + steroid response trial before surgery if diagnosis uncertain.
LUNG ADENOCARCINOMA — BRONCHIAL BRUSHINGS / FNA
Thoracic Bronchial brushings Positive for malignancy — Adenocarcinoma Malignant
Three-dimensional glandular clusters and papillary groups; cells with moderate vacuolated cytoplasm; round-to-oval eccentric nuclei; prominent nucleoli.
Adequacy Criteria
  • Bronchial brushings: adequate if ≥5 well-preserved bronchial epithelial cells. Peripheral lesions may yield scanty material — CT-guided FNA preferred for peripheral masses.
  • Cell block mandatory for molecular testing: EGFR/ALK/ROS1/KRAS/BRAF/MET/RET/NTRK + PD-L1. Triage material allocation before exhausting with IHC.
Reporting System

Descriptive — Positive for malignancy — Adenocarcinoma

Cytological Features
  • Three-dimensional glandular clusters and papillary groups; cells with moderate vacuolated cytoplasm; round-to-oval eccentric nuclei; prominent nucleoli.
  • Intracytoplasmic mucin vacuoles (Diff-Quik: magenta-staining); occasional signet-ring-like cells in mucinous variant; acinar rosette formation.
  • Background: clean or mucoid; necrotic debris in high-grade. Bronchioloalveolar pattern (lepidic): flat honeycombed sheets — low-grade; may mimic reactive cells.
✚ Positive
TTF1 – diffuse nuclear (cell block)Napsin A – granular cytoplasmic (cell block)CK7 – diffuse
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
p40 – negativeCK20 – negativeCalretinin – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Reactive bronchial epithelial cellsCiliated columnar cells in flat sheets; terminal bar; cilia; no 3D clustering; smooth nuclear membranes; low N:C ratio; degenerative vacuolation
Squamous cell carcinoma (lung)p40+, CK5/6+; TTF1−; keratinisation/dense cytoplasm; no mucin vacuoles; central location
Metastatic adenocarcinomaTTF1 negative (unless lung/thyroid primary); site-specific markers; clinical history; bilateral pulmonary disease
⚠ Pitfalls
  • Lepidic adenocarcinoma (AIS/MIA): flat honeycomb sheets of mildly atypical cells — easily under-called as reactive. Nuclei slightly enlarged, occasional nucleoli, no cilia.
  • Creola bodies: tight papillary clusters of normal bronchial epithelium in asthma/bronchiectasis — mimics adenocarcinoma. Clinical context + cilia essential.
  • Reserve sufficient material on cell block for molecular testing before ordering IHC — small bronchial biopsies exhausted by stains are a major clinical problem.
  • Mucinous adenocarcinoma: abundant mucin, low-grade nuclei — may be under-called. Single cells with intracytoplasmic mucin in a mucinous background.
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Report must include: 'Positive for malignancy — adenocarcinoma. Recommend cell block for IHC (TTF1/Napsin A) and molecular testing (EGFR/ALK/ROS1/KRAS/BRAF/MET/RET/NTRK/PD-L1).'
  • EGFR IHC not reliable — EGFR mutation must be confirmed by PCR/NGS. ALK IHC (D5F3) on cell block: sensitive and specific; positive ALK IHC → confirm with FISH or NGS.
  • ROS1 IHC (D4D6): sensitivity ~100%, specificity ~92% — positive IHC requires FISH confirmation.
  • PD-L1 22C3 pharmDx: score tumour proportion score (TPS) — ≥50% eligible for pembrolizumab monotherapy; 1–49% pembrolizumab + chemotherapy.
  • NMCS (non-small cell carcinoma, NOS): appropriate if morphology non-committal and IHC exhausted — must state 'molecular testing not feasible on available material.'
🔬 Histology Counterpart
↗ LUNG ADENOCARCINOMA
SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA (LUNG) — BRONCHIAL BRUSHINGS / FNA
Thoracic Bronchial brushings Positive for malignancy — Squamous cell carcinoma Malignant
Keratinising SCC: bizarre pleomorphic cells with dense orangeophilic cytoplasm (Papanicolaou); 'tadpole' and 'fibre' cells; keratin pearls; pyknotic/hyperchromatic nuclei; background tumour diathesis (necrosis).
Adequacy Criteria
  • Central SCC: bronchial brushings usually highly cellular. Exfoliating tumour cells may be present in sputum.
  • Cell block for IHC if non-keratinising SCC — p40/CK5/6 confirmation. No molecular testing routinely required for SCC (no targetable drivers).
Reporting System

Descriptive — Positive for malignancy — Squamous cell carcinoma

Cytological Features
  • Keratinising SCC: bizarre pleomorphic cells with dense orangeophilic cytoplasm (Papanicolaou); 'tadpole' and 'fibre' cells; keratin pearls; pyknotic/hyperchromatic nuclei; background tumour diathesis (necrosis).
  • Non-keratinising SCC: cohesive sheets and single cells with high N:C ratio; irregular hyperchromatic nuclei; coarse chromatin; squamoid cytoplasm (dense, homogeneous); no mucin vacuoles.
  • Background: necrotic tumour diathesis; inflammatory cells; haemorrhage. Keratinous debris in keratinising variant.
✚ Positive
p40 – diffuse nuclear (cell block)CK5/6 – diffuse (cell block)p63 – diffuse
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
TTF1 – negativeNapsin A – negativeSynaptophysin/Chromogranin – negative
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Adenocarcinoma (lung)TTF1+, Napsin A+; mucin vacuoles; glandular/papillary clusters; p40−; no keratinisation
SCLCSmall cells; nuclear moulding; crush artefact; Azzopardi effect; Synaptophysin+/CD56+; p40−; scant cytoplasm
Metastatic SCC (head & neck, cervical, oesophageal)Clinical history; HPV ISH (oropharyngeal/cervical); p16 (surrogate HPV); site imaging; bilateral/multiple lesions unusual for primary
⚠ Pitfalls
  • Squamous metaplasia: reactive squamous cells from inflamed bronchus — orderly maturation; no atypia; no tumour diathesis; clinical context (smoker, inflammation).
  • Aspergillus hyphae: septate branching hyphae with 45° angles in necrotic background can mimic keratinous debris — GMS/PAS stain if fungal infection suspected.
  • Non-keratinising SCC without IHC: difficult to distinguish from large cell carcinoma — always perform p40/TTF1 on cell block before reporting as NSCLC-NOS.
  • Radiation changes: marked nuclear enlargement with degenerate vacuolated cytoplasm — history of prior RT essential to avoid false positive.
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Keratinising SCC: morphological diagnosis — no IHC needed if classic features (orangeophilic cytoplasm, keratin pearls, bizarre cells).
  • No routine molecular testing for lung SCC — EGFR/ALK mutations rare. PD-L1 mandatory for immunotherapy stratification.
  • Pembrolizumab + chemotherapy (carboplatin/paclitaxel/nab-paclitaxel): standard first-line for advanced SCC regardless of PD-L1 score.
  • Sputum cytology: specific for central SCC (~70–80%) — excellent specificity for SCC if keratinising cells identified. Requires 3 consecutive morning samples.
🔬 Histology Counterpart
↗ SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA (LUNG)
EOSINOPHIL-RICH PLEURAL EFFUSION — DIFFERENTIAL FRAMEWORK (IAC Harrogate)
Thoracic Effusion Negative for malignancy — Eosinophil-rich Benign 📋 2×
Eosinophil-predominant: bilobed eosinophilic granular cells >10% of differential count; admixed reactive mesothelial cells; lymphocytes; macrophages.
Adequacy Criteria
  • IAC Harrogate: eosinophilic effusion defined as >10% eosinophils in differential cell count.
  • Cell block for IHC if any atypical cells identified. Flow cytometry if lymphoid component prominent.
Reporting System

IAC Harrogate (Effusion) — Negative for malignancy — Eosinophil-rich

Cytological Features
  • Eosinophil-predominant: bilobed eosinophilic granular cells >10% of differential count; admixed reactive mesothelial cells; lymphocytes; macrophages.
  • Causes — air/blood in pleural space most common: pneumothorax (iatrogenic/spontaneous), haemothorax, post-thoracentesis. Also: drugs (nitrofurantoin, dantrolene), parasites (Löffler), fungal infection, benign asbestos effusion, pulmonary infarction.
  • Malignancy rare cause (<5%) — eosinophilia does NOT exclude malignancy; always assess for atypical cells.
✚ Positive
N/A — morphological diagnosis; eosinophils: CD16−, CD15+, MBP+
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
Malignant cells absent (report as negative for malignancy if no atypia)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Reactive mesothelial hyperplasiaMesothelial cells predominant; eosinophils minor component; BAP1 retained; no p16 deletion; clinical context
Löffler syndrome / parasitic eosinophiliaPeripheral eosinophilia; Toxocara/Strongyloides serology; bilateral; associated with asthma; systemic eosinophilia
Drug-induced eosinophilic pleuritisDrug history (nitrofurantoin, dantrolene, bromocriptine, amiodarone); bilateral/unilateral; resolves on withdrawal
⚠ Pitfalls
  • Air/blood in pleural space is the most common cause — always ask about recent pneumothorax or thoracentesis on the request form.
  • Malignancy is an uncommon but real cause of eosinophilic effusion — do not report as benign without careful assessment of all cell populations.
  • Benign asbestos-related pleural effusion (BAPE): eosinophil-rich; asbestos exposure; exudate; bilateral or unilateral; diagnosis of exclusion after excluding malignancy/infection.
  • Churg-Strauss (EGPA): eosinophilic pleuritis + asthma + sinusitis + vasculitis + ANCA — systemic context.
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • IAC Harrogate: report as 'Negative for malignancy — eosinophil-rich effusion. Commonest causes include recent pneumothorax/haemothorax/thoracentesis and drug reactions. Clinical correlation recommended.'
  • Benign asbestos pleural effusion (BAPE): must exclude mesothelioma — BAP1/p16 FISH on mesothelial cells if any atypia; radiological follow-up.
  • Post-cardiac injury syndrome (Dressler): eosinophilic pericardial/pleural effusion weeks after MI or cardiac surgery; anti-heart antibodies.
  • Recurrent eosinophilic effusion despite treatment → pleural biopsy (VATS) to exclude mesothelioma or lymphoma.
BREAST FNA — FIVE-CATEGORY REPORTING (C1–C5)
Breast FNA C1–C5 framework Variable
C1 — Inadequate: acellular or insufficient epithelial cells; blood/fat only. Repeat FNA or core biopsy.
Adequacy Criteria
  • Adequate (C1–C5): ≥6 well-preserved epithelial cell clusters of ≥5 cells. C1 = inadequate/acellular; if repeatedly C1 → core needle biopsy.
  • Triple assessment: clinical (E) + imaging (U1–5/M1–5) + cytology/histology (C1–5/B1–5). All three must be concordant for benign diagnosis.
Reporting System

Descriptive — C1–C5 framework

Cytological Features
  • C1 — Inadequate: acellular or insufficient epithelial cells; blood/fat only. Repeat FNA or core biopsy.
  • C2 — Benign: cohesive flat sheets ('honeycomb'); bipolar naked nuclei in background (myoepithelial — pathognomonic for benign); foam cells; no nuclear atypia. Fibroadenoma: antler-horn sheets + stroma fragments + bipolar naked nuclei.
  • C3 — Atypia probably benign: mild nuclear enlargement or anisonucleosis; cohesive; bipolar naked nuclei present. ROM ~5–10%.
  • C4 — Suspicious: significant atypia; loss of cohesion; absent bipolar naked nuclei; nuclear pleomorphism; but not definitive malignancy. ROM ~80%.
  • C5 — Malignant: clearly malignant features; discohesive cells; nuclear pleomorphism; prominent nucleoli; necrosis; absent bipolar naked nuclei. ROM >95%.
✚ Positive
ER/PR – cell block if C5 (prognostic)GATA3 – breast lineage confirmation if needed
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
N/A — morphological reporting; IHC on core biopsy preferred for receptors
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Fibroadenoma (C2)Antler-horn/branching epithelial sheets; stroma fragments (metachromatic on Diff-Quik); abundant bipolar naked nuclei; no atypia
IDC/NST (C5)Discohesive malignant cells; high N:C ratio; prominent nucleoli; irregular nuclear membranes; absent bipolar nuclei; necrotic background
TNBC (C5)High-grade pleomorphic cells; necrosis; discohesion; often no ER/PR on smear; core biopsy for receptors/HER2
⚠ Pitfalls
  • Bipolar naked nuclei (BNN): oval bare nuclei in background = myoepithelial cells — pathognomonic for benign. Absent in malignancy — most important single cytological feature.
  • Fibroadenoma: hypercellular aspirates with mild atypia → C3; correlate with U2 imaging. Do not over-call as C4/C5.
  • Lactational change: markedly enlarged nuclei with prominent nucleoli + vacuolated cytoplasm — mimics C4/C5. Clinical history of pregnancy/lactation essential.
  • Mucinous carcinoma: abundant mucin + low-grade nuclei — easily under-called as C2/C3. Mucin background with epithelial cells at any grade → suspicious.
  • Core needle biopsy now preferred over FNA in most UK centres — FNA still used for rapid assessment in symptomatic clinics and LN staging.
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • RCPath reporting categories C1–C5 (equivalent to B1–B5 for core biopsy). ROM: C1 ~0%, C2 <1%, C3 ~10%, C4 ~75%, C5 >95%.
  • Bipolar naked nuclei (BNN): the single most important feature. Present = benign; absent = suspicious.
  • Triple assessment concordance: discordant results (e.g. C2 but U4/U5 imaging) → always defer to most abnormal modality and proceed to core/excision.
  • HER2 cannot be reliably assessed on FNA cytology — core biopsy or excision required for receptor status.
  • Inflammatory carcinoma: clinically diagnosed (>1/3 breast skin erythema/oedema/peau d'orange); dermal lymphatic invasion on biopsy (not required for clinical diagnosis). T4d regardless of tumour size.
🔬 Histology Counterpart
↗ INVASIVE BREAST CARCINOMA OF NO SPECIAL TYPE (NST / IDC)
PERITONEAL FLUID / ASCITES / WASHINGS — FRAMEWORK (IAC Harrogate)
LGI Effusion Negative / Atypical / Positive for malignancy Variable 📋 11×
Reactive/benign: mesothelial cells in flat sheets with intercellular windows; two-tone cytoplasm; smooth nuclear membranes; small nucleoli; macrophages; lymphocytes. Reactive atypia: multinucleation, prominent nucleoli — can be alarming.
Adequacy Criteria
  • IAC Harrogate: peritoneal fluid/washings adequate if sufficient cells for morphological assessment. Cell block mandatory for IHC.
  • Peritoneal washings at gynaecological staging: collected before manipulation; positive washings = FIGO staging information (IIIA1 for ovarian; IIIA for endometrial).
Reporting System

IAC Harrogate (Effusion) — Negative / Atypical / Positive for malignancy

Cytological Features
  • Reactive/benign: mesothelial cells in flat sheets with intercellular windows; two-tone cytoplasm; smooth nuclear membranes; small nucleoli; macrophages; lymphocytes. Reactive atypia: multinucleation, prominent nucleoli — can be alarming.
  • Malignant ascites (adenocarcinoma): cohesive three-dimensional clusters; nuclear pleomorphism; prominent nucleoli; mucin vacuoles; necrotic background. Most common: ovarian (HGSC), colorectal, gastric, pancreatic, endometrial, breast (ILC).
  • Peritoneal washings (staging): even single unequivocal malignant cell cluster = positive. Mesothelial hyperplasia = negative — clinical context essential.
✚ Positive
CK7 – adenocarcinomaBerEP4/MOC31 – carcinoma (vs mesothelium)WT1 – HGSC/mesotheliomaSATB2 – colorectalGATA3 – breast/urothelialPAX8 – Müllerian/renal
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
Calretinin – negative in adenocarcinoma (positive in mesothelial cells)CK5/6 – negative in adenocarcinoma
Top Differentials + Discriminators
Reactive mesothelial hyperplasia vs malignant mesotheliomaBAP1 IHC loss + CDKN2A FISH deletion = mesothelioma; reactive: BAP1 retained, p16 intact; clinical/CT context
ILC (lobular breast) in ascitesGATA3+, ER+, mammaglobulin+; E-cadherin lost; discohesive single cells; CK7+; 'targetoid' arrangement around mesothelial cells
ccRCC in peritoneal fluid (rare)PAX8+, CA9+, CD10+; clear cytoplasm with prominent nucleoli; Calretinin−; BerEP4 variable; clinical history of RCC
⚠ Pitfalls
  • Reactive mesothelial atypia: multinucleation, large nucleoli, 'windows' between cells — BAP1 IHC essential if mesothelioma suspected. Do NOT diagnose mesothelioma on cytology without BAP1 loss or p16 FISH.
  • Poorly cohesive carcinoma (gastric/ILC): single discohesive cells — easily mistaken for reactive lymphocytes or macrophages; GATA3/CK7 on cell block essential.
  • Pseudomyxoma peritonei (PMP): mucinous ascites with scant low-grade mucinous epithelium — cells may be very sparse; KRAS/GNAS PCR from fluid can support LAMN/appendiceal origin.
  • Peritoneal washing false positives: mechanical displacement of benign cells — correlate with clinical findings; do not over-call based on rare atypical cells in washing.
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Most common causes of malignant ascites: ovarian carcinoma > colorectal > gastric > pancreatic > breast (ILC) > lymphoma > mesothelioma.
  • IHC panel for unknown primary in ascites: CK7/CK20 + BerEP4 + Calretinin + WT1 + PAX8 + SATB2 + GATA3 + CDX2 — covers most primaries.
  • Signet ring cell in ascites: gastric (MUC5AC+, SATB2−, CDH1 loss) vs ILC (GATA3+, ER+, CDH1 loss) vs SRCC of other origin — CDH1 loss shared; site-specific markers discriminate.
  • PMP cytology: acellular mucin pools = non-diagnostic; scant mucinous epithelium (may be low-grade) = report cellularity and cytological grade; KRAS/GNAS on fluid for molecular support.
SEROUS FLUID — ADENOCARCINOMA SITE-OF-ORIGIN DDx (IHC ALGORITHM)
Thoracic Effusion Positive for malignancy — Adenocarcinoma (site-specific IHC required) Malignant
STEP 1 — MESOTHELIAL vs NON-MESOTHELIAL: Calretinin, WT1, D2-40 positive = mesothelial lineage → proceed to BAP1/MTAP/CDKN2A algorithm (see Mesothelioma entry). BerEP4+, MOC31+, B72.3+ = epithelial (non-mesothelial).
Adequacy Criteria
  • Cell block mandatory for IHC-based site-of-origin workup. Minimum 2 levels from cell block; retain unstained sections for extended panel.
  • If cell block insufficient: repeat tap or liquid biopsy (cfDNA) for molecular site assignment.
Reporting System

IAC Harrogate (Effusion) — Positive for malignancy — Adenocarcinoma (site-specific IHC required)

Cytological Features
  • STEP 1 — MESOTHELIAL vs NON-MESOTHELIAL: Calretinin, WT1, D2-40 positive = mesothelial lineage → proceed to BAP1/MTAP/CDKN2A algorithm (see Mesothelioma entry). BerEP4+, MOC31+, B72.3+ = epithelial (non-mesothelial).
  • STEP 2 — EPITHELIAL SUBCLASSIFICATION: p40/p63+ + Chromogranin− + Synaptophysin− = SCC. Chromogranin+/Synaptophysin+ + p40/p63− = NE neoplasm (SCLC until proven otherwise). p40/p63− + Chromo/Synapto− = Adenocarcinoma → proceed to site-specific panel.
  • STEP 3 — ADENOCARCINOMA SITE-SPECIFIC IHC PANEL: CK7/CK20 + WT1 + PAX8 + TTF1 + NapsinA + GATA3 + ER + CDX2 + SATB2 + Calretinin (as mesothelial control).
  • NON-EPITHELIAL (BerEP4−/MOC31−/B72.3−): consider haematological (CD45/CD20/CD3), melanocytic (S100/SOX10/HMB45/MelanA), mesenchymal (Vimentin/SMA/Desmin), germ cell (PLAP/OCT3-4/AFP).
✚ Positive
HGSC (ovary/FT/peritoneum): WT1++ PAX8+ p53-aberrant p16-block CK7+ BerEP4+Endometrial carcinoma: PAX8+ ER+ CK7+ WT1-NEGATIVE (key vs HGSC)Lung adenocarcinoma: TTF1+ NapsinA+ CK7+ BerEP4+ PAX8−Breast carcinoma: GATA3+ ER+/− Mammaglobulin+ GCDFP-15+ CK7+ WT1− PAX8−Colorectal: CK20+ CDX2++ SATB2+ CK7−/+ BerEP4+Gastric: CK7+ MUC5AC+ CK20−/+ CDX2−/+ SATB2− E-cadherin loss (signet ring)Pancreatic/biliary: CK7+ CK17+ MUC1+ CDX2−/+ (no specific marker; diagnosis of exclusion within adenocarcinoma)ccRCC: PAX8+ CA9+ CD10+ RCC-ma+ CK7−/low Calretinin− BerEP4 variable
✖ Negative (key exclusions)
HGSC: CK20− WT1+ (not negative — listed for clarity); ER−/weak; CDX2−Endometrial: WT1− (KEY discriminator vs HGSC); HNF1β+ NapsinA+ in clear cell typeLung: PAX8− (distinguishes from thyroid/Müllerian); WT1−; GATA3−Breast: WT1− PAX8− TTF1− NapsinA− CDX2−Colorectal: CK7− (classic); WT1−; GATA3−Mesothelioma: BerEP4− MOC31− B72.3− (key panel — all negative in mesothelioma)
Top Differentials + Discriminators
HGSC vs Endometrial serous carcinomaWT1: HGSC positive, endometrial serous NEGATIVE — single most useful discriminator. Morphologically identical. Clinical/imaging context essential (ovarian mass vs uterine primary)
HGSC vs Malignant mesotheliomaBoth WT1+; discriminate: BerEP4/MOC31+ (HGSC), Calretinin/CK5/6/D2-40+ (meso); BAP1 loss + CDKN2A del = mesothelioma. Never rely on WT1 alone
Lung adenocarcinoma vs Thyroid carcinomaBoth TTF1+; Thyroglobulin and NapsinA discriminate (Thyroglobulin+ = thyroid; NapsinA+ = lung; thyroid NapsinA negative)
Gastric signet ring vs ILC (breast)Both: discohesive single cells, E-cadherin loss, CK7+. Discriminate: GATA3+/ER+/mammaglobulin+ = ILC; MUC5AC+/SATB2−/CDH1 mutation = gastric. Clinical history essential
Colorectal vs Appendiceal/mucinous primaryBoth SATB2+/CK20+/CDX2+; appendiceal: mucinous background, KRAS/GNAS mutations, PMP clinical context. IHC cannot reliably distinguish — molecular and clinical correlation required
Pancreatic/biliary vs other adenocarcinomaNo specific positive marker for pancreatic/biliary adenocarcinoma — diagnosis of exclusion when CK7+/CK20−/PAX8−/TTF1−/GATA3−/SATB2−/WT1−. CK17 and MUC1 supportive but non-specific
⚠ Pitfalls
  • WT1 is positive in BOTH HGSC and mesothelioma — NEVER use WT1 alone to diagnose HGSC in an effusion. Always co-stain with BerEP4/MOC31 and Calretinin/CK5/6.
  • HMB45 and MelanA are NEGATIVE in desmoplastic melanoma — always include S100 and SOX10 to exclude melanoma in any BerEP4-negative effusion with pleomorphic cells.
  • Desmoplastic melanoma and spindle cell melanoma can shed into effusions — non-epithelial morphology + S100+ is the clue.
  • NapsinA: also positive in thyroid clear cell carcinoma and type II pneumocytes (reactive) — always co-stain with TTF1 and Thyroglobulin.
  • E-cadherin loss is shared by gastric signet ring and ILC — use GATA3/ER/mammaglobulin to distinguish. Do NOT diagnose ILC on E-cadherin loss alone.
  • SATB2 is positive in colorectal AND appendiceal — cannot distinguish these two by IHC alone.
  • Cell block quality is the rate-limiting step: insufficient block = cannot run full panel = report as 'adenocarcinoma, site-of-origin not determinable on available material; clinical and radiological correlation required'.
Comments — Molecular / Risk / Management
  • Minimum first-line panel for unknown malignant effusion (adenocarcinoma): CK7, CK20, BerEP4, Calretinin, WT1, PAX8, TTF1, NapsinA, GATA3, ER — covers the great majority of primaries and separates from mesothelioma.
  • MTAP IHC: now preferred over p16 FISH as first-line screen for malignant mesothelioma (co-deleted with CDKN2A at 9p21). Loss of MTAP + loss of BAP1 = highly specific for mesothelioma.
  • Most common causes of malignant pleural effusion: lung > breast > lymphoma > mesothelioma > ovary. Most common malignant peritoneal effusion: ovary > colorectal > gastric > pancreatic > breast (ILC).
  • IAC Harrogate reporting: 'Positive for malignancy — adenocarcinoma; IHC profile consistent with [primary site]; clinical and radiological correlation recommended for site confirmation.'
  • Molecular ancillary testing: if IHC panel inconclusive, cfDNA from fluid (liquid biopsy) for somatic mutation profiling (KRAS, BRAF, PIK3CA, TP53 patterns) can support site-of-origin assignment.

Each diagnosis requires correlation of all three modalities.

🔬 LM — architecture, cellularity, crescents, deposits 💛 IF — immunoglobulin/complement localisation and pattern 🔷 EM — deposit electron density, location, substructure
